The filled pouch is sealed and then subjected to **retort sterilization** at 121 °C for 15–45 minutes in a pressurized steam retort. The food is fully cooked and sterilized inside the sealed pouch.

**Shelf-life:** 12–24 months at ambient temperature.

**3. Comparison Table (included within the 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Aseptic Packaging | Retort Pouch Packaging |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Sterilization Method | UHT (product) + H₂O₂ (package) | Retort autoclave (combined) |
| Package Form | Rigid carton / bottle / pouch | Flexible laminated pouch |
| Material Structure | Paperboard / PE / Al foil / PE | PET / Al foil / PA / CPP |
| Process Temperature | 135–150 °C (UHT) | 121 °C (retort) |
| Shelf-Life (ambient) | 6–12 months | 12–24 months |
| Typical Foods | Milk, juice, wine, soup | Curry, ready-meals, pet food |
| Cost | Higher (carton machinery) | Lower (pouch FFS lines) |

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Packaging ≠ Container.** A *packaging system* includes materials, headspace gas, active agents, and intelligent sensors.
- ✅ **The PAI Triangle (Passive – Active – Intelligent)** is a KTU-favorite conceptual question.
- ✅ **MAP** alters gas composition; **Vacuum Packaging** removes air; **Aseptic** sterilizes product and package separately; **Retort** sterilizes inside the package.
- ✅ **N₂ is inert filler gas** — prevents package collapse due to low solubility in water/fat.
- ✅ **Oxygen scavengers** extend MAP shelf-life by 30–100 %.
- ✅ **Permeability equation** $P = D \cdot S$ — high-barrier films have low $P$ (EVOH, PVDC, metallized PET).
- ✅ **Arrhenius shelf-life model:** $S_L \propto e^{E_a/RT}$ — a 10 °C rise can cut shelf-life by half (or more).
- ✅ **TTI kinetics must match food deterioration kinetics** — calibrated via $E_a$.
- ✅ **FSSAI regulates** food contact materials in India; **EU 1935/2004** sets migration limits globally.
- ✅ **Selection logic = product $a_w$ + pH + shelf-life target + cold-chain availability + cost.**
- ✅ **Edible and biodegradable packaging** are emerging sustainable systems — covered as KTU 2024 elective topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Characteristics and Package Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Product Characteristics and Package Requirements --- # 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Official Definition:** > **Product Characteristics and Package Requirements** refers to the systematic identification, analysis, and mapping of the intrinsic physico-chemical, biological, and sensory attributes of a food product to the corresponding functional, barrier, mechanical, and interactive performance specifications demanded of its packaging system. It establishes the *causal design link* between the food being protected and the package being engineered. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** framework, this topic is treated as the **foundational design node** of any packaging project. The official course outcome tied to this module is: > **CO1:** *Identify and analyse the intrinsic characteristics of food products and translate them into specific packaging requirements.* ## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Product Characterization The KTU syllabus divides product characteristics into **three orthogonal pillars**, each of which directly dictates a category of packaging performance: | Pillar | Sub-Attributes Measured | Directly Drives Packaging Property | |---|---|---| | **Physical** | Size, shape, density, moisture, fragility | Mechanical strength, dimensions, cushioning | | **Chemical** | pH, fat content, water activity (aw), reactivity | Barrier (O₂, H₂O, CO₂), chemical resistance | | **Biological** | Microbial load, respiration rate, enzymatic activity | Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP), antimicrobial layer | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bodyguard Fitting" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Pump:** Imagine you are hiring a **bodyguard** for a VIP. You don't assign a 6-foot-5 wrestler to protect a 5-year-old child at a school — the mismatch is dangerous. Similarly, you do not pack **fresh strawberries** in a glass jar (mechanical overkill + light degradation), nor do you pack **hot edible oil** in a thin LDPE pouch (chemical failure). Packaging is **NOT a generic box** — it is a **tailored protective system** engineered to the *exact* fingerprint of the food. The **fingerprint** of the food is the combined vector: $$ \vec{F}_{\text{food}} = \big[\,a_w,\ pH,\ \%\text{fat},\ T_{\text{sens}},\ \text{Resp}_{\text{rate}},\ \text{Fragility}\,\big] $$ The packaging must **mirror** this vector with its own vector of barrier and mechanical properties. This matching process is the heart of Module 1. ## 1.4 Why This Topic Carries High KTU Weightage - It is a **mandatory 14-mark part B question** in nearly every past ESE paper. - It directly feeds into Module 2 (Packaging Materials) and Module 3 (MAP/Active Packaging). - Examiner expects students to **map** specific product traits to **specific package properties** — this is the most common skill tested. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always answer by writing **"Because the product has [trait X], the package must have [property Y]"**. This phrasing scores full marks. --- # 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Product Characteristics ### 2.1.1 Physical Characteristics These are the **dimensionless, observable, and measurable** attributes of the product mass: - **Size and Shape** → dictates *primary package geometry* and *fill volume*. - **Density (ρ)** → determines whether the package must be **rigid** (high-density) or **flexible** (low-density bulk). - **Hardness / Fragility** → dictates **cushioning, drop resistance, and stacking strength** requirements. - **Moisture Content** → drives the required **Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)** of the package. - **Surface Roughness / Abrasion** → affects **friction coefficient** of inner liner. ### 2.1.2 Chemical Characteristics These are the **molecular-level attributes** that dictate barrier and compatibility needs: - **Water Activity (aw)** — *the single most important chemical parameter in food packaging*. - **pH** — acidic foods (pH < 4.5) require **acid-resistant** inner coatings. - **Fat Content / Oil Fraction** — fatty foods trigger **migration of plasticizers** → requires **fat-resistant** layer. - **Oxidative Sensitivity** — high PUFA foods (fish, nuts) require **O₂-barrier ≤ 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹**. - **Aroma Volatility** — strong-flavor foods require **aroma-retention** films. ### 2.1.3 Biological Characteristics These are the **living-system attributes** that change *with time*: - **Respiration Rate (R)** — for fresh fruits/vegetables: $$ R = \frac{mL\ CO_2}{kg \cdot h} $$ - **Ethylene Production Rate** — climacteric fruits (banana, apple) emit C₂H₄ → triggers **ethylene-scavenging** packaging. - **Microbial Spoilage Potential** — high-protein foods (meat, dairy) require **aseptic** or **vacuum** packaging. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU aw Master Rule (High-Yield):** > - aw > 0.85 → Microbial spoilage is the dominant failure mode → requires **moisture + O₂ barrier + refrigeration**. > - 0.60 < aw < 0.85 → Chemical/enzymatic spoilage dominates → requires **O₂ + light barrier**. > - aw < 0.60 → Only *chemical* reactions (oxidation, Maillard) remain → requires **O₂ barrier only**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **board-exam reliable**. Memorize units exactly — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit errors. | # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | Water activity = partial vapour pressure / sat. vapour pressure | dimensionless | | 2 | $\text{WVTR} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | Water Vapour Transmission Rate | g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | | 3 | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{\Delta p \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Oxygen Transmission Rate | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ | | 4 | $Q = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | Steady-state gas flux through film (Fick + Henry combined) | cm³·day⁻¹ | | 5 | $J = -D \,\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | g·cm⁻²·s⁻¹ | | 6 | $t_{sl} = \dfrac{m_{\text{crit}}}{R_{\text{O}_2}\cdot A}$ | Estimated shelf life from O₂ ingress | days | | 7 | $R_{\text{fresh}} = k \cdot \dfrac{dm}{dt}$ | Respiration rate of fresh produce | mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹ | | 8 | $\%M = \dfrac{m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (% wet basis) | % | | 9 | $K_{\text{mig}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{food}} \cdot V_{\text{food}}}{C_{\text{polymer}} \cdot V_{\text{polymer}}}$ | Migration partition coefficient | dimensionless | | 10 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Bulk density | kg·m⁻³ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Unit Trap:** Never write WVTR as "g/day" — you **must** normalize to area (m²) and time (day). Examiners deduct **0.5 marks** for missing units. --- ## 2.3 The "Product → Package" Mapping Logic This is the **central algorithm** of Module 1, and it is what KTU examiners love to test: > **Step 1:** Identify *all* intrinsic characteristics of the product (build the fingerprint vector). > **Step 2:** Rank them by *severity of consequence* if the package fails to address them. > **Step 3:** Translate each high-severity characteristic into a **specific package property**. > **Step 4:** Cross-check with **regulatory** (FSSAI, FDA, EU 10/2011) and **sustainability** constraints. ### 2.3.1 Engineering Utility in Real Industry This mapping logic is used daily in: - **Shelf-stable ready-to-eat meals** (MTR, ITC) - **Dairy packaging** (Amul, Mother Dairy) - **Pharma-foods** (GSK, Himalaya) - **Frozen foods** (McCain, Venky's) The same logic governs **military ration packaging (MRE)** — the ultimate KTU-relevant real-world example because it touches *every* product characteristic simultaneously. --- # 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shelf Life from Oxygen Ingress This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU ESE for this module. We derive the relationship between the package's OTR and the product's acceptable shelf life. ### Starting Premise (Fick's First Law + Henry's Law) A polymer film of thickness *L* separates an *outside atmosphere* (partial pressure p₁) from an *inside package headspace* (partial pressure p₂). The steady-state flux of O₂ through the film is given by Fick's law combined with Henry's solubility: $$ J = \frac{P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L} $$ Where: - $J$ = oxygen flux per unit area (cm³ O₂ · m⁻² · day⁻¹) - $P$ = permeability coefficient of the polymer for O₂ - $L$ = film thickness - $(p_1 - p_2)$ = partial pressure gradient of O₂ ### Step 1 — Total O₂ entering the package per day If the package has internal surface area $A$, then the total O₂ volume entering per day is: $$ Q_{\text{day}} = J \cdot A = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L} $$ > **Logic step:** We multiply the per-area flux by the total area because the *whole* package surface contributes. ### Step 2 — Expressing (p₁ − p₂) using Henry's Law The concentration of O₂ inside the food is related to the headspace partial pressure by Henry's constant $k_H$: $$ C_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{food}} = k_H \cdot p_2 $$ Assuming the food tolerates a *critical* oxygen concentration $C_{\text{crit}}$ before spoilage, the corresponding critical partial pressure is: $$ p_{\text{crit}} = \frac{C_{\text{crit}}}{k_H} $$ The driving gradient is therefore: $$ p_1 - p_2 = p_1 - p_{\text{crit}} $$ ### Step 3 — Defining the critical O₂ dose The food can tolerate a finite *total mass* of O₂ before spoilage. If the food mass is $m_f$, then: $$ m_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{crit}} = C_{\text{crit}} \cdot m_f $$ ### Step 4 — Time to reach critical O₂ The shelf life is the time for the integrated ingress to equal $m_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{crit}}$. Assuming constant gradient (valid for short films, < 100 µm): $$ t_{sl} = \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{crit}}}{J \cdot A} $$ Substituting: $$ t_{sl} = \frac{C_{\text{crit}} \cdot m_f \cdot L}{P \cdot A \cdot (p_1 - p_{\text{crit}})} $$ > **Final boxed result** — this is the **formula you write in the exam:** $$ \boxed{\,t_{sl} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\text{crit}}\, m_f\, L}{P\, A\, (p_1 - p_{\text{crit}})}\,} $$ ### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation - Doubling the **film thickness** $L$ → doubles shelf life (linear). - Reducing **permeability** $P$ (e.g., adding EVOH layer) → linear improvement. - Increasing **package area** $A$ → *shortens* shelf life (more ingress surface). - Reducing **external O₂ partial pressure** $p_1$ (e.g., vacuum or N₂ flush) → extends shelf life dramatically. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Water Activity and Equilibrium Moisture Content A second high-yield derivation connects aw to the critical moisture gain of a dehydrated product. ### Sorption Isotherm (BET/Oswin simplified form) For many dry foods at intermediate moisture, the Oswin equation gives: $$ a_w = \left[\, 1 + \left(\dfrac{m_0}{m}\right)^{n} \,\right]^{-1} $$ Where $m_0$ is the monolayer moisture and $n$ is a product-specific constant. ### Step 1 — Critical moisture for texture failure A crisp product (chips, biscuits) loses crispness when $a_w$ rises above a critical value $a_w^{\text{crit}}$. Rearranging for moisture content $m_{\text{crit}}$: $$ m_{\text{crit}} = m_0 \cdot \left[\, \dfrac{1 - a_w^{\text{crit}}}{a_w^{\text{crit}}} \,\right]^{1/n} $$ ### Step 2 — Moisture gain from package The mass of water vapour entering per day through a film of WVTR is: $$ \Delta m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \text{WVTR} \cdot A \cdot t $$ ### Step 3 — Shelf life from crispness loss $$ \boxed{\,t_{sl}^{\text{crisp}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{crit}} - m_{\text{initial}}}{\text{WVTR} \cdot A}\,} $$ > [!TIP] > **Numerical habit:** If a 50 g pack of biscuits is exposed to WVTR = 5 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ through an effective area of 0.02 m², it gains 0.1 g water/day. If crispness fails at 3.0 g water gain, shelf life = 30 days. Always do this *back-of-envelope* check in exam. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Product-to-Package Mapping Table This matrix is the **single most exam-useful artifact** of Module 1. Memorize the logic, not the words. | Product Characteristic | Consequence If Ignored | Required Package Property | |---|---|---| | High aw (> 0.85) | Microbial spoilage | High O₂ + moisture barrier; refrigerate | | High fat content | Lipid migration into polymer; rancidity | Fat-resistant inner layer (PET, EVOH) | | High acidity (pH < 4.5) | Corrosion of metal can; acid-hydrolysis of polymer | Acid-resistant coating (epoxy-phenolic) | | Light-sensitive (vitamins, beer) | Photo-oxidation, off-flavour | Opaque / UV-blocking pigment (TiO₂, carbon black) | | Fragile (chips, eggs) | Mechanical breakage | Cushioning, air-cell, bubble wrap | | Respiring (fresh fruits) | Anaerobic respiration, ethanol build-up | Micro-perforated film for gas exchange | | Ethylene-emitting (banana, apple) | Accelerated ripening of neighbours | Ethylene scavenger sachet (KMnO₄) | | Aroma-strong (cheese, coffee) | Aroma loss / cross-contamination | High aroma-barrier (EVOH, Al-foil) | | Free-flowing powder (flour, milk) | Moisture caking, lumping | Moisture barrier + easy-pour spout | | Hot-fill (> 80 °C) | Package deformation | Heat-set polymer (CPP, C-PET) | | Frozen (< −18 °C) | Film brittleness, freezer burn | Low-temperature flexible film (LDPE, EVA) | --- # 🟢 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Product-Characteristics-to-Package Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A["FOOD PRODUCT ENTERS DESIGN STAGE"] --> B["CHARACTERIZE THE PRODUCT"] B --> B1["PHYSICAL: size, density, fragility"] B --> B2["CHEMICAL: a_w, pH, fat %"] B --> B3["BIOLOGICAL: microbes, respiration"] B1 --> C1["DEFINE MECHANICAL NEEDS"] B2 --> C2["DEFINE BARRIER NEEDS"] B3 --> C3["DEFINE ATMOSPHERE NEEDS"] C1 --> D["SELECT PACKAGING SYSTEM"] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E1["PRIMARY: direct contact"] D --> E2["SECONDARY: display, grouping"] D --> E3["TERTIARY: transport pallet"] E1 --> F["VALIDATE: shelf life, migration, regulations"] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G["FINAL PACKAGING SPECIFICATION"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The Product Fingerprint Vector & Package Response ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FOOD["PRODUCT FINGERPRINT VECTOR F_food"] P1["a_w (water activity)"] P2["pH (acidity)"] P3["Fat content (%)"] P4["Respiration rate (R)"] P5["Fragility index"] P6["Light sensitivity"] end subgraph PKG["PACKAGING RESPONSE VECTOR P_pkg"] Q1["WVTR requirement"] Q2["Acid-resistant coating"] Q3["Fat-barrier inner layer"] Q4["MAP gas mixture"] Q5["Cushioning, drop-test rating"] Q6["UV-blocking pigment"] end P1 -->|"maps to"| Q1 P2 -->|"maps to"| Q2 P3 -->|"maps to"| Q3 P4 -->|"maps to"| Q4 P5 -->|"maps to"| Q5 P6 -->|"maps to"| Q6 style FOOD fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style PKG fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Shelf Life Failure Modes as a Function of aw ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PRODUCT a_w VALUE"] --> B{"a_w > 0.85 ?"} B -->|"YES"| C["MICROBIAL SPOILAGE
Bacteria, Yeast, Mould
Need: HIGH BARRIER + COLD CHAIN"] B -->|"NO"| D{"0.60 < a_w <= 0.85 ?"} D -->|"YES"| E["ENZYMATIC + CHEMICAL
Lipolysis, Browning
Need: O2 + LIGHT BARRIER"] D -->|"NO"| F["a_w < 0.60
DRY PRODUCTS
Only Oxidation, Maillard
Need: O2 BARRIER ONLY"] C --> G["PACKAGE: METAL CAN / RETORT POUCH / VACUUM"] E --> H["PACKAGE: MAP / EVOH MULTILAYER"] F --> I["PACKAGE: AL-FOIL LAMINATE / MET-PET"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Product-Classification-to-Packaging Map (KTU Favorite Sketch) ```mermaid flowchart TD S["START: Classify the food"] --> T1["LOW a_w DRY FOODS
e.g. biscuits, milk powder"] S --> T2["INTERMEDIATE a_w
e.g. cheese, bread"] S --> T3["HIGH a_w WET FOODS
e.g. meat, milk, fresh fruits"] S --> T4["LIQUID FOODS
e.g. oil, juice, sauce"] T1 --> U1["Flexible laminate:
BOPP / MET-PET / PE"] T2 --> U2["Modified Atmosphere:
PET / EVOH / PE"] T3 --> U3["Vacuum or MAP:
PA / EVOH / PE pouch
or rigid PP tray"] T4 --> U4["Aseptic carton or
glass / PET bottle"] style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style T1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style T2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style T3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style T4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style U1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style U2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style U3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style U4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` --- # 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define water activity (aw) and state its significance in deciding food packaging requirements.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Water activity (aw) is defined as the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in the food ($p$) to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature ($p_0$): > $$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$ > It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 (bone-dry) to 1.0 (pure water). > **Significance (1.5 marks):** aw determines the *dominant spoilage mechanism* of a food and therefore dictates the *required barrier properties* of its package. Foods with aw > 0.85 are prone to microbial spoilage and need high moisture + O₂ barrier + cold chain. Foods with 0.60 < aw < 0.85 undergo enzymatic/chemical spoilage and require O₂ + light barrier. Foods with aw < 0.60 require only O₂ barrier to prevent oxidation and Maillard browning. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any six intrinsic characteristics of a food product that influence packaging design.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Water activity (aw)** — drives barrier and cold-chain requirements. 2. **pH** — drives acid-resistance of inner coating. 3. **Fat content** — drives fat-resistance and migration considerations. 4. **Mechanical fragility** — drives cushioning and drop-test specifications. 5. **Light sensitivity** — drives opacity/UV-blocker requirement. 6. **Respiration rate** (for fresh produce) — drives MAP gas mixture and perforation design. *(Acceptable alternatives: moisture content, aroma volatility, microbial load, ethylene emission, oxygen sensitivity, free-flow characteristics, bulk density.)* --- ## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### 📌 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks > `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]` > **(a)** Explain the **physical and chemical characteristics of food products** that determine their packaging requirements. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **water activity (aw)** dictates the choice of packaging material and barrier properties. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Physical and Chemical Characteristics (7 marks)** The packaging engineer must systematically characterize the product along **two major axes**: **(i) Physical Characteristics (3.5 marks):** | Attribute | Packaging Implication | |---|---| | **Size & Shape** | Determines primary package geometry and fill volume | | **Bulk Density (ρ)** | High-density products need rigid packs; low-density bulk products use flexible pouches with air-cushion | | **Mechanical Fragility** | Eggs, chips, biscuits need cushioning, drop resistance | | **Moisture Content** | Drives WVTR specification of the package | | **Surface texture** | Affects friction coefficient and inner-liner selection | **(ii) Chemical Characteristics (3.5 marks):** | Attribute | Packaging Implication | |---|---| | **Water Activity (aw)** | Master driver of barrier and refrigeration requirements | | **pH** | Acidic foods need epoxy-phenolic inner coating | | **Fat content** | High-fat foods need fat-resistant polymer to prevent migration | | **Oxidative sensitivity** | PUFA-rich foods (fish, nuts) need OTR ≤ 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | | **Aroma volatility** | Coffee, cheese need high aroma-barrier (EVOH, Al-foil) | > **Synthesis:** Each physical and chemical trait is translated into a *measurable packaging specification* (WVTR, OTR, cushion thickness, pH-resistance rating). --- **(b) Water Activity and Packaging Choice — Worked Example (7 marks)** **Example chosen:** *Roasted and ground coffee* (aw ≈ 0.10 – 0.30, dry product). **Step 1 — Identify dominant spoilage mode (2 marks):** Since aw < 0.60, microbial growth is impossible. The *only* relevant spoilage mechanisms are: - **Lipid oxidation** (rancidity) - **Aroma loss** (volatilization of coffee aromatics) - **Maillard browning** (slow) **Step 2 — Translate to barrier properties (3 marks):** | Spoilage Mode | Required Package Property | Material Choice | |---|---|---| | Lipid oxidation | OTR ≤ 1 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ | EVOH layer or Al-foil | | Aroma loss | Aroma-barrier (high) | Al-foil laminate (zero OTR) | | Moisture re-absorption | WVTR ≤ 0.5 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | Al-foil + LDPE sealing layer | | Light-induced staling | Opaque | Metallized PET | **Step 3 — Final package specification (2 marks):** A typical laminate for ground coffee becomes: **PET (print) / Al-foil (9 µm) / LDPE (seal)** — a 3-ply structure offering essentially zero O₂, zero moisture, zero light, and zero aroma transmission. > **Conclusion:** The product's aw alone *eliminates* the need for refrigeration, *eliminates* antimicrobial packaging, and *concentrates* all design effort on oxygen, light, and aroma barriers. This is a textbook example of aw-driven packaging selection. --- ### 📌 Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks > `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]` > **(a)** Discuss the **biological characteristics** of fresh fruits and vegetables that affect packaging requirements. How does **modified atmosphere packaging (MAP)** address these? **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A 250 g block of frozen fish is packaged in a multilayer pouch of effective area 0.03 m², OTR 8 cm³·m²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹, and permeability coefficient $P = 1.2 \times 10^{-13}$ cm³·cm·cm⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹. The critical oxygen concentration in fish is 5 mg/100 g, beyond which rancidity sets in. Estimate the **shelf life** in days. Assume $p_1 - p_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.21$ atm, fish density ≈ 1.05 g/cm³. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Biological Characteristics of Fresh Produce (7 marks)** Fresh fruits and vegetables are **living tissues** that continue to respire after harvest. This makes them *unique* in the packaging universe. **(i) Respiration (2 marks):** Consumes O₂ and releases CO₂: $$ C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \longrightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Energy} $$ The respiration rate $R$ (mL CO₂·kg⁻¹·h⁻¹) classifies produce as: - Very low: potato, onion - Low: apple, citrus - Moderate: banana, tomato - High: strawberry, broccoli **(ii) Ethylene Emission (1.5 marks):** Climacteric fruits (banana, apple, tomato, mango) emit C₂H₄ which *accelerates ripening* of neighbouring produce. Packaging must either: - **Vent** the ethylene (perforated PE), or - **Scavenge** it (KMnO₄ sachet inside MAP). **(iii) Transpiration & Moisture Loss (1.5 marks):** Fresh produce loses water through skin, causing wilting and shrivelling. MAP must maintain **high relative humidity (90–95 %)** *inside* the pack while still permitting gas exchange. **(iv) Microbial Spoilage (2 marks):** Surface moulds and bacteria (especially in cut produce) demand: - Sanitized handling, - Low-temperature chain, - Sometimes antimicrobial coatings (chitosan, essential oils). **How MAP Addresses These (Integrated throughout):** MAP replaces the package atmosphere with a tailored mix (typically 2–5 % O₂, 3–10 % CO₂, balance N₂). This: - **Suppresses respiration** by ~30–50 %, - **Inhibits microbial growth** (CO₂ is bacteriostatic), - **Delays ethylene action**, - **Reduces transpiration** by saturating internal RH. > **Example:** Strawberry in 5 % O₂ + 10 % CO₂ + 85 % N₂ at 5 °C extends shelf life from 3 days (ambient) to **7–10 days**. --- **(b) Numerical Shelf Life of Frozen Fish (7 marks)** **Given data:** - Fish mass $m_f = 250$ g - Critical O₂ concentration $C_{\text{crit}} = 5\ \text{mg}/100\ \text{g}$ - Effective package area $A = 0.03\ \text{m}^2$ - $p_1 - p_{\text{crit}} = 0.21\ \text{atm}$ - Permeability coefficient $P = 1.2 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{cm}^3\cdot\text{cm}\cdot\text{cm}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}\cdot\text{Pa}^{-1}$ - Film thickness (assume typical) $L = 100\ \mu\text{m} = 100 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm} = 0.01\ \text{cm}$ > **Note to students:** For numeric consistency, KTU often provides $L$ implicitly through OTR. Since OTR is given, we use a *simpler equivalent formula*: > $$J = \text{OTR} \cdot (p_1 - p_{\text{crit}})$$ **Step 1 — Compute the critical O₂ mass (1 mark):** $$ m_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{crit}} = \frac{5\ \text{mg}}{100\ \text{g}} \times 250\ \text{g} = 12.5\ \text{mg} $$ Convert to volume at STP (1 mg O₂ ≈ 0.7 cm³ at NTP): $$ V_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{crit}} = 12.5 \times 0.7 = 8.75\ \text{cm}^3\ \text{O}_2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute daily O₂ ingress (2 marks):** $$ Q_{\text{day}} = \text{OTR} \cdot A \cdot (p_1 - p_{\text{crit}}) $$ $$ Q_{\text{day}} = 8 \times 0.03 \times 0.21 = 0.0504\ \text{cm}^3/\text{day} $$ **Step 3 — Compute shelf life (2 marks):** $$ t_{sl} = \frac{V_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{crit}}}{Q_{\text{day}}} = \frac{8.75}{0.0504} \approx 173.6\ \text{days} $$ **Step 4 — Apply safety factor (1 mark):** Apply a 50 % safety factor (industry standard, accounts for temperature abuse and seal leakage): $$ t_{sl}^{\text{safe}} = 173.6 \times 0.5 \approx 87\ \text{days} \approx 3\ \text{months} $$ **Step 5 — State the conclusion (1 mark):** > **Answer:** The estimated shelf life of the frozen fish block is approximately **87 days (~ 3 months)** under the given package specifications. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students write OTR × A without converting $(p_1 - p_{\text{crit}})$ to atm. Always check units match before multiplying. > 2. **Forget the safety factor:** Real shelf life is *not* the theoretical value. Examiners give full marks only if a safety factor is applied. > 3. **Using mass vs volume confusion:** OTR is given as cm³ (volume), so $m_{\text{O}_2}^{\text{crit}}$ must be converted to volume before dividing. > 4. **Permeability coefficient misuse:** If KTU gives both $P$ *and* OTR, use OTR (it's already normalized). Mixing both wastes 2–3 minutes and risks arithmetic error. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision (Print This Section)** - ✅ Water activity is the **single most influential** product characteristic for packaging. - ✅ aw > 0.85 → microbial → high barrier + refrigeration. - ✅ 0.60 < aw < 0.85 → chemical/enzymatic → O₂ + light barrier. - ✅ aw < 0.60 → only oxidation → O₂ barrier alone. - ✅ pH < 4.5 → acid-resistant coating (epoxy-phenolic). - ✅ High fat → fat-resistant inner layer (prevent migration). - ✅ Light-sensitive → opaque, UV-blocked (TiO₂, carbon black). - ✅ Fragile → cushioning, drop-test pass. - ✅ Respiring fresh produce → MAP (2–5 % O₂, 3–10 % CO₂, balance N₂). - ✅ Ethylene-emitting → scavenger (KMnO₄ sachet). - ✅ Three pillars: **Physical + Chemical + Biological**. - ✅ Shelf life formula (linear form): $$t_{sl} = \frac{C_{\text{crit}} \, m_f \, L}{P \, A \, (p_1 - p_{\text{crit}})}$$ - ✅ Crispness-loss shelf life: $$t_{sl}^{\text{crisp}} = \frac{m_{\text{crit}} - m_{\text{initial}}}{\text{WVTR} \cdot A}$$ - ✅ Always apply a **50 % safety factor** in shelf-life estimates. - ✅ Use **OTR × A × Δp** directly when OTR is given — do not re-multiply by permeability. - ✅ Migration equation (partition-based): $$K_{\text{mig}} = \frac{C_{\text{food}} \cdot V_{\text{food}}}{C_{\text{polymer}} \cdot V_{\text{polymer}}}$$ - ✅ Translate every product trait with the phrasing: *"Because the product has [X], the package must have [Y]."* - ✅ FSSAI / FDA / EU 10/2011 are the **regulatory backstops** for any package selection. - ✅ Three-layer logic: *Characterize → Translate → Validate*. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — TOPIC: PRODUCT CHARACTERISTICS AND PACKAGE REQUIREMENTS** > **Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4).**

topics.title: Rigid, Semi-Rigid, Flexible Forms of Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rigid, Semi-Rigid, Flexible Forms of Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rigid Packaging** refers to packaging structures that retain their **fixed geometrical shape and dimensions** under normal handling, filling, and stacking conditions without deformation. They possess high **flexural rigidity** (typically **> 0.5 N·m** bending modulus) and cannot be folded flat without breaking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-Rigid Packaging** denotes intermediate structures that maintain their general form under stress but exhibit **limited elastic deformation**, allowing shallow bending or shape recovery. Their flexural modulus typically lies between **0.1 N·m and 0.5 N·m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexible Packaging** comprises structures made from pliable films, foils, or laminates that can be **easily bent, folded, wrapped, or twisted** without rupturing. They offer negligible structural rigidity and conform entirely to the product geometry.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

| Form | Everyday Analogy | Student-Friendly Explanation |
|------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Rigid** | An empty glass Coke bottle on a shelf | Try squeezing it — it doesn't bend. It stands tall, holds its shape even when empty. |
| **Semi-Rigid** | A yogurt plastic cup or aluminum take-away tray | You can press the side a little, it dents slightly and comes back — like a memory foam pillow, but for packaging. |
| **Flexible** | A potato chips packet or a milk poly-bag | You can crumple it, fold it, twist it. It has no memory of its own shape — it just hugs whatever is inside. |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics for Classification**
> - **Flexural Rigidity (G):** Measured in **N·m²** or **mg·cm** (Taber stiffness units)
> - **Bursting Strength:** for paperboards in **kPa**
> - **Tensile Strength:** for films in **MPa**
> - **Young's Modulus threshold:** **> 1 GPa** (Rigid); **0.1–1 GPa** (Semi-Rigid); **< 0.1 GPa** (Flexible)
> - **Typical Thickness Ranges:**
>   - Rigid plastics: **> 250 µm**
>   - Semi-rigid: **100–250 µm**
>   - Flexible: **< 100 µm**

---

## 1.4 Why the Distinction Matters (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes this classification because the **package form directly governs:**
> 1. The choice of **packaging machinery** (form-fill-seal vs. bottle-filling lines)
> 2. The **distribution logistics** (stacking ability, palletization)
> 3. The **product-package interaction** (migration, shelf-life)
> 4. The **regulatory compliance** (FSSAI, EU 10/2011 for food contact materials)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Classification Logic (Step-by-Step)

The classification of a packaging form is determined by a hierarchy of mechanical and barrier properties evaluated in the following sequence:

- **Step 1 — Flexural Modulus Evaluation:** Measure the material's Young's modulus ($E$) under ASTM D790 conditions.
  - If $E \geq 1 \text{ GPa}$ → candidate for **Rigid**
  - If $0.1 \text{ GPa} \leq E < 1 \text{ GPa}$ → candidate for **Semi-Rigid**
  - If $E < 0.1 \text{ GPa}$ → candidate for **Flexible**

- **Step 2 — Elastic Recovery Test:** Apply 5% strain and observe recovery after 60 s.
  - Full recovery → **Rigid/Semi-Rigid** elastic region
  - Permanent deformation → **Semi-Rigid** plastic region

- **Step 3 — Fold Endurance Test (MIT Test):** Count double-folds until failure.
  - **> 1000 folds** → Flexible
  - **< 10 folds** → Rigid
  - **10–1000 folds** → Semi-Rigid

- **Step 4 — Forming Behavior:** Determine whether the structure is produced by:
  - Injection molding / blow molding / glass forming → **Rigid**
  - Thermoforming at moderate temperatures → **Semi-Rigid**
  - Co-extrusion / lamination / casting → **Flexible**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are frequently asked in Part B (14 mark) questions.**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Equation} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Application} & \textbf{Units} \\
\hline
\text{Permeability} & P = D \cdot S & \text{Fick + Henry combined} & \text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cm}/\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa} \\
\hline
\text{WVTR} & \text{WVTR} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} & \text{Water vapor barrier} & \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \\
\hline
\text{OTR} & \text{OTR} = \dfrac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p_{O_2}}{L} & \text{Oxygen barrier} & \text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \\
\hline
\text{Shelf Life (Arrhenius)} & t_s = \dfrac{[C_{\text{crit}} - C_0]}{k_0 \, e^{-E_a/RT}} & \text{Oxidative deterioration} & \text{days} \\
\hline
\text{Flexural Rigidity} & G = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} & \text{Package stiffness} & \text{N·m} \\
\hline
\text{Burst Strength} & \sigma_b = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t} & \text{Can/container failure} & \text{MPa} \\
\hline
\text{Creep Strain} & \varepsilon(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{E} + K \sigma_0^n t^m & \text{Semi-rigid creep} & \text{dimensionless} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Symbol Legend (must memorize):**
> $D$ = Diffusion coefficient, $S$ = Solubility coefficient, $L$ = Film thickness, $\Delta p$ = Partial pressure gradient, $E_a$ = Activation energy, $k_0$ = Pre-exponential factor, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $t$ = Thickness.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rigid** packaging dominates the **beverage, canned food, and pharmaceutical** industries where **stackability, gas-tightness, and reusability** are paramount.
- **Semi-Rigid** packaging bridges the gap for **yogurt, ready-meals, and confectionery** trays, providing moderate mechanical protection with reduced material cost.
- **Flexible** packaging is the **fastest-growing segment** (> **5% CAGR**), dominating **snacks, retort pouches, and aseptic liquid packaging** due to lightweight, low-carbon footprint, and superior barrier per unit mass.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Permeation Through a Flexible Film

We derive the **steady-state permeation rate** (the core equation behind barrier performance of flexible packaging).

> **Starting Premise — Fick's First Law of Diffusion**

The molar flux $J$ of a permeant through a film is proportional to the concentration gradient across the thickness:

$$
J = -D \, \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

where:
- $J$ = steady-state flux (mol·cm⁻²·s⁻¹)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²·s⁻¹)
- $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ = concentration gradient across thickness

**Step 1 — Apply Henry's Law at the boundary surfaces.**

At the upstream face ($x = 0$): $C_1 = S \cdot p_1$

At the downstream face ($x = L$): $C_2 = S \cdot p_2$

where $S$ is the solubility coefficient and $p_1, p_2$ are partial pressures of the permeant on either side.

**Step 2 — Linearize the concentration profile.**

Assuming steady-state, $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ is constant across thickness:

$$
\frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{C_2 - C_1}{L} = \frac{S(p_2 - p_1)}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Fick's First Law.**

$$
J = -D \cdot \frac{S(p_2 - p_1)}{L}
$$

Re-arranging for the high-to-low pressure direction ($p_1 > p_2$):

$$
J = \frac{D \cdot S \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Permeability Coefficient.**

Defining permeability $P = D \cdot S$:

$$
J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}
$$

This is the **fundamental permeability equation** used universally in food packaging engineering to compare barrier performance.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Side-by-Side Property Comparison

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Property} & \textbf{Rigid} & \textbf{Semi-Rigid} & \textbf{Flexible} \\
\hline
\text{Common Materials} & \text{Glass, Tinplate, PET, HDPE} & \text{PP, PS, Al trays, PVDC} & \text{PE, PP, PET, Al foil, Paper} \\
\hline
\text{Typical Thickness} & > 250\,\mu\text{m} & 100\text{–}250\,\mu\text{m} & < 100\,\mu\text{m} \\
\hline
\text{Young's Modulus} & > 1\,\text{GPa} & 0.1\text{–}1\,\text{GPa} & < 0.1\,\text{GPa} \\
\hline
\text{Weight (relative)} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} & \text{Low} \\
\hline
\text{Barrier (O}_2\text{)} & \text{Excellent (Glass/Metal)} & \text{Good} & \text{Variable (Poor to High)} \\
\hline
\text{Forming Process} & \text{Blow/Injection Molding} & \text{Thermoforming} & \text{Co-extrusion/Lamination} \\
\hline
\text{Recyclability} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} & \text{Complex (multi-layer)} \\
\hline
\text{Cost (relative)} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} & \text{Low} \\
\hline
\text{Machinability} & \text{Slow} & \text{Moderate} & \text{Fast (FFS lines)} \\
\hline
\text{Carbon Footprint} & \text{High} & \text{Medium} & \text{Low} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for students:** In board examinations, when asked to "compare," always tabulate with **at least 6 comparison parameters** for full marks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Frequently Tested Pattern)

> **Problem:** A flexible BOPP film of thickness $L = 25\,\mu\text{m}$ exhibits oxygen permeability $P_{O_2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cm}/\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. If the partial pressure of oxygen inside the package is $p_1 = 0$ Pa (oxygen-scavenged) and outside is $p_2 = 21000$ Pa (air), calculate the OTR.

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**

$L = 25 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm}$ (convert µm to cm)

$\Delta p = p_2 - p_1 = 21000 - 0 = 21000\,\text{Pa}$

**Step 2 — Apply the OTR equation.**

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-10}) \times 21000}{25 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify numerator and denominator.**

Numerator: $1.2 \times 10^{-10} \times 2.1 \times 10^{4} = 2.52 \times 10^{-6}$

Denominator: $25 \times 10^{-4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{2.52 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.008 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm}^3/\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s}
$$

Converting to standard units (cm³/m²·day):

$$
\text{OTR} \approx 87\,\text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}
$$

This classifies the film as a **moderate O₂ barrier**, suitable for **snacks and dry foods** but insufficient for oxygen-sensitive products without additional layers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Packaging Forms]:::root
    A --> B[Rigid]:::rigid
    A --> C[Semi-Rigid]:::semi
    A --> D[Flexible]:::flex

    B --> B1[Glass Bottles and Jars]
    B --> B2[Metal Cans Tinplate Aluminum]
    B --> B3[Rigid Plastics PET HDPE PP]
    B --> B4[Ceramic Containers]

    C --> C1[Thermoformed Trays PS PP PET]
    C --> C2[Aluminum Trays and Containers]
    C --> C3[PVDC Coated Paperboard]
    C --> C4[Plastic Tubs and Cups]

    D --> D1[Single Films PE PP BOPP PET]
    D --> D2[Multi-layer Laminates]
    D --> D3[Aluminum Foil Laminates]
    D --> D4[Pouches and Sachets]
    D --> D5[Retort and Aseptic Pouches]

    classDef root fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    classDef rigid fill:#cce,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef semi fill:#fec,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef flex fill:#cfc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: Selection Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Product Analysis]:::startNode
    S1 --> Q1{Product State?}
    Q1 -->|Liquid| Q2{Heat Sensitive?}
    Q1 -->|Solid| Q3{Mechanical Load?}
    Q1 -->|Powder or Granule| Q4{Moisture Sensitive?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Flexible Aseptic Pouch]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Rigid Glass Bottle]

    Q3 -->|High| R3[Rigid Metal Can]
    Q3 -->|Medium| R4[Semi-Rigid Tray]
    Q3 -->|Low| R5[Flexible Film Wrap]

    Q4 -->|High| R6[Multi-layer Foil Laminate]
    Q4 -->|Medium| R7[Semi-Rigid PVDC Carton]
    Q4 -->|Low| R8[Single PE Film Bag]

    classDef startNode fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Permeation Pathway Through Flexible Packaging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Environment]:::ext --> B[Film Surface]:::boundary
    B --> C[Polymer Matrix Diffusion]:::matrix
    C --> D[Inner Surface]:::boundary2
    D --> E[Food Product]:::food

    subgraph BarrierProcess
    B
    C
    D
    end

    classDef ext fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064
    classDef boundary fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    classDef matrix fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    classDef boundary2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    classDef food fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between rigid and flexible packaging with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
Rigid packaging retains its shape and cannot be folded without breaking (e.g., glass bottles, tinplate cans). Flexible packaging can be easily bent, folded, or wrapped without damage (e.g., PE films, retort pouches). Rigid offers superior barrier and stacking; flexible offers lightweight and low cost.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by semi-rigid packaging? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Semi-rigid packaging possesses intermediate rigidity — it maintains general form but allows limited elastic deformation (e.g., yogurt plastic cups, aluminum foil trays). It bridges the gap between rigid containers and flexible pouches in terms of mechanical protection and formability.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both must be answered within the module's scope.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**A (a)** Classify the various forms of food packaging. Discuss the **materials used, properties, and typical applications** of rigid packaging with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Rigid packaging retains fixed geometric form; Young's modulus > 1 GPa; cannot be folded without breaking.
- **Materials (3 marks):**
  - *Glass* — excellent barrier, chemically inert, transparent (beverages, sauces).
  - *Metal (Tinplate, Aluminum)* — total light/oxygen barrier, hermetic seal (canned foods, beverages).
  - *Rigid Plastics (PET, HDPE, PP)* — shatterproof, lightweight, moldable (water bottles, milk jugs).
- **Properties and Applications (2 marks):** High mechanical strength, hermetic sealing, retortability, recyclability. Applications in carbonated drinks, sterilized milk, pharmaceutical containers.

**A (b)** Derive the equation for **steady-state permeation through a flexible film** and explain the role of **diffusion (D) and solubility (S) coefficients** in packaging barrier design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Fick's First Law statement (2 marks):** $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$
- **Henry's Law application at boundaries (2 marks):** $C = S \cdot p$ at $x=0$ and $x=L$
- **Final derivation (2 marks):** $J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ where $P = D \cdot S$
- **Interpretation (1 mark):** High $D$ → easy diffusion; High $S$ → strong sorption; $P$ determines overall barrier performance.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**B (a)** Compare **flexible and semi-rigid packaging** with respect to **mechanical properties, barrier performance, cost, and sustainability**. Use a tabulated form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Tabular comparison** with minimum **6 parameters** (5 marks): Material examples, thickness, Young's modulus, OTR/WVTR, cost per unit, recyclability.
- **Discussion (2 marks):** Flexible wins on cost, weight, and carbon footprint but is limited in barrier unless multi-layered. Semi-rigid provides moderate barrier and formability suitable for thermoformed trays.

**B (b)** A **PP film of thickness 30 µm** is used to package a snack. The water vapor permeability is $P_{H_2O} = 5 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{g·cm/cm}^2\text{·s·Pa}$. Outside humidity corresponds to $p_1 = 2500$ Pa and inside the package $p_2 = 500$ Pa. Calculate the **WVTR** in standard units. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- Given: $L = 30 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm} = 3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{cm}$, $\Delta p = 2500 - 500 = 2000\,\text{Pa}$.
- Using **WVTR equation** (2 marks):

$$
\text{WVTR} = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}
$$

- Substituting (2 marks):

$$
\text{WVTR} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-12}) \times 2000}{3 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1 \times 10^{-8}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{g/cm}^2 \cdot \text{s}
$$

- **Convert to g/m²·day** (2 marks):

$$
3.33 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^4 \times 86400 \approx 2.88\,\text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}
$$

- **Conclusion (1 mark):** This is a moderately high WVTR; suitable only for low-moisture foods unless laminated with high-barrier layers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing thickness units:** Always convert **µm → cm** before substitution (1 µm = $10^{-4}$ cm). KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Omitting the discussion:** A derivation without a concluding engineering interpretation loses **2–3 marks** in Part B.
> 3. **Missing the comparison table:** For "compare" questions, a **minimum 6-parameter table** is expected. Bare bullet points fetch only partial credit.
> 4. **Mixing up $D$ and $P$:** Remember $P = D \cdot S$ is **permeability**, $D$ alone is **diffusivity**.
> 5. **Not citing examples:** Every packaging form question **must** include at least **2 industry examples**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rigid, Semi-Rigid, Flexible Packaging**

- ☐ Rigid: $E > 1$ GPa, **glass, metal, thick plastics**; cannot fold; high barrier & stackability.
- ☐ Semi-Rigid: $E = 0.1$–$1$ GPa; **thermoformed trays, Al containers, plastic tubs**; moderate barrier.
- ☐ Flexible: $E < 0.1$ GPa; **films, pouches, sachets**; lightweight; barrier via lamination.
- ☐ **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ — foundation of permeation.
- ☐ **Henry's Law:** $C = S \cdot p$ — links concentration to partial pressure.
- ☐ **Master Equation:** $J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ where $P = D \cdot S$.
- ☐ **OTR, WVTR** must always be reported in **standard units** (cm³/m²·day or g/m²·day).
- ☐ **Conversion:** 1 µm = $10^{-4}$ cm; 1 Pa = 0.0075 mmHg (useful for older references).
- ☐ **Fastest-growing segment:** Flexible packaging (>5% CAGR globally).
- ☐ **Recycling hierarchy:** Rigid > Semi-Rigid > Flexible (multi-layer films pose end-of-life challenges).
- ☐ **Always cite applications** — KTU examiners reward practical industry linkage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wooden Boxes and Crates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wooden Boxes and Crates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wooden Boxes and Crates (KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition)**
> A **wooden box** is a rigid, three-dimensional, fully enclosed container fabricated from solid wood panels, plywood, veneers, or fiberboard, joined by nails, staples, screws, or adhesives, primarily designed for the storage, transportation, and bulk handling of agricultural produce, processed foods, and heavy industrial commodities. A **wooden crate** is a partial-enclosure or skeletonized rigid container characterized by an open framework of wooden members (slats, battens, cleats) designed to provide mechanical support, ventilation, and visibility of contents while offering significant compression and impact resistance.

In the context of **food packaging engineering**, wooden boxes and crates occupy the segment of *rigid industrial transport packaging* and are governed by standards such as **ISPM-15** (International Standards for Phytosanitary Measures No. 15) for international trade, **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) IS 2771**, and **ASTM D4169** (Standard Practice for Performance Testing of Shipping Containers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box: Think of it like this...**
> Imagine you are moving houses. A **wooden box** is like a *completely sealed wooden trunk* used by your great-grandparents to ship belongings overseas — fully enclosed, every face is a solid wooden panel, and you cannot see inside without opening it. A **wooden crate**, on the other hand, is like the *open-slatted wooden cage* used to transport mangoes or watermelons at the railway station — you can clearly see the fruit inside, slats run in parallel, and air passes through freely, but the structure is rigid enough to be stacked ten units high without crushing.

In simple terms:
- **Box = SOLID = Enclosed = Heavier protection = Lower visibility**
- **Crate = SKELETAL = Open = Better ventilation = Visible contents**

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (At a Glance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Classification Matrix**

| Container Type | Enclosure | Primary Use | Typical Wall |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Wirebound Box** | Partial (wood + wire) | Heavy apples, cabbages | Wood slats + steel wires |
| **Nailed Wooden Box** | Full | Machinery, dried foods | Solid wood + nails |
| **Plywood Box** | Full | Export-grade foods | Plywood panels |
| **Open Crate (Slatted)** | None / Open | Fresh produce | Parallel slats |
| **Closed Crate** | Partial (top + bottom) | Bulk grains | Slatted + panels |
| **Framed Crate** | Skeletal frame | Heavy machinery | Studded frame |
| **Skeleton Crate** | Minimum members | Bottles, fruits | Corner posts + slats |

## 1.4 Key Engineering Parameters

- **Box Compression Strength (BCT)**: The maximum vertical load (in **kgf** or **N**) a box can withstand before buckling.
- **Bursting Strength**: Resistance to internal/external pressure (measured in **kg/cm²**).
- **Moisture Content of Wood**: Standard range for packaging wood is **12% to 18%** (wet basis).
- **Standard Wood Density**: Softwood ranges from **400 to 600 kg/m³**; hardwood ranges from **600 to 900 kg/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Wooden Boxes — Detailed Engineering Breakdown

### A. Wirebound Boxes
- Constructed from wooden slats held together by **galvanized steel wires** (typically **1.5 mm to 2.5 mm diameter**).
- Wires run along all four edges and diagonals.
- Provide excellent stacking strength, easy assembly/disassembly.
- **Engineering Application**: Apples, citrus fruits, cabbage shipments.

### B. Nailed Wooden Boxes (Cleated Plywood Boxes)
- Built from plywood panels reinforced with wooden cleats (battens).
- Cleats are nailed or screwed to the panels.
- **Two sub-types**:
  - **Open Cleated Plywood Box** — top and bottom only.
  - **Tight Cleated Plywood Box** — fully enclosed with cleats on all edges.

### C. Plywood & Veneer Boxes
- Use **3-ply to 7-ply** commercial plywood (BWR or BWP grade).
- Lightweight, dimensionally stable, and resistant to warping.
- Standard plywood thickness for packaging: **4 mm, 6 mm, 9 mm, 12 mm, 18 mm, 25 mm**.

### D. Corrugated Wooden-Fiber Boxes (Hybrids)
- Combine a corrugated fiberboard inner liner with wooden reinforcement.

## 2.2 Types of Crates — Detailed Engineering Breakdown

### A. Open/Slatted Crates
- Consists of a base, four corner posts, and horizontal slats.
- Used for **fresh fruits, vegetables, eggs, and live plants** requiring airflow.

### B. Closed/Sheathed Crates
- Have additional sheathing (plywood or sheet metal) over the slats.
- Used for **bulk grains, powders, and granular foods**.

### C. Framed Crates
- Rigid framework of structural members.
- Used for **heavy industrial items and bulk transport**.

### D. Skeleton Crates
- Minimum number of wooden members.
- Used for **bottled beverages (soda crates)** and similar rigid items.

## 2.3 Why and How — Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineering "Why" Behind Wooden Containers**

1. **Mechanical Strength**: Wood's high strength-to-weight ratio (specific strength of ~**200 kN·m/kg**) makes it ideal for stacking.
2. **Thermal Insulation**: Wood's low thermal conductivity (**0.10–0.15 W/m·K**) protects temperature-sensitive foods.
3. **Vibration Damping**: Wood's cellular structure absorbs shock during transit.
4. **Reusability & Sustainability**: Wood is biodegradable and renewable.
5. **Customization**: Boxes and crates can be fabricated to any size, shape, or load requirement.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Sheet for Wooden Boxes & Crates**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Box Compression Strength (BCT)** | $BCT = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot d$ | $\text{N}$ | Modified McKee formula |
| 2 | **Edge Crush Test (ECT)** | $ECT = \sigma_{crush} \cdot t$ | $\text{kN/m}$ | Linerboard crush resistance |
| 3 | **Stacking Load** | $L = n \cdot W \cdot F_s$ | $\text{kg}$ | $n$=boxes stacked, $F_s$=safety factor |
| 4 | **Bending Stress in Slat** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | $M$=bending moment |
| 5 | **Moment of Inertia (Rectangular Slat)** | $I = \dfrac{b \cdot h^3}{12}$ | $\text{mm}^4$ | $b$=width, $h$=height |
| 6 | **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $MC = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100$ | % | $M_w$=wet mass, $M_d$=dry mass |
| 7 | **Wood Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $m$=mass, $V$=volume |
| 8 | **Volume of Box** | $V_{box} = L \times B \times H$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Internal volume |
| 9 | **Compression Strength Loss with MC** | $P_{MC} = P_{12} \cdot \left[1 - 0.03 \cdot (MC - 12)\right]$ | % | $P_{12}$=strength at 12% MC |
| 10 | **Hygroscopic Equilibrium Moisture (HEM)** | $EMC = f(\phi, T)$ | % | Function of RH & Temp |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Rule Compliance**: All table cells use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid syntax conflicts. Always write $|x|$ as $\vert x\vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wooden boxes and crates are the **workhorses of the cold-chain logistics industry**, especially for:
- **Apples** (Kashmir → Delhi → Export via Mumbai).
- **Citrus fruits** (Nagpur, Maharashtra).
- **Spices in bulk** (Kerala — Malabar pepper, cardamom).
- **Coconut and copra** (Kerala, Tamil Nadu).
- **Bottled beverages** (soda crates — Pepsi, Coca-Cola).

The compliance with **ISPM-15** (heat treatment or methyl bromide fumigation) is mandatory for any wooden packaging material used in international trade to prevent the spread of pests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Box Compression Strength (Modified McKee's Formula)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Box Compression Test (BCT)** is the single most important parameter in the design of any rigid transport container. We will derive the modified McKee formula step by step, as it is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU examinations.

### Step 1: Define the Fundamental Parameters

The **Box Compression Strength (BCT)** depends on:
- The **flexural stiffness** of the side panels (i.e., their ability to resist bending).
- The **edge crush strength** of the panel material (resistance to crushing in the through-thickness direction).

Let:
- $E$ = Flexural modulus of elasticity of the panel material (in $\text{N/mm}^2$).
- $t$ = Thickness of the panel wall (in $\text{mm}$).
- $d$ = Inside perimeter of the box (in $\text{mm}$).
- $\sigma_{crush}$ = Edge crush strength of the panel (in $\text{N/mm}^2$).
- $k$ = Empirical constant (typically $k = 0.0294$ for SI units).

### Step 2: Express the Flexural Rigidity per Unit Width

The **flexural rigidity per unit width** of a panel is given by:

$$
D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12}
$$

This represents the panel's resistance to bending per unit length.

### Step 3: Empirically Correlate BCT to Flexural Rigidity

Experimental data (McKee, Gander, and Wachuta, 1963) showed that the BCT of a corrugated box is empirically related to the cube root of the product of the flexural rigidity and the edge crush strength:

$$
BCT \propto (D \cdot ECT)^{1/3}
$$

Substituting $D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12}$ and $ECT = \sigma_{crush} \cdot t$:

$$
BCT \propto \left( \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12} \cdot \sigma_{crush} \cdot t \right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Cubic Power

The expression inside the parentheses can be written as:

$$
D \cdot ECT = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3 \cdot \sigma_{crush} \cdot t}{12} = \dfrac{E \cdot \sigma_{crush} \cdot t^4}{12}
$$

Now, taking the cube root:

$$
BCT \propto \left( \dfrac{E \cdot \sigma_{crush} \cdot t^4}{12} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \dfrac{1}{12} \right)^{1/3} \cdot E^{1/3} \cdot \sigma_{crush}^{1/3} \cdot t^{4/3}
$$

### Step 5: Introduce the Perimeter and Empirical Constant

The BCT is also proportional to the inside perimeter $d$ of the box (longer perimeter = more support = higher strength). Thus:

$$
BCT = k \cdot E^{1/3} \cdot \sigma_{crush}^{1/3} \cdot t^{4/3} \cdot d
$$

In packaging engineering practice, this is often written in a more compact empirical form (where the constants are lumped):

$$
BCT = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot d
$$

This is the **Modified McKee's Formula** as used in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Problem (KTU Typical):**
> A wooden box is constructed from plywood with the following properties: $E = 7000 \, \text{N/mm}^2$, $t = 9 \, \text{mm}$, and inside perimeter $d = 2400 \, \text{mm}$. Using $k = 0.0294$, calculate the BCT.

**Solution:**

$$
BCT = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot d
$$

$$
BCT = 0.0294 \times 7000 \times 9^{2.33} \times 2400
$$

First, compute $9^{2.33}$:

$$
9^{2.33} = e^{2.33 \cdot \ln(9)} = e^{2.33 \times 2.1972} = e^{5.1195} \approx 167.5
$$

Now substitute:

$$
BCT = 0.0294 \times 7000 \times 167.5 \times 2400
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
0.0294 \times 7000 = 205.8
$$

$$
205.8 \times 167.5 = 34,471.5
$$

$$
34,471.5 \times 2400 = 82,731,600 \, \text{N}
$$

Converting to **kgf** (divide by $g = 9.81$):

$$
BCT = \dfrac{82,731,600}{9.81} \approx 8,433,000 \, \text{kgf}
$$

**Result**: $BCT \approx 8.43 \times 10^6 \, \text{N}$ or approximately **843 tonnes**. This extremely high value indicates that the formula needs careful unit interpretation, and in practice, the constant $k$ is recalibrated for the specific wood species.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Compression Strength Loss with Moisture Content

### Step 1: Establish the Baseline

Wood's mechanical properties are standardized at **12% moisture content (MC)**. Let the BCT at 12% MC be $BCT_{12}$.

### Step 2: Apply the Empirical Rule

For every 1% increase in MC above 12%, wood loses approximately **3% of its compression strength** (empirical rule for softwoods):

$$
BCT_{MC} = BCT_{12} \cdot \left[ 1 - 0.03 \cdot (MC - 12) \right]
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A wooden box has $BCT_{12} = 5000 \, \text{N}$. The box is stored at **25% MC**. Calculate the new BCT.

**Solution:**

$$
BCT_{25} = 5000 \times \left[ 1 - 0.03 \times (25 - 12) \right]
$$

$$
BCT_{25} = 5000 \times \left[ 1 - 0.03 \times 13 \right]
$$

$$
BCT_{25} = 5000 \times \left[ 1 - 0.39 \right]
$$

$$
BCT_{25} = 5000 \times 0.61 = 3050 \, \text{N}
$$

**Result**: $BCT_{25} = 3050 \, \text{N}$ (a **39% reduction** due to moisture absorption). This is why **moisture control is critical** in food packaging.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Bending Stress in a Crate Slat

### Step 1: Define Geometry and Loading

Consider a horizontal slat of a wooden crate of length $L$, width $b$, and height $h$, loaded with a uniformly distributed load $w$ (in $\text{N/mm}$).

### Step 2: Maximum Bending Moment

For a simply supported slat with UDL:

$$
M_{max} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8}
$$

### Step 3: Section Modulus

For a rectangular cross-section:

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{b \cdot h^2}{6}
$$

where $y_{max} = h/2$.

### Step 4: Maximum Bending Stress

Using the flexure formula:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2 / 8}{b \cdot h^2 / 6} = \dfrac{3 \cdot w \cdot L^2}{4 \cdot b \cdot h^2}
$$

### Step 5: Safety Check

The slat is safe if $\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_{allowable}$ for the wood species (e.g., $\sigma_{allow} = 40 \, \text{N/mm}^2$ for pine).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wooden Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1: Functional Architecture of a Standard Nailed Wooden Box**
> *The Mermaid block below maps the structural interactions of the components.*

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Panel: Plywood] --> B[Top Edge Cleat: Pine Wood]
    B --> C[Side Panel: Plywood]
    C --> D[Bottom Edge Cleat: Pine Wood]
    D --> E[Bottom Panel: Plywood]
    F[Corner Post: Hardwood] --> B
    F --> D
    F --> C
    G[Nail: Galvanized Steel] --> B
    G --> C
    G --> D
    G --> F
    H[Food Product: Bulk Grains] --> A
    H --> E

    classDef panel fill:#FCE7B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cleat fill:#B5D8FC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef post fill:#FCB5D8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fastener fill:#D8FCB5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef product fill:#FCF4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class A,C,E panel
    class B,D cleat
    class F post
    class G fastener
    class H product
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Wooden Box Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Lumber] --> R2[Kiln Drying to 12 percent MC]
    R2 --> R3[Cutting and Sawing]
    R3 --> R4[Panel Preparation]
    R4 --> R5[Cleat Cutting]
    R5 --> R6[Assembly Jig Setup]
    R6 --> R7[Nailing and Gluing]
    R7 --> R8[ISPM15 Heat Treatment]
    R8 --> R9[Marking and Branding]
    R9 --> R10[Quality Inspection]
    R10 --> R11[Dispatch to Food Industry]

    classDef stage1 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stage2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stage3 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stage4 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px

    class R1,R2,R3,R4 stage1
    class R5,R6,R7 stage2
    class R8,R9 stage3
    class R10,R11 stage4
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Box vs. Crate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Product Type?}
    S -->|Fresh Fruits and Vegetables| P1[Ventilation Required]
    S -->|Bulk Grains or Powders| P2[Enclosure Required]
    S -->|Bottled Beverages| P3[Skeleton Support Required]
    S -->|Processed Food Cans| P4[Full Enclosure Required]

    P1 --> Q{Stacking Required?}
    Q -->|Yes, High Stack| R1[Open Slatted Crate]
    Q -->|No, Single Layer| R2[Wirebound Box]

    P2 --> T{Multi-Trip Reuse?}
    T -->|Yes| R3[Closed Plywood Crate]
    T -->|No| R4[Single-Use Nailed Box]

    P3 --> U[Plastic or Wire Reinforced Crate]
    P4 --> V[Standard Cleated Plywood Box]

    classDef decision fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outcome fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px

    class S,Q,T decision
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 process
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,U,V outcome
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Map of a Crate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Base Slats] --> B1[Corner Posts Vertical]
    B1 --> C1[Side Slats Horizontal]
    B1 --> D1[Top Slats Optional]
    E1[Diagonal Bracing] --> B1
    E1 --> A1
    F1[Nails and Fasteners] --> A1
    F1 --> B1
    F1 --> C1
    F1 --> D1

    classDef baseComp fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px
    classDef verticalComp fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#1A237E,stroke-width:2px
    classDef horizComp fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bracingComp fill:#F0F4C3,stroke:#827717,stroke-width:2px
    classDef fastenerComp fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px

    class A1 baseComp
    class B1 verticalComp
    class C1,D1 horizComp
    class E1 bracingComp
    class F1 fastenerComp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between a wooden box and a wooden crate with suitable examples used in the food packaging industry.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Wooden Box | Wooden Crate |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Enclosure** | Fully enclosed on all six faces | Partially open / skeletal |
| **Material** | Solid wood, plywood, veneer | Wooden slats, battens, cleats |
| **Visibility** | Contents not visible | Contents clearly visible |
| **Ventilation** | Poor; requires holes/vent | Excellent; natural airflow |
| **Weight** | Heavier | Lighter |
| **Stacking** | High compression strength | Moderate; depends on bracing |
| **Example (Food)** | Plywood box for spice export | Wooden slatted crate for apples |
| **ISPM-15** | Mandatory | Mandatory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern)**: 1 mark for definition of box, 1 mark for definition of crate, 1 mark for at least 2 distinguishing points with food examples.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Q: Explain the importance of ISPM-15 in the context of wooden packaging materials for international food trade.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **International Standards for Phytosanitary Measures No. 15 (ISPM-15)** is a globally recognized phytosanitary standard that governs **all wooden packaging materials (WPM)** used in international trade, including those used for food packaging.

**Key Requirements (3 Marks)**:
1. **Heat Treatment (HT)**: Wood must be heated to a core temperature of **56°C for a minimum of 30 minutes** to eliminate pests and larvae.
2. **Methyl Bromide (MB) Fumigation**: An alternative to heat treatment, involving fumigation with methyl bromide gas.
3. **Marking**: Every wooden box/crate must bear the **IPPC stamp** (wheat-stalk logo) along with the country code, producer code, and treatment code.

**Why It Matters**: Prevents the global spread of invasive pests (e.g., the Asian longhorned beetle, pine wood nematode) that could devastate native forests. Non-compliant wooden packaging is **rejected at the port of entry** and may be destroyed at the importer's expense.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Choice Pattern: Select ANY ONE of the two alternatives.**

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Describe in detail the various types of wooden boxes used in food packaging, highlighting their construction, materials, and applications. Also explain the McKee's formula for box compression strength with a worked example.**

#### Part (a) — Types of Wooden Boxes (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The major types of wooden boxes used in the food packaging industry are:

**1. Wirebound Boxes (2 Marks)**
- Constructed from wood slats bound together with **galvanized steel wires** (1.5–2.5 mm diameter).
- Wires run along the four edges and diagonals.
- Used for heavy produce like **apples, cabbages, citrus fruits**.
- Advantages: High stacking strength, reusable, easy to assemble.

**2. Nailed Wooden Boxes (Cleated Plywood Boxes) (2 Marks)**
- Built from **plywood panels** reinforced with **wooden cleats (battens)**.
- Sub-types: *Open* (top/bottom only) and *Tight* (fully enclosed).
- Used for **spice exports, dried foods, and processed food items**.
- Cleats are nailed at 100–150 mm intervals.

**3. Plywood and Veneer Boxes (1.5 Marks)**
- Use commercial **3-ply to 7-ply plywood** (BWR/BWP grade).
- Standard thickness: 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 25 mm.
- Applications: Export-grade **tea, coffee, dry fruits**.

**4. Solid Wood (Sawn Lumber) Boxes (1.5 Marks)**
- Made from **sawn timber planks** of softwood or hardwood.
- Most expensive, used for **premium foods, wines, and spirits**.
- Moisture content must be **12–15%** to prevent warping.

#### Part (b) — McKee's Formula with Example (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Box Compression Strength (BCT)** is the most critical performance parameter. The **Modified McKee's formula** relates BCT to material properties and geometry:

$$
BCT = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot d
$$

where $E$ = flexural modulus ($\text{N/mm}^2$), $t$ = panel thickness ($\text{mm}$), $d$ = inside perimeter ($\text{mm}$), and $k$ = empirical constant.

**Worked Example (7 Marks breakdown):**
- Statement of problem: 1 mark
- Formula: 1 mark
- Step-by-step substitution: 3 marks
- Final answer with units: 2 marks

> Suppose $E = 7000 \, \text{N/mm}^2$, $t = 9 \, \text{mm}$, $d = 2400 \, \text{mm}$, $k = 0.0294$.

**Step 1**: Substitute into the formula:

$$
BCT = 0.0294 \times 7000 \times 9^{2.33} \times 2400
$$

**Step 2**: Compute $9^{2.33}$:

$$
9^{2.33} = e^{2.33 \cdot \ln 9} = e^{2.33 \times 2.1972} = e^{5.1195} \approx 167.5
$$

**Step 3**: Sequential multiplication:

$$
BCT = 0.0294 \times 7000 \times 167.5 \times 2400 = 82,731,600 \, \text{N}
$$

**Step 4**: Converting to kgf:

$$
BCT = \dfrac{82,731,600}{9.81} \approx 8.43 \times 10^6 \, \text{kgf}
$$

**Conclusion**: The box can withstand a vertical compression load of approximately **8.43 × 10⁶ N** under ideal conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 1**: Many students forget to convert the final answer to the correct units (**N or kgf**). Always specify units. Also, never round off intermediate values — keep them in the calculator.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Discuss the different types of wooden crates used for food packaging with neat sketches. Explain the effect of moisture content on the mechanical strength of wooden containers with a suitable formula.**

#### Part (a) — Types of Wooden Crates (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Open/Slatted Crates (2 Marks)**
- Composed of a base, four corner posts, and parallel horizontal slats.
- Provide **excellent ventilation** for fresh produce.
- Common in **Kerala and Tamil Nadu** for transporting mangoes, pineapples, and vegetables.

**2. Closed/Sheathed Crates (1.5 Marks)**
- Slatted frame with **plywood or metal sheathing** for partial enclosure.
- Used for **bulk grains, rice, sugar, and lentils**.

**3. Framed Crates (1.5 Marks)**
- Built from heavy structural members with a rigid frame.
- Used for **industrial food machinery, large bulk containers**.

**4. Skeleton Crates (2 Marks)**
- Minimum structural members: just corner posts and 2-3 slats per face.
- Used for **bottled beverages (soda crates)** and rigid pre-packaged food containers.

#### Part (b) — Effect of Moisture Content (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Wood is a **hygroscopic material** that absorbs and releases moisture from the surrounding atmosphere. The moisture content (MC) is given by:

$$
MC = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100 \, \%
$$

**Effect of MC on Strength (5 Marks of 7):**

1. **At MC = 12%**: Wood attains its **maximum mechanical strength**. This is the standard reference.
2. **For every 1% increase in MC above 12%**: Compression strength decreases by approximately **3%**.
3. **For every 1% decrease in MC below 12%**: Strength increases slightly, but the wood becomes **brittle**.

**Empirical Formula (2 Marks of 7):**

$$
BCT_{MC} = BCT_{12} \cdot \left[ 1 - 0.03 \cdot (MC - 12) \right]
$$

**Worked Example**: A wooden box has $BCT_{12} = 5000 \, \text{N}$. After storage in a humid warehouse, MC rises to 25%.

$$
BCT_{25} = 5000 \times \left[ 1 - 0.03 \times (25 - 12) \right]
$$

$$
BCT_{25} = 5000 \times \left[ 1 - 0.39 \right] = 5000 \times 0.61 = 3050 \, \text{N}
$$

**Result**: The box has lost **39% of its strength** due to moisture absorption. This explains why wooden containers used for food must be stored in **dry, well-ventilated warehouses** with RH below **65%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 2**: Students often confuse the **wet basis** vs. **dry basis** moisture content. KTU 2024 standard is **wet basis**. Writing the wrong denominator is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Wooden Boxes & Crates**

- ✅ **Box vs. Crate**: Box = enclosed; Crate = open/skeletal.
- ✅ **Wirebound boxes** use **galvanized steel wires** along edges and diagonals.
- ✅ **Cleated plywood boxes** have wooden battens for edge reinforcement.
- ✅ **Plywood grades**: **BWR (Boiling Water Resistant)** and **BWP (Boiling Water Proof)** are used for food-grade packaging.
- ✅ **ISPM-15** mandates **56°C for 30 minutes** heat treatment for all WPM in international trade.
- ✅ **Modified McKee's formula**: $BCT = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot d$.
- ✅ **Moisture Content** is measured on **wet basis**: $MC = (M_w - M_d) / M_w \times 100$.
- ✅ **Strength loss rule**: For every 1% MC increase above 12%, compression strength **drops by ~3%**.
- ✅ **Standard wood MC** for packaging: **12% to 18%**.
- ✅ **Wood thermal conductivity**: **0.10 to 0.15 W/m·K** — useful for cold-chain packaging.
- ✅ **Soda crates** are a classic example of **skeleton crates**.
- ✅ **Apple boxes** in Kashmir use **wooden slatted crates** (typically 18–20 kg capacity).
- ✅ **Major standards**: IS 2771 (India), ASTM D4169 (USA), ISPM-15 (International), TAPPI T 804 (Compression).
- ✅ **Hygroscopic Equilibrium Moisture (HEM/EMC)**: Wood reaches equilibrium with surrounding humidity; never zero moisture in service.
- ✅ **Safety Factor (F_s)** for stacking: typically **2.0 to 3.0** in food packaging.
- ✅ **Reusability**: Wooden boxes can be reused **5 to 10 times** depending on handling.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Wooden Boxes and Crates**
> *Aligns with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome CO2: Design and select appropriate rigid packaging materials for food applications, applying principles of mechanical strength, moisture management, and regulatory compliance.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paper and Paperboards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Paper and Paperboards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Paper and Paperboards: The Foundation of Sustainable Food Packaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Paper** is a thin, flexible material manufactured from processed cellulosic fibers (pulp) derived primarily from wood, recycled paper, or agricultural residues, formed into a sheet through a wet-laid process and subsequently dried. **Paperboard** is a heavyweight, rigid, multi-layered paper-based material with a grammage (basis weight) typically **greater than 224 g/m² (GSM)**, engineered to provide structural strength, printability, and barrier properties suitable for primary and secondary food packaging applications.

The defining boundary between paper and paperboard, as recognized by the **TAPPI (Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry)** and the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 536)**, is the **grammage threshold of 224 g/m²** (sometimes cited as 250 g/m² in European standards).

| Parameter | Paper | Paperboard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Grammage (GSM)** | < 224 g/m² | ≥ 224 g/m² |
| **Caliper (Thickness)** | 0.03 mm – 0.3 mm | 0.3 mm – 1.5 mm |
| **Typical Stiffness** | Low (flexible) | High (semi-rigid) |
| **Primary Food Use** | Wrapping, interleaving, sachets | Cartons, boxes, trays, cups |
| **Recyclability Rate** | ~ 67 % globally | ~ 81 % globally |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Origami vs Cardboard Box" Analogy**
