> Think of paper as a single sheet of **origami paper** — flexible, foldable, perfect for wrapping a sandwich or lining a baking tray. Paperboard, in contrast, behaves like a **cereal box** or the rigid tray holding your frozen pizza — it stands up on its own, resists crushing, and protects food from mechanical damage during transport and stacking. The difference is purely structural: paperboard stacks multiple fiber layers to achieve rigidity, just like plywood achieves strength by gluing thin wood veneers together at alternating angles.

## Sources of Cellulosic Fibers

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Fiber Sources Used in Food-Grade Paper and Paperboard:**
> - **Softwood (Coniferous trees):** Pine, Spruce, Fir — provide **long fibers** (2–4 mm), contributing to **tensile strength** and **tear resistance**.
> - **Hardwood (Deciduous trees):** Eucalyptus, Birch, Acacia — provide **short fibers** (0.5–1.5 mm), contributing to **smoothness**, **opacity**, and **printability**.
> - **Recycled fibers (OCC, MOW):** Old Corrugated Containers (OCC) and Mixed Office Waste (MOW) — used in non-contact layers.
> - **Non-wood fibers:** Bagasse (sugarcane), Bamboo, Straw, Hemp — emerging sustainable alternatives with shorter fibers but higher cellulose content (~45–55 %).

## Classification Tree for Paper-Based Packaging Materials

```
PAPER-BASED PACKAGING MATERIALS
        │
        ├─── PAPER (< 224 GSM)
        │       ├─── Kraft Paper (Unbleached)
        │       ├─── Bleached Paper
        │       ├─── Greaseproof Paper
        │       ├─── Glassine Paper
        │       ├─── Vegetable Parchment
        │       └─── Tissue Paper
        │
        └─── PAPERBOARD (≥ 224 GSM)
                ├─── Folding Box Board (FBB)
                ├─── Solid Bleached Sulfate (SBS)
                ├─── Solid Unbleached Sulfate (SUS)
                ├─── Coated Unbleached Kraft (CUK)
                ├─── Coated Recycled Board (CRB)
                └─── Corrugated Board (Single/Double/Triple Wall)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manufacturing of Paper and Paperboard: The Four-Stage Process

The transformation from a tree to a pizza box is a fascinating journey involving chemistry, physics, and precision engineering. The manufacturing chain consists of four sequential stages:

### Stage 1: Pulping

Pulping separates cellulosic fibers from lignin, the natural "glue" binding wood fibers together. Two dominant methods exist:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanical Pulping (Groundwood / TMP / CTMP):**
> - Uses mechanical grinding or refining to physically separate fibers.
> - Retains most of the **lignin**, resulting in a **yellowish** pulp with high yield (~85–95 %).
> - Produces weaker fibers but **higher opacity** and bulk.
> - Used for **newsprint** and lower-grade packaging (non-food contact outer layers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Pulping (Kraft Process — Dominant in Food Packaging):**
> - Uses a **hot alkaline solution of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium sulfide (Na₂S)** — the "white liquor" — at 160–180 °C for 1–2 hours.
> - This is called the **Kraft process** (German: *kraft* = strength) or **sulfate process**.
> - **Dissolves ~90 % of lignin**, yielding strong, dark-brown unbleached pulp.
> - **Bleaching** with chlorine dioxide (ClO₂) or hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) produces white pulp for direct food contact.
> - Pulp yield: ~45–55 %.

The **Kraft reaction** can be simplified as:

$$
\text{Wood (Cellulose + Lignin)} + \text{NaOH + Na}_2\text{S} \xrightarrow{170°C} \text{Cellulose Fibers} + \text{Lignin By-products}
$$

The **lignin by-products** are recovered and burned to provide **process energy**, making Kraft mills largely self-sufficient.

### Stage 2: Bleaching and Refining

- **Bleaching** decolorizes the brown Kraft pulp using a multi-stage sequence (e.g., **ECF — Elemental Chlorine Free** sequence: D₀-E(OP)-D₁-E(P)-D₂).
- **Refining** mechanically treats fibers in a disk refiner to improve **fiber bonding** and surface fibrillation, increasing **tensile strength** and **air resistance**.

### Stage 3: Sheet Forming (Wet-Laid Process)

- Pulp slurry (99 % water, 1 % fiber) is fed onto a moving **Fourdrinier wire mesh** (or cylinder former for multi-ply boards).
- Water drains by gravity and vacuum; fibers mat together through **hydrogen bonding** as water is removed.
- For **paperboard**, multiple plies are formed and laminated wet-in-wet to build thickness and achieve specific layer functions.

### Stage 4: Pressing, Drying, and Calendering

- **Pressing** squeezes out remaining water (~50 % moisture) using rotating felt-covered rollers.
- **Drying** is performed on steam-heated cast-iron cylinders at 100–150 °C, reducing moisture to a final **5–8 %**.
- **Calendering** passes the dried sheet through polished steel rollers to control **smoothness**, **gloss**, and **caliper**.

## 2.2 Critical Properties of Paper and Paperboard for Food Packaging

The following table summarizes the **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet** for paper and paperboard characterization:

| Property | Symbol / Unit | Standard Test Method | Typical Range (Food Packaging) | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Grammage (Basis Weight)** | $g_m$ [g/m² or GSM] | ISO 536 / TAPPI T410 | 30 – 600 GSM | Mass per unit area; primary grade-defining parameter |
| **Caliper (Thickness)** | $t$ [µm or mm] | ISO 534 / TAPPI T411 | 30 – 1500 µm | Affects stiffness, printability, and rigidity |
| **Burst Strength** | $B$ [kPa] | ISO 2758 / TAPPI T810 | 100 – 800 kPa | Resistance to puncture under pressure (sacks, bags) |
| **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_t$ [kN/m] | ISO 1924 / TAPPI T494 | 2 – 12 kN/m | Resistance to in-plane pulling force |
| **Tear Resistance** | $T_r$ [mN] | ISO 1974 / TAPPI T414 | 200 – 1500 mN | Resistance to propagation of an initiated tear |
| **Cobb Value (Water Absorbency)** | $C_{60}$ [g/m²] | ISO 535 / TAPPI T441 | 15 – 30 g/m² | Water absorbed in 60 s — critical for wet-strength |
| **Stiffness (Taber)** | $S$ [mN·m or g·cm] | ISO 2493 / TAPPI T489 | 5 – 500 mN·m | Resistance to bending — controls "feel" and formability |
| **Moisture Content** | $M$ [%] | ISO 287 / TAPPI T412 | 5 – 8 % | Equilibrium humidity; affects dimensional stability |
| **Brightness (ISO)** | $B_r$ [%] | ISO 2470 / TAPPI T525 | 70 – 92 % | Whiteness; affects visual appeal of packaging |
| **Air Resistance (Gurley)** | $G$ [s/100 mL] | ISO 5636 / TAPPI T460 | 10 – 1000 s | Porosity indicator — critical for greaseproof grades |
| **Surface Smoothness (Bendtsen)** | $B_s$ [mL/min] | ISO 8791-2 | 50 – 500 mL/min | Affects print quality and coating uniformity |

## 2.3 Key Mathematical Relationships

### Grammage Calculation

The grammage is calculated as the ratio of mass to area:

$$
g_m = \frac{m}{A}
$$

where $m$ is the mass in grams and $A$ is the area in square meters. **Units: g/m² (GSM).**

### Bulk (Specific Volume)

Bulk indicates the volume occupied per unit mass, providing insight into fiber packing density:

$$
\text{Bulk} = \frac{t}{g_m}
$$

where $t$ is caliper in µm and $g_m$ is grammage in g/m². **Units: cm³/g.** A higher bulk means a "fluffier," more porous paper.

### Burst Index (Normalized Burst Strength)

$$
\text{Burst Index} = \frac{B}{g_m}
$$

where $B$ is burst strength in kPa and $g_m$ is grammage in g/m². **Units: kPa·m²/g.** This normalization allows comparison of pulps across different basis weights.

### Tensile Index (Normalized Tensile Strength)

$$
\text{Tensile Index} = \frac{\sigma_t}{g_m}
$$

**Units: N·m/g.**

### Cobb Test Formula

$$
C_{60} = (m_2 - m_1)
$$

where $m_1$ is the initial mass of the test piece and $m_2$ is the mass after 60 seconds of water exposure. **Units: g/m².**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These properties are not merely academic — they are the **language of food packaging specification**. When a multinational brand like Nestlé or ITC specifies a packaging material, the technical datasheet will quote **GSM**, **Cobb value**, **burst strength**, and **stiffness**. Understanding these parameters is essential for KTU graduates entering packaging QC, R&D, or procurement roles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Paper vs. Paperboard Selection for Specific Food Products

The selection of paper or paperboard for a given food application requires matching **product requirements** (moisture, fat content, mechanical fragility, shelf life) with **material properties** (barrier, strength, migration safety, printability).

### Detailed Sub-Question Derivation: Calculating Material Requirements for a Flour Sack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**
> A food company wishes to pack 25 kg of wheat flour in a multiwall paper sack. Each ply of the sack wall is made from Kraft paper. The sack must withstand a stacking load equivalent to 8 sacks stacked vertically without bursting. Each sack has a base area of 0.06 m². The maximum compressive load at the bottom sack's base is **1960 N**. Given the burst index of unbleached Kraft paper is **4.5 kPa·m²/g**, calculate the minimum grammage required for a single ply, and the total grammage if the sack uses 4 plies.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the pressure acting on the bottom sack's base.**

The pressure $P$ is the force per unit area:

$$
P = \frac{F}{A}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
P = \frac{1960 \text{ N}}{0.06 \text{ m}^2}
$$

$$
P = 32666.67 \text{ Pa} \approx 32.67 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the burst strength required for a single ply.**

Since 4 plies share the load equally, each ply must withstand:

$$
B_{\text{ply}} = \frac{P}{n_{\text{plies}}}
$$

where $n_{\text{plies}} = 4$:

$$
B_{\text{ply}} = \frac{32.67 \text{ kPa}}{4}
$$

$$
B_{\text{ply}} = 8.167 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the burst index formula to find grammage.**

Recall the burst index formula:

$$
\text{Burst Index} = \frac{B}{g_m}
$$

Rearranging for grammage:

$$
g_m = \frac{B_{\text{ply}}}{\text{Burst Index}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
g_m = \frac{8.167 \text{ kPa}}{4.5 \text{ kPa·m}^2/\text{g}}
$$

$$
g_m = 1.815 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — this result is absurdly low.** This indicates an error in assumption: the burst index formula normalizes against grammage, meaning **higher grammage = higher burst strength**. A single Kraft ply for a 25 kg flour sack typically requires **at least 70–80 g/m²**. Let us reframe the problem using direct burst strength data rather than burst index.

### Corrected Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 (Corrected):** Calculate required total burst strength for the bottom sack:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{1960}{0.06} = 32666.67 \text{ Pa} = 32.67 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 (Corrected):** Apply a **safety factor (SF)** of 3.0 for dynamic stacking impacts:

$$
P_{\text{design}} = P_{\text{total}} \times SF = 32.67 \times 3.0 = 98.0 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 (Corrected):** Distribute among 4 plies:

$$
B_{\text{ply}} = \frac{P_{\text{design}}}{4} = \frac{98.0}{4} = 24.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 (Corrected):** Use empirical Kraft paper burst-strength data to select grammage. From standard tables:

| Grammage (g/m²) | Burst Strength (kPa) — Unbleached Kraft |
| :--- | :--- |
| 70 | ~ 230 |
| 80 | ~ 275 |
| 100 | ~ 340 |
| 120 | ~ 410 |

Since each ply requires **24.5 kPa**, and even the lightest grade (70 GSM) provides ~230 kPa (a **~9.4× safety margin**), the minimum grammage is **70 g/m²**.

**Step 5 (Corrected):** Total grammage of the multiwall sack:

$$
g_{m,\text{total}} = n_{\text{plies}} \times g_m = 4 \times 70 = 280 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:**
> The corrected derivation illustrates a critical principle: **theoretical calculations must be tempered by empirical performance data and safety factors.** KTU examiners value students who recognize when a mathematical result is physically unreasonable and apply engineering judgment to refine the solution.

## 3.2 Cobb Value Interpretation and Barrier Coating Thickness Estimation

The Cobb test quantifies water absorption. For food packaging with high moisture exposure (e.g., frozen fish packaging), a Cobb₆₀ < 20 g/m² is mandated. This is achieved through surface sizing or coating.

**The Cobb value can be correlated with coating thickness** using the following empirical relationship:

$$
C_{60} \approx C_{0} \cdot e^{-k \cdot d_c}
$$

where:
- $C_{60}$ is the Cobb value of coated paper [g/m²]
- $C_{0}$ is the Cobb value of uncoated base paper [g/m²]
- $k$ is the coating efficiency constant [µm⁻¹] (typically 0.5 – 2.0 depending on coating chemistry)
- $d_c$ is the coating thickness [µm]

**Worked Example:**

A base Kraft paper has Cobb₆₀ = 45 g/m². A water-based acrylic coating ($k = 1.2$ µm⁻¹) is applied. What coating thickness is required to achieve Cobb₆₀ ≤ 18 g/m²?

Solving for $d_c$:

$$
d_c = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C_{60}}\right)
$$

$$
d_c = \frac{1}{1.2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{45}{18}\right)
$$

$$
d_c = \frac{1}{1.2} \cdot \ln(2.5)
$$

$$
d_c = \frac{1}{1.2} \cdot 0.9163
$$

$$
d_c = 0.764 \text{ µm}
$$

Therefore, a coating thickness of **at least 0.76 µm** is required to achieve the target barrier performance.

## 3.3 Migration Analysis — A Simplified Diffusion Model

For food-contact paper, chemical migration from packaging to food is governed by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

For a packaging material of thickness $L_p$ in contact with a food of volume $V_f$, assuming a partition coefficient $K_{p,f}$ between polymer and food, the migration at equilibrium is:

$$
\frac{m_{F,\infty}}{m_{F,0}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{V_f}{K_{p,f} \cdot V_p}}
$$

where:
- $m_{F,\infty}$ = migrant mass in food at equilibrium [mg]
- $m_{F,0}$ = initial migrant mass in packaging [mg]
- $V_p$ = packaging volume [cm³]
- $K_{p,f}$ = partition coefficient (typically 1 – 1000 for paper/food systems)

> [!NOTE]
> **EU Regulation Benchmark:**
> Under **EU Regulation No. 10/2011** (applicable to plastics and increasingly referenced for paper), the overall migration limit (OML) is **10 mg/dm²** of food-contact surface, and specific migration limits (SML) are substance-dependent. For paper, **BfR (German Federal Institute for Risk Assessment)** recommendations and **FDA 21 CFR §176** govern food-contact compliance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Multi-Ply Paperboard Layered Architecture

```mermaid

        A1[Clay or Pigment Coating 5 to 20 gsm]:::coat
        A2[Bleached Chemical Pulp 30 to 80 gsm]:::top
    end

        B1[Mechanical or Recycled Pulp 60 to 120 gsm]:::under
    end

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Layered Architecture:**
> - The **top coated ply** provides a smooth, white, printable surface for graphics.
> - The **under ply** provides brightness and additional smoothness.
> - The **middle plies** contribute bulk, stiffness, and economy (using cheaper recycled fibers).
> - The **back ply** provides dimensional stability and may carry secondary printing.

## 4.4 Cross-Section of a Corrugated Board (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SingleWall [SINGLE WALL CORRUGATED BOARD]
        L1[Outer Liner 125 to 200 gsm Kraft]:::liner
        L2[Fluting Medium 90 to 150 gsm Semichemical]:::flute
        L3[Inner Liner 125 to 200 gsm Test Liner]:::liner
    end

    L1 --- L2 --- L3

    classDef liner fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000
    classDef flute fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The corrugated medium acts as a **structural I-beam** when viewed in cross-section. The two flat liners act as the flanges, while the fluting acts as the web. This geometry provides exceptional **stiffness-to-weight ratio**, which is why corrugated board dominates secondary and tertiary food packaging (shipping cartons for fruits, beverages, cereals, and frozen foods).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between paper and paperboard. List any four major types of paperboard used in food packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Paper | Paperboard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Grammage | < 224 g/m² | ≥ 224 g/m² |
| Thickness | < 0.3 mm | ≥ 0.3 mm |
| Rigidity | Flexible | Rigid/Semi-rigid |
| Typical Use | Wrapping, bags | Cartons, boxes |

**Four Major Types of Paperboard:**

1. **Solid Bleached Sulfate (SBS):** Made from bleached virgin chemical pulp; bright white, odorless, FDA-approved for direct food contact. Used in **milk cartons, juice boxes, frozen food packaging**.

2. **Solid Unbleached Sulfate (SUS):** Made from unbleached Kraft pulp; brown color, high strength. Used in **beverage carriers, heavy-duty food containers**.

3. **Folding Box Board (FBB):** Multi-ply board with mechanical pulp middle plies; excellent stiffness and printability. Used in **cereal boxes, confectionery cartons, pharmaceutical packaging**.

4. **Coated Recycled Board (CRB):** Made predominantly from recycled fibers; economical, used in **cereal boxes, shoe boxes, secondary packaging**.

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition distinction (1 mark) + Four types listed with one-line descriptions (2 marks).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the Cobb test? State its significance in food packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cobb Test Definition:**
> The Cobb test (ISO 535 / TAPPI T441) measures the **water absorbency** of paper or paperboard by determining the mass of water absorbed per unit area in a specified time (typically 60 seconds), expressed in **g/m²**.

**Significance in Food Packaging:**

1. **Predicts Wet Strength Retention:** A low Cobb value indicates the material will retain its mechanical integrity when exposed to moist foods (frozen vegetables, ice cream, fresh meat).

2. **Indicates Coating Effectiveness:** Cobb values quantify the performance of barrier coatings (PE, wax, acrylic). Direct food-contact grades require Cobb₆₀ < 20 g/m².

3. **Quality Control Benchmark:** Used as a release specification in supplier quality agreements and ISO 9001 quality systems.

**Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition with test standard (1 mark) + Two significance points (2 marks).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and features an **internal choice**. Students must answer EITHER Option A OR Option B. Each option typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question 3 — Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q: (a) Describe the Kraft process for pulp manufacturing. What are its advantages over mechanical pulping for food-grade applications? (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the manufacturing of corrugated board with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of fluting in providing structural strength. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**The Kraft Process (Sulfate Process):**

The Kraft process is the dominant chemical pulping method for food-grade paper and paperboard.

1. **Wood Preparation:** Logs are debarked and chipped into uniform flakes (~25 mm length, 3–5 mm thickness).

2. **Digestion:** Wood chips are charged into a continuous digester along with **white liquor** (a solution of NaOH and Na₂S). Digestion occurs at:
   - Temperature: **160–180 °C**
   - Pressure: **8–10 bar**
   - Time: **1–2 hours**
   - Liquor-to-wood ratio: **3:1 to 4:1**

3. **Chemistry:** NaOH hydrolyzes lignin and carbohydrates; Na₂S accelerates delignification while protecting cellulose from degradation. The active alkali is **NaOH + Na₂S** (expressed as Na₂O equivalent).

4. **Washing and Screening:** The cooked pulp is washed with counter-current water to remove black liquor, then screened to remove shives (uncooked fiber bundles).

5. **Bleaching (Optional):** For food-grade white pulp, ECF bleaching with **ClO₂, H₂O₂, and O₂** stages achieves brightness > 88 % ISO.

6. **Recovery:** The black liquor is concentrated and burned in a recovery boiler to regenerate NaOH and Na₂S, making the process thermally self-sufficient.

**Advantages of Kraft Over Mechanical Pulping for Food Packaging:**

| Parameter | Kraft Pulp | Mechanical Pulp |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lignin content | < 5 % | ~ 25–30 % |
| Fiber strength | High (long, intact fibers) | Low (fiber damage from grinding) |
| Brightness potential | Up to 92 % ISO | Limited to ~ 60 % ISO |
| Yellowing on aging | Minimal | Severe (photo-oxidation of lignin) |
| Odor/Taste transfer to food | Negligible | Noticeable |
| Yield | 45–55 % | 85–95 % |
| FDA food-contact approval | Yes | Restricted |

**Marks (7):** Process steps (4) + Advantages comparison table (3).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Corrugated Board Manufacturing:**

The corrugated board is a sandwich structure consisting of two flat liner plies bonded to a central fluted (corrugated) medium.

1. **Liner Preparation:** Kraft or test liner rolls are fed from unwind stands.

2. **Corrugation:** The fluting medium passes through the **corrugator**, where it is pressed between two fluted (ridged) rolls heated to **160–180 °C**. This gives the medium its characteristic wave shape.

3. **Adhesive Application:** Starch-based adhesive (a mixture of **corn starch, NaOH, borax, and water**) is applied to the flute tips using a glue unit.

4. **Bonding:** The fluted medium is pressed against the outer liner at the **hot plate section** (~180 °C), where starch gelatinizes and bonds the plies.

5. **Slitting and Scoring:** The continuous board is slit to width and scored for fold lines.

6. **Cutting:** The board is cut into sheets using a rotary cutoff.

**Role of Fluting in Structural Strength:**

The fluting acts as an **I-beam** in cross-section:
- The **flute tips** (bonded to liners) act as the **flanges** that resist bending.
- The **flute webs** (vertical portions) act as the **shear web** that maintains flute geometry under load.

**Flute Types:**

| Flute Designation | Flute Height | Pitch (flutes per meter) | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A-Flute | 4.5–5.0 mm | ~ 105 | Heavy-duty shipping (glass, cans) |
| B-Flute | 2.5–3.0 mm | ~ 150 | Display cartons, die-cut boxes |
| C-Flute | 3.5–4.0 mm | ~ 120 | General shipping cartons (most common) |
| E-Flute | 1.0–1.5 mm | ~ 300 | Lightweight retail packaging |
| B+C (Double Wall) | Combined | — | Heavy food packaging (appliances, bulk produce) |

**Marks (7):** Manufacturing steps (3) + I-beam analogy explanation (2) + Flute types table (2).

---

### Question 3 — Option B (Alternative 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q: (a) Classify the various types of paper used in food packaging. Describe greaseproof paper and glassine paper in detail. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the concept of functional barriers and migration in paper-based food packaging. What regulations govern the use of paper in food contact applications? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Classification of Papers Used in Food Packaging:**

1. **Kraft Paper:** Unbleached, high-strength paper made from Kraft pulp. Used for flour sacks, sugar bags, grocery bags. Strong, rough texture, brown color.

2. **Bleached Paper:** White paper from bleached chemical pulp; used for bread wrapping, sandwich wraps, decorative packaging.

3. **Greaseproof Paper:** High-density paper made by **heavy beating (refining)** of pulp, which causes fibers to collapse and close the pore structure. Air resistance (Gurley) > 350 s. Used for **fast food wrappers, butter wrapping, bakery liners**.

4. **Glassine Paper:** Super-calendered greaseproof paper with extremely high density and transparency. Achieved by passing greaseproof paper through **super-calender stacks** (alternating steel and cotton/paper rolls) at high pressure (~500 bar) and temperature (80–100 °C). Used for **candy wrappers, bakery windows, interleaving for sticky foods**.

5. **Vegetable Parchment:** Paper passed through a **sulfuric acid bath** (75 % H₂SO₄, 5–15 s) that partially dissolves and gelatinizes surface cellulose, creating a dense, grease- and water-resistant structure. Used for **butter wrapping, baking cups, pet food**.

6. **Tissue Paper:** Very low grammage (17–30 g/m²) paper used for **fruit wrapping, delicate product protection, decorative interleaving**.

**Greaseproof vs. Glassine Comparison:**

| Parameter | Greaseproof | Glassine |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Manufacturing | Heavy refining | Greaseproof + super-calendering |
| Density | High | Very High (1.1 g/cm³) |
| Transparency | Translucent | Highly transparent |
| Air resistance (Gurley) | > 350 s | > 2000 s |
| Grease resistance | Good | Excellent |
| Surface | Slightly rough | Smooth, glossy |
| Cost | Moderate | High |

**Marks (7):** Classification (3) + Greaseproof detailed (2) + Glassine detailed (2).

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Functional Barriers and Migration in Paper Packaging:**

Unlike plastics, paper is a **porous, hygroscopic, multi-component material**. It contains cellulose, hemicellulose, residual lignin, sizing agents, wet-strength resins, optical brighteners, and mineral fillers. Each of these can potentially migrate into food.

**Migration Mechanisms:**

1. **Set-off Migration:** Contaminants transfer from the printed outer surface to the inner (food-contact) surface during reel winding under pressure.

2. **Diffusion Migration:** Low molecular weight substances (e.g., plasticizers, sizing agents) diffuse through the paper matrix over time.

3. **Gas-Phase Migration:** Volatile compounds (e.g., solvents, monomers) transfer through the air gap between packaging and food.

4. **Penetration Migration:** Gases, moisture, and oils penetrate through pinholes and fiber pores, carrying contaminants.

**Functional Barrier Concept:**

A **functional barrier** is an inner layer (typically PE extrusion, aluminum foil, or EVOH coating) that prevents migration of outer-layer contaminants into food. For example, in a printed SBS milk carton, a **15–20 µm PE extrusion layer** acts as the functional barrier between the printed surface and the milk.

**Regulations Governing Paper in Food Contact:**

| Region | Regulatory Body | Key Regulation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **European Union** | EFSA (European Food Safety Authority) | Framework Regulation (EC) No. 1935/2004; BfR Recommendations (Germany) |
| **United States** | FDA (Food and Drug Administration) | 21 CFR §176.170 (Paper in contact with aqueous/ fatty foods); 21 CFR §176.180 (Paper in contact with dry foods) |
| **India** | FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) | FSSAI Packaging Regulations, 2018 (Part V, Section 7); IS standards from BIS |
| **Germany** | BfR | BfR Recommendations on Paper (XXXVI) and Board (XXXVI/1) |

**Specific Migration Limits (SML) Examples:**
- **Diisopropyl naphthalene (DIPN):** < 5 mg/kg food (used as solvent in recycled board)
- **Mineral oil hydrocarbons (MOH):** EU has proposed limits under **Regulation (EU) 2023/1627** for MOAH (1 mg/kg for dry foods with effective barrier, 0.5 mg/kg without)
- **Formaldehyde:** < 15 mg/kg (from wet-strength resins)

**Marks (7):** Migration mechanisms (3) + Functional barrier concept (2) + Regulatory framework table (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing paper and paperboard grammage thresholds:** Students often state 200 g/m² or 250 g/m² without specifying the standard (TAPPI: 224; European: 250).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention bleachability in Kraft process:** Simply stating "Kraft uses NaOH" without noting Na₂S is incomplete.
> 3. **Mixing up SBS (Solid Bleached Sulfate) with SUS (Solid Unbleached Sulfate):** SBS is white, FDA-approved; SUS is brown. These are distinct grades.
> 4. **Ignoring migration and food safety:** Paper is not inherently food-safe. Examiners expect discussion of regulations (FDA 21 CFR, BfR, FSSAI) and barrier coatings.
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams:** Part B questions on corrugated board and multi-ply board structure lose up to **2 marks** without a labelled cross-section.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Paper and Paperboards**

- [x] **Paper < 224 GSM; Paperboard ≥ 224 GSM** (TAPPI standard)
- [x] **Kraft process** uses NaOH + Na₂S at 160–180 °C; removes ~90 % lignin; high fiber strength
- [x] **Mechanical pulping** retains lignin → yellowish, weaker, cheaper, non-food-grade
- [x] **Bleaching sequences:** ECF (ClO₂-based) is industry standard for food-grade pulp
- [x] **Fourdrinier machine** is the dominant wet-laid sheet-forming technology
- [x] **Paperboard types:** SBS (white, direct food contact), SUS (brown, strong), FBB (multi-ply, printable), CRB (recycled, economical), CUK (coated Kraft)
- [x] **Paper types:** Kraft, Bleached, Greaseproof (heavy refining), Glassine (super-calendered), Vegetable parchment (H₂SO₄ treated), Tissue
- [x] **Corrugated board = I-beam analogy:** Flutes = web; Liners = flanges
- [x] **Flute types:** A (largest), C (most common), B (smaller), E (lightweight), BC (double-wall)
- [x] **Cobb₆₀ < 20 g/m²** required for direct food-contact wet applications
- [x] **Burst Index = B / g_m** [kPa·m²/g]
- [x] **Tensile Index = σ_t / g_m** [N·m/g]
- [x] **Bulk = t / g_m** [cm³/g]
- [x] **Regulatory bodies:** FDA (US), EFSA/BfR (EU), FSSAI (India)
- [x] **Functional barriers** (PE, Al-foil, EVOH) prevent migration into food
- [x] **MOAH migration** is a current regulatory concern in recycled paperboard
- [x] **Sustainability advantage:** Paper has the highest recycling rate of all packaging materials (~67 % paper, ~81 % paperboard globally)

topics.title: Glass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Glass

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Glass** is defined by the **American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM)** as *"an inorganic product of fusion which has been cooled to a rigid condition without crystallizing."* In the context of food packaging, glass is an **amorphous, isotropic, transparent (or translucent), and dimensionally stable** container material formed primarily from **silica (SiO₂)**, combined with various network modifiers and stabilizers.

For food packaging applications, glass is one of the oldest, most trusted, and most chemically inert materials ever engineered. It functions as a **rigid, hermetically sealable container** that protects food from environmental, chemical, and biological degradation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Glass Vault of the Pharaoh"**
> Imagine ancient Egyptian royalty storing precious oils, wines, and grains in beautifully crafted glass vessels. Even after 3,000 years, archaeologists recover these contents remarkably intact. This is exactly what glass does for modern food — it acts as a **near-perfect time capsule**.

Think of glass packaging as a **three-layer protective shield** for your food:

| Shield Layer | Function in Food Packaging |
|---|---|
| **Chemical Shield** | Glass is virtually inert — it does not react with acidic, alkaline, or fatty foods. |
| **Barrier Shield** | It is impermeable to gases (O₂, CO₂), water vapor, and aroma compounds. |
| **Thermal Shield** | Can be sterilized at high temperatures (up to 300 °C) without deforming. |

In simple words: **Glass is a "silent guardian"** that protects food's taste, aroma, color, and nutritional value from the moment of filling until the moment of consumption.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical & Engineering Constants for Packaging Glass:**
> - **Density (ρ):** $\mathbf{2.5 \ g/cm^3}$ (soda-lime-silica glass)
> - **Young's Modulus (E):** $\mathbf{70 - 90 \ GPa}$
> - **Thermal Expansion Coefficient (α):** $\mathbf{8 - 9 \times 10^{-6} \ /°C}$
> - **Softening Point:** $\mathbf{700 - 750 \ °C}$
> - **Working Point:** $\mathbf{1000 - 1050 \ °C}$
> - **Refractive Index (n):** $\mathbf{1.5 - 1.55}$
> - **Recyclability:** **100%** — glass can be recycled infinitely without loss of quality.

---

## 1.4 Historical Context for KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently ask about the **transition from natural glass (obsidian) to manufactured glass**. Mesopotamia (circa **1500 BC**) is credited with the first man-made glass containers. The industrial revolution in the early 20th century introduced **automatic bottle-blowing machines** (Michael Owens, 1903), revolutionizing food packaging.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Chemical Composition of Packaging Glass

Glass is a complex multi-component system. The components are categorized based on their **functional role** in the silica network:

### A. Major Constituents

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Silica (SiO₂) — The Network Former (60–72%)**
> - The fundamental building block. It forms a continuous three-dimensional random network of SiO₄ tetrahedra.
> - Pure SiO₂ (fused quartz) has an extremely high melting point (~1700 °C) and is too viscous to work with commercially.

> [!NOTE]
> **2. Soda Ash (Na₂CO₃) — The Flux / Network Modifier (12–18%)**
> - Lowers the melting point of silica from ~1700 °C to a workable ~800 °C.
> - However, it makes the glass water-soluble (an undesirable property called "devitrification tendency").

> [!NOTE]
> **3. Limestone (CaCO₃) — The Stabilizer (5–15%)**
> - Reacts with the sodium to form a stable, insoluble, durable network.
> - Prevents the glass from dissolving in water.

### B. Minor / Functional Additives

| Additive | Chemical Formula | Function | Typical % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boric oxide | B₂O₃ | Increases chemical & thermal resistance (borosilicate glass) | 5 – 12 |
| Alumina | Al₂O₃ | Improves durability & thermal shock resistance | 1 – 4 |
| Lead oxide | PbO | Increases refractive index (crystal/decorative glass) | 5 – 30 |
| Iron sulfide | FeS / Fe₂O₃ | Colorant (amber/brown glass) | < 1 |
| Cobalt oxide | CoO | Colorant (blue glass) | < 0.1 |
| Manganese dioxide | MnO₂ | Decolorizer | < 0.5 |
| Sulfates | SO₃ | Fining agent (removes bubbles) | < 0.5 |

---

## 2.2 Structural Theory of Glass — The Random Network Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zachariasen's Random Network Theory (1932)** is the most accepted structural model and is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

**Core Principles (Why and How):**

1. **Why does glass lack long-range order?** — Because the atoms are frozen in a disordered arrangement as the melt is cooled rapidly (quenched), preventing crystal formation.
2. **How is the network built?** — Each silicon atom is bonded to **4 oxygen atoms** in a tetrahedral arrangement (SiO₄⁴⁻).
3. **What is the role of modifiers?** — Network modifiers (Na⁺, Ca²⁺) break some Si–O–Si bonds, creating non-bridging oxygens (NBO) that lower viscosity and melting temperature.

**Visualizing the network:**

- The **SiO₄ tetrahedron** is the basic unit.
- In a **crystal** (like quartz), these tetrahedra are arranged in a perfect periodic pattern.
- In **glass**, they are arranged in a **random, irregular, yet spatially continuous** pattern — imagine throwing a deck of cards in the air and freezing them in mid-fall.

---

## 2.3 Properties of Glass Relevant to Food Packaging

### 2.3.1 Chemical Properties
- **Chemical Inertness:** Glass does not react with most food products (pH 1 to pH 14).
- **No Permeation:** It is a 100% barrier to O₂, CO₂, water vapor, and aromas.
- **No Leaching:** Unlike plastics, glass does not release monomers or additives into food.

### 2.3.2 Physical Properties
- **Transparency:** Allows the consumer to see the product (important for marketing).
- **Rigidity:** Maintains shape under pressure; ideal for vacuum-packed and carbonated beverages.
- **Hardness:** Resistant to scratching (Mohs hardness ~5.5 – 6.5).
- **Brittleness:** Low fracture toughness; breaks under sudden impact.

### 2.3.3 Thermal Properties
- **Heat Resistance:** Can be autoclaved, pasteurized, and retorted.
- **Thermal Shock Resistance:** Determined by $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{E \cdot \alpha}$, where:
  - $\sigma_t$ = tensile strength
  - $E$ = Young's modulus
  - $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion
  - *Lower α means higher thermal shock resistance.*

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMINATION (Glass in Food Packaging)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Glass density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ | Quality control of container weight |
| 2 | Coefficient of thermal expansion | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot \Delta T}$ | $\mathrm{per \ °C}$ | Thermal shock resistance calculation |
| 3 | Maximum thermal shock | $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{E \cdot \alpha}$ | $\mathrm{°C}$ | Predicting container failure during pasteurization |
| 4 | Refractive index | $n = \dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$ | dimensionless | Optical clarity measurement |
| 5 | Stress in container wall (hoop stress) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot d}{2t}$ | $\mathrm{MPa}$ | Pressure resistance of bottles |
| 6 | Stress optical coefficient | $C = \dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{d \cdot \sigma}$ | $\mathrm{Brewsters}$ | Quality inspection under polarized light |
| 7 | Solar energy transmittance | $T = \dfrac{I_{transmitted}}{I_{incident}}$ | dimensionless | UV protection by colored glass |
| 8 | Network connectivity | $N_c = 4 - \text{(non-bridging O/Si)}$ | dimensionless | Predicting chemical durability |
| 9 | Recyclability efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{input}} \times 100$ | $\mathrm{\%}$ | Sustainability assessment |
| 10 | Annealing point (viscosity $= 10^{13}$ poise) | $T_{anneal}$ | $\mathrm{°C}$ | Defines stress-relief temperature |

> [!WARNING]

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Carbonated Beverage Industry:** Pressure-resistant glass bottles (champagne withstands ~6 atm internal pressure).
- **Baby Food Industry:** Sterilizable glass jars (thermal shock up to 60 °C ΔT).
- **Premium Spirits & Wines:** Amber glass blocks UV light, preventing photodegradation.
- **Pharmaceutical Cross-Use:** Type I borosilicate glass is used for injectables (linked to KTU Pharmaceutical Packaging modules).
- **Sustainability & Circular Economy:** Glass is the only food packaging material that is **infinitely recyclable** without quality loss.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Thermal Shock Resistance of a Glass Container

This is a **standard 7-mark KTU question** for derivation-based problems.

### Problem Statement
> Derive the expression for the maximum temperature difference ($\Delta T_{max}$) a glass container can withstand before fracture occurs due to sudden thermal stress.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** When a glass surface is suddenly cooled, the surface contracts while the inner bulk remains hot and expanded. This creates **tensile stress** on the surface.

**Step 2:** According to the linear thermal expansion law, the strain ($\epsilon$) produced by a temperature change is:

$$
\epsilon = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = coefficient of linear thermal expansion (per °C)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (°C)

**Step 3:** For an elastic material, stress is related to strain by Hooke's Law:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon
$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = stress developed (Pa)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa)

**Step 4:** Substitute the strain expression from Step 2 into Step 3:

$$
\sigma = E \cdot (\alpha \cdot \Delta T)
$$

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 5:** Glass fractures when the surface tensile stress reaches its **tensile strength** ($\sigma_t$). Therefore, the maximum allowable temperature difference is found by setting $\sigma = \sigma_t$:

$$
\sigma_t = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{max}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging for $\Delta T_{max}$:

$$
\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{E \cdot \alpha}
$$

**Final Derived Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{E \cdot \alpha}}
$$

### Numerical Evaluation (for better understanding)

> Given: $\sigma_t = 35 \ \mathrm{MPa}$, $E = 70 \ \mathrm{GPa}$, $\alpha = 9 \times 10^{-6} \ /°C$

$$
\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{35 \times 10^6}{70 \times 10^9 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{35 \times 10^6}{630 \times 10^3} = \dfrac{35}{0.63} \approx 55.6 \ °C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A standard soda-lime glass bottle can typically survive a **thermal shock of ~50–60 °C**. This is why glass containers taken from a refrigerator should not be placed directly into boiling water (ΔT can exceed 100 °C → instant fracture).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hoop Stress in a Pressurized Glass Bottle

Used to determine the **burst pressure** of carbonated beverage bottles.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a thin-walled cylindrical glass bottle of:
- Internal diameter = $d$
- Wall thickness = $t$
- Internal pressure = $P$

**Step 2:** The circumferential (hoop) force is balanced by the tensile stress in the cross-section of the wall. Equilibrium gives:

$$
P \cdot d \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L
$$

where:
- $L$ = length of cylinder under consideration
- $\sigma_h$ = hoop stress (circumferential stress)
- $2 t L$ = cross-sectional area resisting the burst force (two walls)

**Step 3:** Cancel $L$ from both sides:

$$
P \cdot d = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for hoop stress:

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot d}{2t}
$$

**Step 5:** The **maximum safe pressure** (burst pressure) is reached when $\sigma_h$ equals the tensile strength $\sigma_t$:

$$
\boxed{P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot t}{d}}
$$

### Numerical Evaluation

> Given: $\sigma_t = 35 \ \mathrm{MPa}$, $t = 3 \ \mathrm{mm} = 0.003 \ \mathrm{m}$, $d = 60 \ \mathrm{mm} = 0.06 \ \mathrm{m}$

$$
P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \times 35 \times 10^6 \times 0.003}{0.06}
$$

$$
P_{burst} = \dfrac{210000}{0.06} = 3.5 \times 10^6 \ \mathrm{Pa} \approx 34.5 \ \mathrm{atm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Champagne bottles are designed to withstand **6 atm**; standard soda bottles to **4 atm**; beer bottles to **3 atm**. The KTU 2024 ESE often asks students to compare this with PET bottle burst pressures (~10–12 atm) and explain the trade-off.

## 3.4 Glass Manufacturing Process — Analytical Step Trace

The **four-stage manufacturing process** for packaging glass (high-yield for KTU Part B):

1. **Batching:** Raw materials (silica, soda ash, limestone, cullet) are weighed precisely and mixed.
2. **Melting:** Heated in a furnace to **1500 – 1600 °C** to form a homogeneous molten glass.
3. **Forming:** Molten glass is shaped using:
   - **Blow-and-Blow process** (for narrow-neck bottles)
   - **Press-and-Blow process** (for wide-mouth jars)
4. **Annealing:** Glass passes through a **lehr** (an annealing oven) at **500 – 600 °C** to relieve internal stresses.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the **annealing point** with the **softening point**. The annealing point is when viscosity $= 10^{13}$ poise (stress relief), while the softening point is when viscosity $= 10^{7.6}$ poise (deformation begins). The KTU examiner will deduct marks if you mix these up.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define glass. List any four characteristic properties that make glass a suitable material for food packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Glass is an amorphous, inorganic, non-crystalline solid prepared by fusion of silica (SiO₂) with various oxides, cooled to a rigid state without crystallization (as per ASTM).

**Four characteristic properties:**
1. **Chemical Inertness** — does not react with food constituents across a wide pH range.
2. **Impermeability** — total barrier to gases, vapors, and aromas.
3. **Transparency** — allows product visibility and aesthetic presentation.
4. **Thermal Resistance** — can be sterilized at high temperatures (pasteurization, retorting).
5. *(Bonus point)* **100% Recyclability** — can be re-melted infinitely without quality loss.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for any 4 properties — total 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **composition of packaging glass**. Discuss the role of silica, soda ash, limestone, and cullet with relevant chemical reactions.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for **maximum thermal shock** a glass container can withstand. Using this expression, calculate the maximum temperature difference for a soda-lime glass bottle with: $\sigma_t = 35$ MPa, $E = 70$ GPa, $\alpha = 8.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**1. Silica (SiO₂) — 60 to 72% (Network Former)**
- Forms the basic structural network of SiO₄ tetrahedra.
- Pure silica has a melting point of ~1700 °C (impractical).
- **Reaction:** No reaction; remains as SiO₂ forming the backbone.

**2. Soda Ash (Na₂CO₃) — 12 to 18% (Flux / Network Modifier)**
- Lowers the melting point of silica to ~800 °C by breaking Si–O–Si bonds.
- **Chemical reaction during melting:**

$$
\mathrm{Na_2CO_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} Na_2O + CO_2 \uparrow}
$$

- The Na₂O produced acts as a network modifier:

$$
\mathrm{Na_2O + \equiv Si-O-Si \equiv \longrightarrow \equiv Si-O^- Na^+ + Na^+ ^-O-Si \equiv}
$$

- **Drawback:** Makes glass water-soluble; hence, a stabilizer is needed.

**3. Limestone (CaCO₃) — 5 to 15% (Stabilizer)**
- Combines with Na₂O to form a stable, durable, water-insoluble network.
- **Chemical reactions during melting:**

$$
\mathrm{CaCO_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} CaO + CO_2 \uparrow}
$$

$$
\mathrm{CaO + Na_2O \longrightarrow Ca-Na \ silicate \ (stable \ glass \ network)}
$$

**4. Cullet — 20 to 60% (Recycled Glass)**
- Crushed waste glass added to the batch.
- **Advantages:**
  - Lowers energy consumption (faster melting).
  - Reduces raw material cost by 30–40%.
  - Promotes sustainable packaging.
  - **Environmental bonus:** Supports circular economy — every ton of cullet saves ~315 kg of CO₂ emissions.

> **Conclusion:** The carefully balanced composition yields a transparent, durable, chemically inert, and thermally stable material — perfect for food packaging. *(7 marks: 1 mark for each component + 1 for reactions + 1 for cullet role + 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Theory:**
When a glass container is suddenly cooled, the surface contracts while the inner hot bulk resists the contraction. This generates **tensile stress ($\sigma$)** on the surface. The strain ($\epsilon$) produced is:

$$
\epsilon = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Hooke's Law:**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 3 — Failure criterion:**
The glass fractures when tensile stress reaches its tensile strength $\sigma_t$:

$$
\sigma_t = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T_{max}
$$

**Step 4 — Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{E \cdot \alpha}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical calculation:**
Given:
- $\sigma_t = 35$ MPa $= 35 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $E = 70$ GPa $= 70 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\alpha = 8.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C

$$
\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{35 \times 10^6}{70 \times 10^9 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{35 \times 10^6}{595 \times 10^3} = \dfrac{35}{0.595} \approx 58.8 \ °C
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**
A standard soda-lime glass bottle can survive a **maximum temperature difference of ~58.8 °C**. This is why refrigerated glass jars should not be immersed in hot water directly.

*(7 marks: 2 marks derivation + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark final answer + 2 marks interpretation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Classify **glass used for food packaging** with suitable examples. Explain the **USP classification** of glass in detail with focus on Type I, II, III, and NP glass.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of glass as a food packaging material. Compare it with **PET plastic** in terms of barrier properties, recyclability, and consumer perception.
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Introduction:**
Glass used in food (and pharmaceutical) packaging is broadly classified by the **United States Pharmacopoeia (USP)** based on **chemical resistance and intended use**.

**Classification Table:**

| USP Type | Composition | Surface Treatment | Chemical Resistance | Typical Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | Borosilicate (high B₂O₃) | None | **Highest** (USP <660> test) | Premium baby food, labware, premium coffee |
| **Type II** | Soda-lime (dealkalized) | Treated with SO₂ / ammonium sulfate | High | IV fluids, sensitive liquid foods |
| **Type III** | Regular soda-lime | None | Moderate | Jams, sauces, soft drinks, pickles |
| **Type NP** (Type IV) | Soda-lime (general) | None | Lowest | Oral solid foods, dry snacks, cosmetics |

**Key Process: Dealkalization (Type II):**
- The surface of soda-lime glass is treated with SO₂ gas at high temperature.
- This converts the surface Na₂O to Na₂SO₄, which is washed off.
- The remaining **leached silica-rich surface layer** has superior chemical resistance.

**Why this matters in food packaging:**
- Acidic foods (pH < 4.5) like tomato sauce can extract alkali from Type III glass, raising pH and causing container failure.
- Type I or II glass prevents this and protects food quality.

*(7 marks: 2 for classification + 2 for surface treatment + 2 for food application + 1 for relevance)*

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Advantages of Glass:**
1. **Total barrier:** Zero permeability to O₂, CO₂, water vapor, and aromas.
2. **Chemical inertness:** No leaching of chemicals into food.
3. **Heat resistance:** Can be retorted up to 121 °C.
4. **Transparency:** Excellent product visibility.
5. **100% recyclable:** No quality loss on remelting.
6. **Premium perception:** Associated with high quality, taste, and tradition.
7. **Microwave & dishwasher safe** (for food-grade types).

**Limitations of Glass:**
1. **Brittleness:** Breaks on impact → safety hazard.
2. **High weight:** Increases transportation cost & carbon footprint.
3. **Energy-intensive production:** Melting requires ~1500 °C.
4. **Higher cost** than plastic and metal cans.
5. **Slow filling line speeds** compared to PET.

**Comparison with PET:**

| Parameter | Glass | PET |
|---|---|---|
| **O₂ Permeability** | 0 (perfect barrier) | 5–12 cc/m²/day (moderate) |
| **Weight (1L bottle)** | ~380 g | ~35 g |
| **Recyclability** | Infinite (100%) | Limited (downcycled) |
| **Breakage** | Yes (hazard) | No (ductile) |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Higher (production energy) | Lower |
| **Consumer Perception** | Premium, "healthy" | Convenient, "modern" |
| **CO₂ Barrier (carbonated drinks)** | Excellent | Good (multi-layer improves) |

> **Conclusion:** Glass is the **gold standard for food quality and safety**, while PET is the **gold standard for cost, weight, and convenience**. The choice depends on the food product, market segment, and sustainability goals. *(7 marks: 3 for advantages, 2 for limitations, 2 for comparison table)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing annealing point with softening point.** The annealing point is at viscosity $10^{13}$ poise; softening point is at $10^{7.6}$ poise. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting units in thermal shock derivation.** Always write $\alpha$ in /°C, $E$ in Pa, $\sigma_t$ in Pa. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing USP Type I (borosilicate) with industrial glass types.** USP classification is for **pharmaceutical** glass; while it overlaps with food packaging for injectables, do not assume all food glass is USP-graded. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing the role of soda ash and limestone.** Soda ash is the **flux** (melting point reducer); limestone is the **stabilizer** (water solubility preventer). Reversing these roles costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring cullet** in the composition answer. Examiners expect 1 mark specifically for cullet's environmental role. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Not concluding Part B answers.** Always end with a one-line "engineering conclusion" — examiners award 0.5 to 1 mark for this.
> 7. **Writing glass types as "Type 1, 2, 3" with Arabic numerals** instead of Roman numerals (Type I, II, III) — minor but examiners do note inconsistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Glass in Food Packaging (Module 2)**

- **Definition (ASTM):** Glass is an *inorganic product of fusion cooled to a rigid condition without crystallizing.*
- **Main Composition:** SiO₂ (60–72%) + Na₂O (12–18%) + CaO (5–15%) + Cullet + Minor additives.
- **Network Former:** SiO₂ (forms the basic 3D random network).
- **Network Modifier / Flux:** Na₂O (lowers melting point).
- **Stabilizer:** CaO (prevents water solubility).
- **Theoretical Model:** Zachariasen's Random Network Theory (1932).
- **USP Glass Types:** I (borosilicate) → II (dealkalized soda-lime) → III (regular soda-lime) → NP (Type IV, general soda-lime).
- **Standard Density:** $\rho \approx 2.5 \ \mathrm{g/cm^3}$.
- **Standard Thermal Expansion:** $\alpha \approx 8.5 \times 10^{-6} \ /°C$ (soda-lime).
- **Borosilicate Thermal Expansion:** $\alpha \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-6} \ /°C$ (much lower).
- **Maximum Thermal Shock Formula:** $\Delta T_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{E \cdot \alpha}$.
- **Hoop Stress Formula:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot d}{2t}$.
- **Burst Pressure Formula:** $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_t \cdot t}{d}$.
- **Manufacturing Process:** Batching → Melting (1500–1600 °C) → Forming (Blow-Blow or Press-Blow) → Annealing (500–600 °C) → Inspection → Surface coating (SnO₂ or polymer).
- **Annealing Point:** Viscosity $= 10^{13}$ poise.
- **Softening Point:** Viscosity $= 10^{7.6}$ poise.
- **Working Point:** Viscosity $= 10^{4}$ poise (~1000 °C).
- **Recyclability:** 100% — infinitely recyclable without quality loss.
- **Key Advantages:** Inert, impermeable, transparent, heat-resistant, recyclable, premium perception.
- **Key Limitations:** Brittle, heavy, energy-intensive, costly.
- **Comparison Must-Knows:** Glass = perfect barrier, PET = better convenience, Glass = heavier, PET = lighter, Glass = infinite recyclability, PET = downcycled.
- **Amber Glass:** Blocks UV light — used for beer, oils, vitamins, light-sensitive foods.
- **Opal Glass:** Opaque white — used for yogurt, desserts, cosmetics.
- **Cullet Benefits:** Saves energy (~30%), reduces CO₂ emissions (~315 kg/ton), lowers cost.
- **Forming Process:** Blow-Blow for narrow-neck bottles; Press-Blow for wide-mouth jars.
- **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always write units in derivations, use Roman numerals for USP types, and conclude Part B answers with a one-line engineering statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (As per KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Metal Containers** are rigid, hermetically sealable packaging structures manufactured primarily from **tinplate (tin-coated low-carbon steel)**, **aluminum (Al)**, **tin-free steel (TFS/ECCFS)**, and **stainless steel (SS)**, used predominantly for the long-term ambient storage, thermal processing (retorting), and distribution of food products.

In the KTU framework, metal containers are positioned as the **gold standard for shelf-stable food packaging** because they offer a unique combination of total barrier protection (against light, oxygen, moisture, and microorganisms) and mechanical strength sufficient to withstand high-pressure thermal sterilization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Fortress Wall"**
>
> Imagine a medieval fortress protecting a kingdom's treasure:
>
> - The **steel base plate** of a metal can is the **outer fortress wall** — thick, strong, and structurally rigid, capable of resisting physical impacts during transportation.
> - The **electrolytic tin coating** acts as the **inner smooth plaster** — it does not let the food (treasure) react chemically with the steel (stone wall).
> - The **organic lacquer** is the **decorative inner lining** — an additional protective barrier, like silk wallpaper inside a king's chamber.
> - The **hermetic double seam** at the can ends is the **locked main gate** — sealed so perfectly that even a single bacterium cannot breach it.
>
> Just as the fortress protects the treasure for years, a metal can preserves food for **2 to 5+ years** without refrigeration.

## 1.3 Classification of Metal Packaging Materials

Metal containers in food packaging are manufactured from four principal substrates. Each has a specific role:

| Substrate | Symbol / Designation | Key Property | Primary Food Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tinplate | T.P. | Steel coated with thin tin layer | Cans, closures, aerosol tops |
| Tin-Free Steel | TFS / ECCFS | Steel coated with chromium/chromium oxide | Can ends, drawn cans |
| Aluminum | Al | Lightweight, ductile | Beverage cans, foil trays |
| Stainless Steel | SS (304, 316) | Corrosion-resistant alloy | Bulk containers, processing |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard metrics are universally referenced in KTU question papers and industry specifications (EN 10202 / ASTM A624):
>
> - **Tin coating designation system:** E-numbers (e.g., E1, E2, E4, E8) where the digit indicates grams of tin per **square metre per side**.
> - **Base steel thickness range:** typically **0.14 mm to 0.49 mm**.
> - **Temper grade range:** T-1 (deep draw) to T-5 (rigid).
> - **Chromium passivation film (TFS):** **5 to 15 mg/m²** of total chromium.
> - **Standard can end diameters:** 73 mm (small), 99 mm (medium), 153 mm (large).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping (CO Alignment):**
> This module primarily addresses **CO2 — Analyse advanced packaging materials and their barrier properties**, with specific focus on **Bloom's Taxonomy Level L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyse)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Tinplate Sheet — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

A finished tinplate sheet is a sandwich of five distinct layers. From the food-contact side outward, the layered architecture is:

> **Food → Lacquer → Tin (Sn) layer → Iron-Tin Alloy Layer (FeSn₂) → Steel Base (Fe)**

### Logic Sequence (The "Why" and "How"):

1. **Steel Base Plate (Fe)** — Provides the bulk mechanical strength and formability. The steel is a low-carbon (< 0.12% C) cold-rolled strip with carefully controlled metallurgy to ensure deep-drawing capability without cracking.

2. **Iron-Tin Alloy Layer (FeSn₂)** — Formed during the **reflow operation** (heating tin above its melting point of **231.9 °C**). This intermetallic layer provides the **true adhesion** between tin and steel, since pure tin does not bond well to bare iron.

3. **Free Tin Layer (Sn)** — The unalloyed tin on top, providing the bright, reflective, corrosion-resistant surface that gives tinplate its name and solderability.

4. **Passivation Film (Cr / Cr-Oxide)** — An extremely thin (**~1 nm**) layer of hydrated chromium oxides applied by electrochemical chromate treatment. It prevents **tin oxide growth (tarnish)** during storage and improves lacquer adhesion.

5. **Oil Film** — A thin (**~5 mg/m²**) layer of food-grade oil (typically DOS — di-octyl sebacate) applied to prevent scratching during coil handling.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are examinable and frequently appear in numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Specification | Symbolic Form | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tin Coating Weight | $W_{Sn} = A \times d_{Sn}$ | grams | $A$ = area (m²); $d_{Sn}$ = coating mass per side (g/m²) |
| 2 | Tin Coating from Thickness | $d_{Sn} = \rho_{Sn} \times t_{Sn} \times 10^{3}$ | g/m² | $\rho_{Sn}$ = **7.28 g/cm³**; $t_{Sn}$ = thickness ($\mu$m) |
| 3 | Passivation Film Density | $d_{Cr} = \rho_{Cr} \times t_{Cr} \times 10^{3}$ | g/m² | $\rho_{Cr}$ = **7.19 g/cm³** |
| 4 | Dimensional Yield | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{food}}{m_{can}} \times 100$ | % | Mass efficiency of a can |
| 5 | Tinplate Gauge to Thickness | $t = 0.0254 \times \dfrac{1}{\text{gauge}}$ | mm | For approximate conversion |
| 6 | Differentially Coated Plate | $E_{\text{top}} \neq E_{\text{bottom}}$ | g/m² | Top side (food) typically heavier |
| 7 | Yield Strength (Temper) | T-1 ($\leq 360$ MPa) to T-5 ($\geq 550$ MPa) | MPa | Increasing temper = increasing rigidity |

### Boundary Conditions & Limits:

- **Minimum tin coating:** **E1 (1.0 g/m² per side)** for non-aggressive foods like dry powders.
- **Maximum tin coating:** **E8 (8.0 g/m² per side)** for highly corrosive products like cherries, pickles, sauerkraut.
- **Steel thickness range for food cans:** **0.17 mm (No. 107 can) to 0.32 mm (No. 2.5 can)** in USA standard designations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:**
>
> - The **FeSn₂ alloy layer** is the unsung hero — without it, the tin would simply flake off during the deep-drawing operation used to form the can body.
> - **Passivation film chemistry** is the difference between a can that lasts 6 months on a shelf versus 6 years. Without chromium oxide, tinplate oxidizes and forms unsightly black SnO₂ patches that consumers reject.
> - **Differential tin coating** (e.g., E4/E2) is a cost-saving engineering trick: heavier tin on the food-contact side (more corrosion risk), lighter tin on the outside (less corrosion risk).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Tin Coating Weight from Thickness

> This is a standard **KTU numerical problem** (typically worth 3-4 marks). The full step-by-step derivation is provided.

**Problem Statement:**
A tinplate sheet has a free tin layer of thickness **0.4 µm** on one side. Calculate the tin coating weight in **g/m²**. Given: density of tin $\rho_{Sn} = 7.28$ **g/cm³**.

### Step 1: Identify and Convert All Units to a Common Base

The thickness is given in micrometres ($\mu$m). To convert to centimetres:

$$
t_{Sn} = 0.4 \, \mu m = 0.4 \times 10^{-4} \, cm
$$

Because:
$$
1 \, \mu m = 10^{-6} \, m = 10^{-4} \, cm
$$

### Step 2: Recall the Fundamental Mass Formula

The mass of tin per unit area (coating weight) is:

$$
d_{Sn} = \rho_{Sn} \times t_{Sn}
$$

Where:
- $d_{Sn}$ = coating weight in **g/cm²**
- $\rho_{Sn}$ = density in **g/cm³**
- $t_{Sn}$ = thickness in **cm**

### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
d_{Sn} = 7.28 \, \frac{g}{cm^{3}} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4} \, cm
$$

Multiplying the numerical coefficients:

$$
d_{Sn} = 7.28 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
d_{Sn} = 2.912 \times 10^{-4} \, \frac{g}{cm^{2}}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Standard Industry Units (g/m²)

Since $1 \, m^{2} = 10^{4} \, cm^{2}$, we multiply by $10^{4}$:

$$
d_{Sn} = 2.912 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{4} \, \frac{g}{m^{2}}
$$

$$
d_{Sn} = 2.912 \, \frac{g}{m^{2}}
$$

### Step 5: Final Answer with Interpretation

$$
\boxed{d_{Sn} \approx 2.91 \, g/m^{2}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This corresponds to a tinplate designated **E3** (between the standard E2 and E4 grades), suitable for moderately corrosive foods such as vegetables in brine.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tinplate Temper Grades (Derivation Logic)

The temper grade governs formability. Each step in the temper range corresponds to a different cold-rolling reduction percentage:

$$
\text{Temper Reduction (\%)} = \frac{t_{annealed} - t_{rolled}}{t_{annealed}} \times 100
$$

### Step-by-Step Computation Logic:

1. **T-1 (No temper rolling, fully annealed):** Yield strength $\leq 360$ MPa. Used for very deep-drawn cans (e.g., drawn redrawn tuna cans).

2. **T-2 (Light temper):** ~ 4-6% reduction. Used for shallow drawn ends.

3. **T-3 (Moderate temper):** ~ 7-9% reduction. Used for general can bodies (3-piece welded cans).

4. **T-4 (Hard temper):** ~ 10-12% reduction. Used for can ends requiring high buckle resistance.

5. **T-5 (Extra hard):** ~ 14-16% reduction. Yield strength $\geq 550$ MPa. Used for rigid closures.

> The mathematical trend is monotonic: as cold-work reduction increases, dislocation density in the steel rises, increasing **yield strength ($\sigma_{y}$)** but decreasing **elongation (%)** — a classic engineering trade-off.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Differentially Coated Tinplate

**Problem:**
A tinplate is designated **E4/2** (4.0 g/m² on the food side, 2.0 g/m² on the outside). If the sheet is **0.22 mm** thick and **1 m²** in area, calculate the **total tin mass per sheet** (in grams).

### Step 1: Understand the Designation

Differential coating means the two surfaces carry different tin weights:
- **Side A (food contact):** $d_{A} = 4.0 \, g/m^{2}$
- **Side B (outside):** $d_{B} = 2.0 \, g/m^{2}$

### Step 2: Calculate Total Coating Weight per m²

$$
d_{total} = d_{A} + d_{B}
$$

$$
d_{total} = 4.0 + 2.0 = 6.0 \, g/m^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Multiply by Sheet Area

For a 1 m² sheet:

$$
M_{Sn} = d_{total} \times A
$$

$$
M_{Sn} = 6.0 \, \frac{g}{m^{2}} \times 1 \, m^{2} = 6.0 \, g
$$

### Step 4: Final Answer

$$
\boxed{M_{Sn} = 6.0 \, \text{grams of tin per m}^{2} \text{ of sheet}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite being called "tinplate," only **6 grams of tin** protect a full square metre of sheet — a remarkably thin layer that nonetheless provides complete corrosion protection when intact.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Tinplate Manufacturing Process Flow

The following flowchart illustrates the industrial **electrolytic tinplate manufacturing line**. Each node represents a unit operation, and the arrows depict the material flow direction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Steel Strip Entry Cold Rolled Low Carbon Steel] --> B[Electrolytic Cleaning Alkaline Degreasing]
    B --> C[Pickling Acid Wash to Remove Rust]
    C --> D[Annealing Box Recrystallization 600 to 700 deg C]
    D --> E[Temper Rolling Skin Pass for Hardness Control]
    E --> F[Electrolytic Tinning Cathodic Tin Deposition]
    F --> G[Reflow Inductive Heating Above 231.9 deg C]
    G --> H[Quench Rapid Cooling]
    H --> I[Passivation Electrochemical Chromate Treatment]
    I --> J[Oiling DOS or Cottonseed Oil Application]
    J --> K[Inspection and Coiling Finished Tinplate]
    K --> L[Sheeting and Stacking for Shipment]
```

### Interpretation of the Flow:

- **Nodes A → E** constitute the **steel preparation stage**, ensuring a clean, ductile, and dimensionally precise substrate.
- **Node F** is the **tinning step**, where tin ions (Sn²⁺) are reduced onto the strip from a stannous sulfate or phenolsulfonic acid bath.
- **Node G (Reflow)** is the **most critical step** — it creates the FeSn₂ alloy layer responsible for tin-steel adhesion.
- **Nodes I → J** are the **finishing steps** that ensure storage stability and lacquer adhesion.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Metal Cans

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Metal Food Cans] --> B0[Three Piece Cans 3 PC]
    A0 --> C0[Two Piece Cans 2 PC]
    B0 --> B1[Body with Soldered or Welded Side Seam]
    B0 --> B2[Two Seamed Ends Top and Bottom]
    C0 --> C1[DRD Drawn and Redrawn]
    C0 --> C2[DI Drawn and Ironed]
    B1 --> D0[Common for Vegetables Soups Fruits]
    B2 --> E0[Common for Beers Soft Drinks]
    C1 --> F0[Used for Tuna Fish Pet Food]
    C2 --> G0[Used for Carbonated Beverages]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Can Seaming Process

The **double seam** is the most critical engineered feature of any metal can. It is formed by interlocking the can end (curl) with the can body (flange) through a mechanical seaming operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Can Body Flange] --> B[First Operation Roll Forms Hook]
    C[Can End Curl with Sealing Compound] --> B
    B --> D[Interlocked Hooks Body and End]
    D --> E[Second Operation Roll Presses Tight]
    E --> F[Compressed Sealing Compound Hermetic Seal]
    F --> G[Finished Double Seam Tightness and Wrinkle Test]
```

### Engineering Significance:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The seaming operation transforms two separate pieces (body + end) into a **single hermetically sealed unit**. The seam must withstand:
>
> - Internal pressure from **hot-fill retorting** (up to **0.7 MPa** at 121 °C).
> - External stacking loads of **~ 15 kN** during warehouse storage.
> - Microbial challenge tests (commercial sterility > 5 log reduction of *C. botulinum*).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **List any three advantages of metal containers over other packaging materials.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Total Barrier Protection:** Metal cans are impermeable to light, oxygen, water vapour, and microorganisms, eliminating the need for refrigeration or preservatives for shelf-stable products.

2. **Mechanical Strength:** They withstand high-pressure thermal processing (retorting at 121 °C) and rough handling during long-distance distribution, extending shelf life to **2 to 5 years**.

3. **Formability and Stackability:** Tinplate and aluminum can be formed into diverse shapes (cylindrical, oval, rectangular) and stacked efficiently, optimizing warehouse and shelf space utilization.

> *(Any 3 valid points — each carrying 1 mark — for full 3 marks.)*

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between Tinplate (TP) and Tin-Free Steel (TFS).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Tinplate (TP) | Tin-Free Steel (TFS / ECCFS) |
|---|---|---|
| Coating | Metallic tin (Sn) | Chromium / Chromium oxide (Cr / Cr-Oₓ) |
| Coating weight | 1.0 to 11.2 g/m² per side | 5 to 15 mg/m² per side (chromium) |
| Solderability | Excellent (solderable side) | Not solderable |
| Lacquer adhesion | Good | Excellent (better than TP) |
| Appearance | Bright, reflective | Dull, grayish |
| Main application | Can bodies, easy-open ends | Can ends, drawn cans, twist-off closures |

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A — Choice Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

> **(a)** Discuss the **manufacturing process of electrolytic tinplate** with a neat flowchart. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the various **types of metal cans** used in food packaging, highlighting the difference between **two-piece and three-piece constructions**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a):

The manufacturing of electrolytic tinplate involves the following sequential unit operations:

**Step 1 — Steel Strip Preparation:** A cold-rolled low-carbon steel strip (C < 0.12%, Mn < 0.6%) is the starting raw material. It is supplied in coils of up to **20 tonnes**.

**Step 2 — Electrolytic Cleaning:** The strip passes through an alkaline cleaning bath (typically NaOH + surfactant at **80-90 °C**) to remove rolling oils, mill dirt, and iron fines.

**Step 3 — Pickling:** A dilute sulfuric or hydrochloric acid wash removes surface oxide scale and exposes fresh metallic iron.

**Step 4 — Annealing:** The strip is heated to **600-700 °C** in a protective atmosphere (typically **HNX gas** — 5% H₂ + 95% N₂) for **8-10 hours** in a continuous or batch annealing furnace. This recrystallizes the deformed grains, restoring ductility.

**Step 5 — Temper Rolling:** A light cold-rolling pass (**2-16% reduction**) imparts the required yield strength (T-1 to T-5 temper grade) and surface finish (stone finish, satin finish, or mirror finish).

**Step 6 — Electrolytic Tinning:** The strip acts as the **cathode** in an electrolytic cell containing either a **ferrostan bath (phenolsulfonic acid)** or a **halogen bath (SnCl₂ + KF)**. Current density is **20-50 A/dm²**, with tin ions reduced to metallic tin on the strip surface.

**Step 7 — Reflow:** The strip is heated by induction or resistance heating to **232 °C+** (above tin's melting point), then immediately quenched. This creates the **FeSn₂ alloy layer (~ 0.5 µm thick)** that anchors the tin to the steel.

**Step 8 — Passivation:** An electrochemical chromate treatment deposits a thin film of **mixed chromium oxides (5-15 mg/m²)** to prevent tin tarnish during storage.

**Step 9 — Oiling:** A thin layer (**~ 5 mg/m²**) of **di-octyl sebacate (DOS)** or cottonseed oil is electrostatically applied to prevent scratching during coiling.

*(Award 1 mark for each correctly described step; 7 steps ≈ 7 marks.)*

#### Model Answer for Part (b):

**Three-Piece Cans (3-PC):**

A 3-PC can consists of a **cylindrical body** plus **two ends** (top and bottom). The body is formed from a flat rectangular tinplate sheet that is:
1. Cut to size and **notched**.
2. Rolled into a cylinder and **side-seamed** (by **soldering**, **welding**, or **cementing**).
3. Filled with food.
4. Closed by double-seaming a top end.

**Advantages:** Economical for small batches, versatile sizes, can be made from differential-coated tinplate.

**Disadvantages:** Side seam is a potential weak point (especially soldered seams which may contain lead — now restricted).

**Two-Piece Cans (2-PC):**

A 2-PC can has **no side seam**; the body and bottom are a single deep-drawn piece.

**Sub-types:**
- **DRD (Drawn and Redrawn):** The blank is progressively drawn through multiple dies to achieve the final depth. Wall thickness is roughly uniform. Used for **tuna, pet food, sardines**.
- **D&I (Drawn and Ironed):** The cup is drawn to a shallow depth, then "ironed" through a set of rings that thin the walls while elongating the body. Used for **carbonated beverage cans** — walls become as thin as **0.07 mm** while the bottom remains thick (~ **0.30 mm**).

**Advantages:** No side seam = superior hygiene, no solder, lighter weight, higher burst strength.

**Disadvantages:** Higher capital cost (requires specialized presses), limited to round/oval cross-sections, bottom thickness wastes material.

*(Distribute 7 marks across construction details, examples, advantages, and disadvantages of each.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B — Choice Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`

> **(a)** Discuss the **lacquering systems** used in metal cans for food packaging. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **corrosion mechanisms** in metal containers and the methods to prevent them. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a):

A **lacquer** (also called a can coating or enamel) is an organic film applied to the food-contact side of tinplate or aluminum to provide an additional barrier between the food and the metal. Without lacquer, aggressive foods would corrode the tin and cause **"hydrogen swell"** (bombs) or **"tin pickup"** in the food.

**Classification of Lacquers by Curing Mechanism:**

| Lacquer Type | Composition | Application Method | Cure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy phenolic** | Epoxy resin + phenolic resin | Spray or roller | **210 °C / 10 min** |
| **Organosol** | PVC + epoxy + plasticizer | Spray | **200 °C / 10 min** |
| **Epoxy-anhydride** | Epoxy + acid anhydride | Spray | **200 °C / 10 min** |
| **Acrylic** | Acrylic resin | Spray | **180 °C / 10 min** |
| **Vinyl** | PVC-PVA copolymer | Spray | **175 °C / 10 min** |

**Classification by Function:**

- **Plain (unlined) cans:** For non-aggressive foods like dry milk, sugar, vegetable oils.
- **C-Enamel (corn enamel):** Gold-colored lacquer to mask sulfide staining (e.g., corn, peas).
- **R-Enamel (raspberry enamel):** Red-pigmented to mask beetroot and berry color changes.
- **Meat-resistant lacquer:** Pigmented with aluminum or zinc oxide to resist meat-protein reactions.
- **Aluminum cans:** Coated with **vinyl** or **epoxy** to prevent metal-ion leaching into acidic beverages.

**Quality Specification:** Lacquer film weight typically **40-80 mg/dm²** (dry film). Must pass **WACO (Wet Adhesion, Cross-hatch, Orange peel)** tests.

#### Model Answer for Part (b):

**Corrosion Mechanisms in Metal Cans:**

There are **three principal electrochemical mechanisms**:

1. **Hydrogen Type Corrosion (Detinning):**
   - Occurs in **acidic foods** (citrus, tomato, pineapple).
   - Tin is **more active** than iron in the tin-iron galvanic couple, so tin dissolves preferentially (sacrificial anode).
   - Hydrogen gas evolves at the iron cathode.
   - **Result:** "Hydrogen swell" — the can bulges and may burst.

2. **Oxygen Type Corrosion (Pitting):**
   - Occurs in **neutral or mildly alkaline foods** (peas, corn, meat).
   - Oxygen dissolved in the food acts as the cathodic depolarizer.
   - **Iron** dissolves preferentially (iron is the anode).
   - **Result:** Pitting corrosion, pinholes, dark iron sulfide stains.

3. **Sulfur Type Staining:**
   - Occurs with **sulfur-containing foods** (eggs, meat, fish).
   - Sulfur compounds (H₂S, mercaptans) react with tin to form **black tin sulfide (SnS)**.
   - **Result:** Cosmetic darkening without affecting food safety.

**Prevention Methods:**

| Method | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Proper lacquer selection** | Insulates food from metal |
| **Sulfur-resistant lacquer (meat lacquer)** | Contains zinc oxide to absorb H₂S |
| **Vacuum seaming** | Removes O₂ from headspace |
| **Hot-fill + steam-flow closing** | Displaces O₂ with steam |
| **De-aeration of food** | Reduces dissolved O₂ |
| **Differential tin coating (E4/E2)** | Sacrificial tin on food side |
| **Nitrogen injection** | Replaces headspace O₂ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing tin and iron roles in corrosion:** Many students incorrectly state that iron corrodes first in acidic foods. **Remember:** Tin is the **sacrificial anode** in acidic media (Sn is more electronegative than Fe in acid).
> 2. **Mixing up DRD and D&I can types:** DRD = uniform wall thickness; D&I = thinned walls (ironed). Examiners specifically deduct for this error.
> 3. **Forgetting FeSn₂ alloy layer:** The reflow step creates the FeSn₂ intermetallic — without it, the tin would peel off. Many students describe tinning without mentioning reflow.
> 4. **Wrong lacquer cure temperatures:** Epoxy phenolic cures at **210 °C**, not 150 °C or 250 °C. Memorize the standard cure schedule.
> 5. **Not converting units in numericals:** Coating weight numericals always require careful conversion ($\mu$m → cm → g/cm² → g/m²).

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
