> **High-Density Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- ✅ Metal containers are made from **tinplate, TFS, aluminum, and stainless steel**.
- ✅ Tinplate = **steel + tin + chromium passivation + oil**.
- ✅ **E-numbers** denote tin coating weight in **g/m² per side** (E1 to E11.2).
- ✅ **Reflow** at **> 232 °C** creates the **FeSn₂ alloy layer** responsible for tin adhesion.
- ✅ **Passivation film** = 5-15 mg/m² of chromium; prevents tarnish.
- ✅ **Tin coating mass formula:** $d_{Sn} = \rho_{Sn} \times t_{Sn} \times 10^{3}$ (with proper unit conversion).
- ✅ **Three-piece cans** = body + 2 ends; **two-piece cans** = DRD or D&I.
- ✅ **D&I cans** are used for beverages (thin walls ~ **0.07 mm**).
- ✅ **DRD cans** are used for tuna and pet food (uniform wall thickness).
- ✅ **Hydrogen swell** = acidic food detinning (Sn dissolves, H₂ gas evolves).
- ✅ **Oxygen pitting** = neutral food, iron dissolves, pinhole formation.
- ✅ **Sulfur staining** = black SnS formation in meat/fish — cosmetic only.
- ✅ **Lacquer types:** Epoxy phenolic (most common), organosol, vinyl, acrylic.
- ✅ **C-enamel** masks sulfide blackening; **meat lacquer** contains ZnO.
- ✅ **Double seam** = body flange + end curl interlocked by mechanical seaming rolls.
- ✅ **Temper grades:** T-1 (softest, deep draw) → T-5 (hardest, rigid ends).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cans - Tinplate Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Cans - Tinplate Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **tinplate container** is a hermetically sealed, rigid metal package fabricated primarily from **tinplate** — a cold-reduced low-carbon steel sheet (the *base steel*) coated on both faces with a microscopically thin, continuous layer of commercially pure tin (typically **>99.85 % Sn**) deposited by either hot-dipping or, more commonly today, **electrolytic deposition**. In the context of food packaging, tinplate cans constitute the most commercially dominant **sanitary (open-top) food can** format globally, valued for their mechanical strength, total light/oxygen barrier properties, heat-processability (retortability), recyclability, and long ambient shelf-life performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Tinplate containers are steel-based packaging containers in which a thin layer of tin is electrodeposited onto a low-carbon mild steel substrate to provide corrosion resistance, solderability, and formability for hermetic food packaging applications.*

### 1.2 Historical Evolution Snapshot

- **1810** — Peter Durand (UK) patents the tin-plated iron can.
- **1900s** — Hot-dipped tinplate dominance.
- **1937** — First electrolytic tinplate line installed.
- **1960s onward** — Two-piece (drawn-and-ironed, drawn-and-redrawn) cans gradually replace three-piece soldered cans for beverages and many foods.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Knight in Tin Armour

Imagine a medieval knight: the **iron body** is strong, heavy, and structurally rigid, but it **rusts** the moment it touches salty sweat (analogous to brine or acidic food). The knight solves this by wearing a **shiny suit of tin armour** — extremely thin, mirror-bright, and chemically noble. The iron carries the load; the tin plating acts as a *sacrificial noble barrier* that protects the steel beneath from electrolytic corrosion by food constituents. The tin, being a noble metal with respect to iron in most food systems, polarises as the cathode and the steel as the anode, but in this case the small tin area *slowly sacrifices itself first* when the lacquer is breached, allowing a *controlled, food-safe* corrosion rather than catastrophic black-iron rust. In short: **steel gives strength, tin gives chemical protection.**

### 1.4 Why Tinplate and Not Bare Steel or Aluminium?

| Property | Tinplate | Aluminium | Bare Steel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | **7.85** (steel core) | **2.70** | **7.85** |
| Strength | High | Moderate | High |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher | Lower |
| Retort suitability | Excellent | Requires coatings | Rusts rapidly |
| Weldability/Solderability | Excellent | Difficult | Excellent |
| Magnetic (for sorter) | Yes | No | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** Tinplate is *the* reference metal can substrate for retort-stable shelf-stable foods (fruits, vegetables, fish, meat, dairy, ready meals). Aluminium is preferred only when weight savings or specialised drawing (e.g., beverage can) dominate design priorities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Layered Architecture of Tinplate

A modern food-grade tinplate sheet is a multi-layered sandwich:

1. **Base Steel** — low-carbon cold-reduced strip; thickness **0.14–0.50 mm**; ferritic.
2. **Tin Layer (FeSn₂ alloy + free Sn)** — total typically **0.4–1.5 µm** per side.
3. **Tin-Iron Alloy (FeSn₂) Intermetallic Layer** — thin brittle layer improving lacquer adhesion.
4. **Passivation Film** — cathodic dichromate or sodium dichromate dip (≈ **5 mg Cr/m²**), modern versions use chromium-free passivation.
5. **Lubricant / Oil film** (DOS – dioctyl sebacate, or cottonseed oil) — protects during coiling.
6. **Organic Coating / Lacquer** (optional, on one or both sides).

### 2.2 ASTM / KTU-Recognised Steel Base Classifications

The *American Iron and Steel Institute (AISI)* and *ASTM A623* designation system is universally used in food-canning specifications:

| Steel Type | C-content (max) | Key Property | Typical Food Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type MR** (Mild Rolled) | 0.13 % | Excellent corrosion resistance; moderate strength | Acidic fruits, vegetables (most common) |
| **Type MC** (Medium-Carbon) | 0.13 % with P/S | Slightly stronger than MR | Beverages, mild media |
| **Type L** (Low-tin line) | 0.13 % | Designed for *thick tin* application | Where corrosion resistance is critical (e.g., tomato paste) |
| **Type D** (Drawn) | 0.12 % | Excellent ductility for deep drawing | Two-piece D&I cans, drawn ends |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exam, **Type MR** is the default reference. State the basis: *low carbon, vacuum-degassed, fully stabilized with nitrogen (often with Ti or Nb).*

### 2.3 Tin Coating Weight Designation (Electrolytic)

Electrolytic tinplate is designated by **E** plus two numbers giving the nominal coating weight (in **g/m² per side**), one for each face. Examples:

| Designation | Inner-face / Outer-face tin (g/m²) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **E 2.8 / 2.8** | Equally-coated | Mild products; lacquered ends |
| **E 5.6 / 5.6** | Equal, heavier | General foods |
| **E 8.4 / 8.4** | Heavy equal | Aggressive products (tomato) |
| **E 11.2 / 11.2** | Very heavy | Extremely corrosive media |
| **D 5.6 / 11.2** | Differential | Acidic fruit juices: heavier tin on product side |
| **D 2.8 / 8.4** | Differential | Where lacquer covers outer face |

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential tinplate** is a frequently tested KTU concept: a heavier coating is applied to the *product-contact side* (inner face) to handle corrosive food constituents, while a lighter coating suffices on the *outer face* which only sees atmospheric corrosion. Common ratios are 2:1 or 1:2.

### 2.4 Conversion: Coating Weight → Tin Thickness

The thickness of tin on each side can be derived from the coating weight by:

$$
t_{\text{Sn}} \;=\; \frac{m}{2 \cdot \rho_{\text{Sn}}}
$$

where
- $t_{\text{Sn}}$ = total Sn thickness on one face (m),
- $m$ = coating weight per side (g/m²),
- $\rho_{\text{Sn}}$ = density of tin = **7.28 g/cm³ = 7.28 × 10⁶ g/m³**,
- the factor 2 accounts for both faces if the total weight per area is given.

A worked reference: **E 2.8 g/m²** per side gives
$$
t_{\text{Sn}} \;=\; \frac{2.8 \;\text{g/m}^2}{7.28\times 10^{6}\;\text{g/m}^3} \;\approx\; 3.85\times 10^{-7}\;\text{m} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.385\;\mu m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **High-yield insight:** Even *heavy* tinplate coatings are only **0.4–1.5 µm** — that is roughly **0.1 % of the base-steel thickness**. Tin therefore contributes negligibly to mass but enormously to corrosion behaviour.

### 2.5 Can Geometry — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

For an open-top sanitary can of diameter $D$ (m), height $H$ (m), and wall thickness $t$ (m):

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Body cylinder wall area | $A_{\text{body}} = \pi D H$ | Lateral area |
| Top end area (disk) | $A_{\text{end}} = \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Two ends per can |
| **Total surface area (internal ≈ external)** | $A_{\text{tot}} = \pi D H + \tfrac{\pi D^2}{2}$ | Approximation, ignoring seam overlaps |
| Internal volume | $V = \tfrac{\pi D^2 H}{4}$ | Geometric internal volume |
| Can dimensions — *diameter × height* notation | e.g., **307 × 409** | First 3 digits = 3.07 in (78 mm), last 3 = 4.09 in (104 mm) |
| Approx. nominal capacity (fl oz) | $\dfrac{\pi D^2 H}{4} \times \dfrac{1}{29.5735}$ | For U.S. fluid ounces |
| Steel sheet required (cylindrical body) | $A_{\text{body}} \times \text{seam overlap factor}$ | Overlap ≈ 1.0–3.0 mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board pitfall:** KTU examiners penalise students who write the *total* coating weight (both sides) and forget the factor of 2 when converting to single-side thickness. Always read whether the *given coating weight is per face or total* — the **E-designation is per face**.

### 2.6 The Double Seam — The Heart of Hermetic Can Sealing

Hermetic integrity of food cans depends entirely on the **double seam** joining the can body to the can end (top/bottom). Mechanically formed by a **seaming chuck, seaming roll (1st operation + 2nd operation)**, the seam mechanically interlocks and compresses a rubber gasket (compound) to produce a leaktight closure.

Key quality parameters KTU examiners expect:

| Parameter | Typical Food-Can Value |
|---|---|
| Seam width | **2.8 – 3.2 mm** |
| Seam thickness | **1.2 – 1.5 mm** |
| Tightness (overlap %) | **>70 %** |
| Compound coverage | **>50 % visible** |
| Pressure-tightness test | ≥ 70 kPa internal air, no leak |
| Pull-up (height of interlock) | ≥ 1.0 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The double seam is *the* failure-prone element in canned foods and is heavily tested. KTU commonly asks: *"Why is a 70 % overlap required?"* — Answer: because overlap directly correlates with interlock strength and gasket compression; below 70 % the seam opens during retort.

### 2.7 Corrosion Electrochemistry in Food Cans

In a tin-coated can with breached lacquer:

1. **Anode:** tin (E° ≈ −0.14 V Sn²⁺/Sn) — *slowly* dissolves into food.
2. **Cathode:** iron (E° ≈ −0.44 V Fe²⁺/Fe) — protected because tin area is small and acts sacrificially in this polarisation.
3. Result: pitting of tin (visible as *detinning*) but **no black-iron rust** until the tin is exhausted → shelf life prolonged by months to years.

For *aggressive* acid products (citrus, tomato), anodes/cathodes can invert (tin becomes cathodic) → rapid iron corrosion → must use **heavy tin (E11.2) + lacquer** or *two-piece tin-free-steel can* with organic coating.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Coating Weight ↔ Coating Thickness (Single Face)

**Given:** Coating weight per face, $m$ (g/m²); density of tin, $\rho_{\text{Sn}}$ = 7.28 g/cm³.

**Step 1.** Convert density to SI (g/m³):
$$
\rho_{\text{Sn}} \;=\; 7.28 \;\tfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3}\;\times\;\dfrac{(100\;\text{cm})^3}{1\;\text{m}^3} \;=\; 7.28\times 10^{6}\;\tfrac{\text{g}}{\text{m}^3}
$$

**Step 2.** Coating weight per face in g/m², when spread uniformly over the surface, occupies a *volume per unit area* numerically equal to its thickness:
$$
m \;=\; \rho_{\text{Sn}} \cdot t_{\text{Sn}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad t_{\text{Sn}} \;=\; \frac{m}{\rho_{\text{Sn}}}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute numerical values for E 5.6 (per side, m = 5.6 g/m²):
$$
t_{\text{Sn}} \;=\; \frac{5.6\;\text{g/m}^2}{7.28\times 10^{6}\;\text{g/m}^3} \;\approx\; 7.69\times 10^{-7}\;\text{m} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.77\;\mu m}
$$

**Step 4.** Total Sn thickness (both sides):
$$
t_{\text{Sn, total}} \;=\; 2\,t_{\text{Sn}} \;\approx\; 1.54\;\mu\text{m}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Surface Area of a Standard Can (Cylinder + Two Ends)

**Given:** A 307 × 409 can (D = 78 mm, H = 104 mm). Compute total external surface area $A_{\text{tot}}$ (ignore curl/channels for first-order).

**Step 1.** Convert dimensions to metres: $D = 0.078$ m, $H = 0.104$ m.

**Step 2.** Cylinder lateral area:
$$
A_{\text{body}} \;=\; \pi D H \;=\; \pi (0.078)(0.104) \;\approx\; 2.548\times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^2 \;=\; 254.8\;\text{cm}^2
$$

**Step 3.** Two circular ends (top and bottom):
$$
A_{\text{ends}} \;=\; 2 \times \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.078)^{2}}{2} \;\approx\; 9.557\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2} \;=\; 95.6\;\text{cm}^2
$$

**Step 4.** Total external surface area:
$$
A_{\text{tot}} \;=\; A_{\text{body}} + A_{\text{ends}} \;=\; 254.8 + 95.6 \;\approx\; \mathbf{350.4\;\text{cm}^2}
$$

**Step 5.** Approximate internal volume (geometric):
$$
V \;=\; \frac{\pi D^{2} H}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.078)^{2}(0.104)}{4} \;\approx\; 4.97\times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^{3} \;\approx\; \mathbf{497\;\text{cm}^3}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Approximate Tin Mass per Can (Differential Coating)

**Given:** A 307 × 409 can with coating designation **D 5.6 / 2.8** (heavy tin on inner face, light on outer). Total tin mass per can?

**Step 1.** Internal and external surface areas (assuming negligible seam overlap and equal internal/external geometric area):
$$
A_{\text{inner}} \approx A_{\text{outer}} \approx 350.4\;\text{cm}^{2} \;=\; 3.504\times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2.** Tin mass on inner face:
$$
m_{\text{inner}} \;=\; 5.6\;\tfrac{\text{g}}{\text{m}^{2}}\;\times\;3.504\times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^{2} \;\approx\; 0.196\;\text{g}
$$

**Step 3.** Tin mass on outer face:
$$
m_{\text{outer}} \;=\; 2.8\;\tfrac{\text{g}}{\text{m}^{2}}\;\times\;3.504\times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^{2} \;\approx\; 0.098\;\text{g}
$$

**Step 4.** Total tin mass per can:
$$
m_{\text{Sn, can}} \;=\; m_{\text{inner}} + m_{\text{outer}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.294\;\text{g}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A whole food can carries roughly **0.3 g of tin** — yet this tiny amount defines the entire shelf life. This dramatic leverage explains why coating uniformity and integrity are paramount.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Tightness (Overlap Percentage) of a Double Seam

**Given:** Seam width $W$ (mm), seam thickness $T$ (mm), base steel hook $B$ (mm), cover hook $C$ (mm).

**Step 1.** Projected overlap length:
$$
L_{\text{overlap}} \;=\; B + C \;-\; T
$$

**Step 2.** Actual (mechanical) overlap length:
$$
L_{\text{actual}} \;=\; B + C \;-\; W
$$

**Step 3.** Tightness percentage (typical KTU formula):
$$
\%\,\text{tightness} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{actual}}}{L_{\text{overlap}}}\;\times 100 \;=\; \frac{B + C - W}{B + C - T}\;\times 100
$$

**Step 4.** Worked numerical: $B = 1.8$ mm, $C = 1.5$ mm, $T = 1.3$ mm, $W = 2.5$ mm:
$$
\%\,\text{tightness} \;=\; \frac{1.8+1.5-2.5}{1.8+1.5-1.3}\;\times 100 \;=\; \frac{0.8}{2.0}\;\times 100 \;=\; \mathbf{40\,\%}
$$
→ *Such a seam is loose; corrective action: tighten roll pressure to increase $B+C$ or reduce $W$.* KTU boards accept ≥ **70 %** as commercially acceptable.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three-Piece vs Two-Piece Cans

| Attribute | Three-Piece Soldered/Welded | Three-Piece Cemented | Two-Piece D&I (Drawn & Ironed) | Two-Piece DRD (Drawn & Redrawn) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Body side seam | Yes (Pb-free solder / welded / cemented) | Cemented (nylon) | None | None |
| Bottom end | Double-seamed on | Double-seamed on | Integrally drawn (no seam) | Integrally drawn (no seam) |
| Top end | Double-seemed (factory + consumer) | Double-seemed | Double-seemed (consumer end) | Double-seemed (consumer end) |
| Wall ironing | No | No | Yes — wall thinned | No — re-drawn |
| Maximum pressure rating | High | Moderate | Very high (carbonated beverages) | Moderate |
| Internal lacquer continuity | Broken at side seam | Better | Perfectly continuous | Perfectly continuous |
| Cost for round cans | Moderate | Lower (round only) | High tooling | Moderate |
| Typical product | Fruits, vegetables, fish | Beer, dry foods | Beverages, pet food | Fish, meat, pâté |
| Material utilisation | Lower | Lower | Higher | Higher |

---

### 3.6 Decision Flow — When to Use Which Type of Can

```
Acid food (pH < 4.5) ?  ──► Yes ──► Heavy tin (E 8.4 / 11.2) + LACQUER (epoxy-phenolic)
                                    │                          OR two-piece tinplate
                                    No
                                     │
                                     ▼
Carbonated beverage?     ──► Yes ──► Two-piece aluminium OR two-piece tinplate D&I (high pressure)
                                    No
                                     ▼
Need high internal lacquer continuity?  ──► Yes ──► Two-piece DRD or DWI (welded side seam-free)
                                                  No
                                                   ▼
                                          Three-piece welded/cemented (Pb-free welded preferred)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Section of a Tinplate Sheet (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lacquer Film 5 to 12 microns"] --> B["Tin Layer 0.4 to 1.5 microns"]
    B --> C["FeSn2 Alloy Intermetallic thin layer"]
    C --> D["Base Steel MR or MC type 140 to 500 microns"]
    D --> E["FeSn2 Alloy Intermetallic thin layer"]
    E --> F["Tin Layer 0.4 to 1.5 microns"]
    F --> G["Passivation Chromate 5 mg Cr per m2"]
    G --> H["Lubricant DOS film trace"]
```

> The diagram emphasises that the steel core is **hundreds of microns** thick while tin plating is **sub-micron to 1.5 µm** — yet this tiny layer dominates corrosion behaviour.

---

### 4.2 Manufacturing Process Block-Diagram (Electrolytic Tinplate Line)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Cold Reduced Steel Coil"] --> P1["Cleaning Alkaline Degrease"]
    P1 --> P2["Pickling Sulphuric Acid"]
    P2 --> P3["Electrolytic Tinplating Halogen or Ferrostan Line"]
    P3 --> P4["Tin Fusion Induction Heating 232 C Above Tin Melting"]
    P4 --> P5["Quench Water Spray"]
    P5 --> P6["Passivation Cathodic Dichromate Dip"]
    P6 --> P7["Oiling DOS or Cottonseed Oil"]
    P7 --> P8["Lacquering Optional Roller Coater"]
    P8 --> P9["Coiled Tinplate Sheet ASTM A623 Compliant"]
    P9 --> C1["Sheared into Can Body Blanks or End Stock"]
```

---

### 4.3 Three-Piece Can Production Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Coil Slitting"] --> T2["Sheet Cutting to Body Blanks"]
    T2 --> T3["Notching and Slitting"]
    T3 --> T4["Bodymaking Roll Forming into Cylinder"]
    T4 --> T5["Side Seam Joining Soldered OR Cemented OR Welded"]
    T5 --> T6["Flanging Both Ends"]
    T6 --> T7["Double Seaming Bottom End"]
    T7 --> T8["Washing and Drying"]
    T8 --> T9["Tested for Leaks"]
    T9 --> T10["Palletised for Packager"]
```

---

### 4.4 Two-Piece D&I Can Production Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Tinplate Disk Blank"] --> Q2["Cupping Press into Cup"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Redraw and Iron through Series of Dies"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Wall Thickness Reduced from 0.30 to 0.07 mm"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Trimming to Final Height"]
    Q5 --> Q6["Washing and Surface Treatment"]
    Q6 --> Q7["Internal Spray Lacquering"]
    Q7 --> Q8["Curing Oven"]
    Q8 --> Q9["Necking and Flanging"]
    Q9 --> Q10["Bottom Profiled with Expansion Rings for Pressure Resistance"]
```

---

### 4.5 Hierarchy of Cans (Functional Classification Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1["Metal Cans for Food"] --> Z2["Three Piece Cans"]
    Z1 --> Z3["Two Piece Cans"]
    Z2 --> Z2A["Soldered Side Seam Pb free"]
    Z2 --> Z2B["Cemented Side Seam Nylon Adhesive"]
    Z2 --> Z2C["Welded Side Seam"]
    Z3 --> Z3A["Drawn and Ironed DWI"]
    Z3 --> Z3B["Drawn and Redrawn DRD"]
    Z3 --> Z3C["Drawn only Single Draw"]
    Z2 --> Z2D["Open Top Sanitary Can Most Common"]
    Z2 --> Z2E["General Line Can Closed Top"]
    Z2 --> Z2F["Specialty Can Cone Top or Oblong"]
```

---

### 4.6 Double-Seam Cross Section (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Cover Hook CH from End"] --> S2["Body Hook BH from Can Wall"]
    S2 --> S3["Interlocked Region Countersink Profile"]
    S3 --> S4["Compound Gasket Compressed"]
    S4 --> S5["Seam Thickness T 1.2 to 1.5 mm"]
    S5 --> S6["Seam Width W 2.8 to 3.2 mm"]
```

> The schematic above identifies the four elements KTU examiners always expect: **cover hook, body hook, interlock region, and compressed compound.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **Type MR** and **Type D** tinplate steel bases used in food cans. Give one typical application for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- *Type MR* — Mild Rolled, low-carbon (<0.13 % C), vacuum-degassed, nitrogen-stabilised steel. Excellent corrosion resistance, moderate strength. Used for **acidic fruits, vegetables, and general food cans** (most common, ~ 80 % of all food cans).
- *Type D* — Drawn steel, slightly lower carbon with enhanced ductility. Used for **deep-drawing operations** (two-piece D&I cans, easy-open end stock).

*(1 mark each for the differentiation + 1 mark for correct applications.)*

---

**Q2.** What is **differentially coated tinplate**? Write the meaning of the designation **D 8.4 / 2.8**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Differentially coated tinplate is electrolytic tinplate in which the two faces of the sheet carry **different tin coating weights**, deliberately chosen to match the corrosion exposure on each face.

In **D 8.4 / 2.8** — by ASTM convention the **first number denotes the coating weight on the surface that will become the INNER (product-contact) face** of the can:
$$
8.4\;\text{g/m}^2 \text{ on the inner face},\quad 2.8\;\text{g/m}^2 \text{ on the outer face}
$$

Hence, heavier tin on the food-contact side where acidic/oxidative constituents act, lighter on the outer face which only sees atmospheric corrosion. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark convention + 1 mark reason.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **manufacturing process of electrolytic tinplate** with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of tinplate cans** based on construction. Compare three-piece and two-piece cans in terms of hermetic integrity, material utilisation and end-use. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Ferrostan process** (most common halogen-tin electrolyte route) produces electrolytic tinplate as follows:

1. **Alkaline cleaning:** Cold-reduced steel coils pass through hot alkaline cleaner (NaOH + surfactant) to remove rolling oils and fines.
2. **Pickling:** Sulphuric acid (5–10 %) brushing removes mill scale and oxides.
3. **Electrolytic plating:** Tin is plated from **stannous sulphate + phenolsulphonic acid (PSA)** electrolyte at **~ 25 A/dm²** cathodic current density. Modern *halogen* lines use **stannous chloride + fluoride** systems for higher throughput. Tin plates the moving strip continuously on both faces via vertical or horizontal cells.
4. **Tin fusion (reflow):** Induction heating to **~ 232 °C + 5–10 °C above tin melting point** melts the deposit, producing a bright, reflective, continuous alloy layer (FeSn₂ intermetallic forms).
5. **Quenching:** Rapid water spray to cool, locking a uniform tin surface.
6. **Passivation:** Cathodic treatment in sodium dichromate solution to grow a thin chromium-oxide film (~ **5 mg Cr/m²**) suppressing tin-oxide growth (tarnish) during storage.
7. **Oiling:** Electrostatic or roller application of **DOS (dioctyl sebacate)** at **~ 5 mg/m²** to prevent scratching during coiling.
8. **Optional lacquering:** Roller-coated organic coating (epoxy-phenolic, vinyl, organosol) cured in oven for product-contact barrier.
9. **Coiling and shearing:** Coils slit into can-body blanks or end stock per customer specification.

*Block diagram marks: 3. Step-wise description: 4.*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Classification based on construction:**

```
Food cans (tinplate)
├── THREE-PIECE CANS
│      ├── Open-top sanitary (most common)
│      ├── General-line (closed-top)
│      └── Specialty (cone-top, oblong, oval)
└── TWO-PIECE CANS
       ├── Drawn-and-ironed (DWI / D&I)
       ├── Drawn-and-redrawn (DRD)
       └── Single-draw shallow
```

**Comparison Table (5 marks for comparative discussion):**

| Parameter | Three-Piece Can | Two-Piece Can |
|---|---|---|
| Hermetic integrity | Has *side seam* — potential leak path; modern **welded** or **cemented** seams make it competitive | **No side seam** — highest hermetic integrity |
| Material utilisation | Lower (overlap area at side seam) | Higher (~ 5–10 % saving) |
| Internal lacquer continuity | Broken at side seam | Perfectly continuous |
| Pressure resistance | Limited by seam | High — ideal for carbonated beverages |
| Tooling cost | Moderate | High (deep-draw tooling) |
| Typical uses | Fruits, vegetables, fish, ready meals | Beverages, pet food, fish |
| Recyclability | Same (steel) | Same (steel) |

*(2 marks: classification diagram + 5 marks: comparison with appropriate examples.)*

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **structure of the double seam** used in food cans. List the quality parameters used to evaluate a good double seam. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 307 × 409 can (D = 78 mm, H = 104 mm) is to be made from tinplate of designation **E 5.6 / 5.6** (g/m² per side). Calculate the **(i)** thickness of tin coating per face, **(ii)** total external surface area, and **(iii)** total tin mass per can (assume equal inner/outer geometric surface area, neglect seam overlap). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Structure of the double seam** — a mechanical interlock formed in two rolling operations between the **can end** (cover hook, CH) and the **flanged can body** (body hook, BH):

*First operation:* seaming rolls curl the cover hook and body hook inward, forming a loose interlock.
*Second operation:* rolls press the hooks tightly together, compressing the **compound (rubber gasket)** to form a hermetic seal.

**Five-element diagram of double seam:**
- (i) Cover Hook (CH)
- (ii) Body Hook (BH)
- (iii) Interlock (overlap region)
- (iv) Compound (gasket)
- (v) Countersink / seam profile

**Quality parameters (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Seam width $W$ | 2.8 – 3.2 mm |
| Seam thickness $T$ | 1.2 – 1.5 mm |
| Body hook length $B$ | ≥ 1.8 mm |
| Cover hook length $C$ | ≥ 1.5 mm |
| Tightness (overlap %) | **≥ 70 %** |
| Compound coverage | ≥ 50 % visible (cross-section) |
| Pressure test (air) | ≥ 70 kPa, no leak |
| Visual: wrinkle rating | ≤ 1 (no wrinkles) |

*(3 marks diagram + 4 marks parameters.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** D = 78 mm = 0.078 m; H = 104 mm = 0.104 m; coating weight per face $m = 5.6$ g/m²; $\rho_{\text{Sn}} = 7.28$ g/cm³ = $7.28\times 10^6$ g/m³.

**(i) Tin thickness per face:**
$$
t_{\text{Sn}} \;=\; \frac{m}{\rho_{\text{Sn}}} \;=\; \frac{5.6\;\text{g/m}^2}{7.28\times 10^{6}\;\text{g/m}^3} \;\approx\; 7.69\times 10^{-7}\;\text{m}
$$
$$
\boxed{t_{\text{Sn}} \;\approx\; 0.77\;\mu\text{m (per face)}}
$$

**(ii) Total external surface area:**
$$
A_{\text{tot}} \;=\; \pi D H \;+\; \frac{\pi D^2}{2}
$$
$$
A_{\text{tot}} \;=\; \pi(0.078)(0.104) \;+\; \frac{\pi(0.078)^2}{2} \;\approx\; 0.02548 \;+\; 0.009557 \;\approx\; 0.03504\;\text{m}^2
$$
$$
\boxed{A_{\text{tot}} \;\approx\; 350.4\;\text{cm}^2 \;\approx\; 0.035\;\text{m}^2}
$$

**(iii) Total tin mass per can (coating on both faces, equal area assumption):**
$$
A_{\text{inner}} \approx A_{\text{outer}} \approx 0.03504\;\text{m}^2
$$
$$
m_{\text{tin}} \;=\; 2 \times (m \times A) \;=\; 2 \times (5.6\;\text{g/m}^2 \times 0.03504\;\text{m}^2)
$$
$$
m_{\text{tin}} \;=\; 2 \times 0.196\;\text{g} \;\approx\; \boxed{0.392\;\text{g per can}}
$$

*(Step-wise marks: (i) 2, (ii) 2, (iii) 3.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Factor of 2 confusion:** Coating weight in ASTM E-designation is *per face*, but thickness conversion must use *single face mass*. Many students incorrectly halve again.
> 2. **Differential convention error:** Writing "D 8.4/2.8 means 8.4 g/m² on outer face" is wrong — the *first number is always the inner face* in ASTM practice.
> 3. **Forgetting the ends in area:** Calculating only $\pi DH$ and missing the two circular ends loses 2–3 marks in surface-area questions.
> 4. **No mention of intermetallic FeSn₂:** Examiners expect students to mention the FeSn₂ alloy layer formed during tin fusion; omitting it is a -1 mark cut.
> 5. **"Soldered" without qualifying "Pb-free":** Modern food cans use *Pb-free solder* (Sn-Ag, Sn-Cu) or have moved to *welded* construction entirely. Generic "soldered" implies lead and is penalised.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Tinplate = low-carbon steel sheet + thin electrolytic tin coating** (often with passivation, oiling, optional lacquer).
- ✅ **ASTM designations:** E (equal coating) and D (differential), followed by **g/m² per side** values; first number = inner face.
- ✅ **Steel base types:** Type **MR** (most common, mild rolled), **MC** (medium strength), **L** (low-tin side), **D** (ductile, deep draw).
- ✅ **Tin thickness is sub-micron** (~ **0.4–1.5 µm per face**) yet chemically decisive.
- ✅ **Tin density ρₛₙ = 7.28 g/cm³**; conversion formula $t_{\text{Sn}} = m/\rho_{\text{Sn}}$.
- ✅ **Can dimension notation "307 × 409"** → 3.07″ × 4.09″ → **78 × 104 mm**.
- ✅ **Three-piece can** = body + two ends; side seam is soldered / cemented / welded.
- ✅ **Two-piece can (D&I)** = one-piece body + bottom, made by deep drawing and wall-ironing; no side seam → superior hermeticity.
- ✅ **Double seam** joins ends to body — formed in two operations; hermetic integrity depends on **≥ 70 % overlap (tightness)** and compound compression.
- ✅ **Differentially coated tinplate (e.g., D 5.6 / 2.8)** allocates more tin to the *product-contact* face for aggressive foods (acidic fruits, tomato).
- ✅ **Passivation film** (Cr-based) prevents tin tarnish; **lacquer** (epoxy-phenolic / vinyl / organosol) acts as a barrier against corrosive food constituents.
- ✅ **Surface area of cylindrical can** $A = \pi DH + \pi D^2/2$ (body + both ends).
- ✅ **Tin mass per can** ≈ 0.3–0.5 g — extraordinarily small yet defining shelf life.
- ✅ **Recycling:** Tinplate is **infinitely recyclable** via the electric-arc-furnace route — a major sustainability advantage over many polymer alternatives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tin Free Steel (TFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tin Free Steel (TFS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tin Free Steel (TFS)**, internationally standardized as **Electrolytic Chromium Coated Steel (ECCS)**, is a cold-reduced low-carbon steel substrate that has been electrolytically coated with a duplex layer consisting of an inner film of **metallic chromium** and an outer film of **chromium oxides/hydroxides**. It is engineered as an economically viable substitute for electrolytic tinplate in selected food packaging applications.

Standardized coating weights (per ISO and JIS conventions):

- **Metallic chromium layer:** $\mathbf{50 \text{ to } 140 \text{ mg/m}^2}$ (nominal $\mathbf{100 \text{ mg/m}^2}$)
- **Chromium oxide/hydroxide layer:** $\mathbf{5 \text{ to } 35 \text{ mg/m}^2}$ (nominal $\mathbf{20 \text{ mg/m}^2}$)

### 1.2 Historical Context & Origin

TFS was commercialized in **Japan in 1961 by Toyo Kohan Co. Ltd.** during the global tin price crisis of the early 1960s. With tin becoming strategically expensive, metallurgists sought a thinner, harder, and cheaper metallic film that could still protect steel and bond with organic lacquers. Chromium emerged as the optimal candidate because:

- It is significantly harder than tin (Vickers hardness: Cr $\approx$ 1000 HV vs. Sn $\approx$ 7 HV).
- It forms a self-passivating oxide skin (analogous to stainless steel) that resists further oxidation.
- It is vastly more abundant and cheaper than tin.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Hard-Coated Notebook"**: Imagine a plain steel diary (the steel base) that gets a thin, mirror-like metallic chromium "laminate" glued onto it (the metallic Cr layer), and on top of that laminate, a transparent oxide "varnish" is naturally formed (the Cr-oxide skin). You cannot write on it with a fountain pen (poor solderability/weldability), but if you spray a thin colored "paint primer" (organic lacquer), the surface holds paint beautifully and resists rust for years. TFS behaves exactly like this notebook in the packaging world.

### 1.4 Distinguishing Features at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Base:** Low-carbon cold-rolled steel (typically 0.02 – 0.15 % C)
> - **Coating thickness:** $\approx 0.01$ to $0.02\ \mu\text{m}$ (extremely thin)
> - **Surface energy / lacquer adhesion:** Excellent (chromium oxide provides polar sites for epoxy/phenolic resins)
> - **Solderability:** Poor (cannot be soldered with conventional tin-lead solders)
> - **Weldability:** Limited (the chromium layer interferes with conventional resistance welding)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Duplex Coating Architecture

The TFS surface is a **two-layer system** produced in a single electrolytic bath:

| Layer | Composition | Function | Typical Mass |
|---|---|---|---|
| Outer (top) | Chromium oxides & hydroxides ($\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$, $\text{CrOOH}$) | Passivation; barrier; anchor for lacquer | $5$–$35\ \text{mg/m}^2$ |
| Inner (bottom) | Metallic chromium (Cr$^0$) | Hardness; wear resistance; base for oxide | $50$–$140\ \text{mg/m}^2$ |
| Substrate | Low-carbon steel (Fe) | Mechanical strength & formability | Bulk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **chromium oxide film is the true reason TFS works as a packaging material.** It is this nano-scale oxide that gives the surface its corrosion resistance and its strong polar bonding sites for organic coatings.

### 2.2 Why Chromium and Not Tin? — The Metallurgical Logic

- **Tin** is soft, ductile, noble (does not protect steel sacrificially), and is prone to tin pest at low temperatures.
- **Chromium** is hard, self-passivating, and forms a thin insulating oxide ($\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$) that prevents further anodic dissolution of the underlying steel.
- The oxide layer is electrically non-conductive, which is precisely why TFS **cannot be soldered** (solder needs a metallically conductive, wettable surface).

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Property

- **Excellent lacquer adhesion →** Chromium oxide has high surface energy and contains polar –OH groups that chemically bond with epoxy, phenolic, polyester, and organosol resins.
- **Good formability →** The chromium coating is so thin ($\sim 0.02\ \mu\text{m}$) that it does not crack under deep-drawing operations like DRD (Draw-Redraw) and DWI (Drawn and Ironed) can-making.
- **Poor weldability →** The non-conductive oxide film prevents the localized melting and fusion needed in resistance welding.
- **Superior sulfide-stain resistance vs. tinplate →** TFS does not react with sulfur compounds released by protein-rich foods (meat, fish), avoiding the black tarnish common on tinplate.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Metrics

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's law (chromium deposition) | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | $F \approx 96485\ \text{C/mol}$ |
| Coating weight | $CW = \dfrac{m}{A}$ | $\text{mg/m}^2$ | $A$ = coated area |
| Chromium molar mass | $M_{\text{Cr}} \approx 52.00$ | $\text{g/mol}$ | — |
| Equivalent coating thickness | $t_{\text{Cr}} = \dfrac{CW}{\rho_{\text{Cr}}}$ | $\mu\text{m}$ | $\rho_{\text{Cr}} \approx 7.19\ \text{g/cm}^3$ |
| Standard current density | $j = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $\text{A/dm}^2$ | Typical $j = 20$–$60\ \text{A/dm}^2$ for ECCS lines |
| Specific gravity of steel base | $\rho_{\text{steel}} \approx 7.85$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | — |
| Tinplate coating (for comparison) | $2.8$–$11.2\ \text{g/m}^2$ | $\text{g/m}^2$ | $\sim 25\times$ thicker than TFS Cr layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units note:** In the table above, absolute-value bars and pipe delimiters have been intentionally avoided; all intervals are written with hyphens for markdown compatibility.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

TFS is the **workhorse substrate for can ends and two-piece DRD cans** in the global canned-food industry (over 60 % of easy-open can ends in Asia use TFS). It is the silent enabler of cost-effective canned vegetables, fish, pet food, and beverage crowns — wherever the package is **not required to be soldered or welded**, TFS displaces tinplate at a fraction of the cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Coating Thickness from Faraday's Law

**Goal:** Show that a $100\ \text{mg/m}^2$ chromium layer corresponds to an ultra-thin film whose thickness is on the order of $10^{-2}\ \mu\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — State Faraday's first law of electrolysis.**

$$
m \;=\; \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

where the symbols carry their usual meanings. For chromium deposition from hexavalent chromic acid:

$$
\text{CrO}_3 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^- \longrightarrow \text{Cr} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

so the electron count per chromium atom is $n = 6$.

**Step 2 — Convert the target coating weight into mass per unit area.**

$$
m/A \;=\; 100\ \text{mg/m}^2 \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the density relation to obtain thickness.**

$$
t_{\text{Cr}} \;=\; \frac{(m/A)}{\rho_{\text{Cr}}}
$$

Insert the values ($\rho_{\text{Cr}} = 7.19\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 7190\ \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$
t_{\text{Cr}} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/m}^2}{7190\ \text{kg/m}^3}
$$

$$
t_{\text{Cr}} \;=\; 1.39 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m} \;\approx\; 0.014\ \mu\text{m} \;\approx\; 14\ \text{nm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The entire metallic chromium layer on TFS is only about **14 nanometres thick** — roughly $1/3000$ the diameter of a human hair. This is why TFS can be deep-drawn without cracking.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Manufacturing Process (Toyo Kohan-Type Process)

**Step 1 — Substrate preparation.** Cold-rolled low-carbon steel strip is degreased in hot alkaline cleaner ($\text{NaOH}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, surfactants at $60$–$80\ ^\circ\text{C}$).

**Step 2 — Pickling.** The strip is acid-pickled in dilute $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or $\text{HCl}$ to remove mill scale and rust, leaving a chemically active surface.

**Step 3 — Rinsing.** Double-counter-flow water rinse to prevent acid carryover into the chromium bath.

**Step 4 — Cathodic electrolysis.** Strip is the **cathode** in a bath containing:

$$
\text{CrO}_3 \;(\sim 150\text{–}250\ \text{g/L}) \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;(\sim 1\text{–}3\ \text{g/L})
$$

at $40$–$60\ ^\circ\text{C}$ with current density $20$–$60\ \text{A/dm}^2$. Metallic Cr is reduced and deposited:

$$
\text{Cr}^{6+} + 6e^- \longrightarrow \text{Cr}^{0}
$$

**Step 5 — Anodic electrolysis (oxide formation).** The polarity is reversed, and the strip is briefly made the **anode** in the same bath. A controlled surface oxidation produces the chromium oxide/hydroxide film:

$$
2\text{Cr} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + 6\text{H}^+ + 6e^-
$$

**Step 6 — Final rinse & drying.** Demineralized-water rinse followed by hot-air drying and recoiling.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TFS vs. Electrolytic Tinplate

| Parameter | TFS (ECCS) | Electrolytic Tinplate (ETP) |
|---|---|---|
| Coating metal | Chromium | Tin |
| Coating mass | $50$–$140\ \text{mg/m}^2$ | $2.8$–$11.2\ \text{g/m}^2$ |
| Coating thickness | $\sim 0.014\ \mu\text{m}$ | $\sim 0.4$–$1.5\ \mu\text{m}$ |
| Lacquer adhesion | Excellent | Good (requires passivation) |
| Solderability | Not possible | Excellent |
| Resistance weldability | Limited | Good |
| Sulfide stain resistance | Excellent | Poor (black tarnish) |
| Cost (relative) | Lower | Higher (volatile Sn price) |
| Direct food contact | **No — lacquer mandatory** | Yes (with passivation) |
| Formability (DRD/DWI) | Excellent | Good |
| Heat resistance | Excellent | Moderate (Sn melts at 232 °C) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the TFS Coating

The following Mermaid block diagram describes the layered cross-sectional architecture of a finished TFS substrate, from the outermost food-contact surface down to the bulk steel:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Layer 1: Organic Lacquer Film - 5 to 15 micrometers - Epoxy Phenolic or Organosol - Direct Food Contact Surface"] --> L2["Layer 2: Chromium Oxide Hydroxide Film - 5 to 35 mg per m2 - Passive Barrier - Lacquer Anchor"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3: Metallic Chromium Film - 50 to 140 mg per m2 - Hardness and Wear Resistance"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4: Low Carbon Steel Substrate - 0.15 to 0.30 mm - Mechanical Strength"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5: Tin Free Steel Coil - Finished Packaging Substrate"]
```

### 4.2 Manufacturing Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Cold Rolled Steel Coil Entry"] --> P2["Step 2: Alkaline Degreasing"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Water Rinse"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Acid Pickling H2SO4 or HCl"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Water Rinse"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Cathodic Treatment in Chromic Acid Bath - Metallic Cr Deposition"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Anodic Treatment in Same Bath - Chromium Oxide Formation"]
    P7 --> P8["Step 8: Demineralized Water Rinse"]
    P8 --> P9["Step 9: Hot Air Drying"]
    P9 --> P10["Step 10: Recoiling as TFS Coil"]
    P10 --> P11["Step 11: Optional Lacquer Application on Separate Line"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Decision Tree: When to Use TFS vs. Tinplate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["Is the package required to be soldered or resistance welded?"] -->|Yes| A1["Use Tinplate ETP"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2["Will the food release sulfur compounds proteins?"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2["Use TFS with Epoxy Phenolic Lacquer"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3["Is cost minimization the primary driver?"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3["Use TFS with appropriate lacquer"]
    Q3 -->|No| A1
```

### 4.4 Application Map: End Uses of TFS in Food Packaging

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TFS["Tin Free Steel ECCS"] --> U1["Easy Open Can Ends"]
    TFS --> U2["DRD Two Piece Cans for Vegetables"]
    TFS --> U3["DRD Cans for Fish and Meat"]
    TFS --> U4["Crown Caps and Bottle Closures"]
    TFS --> U5["Aerosol Cans"]
    TFS --> U6["Pet Food Containers"]
    TFS --> U7["Lid Stock for Glass Jars"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
Define Tin Free Steel. Mention any two advantages of TFS over electrolytic tinplate in food packaging.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Tin Free Steel (TFS), also called Electrolytic Chromium Coated Steel (ECCS), is a low-carbon cold-rolled steel strip coated electrolytically with a duplex film of metallic chromium ($50$–$140\ \text{mg/m}^2$) overlaid by a thin layer of chromium oxides/hydroxides ($5$–$35\ \text{mg/m}^2$).
>
> **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** TFS offers significantly **better lacquer adhesion** because the polar chromium-oxide surface forms chemical bonds with epoxy/phenolic resins, eliminating the need for a separate passivation treatment.
>
> **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** TFS is **cheaper** than tinplate (coating mass is roughly $1/25$ that of tinplate) and provides **superior resistance to sulfide staining** from protein-rich foods such as fish and meat.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
Explain the role of the chromium oxide/hydroxide film in TFS. Why is TFS unsuitable for direct food contact without lacquer?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Role of oxide film (2 marks):** The outer chromium oxide/hydroxide film ($\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$/$\text{CrOOH}$, $5$–$35\ \text{mg/m}^2$) acts as (i) a **passive barrier** that prevents further anodic dissolution of the underlying steel, and (ii) a **chemically active surface** rich in polar –OH groups that bonds covalently with organic lacquer resins, ensuring strong adhesion.
>
> **Why lacquer is essential (1 mark):** Although the oxide film passivates the surface, it is **electrically non-conductive, micro-porous, and is insufficient on its own to prevent long-term ion migration from aggressive food matrices (acidic, salty, or sulphur-containing products).** A food-grade lacquer is therefore mandatory to provide the hermetic, defect-free barrier required for safe direct contact.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Examination Note:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### 5.2.1 Question A — 14 Marks

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]** Describe the complete manufacturing process of TFS (ECCS) with the help of a process flow diagram. Highlight the chemistry of chromium deposition.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Substrate selection (1 mark):** Low-carbon cold-rolled steel strip is fed continuously into a horizontal plating line.
2. **Surface preparation (1 mark):** Alkaline degreasing ($\text{NaOH}$ + surfactants at $60$–$80\ ^\circ\text{C}$) removes rolling oils, followed by acid pickling in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or $\text{HCl}$ to remove mill scale and expose a reactive Fe surface.
3. **Rinsing (0.5 mark):** Double water rinse to prevent chemical carryover.
4. **Cathodic deposition (2 marks):** Strip is the cathode in a chromic acid bath ($\text{CrO}_3$ + catalyst such as $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or fluoride). The reduction reaction is:
   $$\text{Cr}^{6+} + 6e^- \longrightarrow \text{Cr}^{0}$$
   This deposits the metallic chromium layer at $50$–$140\ \text{mg/m}^2$.
5. **Anodic oxidation (1.5 marks):** Polarity is reversed; the strip becomes the anode. The metallic Cr surface is oxidized to $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ / $\text{CrOOH}$, giving the passivating $5$–$35\ \text{mg/m}^2$ oxide skin.
6. **Rinse and dry + recoiling (1 mark):** Final demineralized rinse, hot-air drying, and recoiling into TFS coils. Lacquer is applied on a separate coating line.

**Process flow diagram (to be drawn by the student):**
Cold-rolled steel → Degrease → Rinse → Pickle → Rinse → **Cathodic Cr deposition** → **Anodic Cr-oxide formation** → Rinse → Dry → TFS coil → Lacquer coat.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]** Discuss the major applications and limitations of TFS in food packaging.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Applications (4 marks):**

1. **Easy-open can ends** – the largest single end-use; TFS replaces tinplate here because ends are not soldered/welded.
2. **DRD (Draw-Redraw) two-piece cans** for vegetables, fish, meat, pet food.
3. **Crown caps and bottle closures** – excellent paintability for branded decoration.
4. **Aerosol containers and jar lids** – where formability and lacquer adhesion are key.

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Cannot be soldered** due to the non-conductive chromium oxide film.
2. **Cannot be welded by conventional resistance welding**, restricting use in three-piece can bodies.
3. **Requires mandatory lacquer coating** for food contact, increasing process complexity and cost.
4. **Not suitable for highly acidic foods (pH < 4)** without heavy lacquer weights.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B — 14 Marks

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]** Compare Tin Free Steel (ECCS) with Electrolytic Tinplate (ETP) in terms of composition, coating mass, corrosion behaviour, and typical end uses.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Attribute | TFS (ECCS) | ETP (Tinplate) |
|---|---|---|
| Coating metal | Chromium (Cr) | Tin (Sn) |
| Coating mass | $50$–$140\ \text{mg/m}^2$ | $2.8$–$11.2\ \text{g/m}^2$ |
| Surface film | Duplex (Cr metal + Cr oxide) | Tin + tin-oxide passivation film |
| Lacquer adhesion | Excellent (polar oxide) | Good (requires passivation) |
| Sulfide-stain resistance | Excellent | Poor (forms black SnS) |
| Solderability / weldability | Not possible | Excellent |
| Direct food contact | No — lacquer mandatory | Yes (with proper passivation) |
| Relative cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical end uses | Can ends, DRD cans, crown caps | Three-piece welded cans, food tins |

(2 marks for the table + 1 mark for the comparison narrative + 4 marks for justifying each of the four sub-questions: composition 1, coating mass 1, corrosion 1, end uses 1.)

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]** Describe the duplex coating structure of TFS and explain, with reasoning, why an organic lacquer is indispensable before the substrate can be used for direct food contact.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Duplex structure (3 marks):** TFS possesses a **two-layer coating** on a low-carbon steel substrate:
   - **Inner layer:** Metallic chromium, $50$–$140\ \text{mg/m}^2$, providing hardness and wear resistance (Vickers $\sim 1000$ HV).
   - **Outer layer:** Chromium oxides/hydroxides, $5$–$35\ \text{mg/m}^2$, providing chemical passivity.
   - Total chromium layer thickness is only $\sim 0.014\ \mu\text{m}$ ($14\ \text{nm}$).

2. **Why lacquer is indispensable (4 marks):**
   - **Porosity:** The chromium film is micro-porous; aggressive ions (Cl⁻, H⁺, organic acids) can penetrate to the steel substrate and cause filiform corrosion.
   - **Electrical insulation:** The oxide film is non-conductive, so any pinhole exposes a galvanic cell between Fe (anode) and Cr (cathode) accelerating localized corrosion.
   - **Food-compatibility:** Bare chromium oxide can be attacked by strong acids and certain food pigments, leading to metal leaching and flavour changes.
   - **Mechanical protection:** A $5$–$15\ \mu\text{m}$ epoxy-phenolic or organosol lacquer provides a hermetic, flexible, pinhole-free barrier that bonds chemically to the polar Cr-oxide surface.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - Confusing **TFS (chromium coating)** with **Tinplate (tin coating)** — this is a very common error; always verify the coating metal.
> - Forgetting to mention that **TFS is not suitable for direct food contact** without lacquer.
> - Omitting the **duplex (two-layer) nature** of the coating — examiners specifically look for the distinction between metallic Cr and chromium oxide.
> - Writing that TFS can be soldered — it **cannot**; the non-conductive oxide film prevents solder wetting.
> - In numerical problems, mixing up Faraday's-law constants: remember $n = 6$ for $\text{Cr}^{6+} \rightarrow \text{Cr}^{0}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TFS = ECCS** = Electrolytic Chromium Coated Steel — know both names.
- Developed in **Japan, 1961** as a tin substitute.
- **Two-layer coating:** metallic Cr (inner) + Cr-oxide/hydroxide (outer).
- Coating mass: **$50$–$140\ \text{mg/m}^2$ (Cr metal) + $5$–$35\ \text{mg/m}^2$ (oxide).**
- Thickness of metallic Cr layer $\approx \mathbf{14\ \text{nm}}$ — extremely thin.
- **Properties to memorize:** excellent lacquer adhesion, poor solderability, poor weldability, excellent sulfide-stain resistance.
- **Mandatory lacquer** before food contact — never skip this in an answer.
- **Faraday's law** for chromium deposition: $n = 6$.
- **Major uses:** can ends, DRD cans, crown caps, jar lids, aerosol cans.
- **Cannot be used for:** soldered/welded three-piece can bodies, or direct food contact without lacquer.
- **Key advantage over tinplate:** cost, lacquer adhesion, sulfide resistance.
- **Key disadvantage vs. tinplate:** no solderability, no weldability, requires lacquer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aluminium Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Aluminium Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminium Containers (KTU 2024 Definition):** Aluminium containers are rigid or semi-rigid food packaging formats manufactured from commercially pure aluminium (typically 1xxx series alloys such as AA 1050, AA 3003, AA 3004, AA 5182, and AA 8011) through cold forming, deep drawing, impact extrusion, or stamping operations. They serve as hermetic, opaque, and totally impermeable primary packaging for processed, ready-to-eat, frozen, retort, and convenience foods.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEFTT635, Module 2)**, aluminium containers are studied as a critical subdivision of **metal-based food packaging systems**, distinct from tinplate and stainless steel containers. The standard thickness classification used by the industry and by KTU-recommended textbooks is as follows:

| Form | Thickness Range |
|---|---|
| Aluminium Foil | < 0.2 mm (typically 0.006 – 0.02 mm) |
| Aluminium Sheet | 0.2 – 6 mm |
| Aluminium Container (wrought) | 0.03 – 0.20 mm (typical 60 – 130 µm) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **densest, most impermeable** metallic food packaging is achieved through aluminium because of its face-centered cubic (FCC) crystal structure, which provides a continuous pinhole-free metallic barrier when properly annealed. Its **theoretical density is 2.70 g/cm³**, and its **mass attenuation coefficient for moisture and oxygen is effectively zero**, meaning the polymer or lacquer coating is responsible for *all* food-contact compatibility, not aluminium itself.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Aluminium Tiffin Box" Analogy:**
> Imagine a traditional Indian *dabba* (tiffin box) made of stainless steel — it is sturdy, keeps food safe, is reusable, and does not leak smells. Now imagine the *same* tiffin box, but **lighter, cheaper, easier to shape into fancy compartments, and completely light-proof** so that even UV rays cannot damage the food inside. That is essentially what an aluminium food container is.

### Why Aluminium Works So Well — A Simple Story

- **Light-proof**: Food fats oxidise (turn rancid) when exposed to light. Aluminium is **100% opaque** — it acts like a *blackout curtain* for food.
- **Hermetic seal**: Because aluminium can be rolled into extremely thin sheets (foils), two foils can be heat-sealed together (with a polymer layer) to form a totally airtight pouch. Think of it as a *zip-lock made of metal*.
- **Shape memory / formability**: Aluminium is *soft and ductile*. It can be pressed into a bowl, tray, or deep-drawn can just like clay — but it holds its shape permanently once formed.
- **Recyclability**: An aluminium tray thrown into a recycling bin today can become a new tray in **6–8 weeks** (unlike plastic, which often downcycles). It is the **"eternal cycle"** metal.

---

## 1.3 Engineering Significance in Food Packaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Aluminium containers dominate the **ready-to-eat (RTE) meal trays, airline catering, bakery, frozen-food, retort (sterilised) food, and pet-food** markets globally because of the unique combination of:
> 1. Total light + gas + moisture barrier
> 2. Heat tolerance up to **660 °C** (well beyond any food-processing temperature)
> 3. Compatibility with oven, microwave (with care), freezer, and retort
> 4. Non-toxic, food-safe (after internal lacquer coating)

The **standard food-grade alloy AA 3003** is the most widely cited in KTU-recommended content, having a composition of roughly **Mn 1.0–1.5%, Cu 0.05–0.20%, Fe ≤ 0.7%, Si ≤ 0.6%, balance Al**. Its **tensile strength is ~110–150 MPa** in the H14 temper and ~75–100 MPa in the O (annealed) temper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why Aluminium Acts as a Total Barrier

The "barrier" property of aluminium is rooted in quantum mechanics and crystallography, but at the engineering level we can express it through three measurable quantities:

### (a) Light Barrier (Opacity)

Aluminium reflects approximately **85–95%** of incident visible light and **~97%** of infrared radiation across the **0.4 – 14 µm wavelength range**. Because no photon passes through, photo-oxidation of lipids (rancidity) and destruction of vitamins (riboflavin, vitamin A, vitamin D) is eliminated.

### (b) Gas Barrier (Permeability)

The permeability coefficient of aluminium to oxygen and water vapour is effectively **zero** at thicknesses above ~15 µm because the metallic crystal lattice has no continuous pores. In contrast, typical plastic films have permeabilities in the range of:

$$\text{OTR}_{\text{LDPE}} \approx 4000 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$$

$$\text{OTR}_{\text{Al (15 µm)}} \approx 0 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$$

### (c) Moisture Barrier

Aluminium is hydrophobic and does not absorb water. Its **Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)** is also effectively zero above the pinhole-free threshold.

---

## 2.2 The Lacquering / Internal Coating System (Critical Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bare aluminium is NOT food-safe in acidic or salty conditions!** This is a frequently tested KTU concept. Pure aluminium forms a thin native oxide (Al₂O₃) of thickness 2–3 nm, but in contact with:
> - Acidic foods (tomato, citrus, vinegar) → pitting corrosion
> - Salty foods (brine, pickles, seafood) → galvanic pitting
> - Carbonated beverages → dissolution
>
> The aluminium dissolves as **Al³⁺ ions**, which can cause metallic off-flavours and exceed the **WHO permissible limit of 2 mg/kg body weight/week** for human aluminium intake.

**Solution:** The inner surface of every food-grade aluminium container is **lacquered** (coated) with a thermoset or thermoplastic polymer film of thickness **5 – 15 µm**. Common lacquers used are:

| Lacquer Type | Code | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Epoxy-phenolic | EP | Acidic foods, beverages, fish |
| Vinyl organosol | VO | Acidic foods, drawn cans |
| Polyester | PET | Dual-ovenable trays |
| Polyolefin dispersion | POD | Microwave-safe containers |
| Acrylic | AC | Trays, lids |
| Epoxy-anionic | EA | Soft drinks, beer cans (3-piece) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **epoxy-phenolic lacquer** was historically the industry standard for fish, meat, and acidic vegetable cans because of its excellent chemical resistance. After 2020 regulatory concerns about Bisphenol-A (BPA) migration, **BPA-NI (Non-Intent) polyester and acrylic lacquers** have become the new industry standard — a fact that has been appearing in **KTU University Exam questions since 2022**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical density of aluminium | $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ | g/cm³ | Why aluminium containers are **light** |
| 2 | Melting point | $T_m = 660.3$ | °C | Heat resistance ceiling |
| 3 | Thermal conductivity | $k = 237$ | W/(m·K) | Excellent heat transfer for retort/freeze |
| 4 | Specific heat capacity | $c_p = 0.897$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Energy required to heat container |
| 5 | Young's modulus | $E = 69$ | GPa | Stiffness vs. steel (200 GPa) — easier to draw |
| 6 | Pinhole-free threshold thickness | $t_{pf} \geq 15$ | µm | Minimum foil thickness for O₂ barrier |
| 7 | Aluminium mass per tray (engineering) | $m_{Al} = \rho \cdot A \cdot t$ | g | Mass calculation for trays |
| 8 | Lacquer coating weight | $W_L = \rho_L \cdot A \cdot t_L$ | g/m² | Typical 4–10 g/m² |
| 9 | Theoretical OTR aluminium foil | $\text{OTR} \approx 0$ | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | Total oxygen barrier |
| 10 | Sheet gauge-to-mm conversion | $\text{No. gauge} = \frac{1000}{t_{mm} \cdot 39.37}$ | — | Used for wrought containers |
| 11 | Yield strength (AA 3003-H14) | $\sigma_y \approx 145$ | MPa | Drawing resistance |
| 12 | Allowable migration limit (EU) | $\leq 5$ | mg/dm² (overall) | Food contact regulation |
| 13 | Recyclability energy saving | $\Delta E \approx 95$ | % | vs. primary aluminium production |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Conversion Pitfall (KTU Valuation Note):** When using gauge numbers, the formula above is for **Aluminium B&S gauge**, NOT the steel gauge. Students often confuse these. Always verify the standard (B&S vs. ISO vs. USS) before numerical substitution.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aerospace-catering trays** — Aluminium containers (single-compartment rectangular 1/1 GN size 530 × 325 mm) are the *de facto* airline standard because they tolerate -40 °C to +250 °C thermal cycling.
2. **Pet-food cans** — Three-piece aluminium cans (drawn + wall-ironed) replaced tinplate in the 1990s for cat/dog food due to **35% mass savings**.
3. **Retort pouches with aluminium foil layer** — The aluminium foil (typically 12–15 µm) provides the only gas barrier in retort pouches used for Indian ready-to-eat curries (MTR, Kohinoor brands).
4. **Pharmaceutical blister packs** — Aluminium foil (20 µm) + PVC/PVDC forms the universal blister pack.
5. **Tetra-Pak-style cartons** — Contain a **6.5 µm aluminium foil layer** sandwiched between PE and paper layers — even though the consumer calls it a "paper" carton.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Aluminium Required for a Rectangular Tray

> **Problem Statement (typical 7-mark KTU question):**
> A rectangular aluminium food tray has external dimensions **length L = 220 mm**, **width W = 150 mm**, and **depth D = 35 mm**, with a uniform wall thickness **t = 80 µm** and base thickness **t_b = 120 µm**. Calculate the **mass of aluminium required** to manufacture the tray, assuming density ρ_Al = **2.70 g/cm³**.

### Step 1 — Convert all dimensions into centimetres (cm)

$$L = 220 \text{ mm} = 22.0 \text{ cm}$$
$$W = 150 \text{ mm} = 15.0 \text{ cm}$$
$$D = 35 \text{ mm} = 3.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$t = 80 \text{ µm} = 80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 0.0080 \text{ cm}$$
$$t_b = 120 \text{ µm} = 120 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 0.0120 \text{ cm}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the base area (rectangular, flat)

The base is a flat rectangle.

$$A_{base} = L \times W = 22.0 \times 15.0 = 330.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute the wall (side) surface area

Each of the 4 walls is a vertical rectangle of size (L or W) × D.

$$\text{2 walls of size } L \times D = 2 \times (22.0 \times 3.5) = 154.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$\text{2 walls of size } W \times D = 2 \times (15.0 \times 3.5) = 105.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{walls} = 154.0 + 105.0 = 259.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Step 4 — Compute the flange (top rim) area

Standard tray design includes a **flat rim of width r ≈ 5 mm = 0.5 cm** running around the top perimeter. The flange forms a hollow rectangular frame.

Outer perimeter (top outer): $P_o = 2(L+W) = 2(22+15) = 74$ cm

Inner perimeter (just below rim): $P_i = 2(L-2r + W-2r) = 2(22-1 + 15-1) = 2(21+14) = 70$ cm

Mean perimeter: $P_m = \frac{P_o + P_i}{2} = \frac{74 + 70}{2} = 72$ cm

Flange area: $A_{flange} = P_m \times r = 72 \times 0.5 = 36.0 \text{ cm}^2$

### Step 5 — Total aluminium surface area

$$A_{total} = A_{base} + A_{walls} + A_{flange} = 330.0 + 259.0 + 36.0 = 625.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Step 6 — Apply the weighted thickness

The base is thicker than the walls and flange. We must weight the area appropriately.

$$\text{Base volume} = A_{base} \times t_b = 330.0 \times 0.0120 = 3.96 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\text{Wall + flange volume} = (A_{walls} + A_{flange}) \times t = (259.0 + 36.0) \times 0.0080 = 295.0 \times 0.0080 = 2.36 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 7 — Total volume of aluminium

$$V_{Al} = V_{base} + V_{walls+flange} = 3.96 + 2.36 = 6.32 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 8 — Mass calculation

$$m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \times V_{Al} = 2.70 \times 6.32$$

$$\boxed{m_{Al} \approx 17.06 \text{ g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical PET plastic tray of identical dimensions weighs ~28 g. Thus the aluminium tray is **~39% lighter** — a key sustainability and logistics argument frequently tested in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Comparison of Barrier Performance (Analytical)

> **Problem:** A 12 µm aluminium foil is laminated with a 25 µm PET layer. The PET alone has an OTR of 110 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ and the laminate OTR is 0.05 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹. Show by what factor aluminium improves the oxygen barrier.

### Step 1 — Define the barrier improvement factor (BIF)

$$\text{BIF}_{O_2} = \frac{\text{OTR}_{\text{PET alone}}}{\text{OTR}_{\text{laminate}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$\text{BIF}_{O_2} = \frac{110}{0.05}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$\text{BIF}_{O_2} = 2200$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even a 12 µm aluminium foil (which is about **1/6th the thickness of a human hair**) reduces oxygen permeation by a factor of **2200×** when laminated with PET. This is the engineering basis of retort pouches and aseptic cartons.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Aluminium Container Selection Guide

| Food Product | Recommended Alloy | Recommended Lacquer | Container Form | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbonated beverages (soda, beer) | AA 5182 (top) + AA 3004 (body) | Epoxy-anionic or BPA-NI polyester | 2-piece DWI can | Internal pressure resistance |
| Acidic fruit juices | AA 3004 | Vinyl organosol | 2-piece DWI can | Acid resistance |
| Tuna, salmon, pet food | AA 3004 | Epoxy-phenolic | 2-piece DWI can | Sulphur stain resistance (S-resistance) |
| Tomato sauce, ketchup | AA 3003 | Vinyl organosol + epoxy phenolic | 3-piece can or pouch | Acid + heat resistance |
| Ready-to-eat meals | AA 8006 / AA 8011 | Polyester (PET) coating | Wrought tray (smoothwall) | Dual-ovenable |
| Airline catering | AA 3003 | Polyolefin dispersion | Wrought tray | Microwave-safe |
| Chocolate, confectionery | AA 8079 | None / heat-seal lacquer | Foil wrap | Dead-fold property |
| Butter, margarine | AA 8011 + paper/PET laminate | None | Laminated tub | Light + O₂ barrier |
| Pharmaceutical tablets | AA 8021 / AA 8079 | PVC / PVDC blister | Push-through blister | Child resistance |
| Sterile milk (Tetra-Pak) | AA 8079 | LDPE + paper | Aseptic carton (6.5 µm Al) | Light + O₂ barrier |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sulphur-Staining" Effect:** When aluminium is in contact with protein-rich foods (meat, fish) under retort conditions, sulphur compounds (H₂S) react with the surface to form **black aluminium sulphide (Al₂S₃)**, which is harmless but cosmetically undesirable. To counter this, **sulphur-resistant lacquers (S-lacquers) such as epoxy-phenolic with zinc oxide or phenolic-oleoresinous** are applied. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Manufacturing Flow of Aluminium Containers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawA[Primary Aluminium Ingot] --> meltA[Melting Furnace 700 to 750 C]
    meltA --> castA[Continuous Casting into Slab]
    castA --> hotRoll[Hot Rolling 400 to 500 C]
    hotRoll --> coldRollA[Cold Rolling Pass 1]
    coldRollA --> annealA[Intermediate Annealing 350 C]
    annealA --> coldRollB[Cold Rolling to Final Gauge 60 to 130 micron]
    coldRollB --> lubeClean[Degreasing and Surface Cleaning]
    lubeClean --> lacquer[Lacquer Coating Epoxy Phenolic OR Polyester]
    lacquer --> cure[Curing Oven 200 C for 10 minutes]
    cure --> blank[Alu Blank Shearing]
    blank --> draw[Deep Drawing or Impact Extrusion]
    draw --> trimA[Trimming and Flanging]
    trimA --> inspect[Quality Inspection Pinhole Test and Lacquer Adhesion]
    inspect --> pack[Automated Packing and Sterilisation]
    pack --> ship[Ship to Food Filler]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The left side is bulk raw-material processing; the right side is container-forming operations. The **lacquering step** is highlighted as it is the most critical step for food safety and is often asked as a 7-mark question.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Lacquer System Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    food[Food Product pH 2 to 7] -->|direct contact| innerLacq[Inner Lacquer 5 to 15 micron]
    innerLacq -->|epoxy phenolic OR polyester| alOx[Al2O3 Native Oxide 2 to 3 nm]
    alOx -->|metallic bond| alBulk[Aluminium Alloy 60 to 130 micron]
    alBulk -->|metallic bond| alOx2[Al2O3 Native Oxide 2 to 3 nm]
    alOx2 -->|bonded to| outerLacq[Outer Lacquer or Print Coat]
    outerLacq --> print[Brand Printing and Colour]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Selection of Aluminium Container Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Food Type and Process} --> q1{Is it a beverage under pressure}
    q1 -- yes --> canDWI[2 Piece DWI Aluminium Can 5182 and 3004]
    q1 -- no --> q2{Is it thermally processed retort}
    q2 -- yes --> q3{Is food acidic pH less than 4 point 5}
    q3 -- yes --> threePiece[3 Piece Aluminium Can with Vinyl Organosol]
    q3 -- no --> twoPiece[2 Piece Aluminium Can with Epoxy Phenolic]
    q2 -- no --> q4{Is it a ready to eat hot fill meal}
    q4 -- yes --> smoothWall[Smoothwall Wrought Tray AA 8011 with PET]
    q4 -- no --> q5{Is it frozen or chilled}
    q5 -- yes --> foilTray[Wrought Foil Tray AA 3003 with POD Coating]
    q5 -- no --> foilWrap[Foil Wrap or Pouch AA 8079 with Heat Seal Lacquer]
```

---

## 4.4 Text Schematic — Three-Piece vs. Two-Piece Aluminium Cans

```
THREE-PIECE ALUMINIUM CAN (legacy, for tuna, soups)
==================================================
|   END (lid)        |   SIDE SEAM (welded)    |
|   EASY-OPEN END    |   + BOTTOM END          |
==================================================
        |                  |
        |                  |
        |     BODY (3 pieces total: lid, body, bottom)
        |________________________________________
        |   The side seam is welded (modern) or
        |   cemented (older). Heavier, more versatile.

TWO-PIECE ALUMINIUM CAN (modern, for beverages)
==================================================
         /-------------------\
        /  ALUMINIUM ALLOY    \
       |   Drawn and Wall      |
       |   Ironed (DWI) into   |
       |   ONE seamless cup    |
        \                      /
         \--------------------/
              ^  ^  ^  ^
         |    |  |  |  |   |
         |   bottom end is  |  --> 1 piece body + 1 piece top end = 2-piece
         |   integral; only |       (no side seam; pressure-safe; lighter)
         |   top is seamed  |
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Barrier Mechanism of Aluminium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uv[UV Light] --> refl[Reflected 95 percent by Aluminium Surface]
    o2[Oxygen Molecule] --> block[Crystal Lattice Blocks Diffusion]
    h2o[Water Vapour] --> block2[Hydrophobic Surface Zero Absorption]
    arom[Flavour Aroma Molecules] --> block3[Trapped Inside Sealed Container]
    refl --> protect[Protected Food Quality]
    block --> protect
    block2 --> protect
    block3 --> protect
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Why is aluminium considered a 'total barrier' material in food packaging?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aluminium is called a *total barrier* material because it provides **complete protection** against **all four major deteriorative agents** simultaneously:

1. **Light Barrier** — Aluminium is opaque and reflects ~95% of visible and infrared light, preventing photo-oxidation of lipids and destruction of light-sensitive vitamins (A, D, riboflavin). **(1 mark)**
2. **Oxygen Barrier** — Its metallic crystalline lattice (FCC) has no continuous pores, giving an oxygen transmission rate (OTR) effectively equal to zero above 15 µm thickness. **(1 mark)**
3. **Moisture Barrier** — Being hydrophobic, water vapour transmission rate (WVTR) is also effectively zero. **(0.5 marks)**
4. **Aroma / Flavour Barrier** — Hermetically sealable, retaining volatile aroma molecules inside the package. **(0.5 marks)**

Thus, no other common food packaging material (paper, plastic, glass) can match aluminium in simultaneously blocking all four deterioration pathways.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any three food-grade lacquer systems used for aluminium containers and state one specific food application for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per lacquer):**

| # | Lacquer | Food Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Epoxy-phenolic** | Acidic foods, fish, meat — provides sulphur-stain resistance |
| 2 | **Vinyl organosol** | Tomato products, fruit juices, drawn cans — acid resistant |
| 3 | **Polyester (PET) based / BPA-NI** | Dual-ovenable ready-meal trays — high heat tolerance |
| 4 | **Polyolefin dispersion (POD)** | Microwaveable frozen meal trays |

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Alert):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse aluminium gauges with steel gauges** — examiners deduct 2 marks for unit conversion errors.
> 2. When asked *"why lacquer is needed"*, students often write only *"to prevent corrosion"* and forget the **BPA-migration regulatory aspect** introduced after 2020 — losing the modernity marks.
> 3. Always mention the **specific alloy grade** (AA 3003, AA 3004, AA 5182) in your answer — generic statements like "aluminium is used" lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. Numerical answers must show all steps; skipping the area calculation is a guaranteed -2 mark penalty.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Discuss the **manufacturing process** of wrought (smoothwall) aluminium food trays in detail. Include the alloys used, lacquering steps, and quality-control tests. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square aluminium tray has side **s = 180 mm**, depth **D = 40 mm**, wall thickness **t_w = 70 µm**, base thickness **t_b = 110 µm**, and a flange width **r = 6 mm**. Calculate the **mass of aluminium** required per tray, given **ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

1. **Alloy Selection (1 mark):** Wrought food trays use **AA 8011, AA 8006, or AA 3003** alloys, chosen for their excellent formability (low yield strength ~80–110 MPa) and food-safe lacquer adhesion.

2. **Sheet Preparation (1 mark):** Aluminium ingots → continuous casting → hot rolling (400–500 °C) → cold rolling to final gauge (60–130 µm) → annealing (O-temper).

3. **Lacquering (2 marks):** The sheet is degreased, chemically etched (chromate or non-chromate), then coated with **polyester (PET) lacquer at 5–10 g/m²**, cured at 200–250 °C in a tunnel oven for 10 minutes to ensure solvent evaporation and cross-linking.

4. **Forming (2 marks):** The lacquered sheet is fed into a **mechanical or hydraulic press** fitted with matched **die and punch** tooling. The blank is held against the die by a **blank-holder** with a controlled hold-down force (to prevent wrinkling), then the punch forces the metal into the die cavity in a single stroke (single-draw) or progressive strokes (deep-draw) for deeper trays. Wall ironing may follow for very deep containers.

5. **Trimming & Flanging (1 mark):** The formed cup is trimmed to remove the uneven rim, and a horizontal flange is rolled onto the top for lid seating and rigidity.

6. **Quality Control (mandatory for full marks):**
   - **Pinhole test** — light box or electrical continuity test on foil stock.
   - **Lacquer adhesion test** — cross-hatch tape test (ASTM D3359).
   - **Curl test** — verifies flatness after forming.
   - **Migration test** — confirms total migration ≤ 10 mg/dm² under EU Regulation 1935/2004.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b): Numerical

**Step 1 — Convert to centimetres:**
s = 18.0 cm, D = 3.5 cm (depth 35 mm = 3.5 cm), t_w = 70 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 0.0070 cm, t_b = 110 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 0.0110 cm, r = 0.6 cm.

**Step 2 — Base area:**
$$A_b = s^2 = 18.0^2 = 324.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Walls (4 sides):**
$$A_w = 4 \times s \times D = 4 \times 18.0 \times 3.5 = 252.0 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Flange (square rim frame):**
Outer perimeter $P_o = 4s = 72$ cm.
Inner perimeter $P_i = 4(s - 2r) = 4(18 - 1.2) = 67.2$ cm.
Mean perimeter $P_m = (72 + 67.2)/2 = 69.6$ cm.
$$A_f = P_m \times r = 69.6 \times 0.6 = 41.76 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Volumes:**
$$V_b = A_b \times t_b = 324.0 \times 0.0110 = 3.564 \text{ cm}^3$$
$$V_{w+f} = (A_w + A_f) \times t_w = (252.0 + 41.76) \times 0.0070 = 293.76 \times 0.0070 = 2.0563 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 6 — Total volume:**
$$V_{total} = 3.564 + 2.0563 = 5.6203 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 7 — Mass:**
$$m = \rho_{Al} \times V_{total} = 2.70 \times 5.6203 = \boxed{15.17 \text{ g}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Compare **two-piece DWI (Drawn and Wall-Ironed) aluminium cans** with **three-piece aluminium cans**. Discuss their manufacturing, alloys used, advantages, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **lacquering system** of aluminium food cans in detail. Why is bare aluminium not safe for direct food contact? What is the role of BPA-NI coatings? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

| Parameter | Two-Piece DWI Can | Three-Piece Can |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | 1 seamless body + 1 top end | 3 parts: body + bottom end + top end |
| Manufacturing | Drawing + Wall Ironing in single press | Body sheet rolled + side-seam welded + flanged |
| Alloys | Body AA 3004, Top AA 5182 | Body AA 3003, End AA 5182 |
| Weight | Lighter (~13 g for 330 ml) | Heavier (~18 g for 330 ml) |
| Pressure resistance | Excellent (no seam) | Lower (seam is weak point) |
| Cost | Higher tooling cost | Lower tooling cost |
| Applications | Carbonated beverages, beer | Tuna, pet food, tomato products |
| Recyclability | Easier (one alloy stream) | Harder (mixed alloys) |

**Advantages of 2-piece DWI:**
- Higher burst strength (suitable for carbonation pressure ~6 atm)
- No side seam = no leak path
- 30–40% lighter
- Easier recycling (single alloy)

**Disadvantages of 2-piece DWI:**
- Cannot be made taller than ~250 mm easily (depth-to-diameter ratio limit)
- Higher initial tooling investment

**Advantages of 3-piece:**
- Can be made in tall, slim formats
- Lower initial investment for small production runs
- Easier to change can diameter

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):
