**Why bare aluminium is NOT food-safe (4 marks):**

1. **Corrosion in acidic media:** pH < 4 attacks the native Al₂O₃ layer, exposing fresh Al which dissolves as Al³⁺. Reaction: 2 Al + 6 H⁺ → 2 Al³⁺ + 3 H₂↑. The hydrogen evolution can also cause "hydrogen swells" in sealed cans. **(1 mark)**
2. **Pitting in chloride media:** Salty foods (NaCl → Cl⁻) cause localised pitting corrosion, leading to pinhole leaks. **(1 mark)**
3. **Sulphur staining:** Proteins release H₂S during retort, forming black Al₂S₃ on the can wall — cosmetic defect. **(1 mark)**
4. **Toxicological concern:** Dissolved Al³⁺ exceeds WHO permissible intake (2 mg/kg body weight/week) and is implicated (controversially) in neurodegenerative conditions. **(1 mark)**

**Lacquer System — Components (2 marks):**
- A typical food can lacquer has **3 layers**: (i) a **primer** for metal adhesion, (ii) a **body coat** for barrier properties, (iii) a **top coat / decorative print** (often ink + overprint varnish). Total coating weight = **4 – 10 g/m²**.

**Role of BPA-NI Coatings (1 mark):**
- **BPA (Bisphenol-A)** is the monomer traditionally used in epoxy-phenolic lacquers. Regulatory bodies (EFSA 2023) reduced the **TDI (Tolerable Daily Intake) of BPA to 0.2 ng/kg body weight/day** — essentially a near-zero limit.
- **BPA-NI (Non-Intent)** coatings use **polyester, acrylic, or polyolefin** resins that are BPA-free by design.
- They provide equivalent acid resistance and retort stability without BPA migration, complying with **EU Regulation 2018/213** and **FSSAI (India) 2020** regulations.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Aluminium Containers" (Module 2):**

- ✅ Aluminium is a **total barrier** (light + O₂ + moisture + aroma).
- ✅ Density **2.70 g/cm³**, melting point **660.3 °C**, Young's modulus **69 GPa**.
- ✅ Pinhole-free threshold thickness for barrier ≈ **15 µm**.
- ✅ Standard food-grade alloys: **AA 3003, AA 3004, AA 5182, AA 8011, AA 8006, AA 8079, AA 8021**.
- ✅ Bare aluminium is **NOT food-safe** — lacquer is mandatory for acidic, salty, and protein-rich foods.
- ✅ Common lacquers: **Epoxy-phenolic, Vinyl organosol, Polyester (PET), Polyolefin dispersion (POD), Acrylic, Epoxy-anionic**.
- ✅ **Sulphur-staining (Al₂S₃ blackening)** in protein foods → mitigated by **S-lacquers** with ZnO.
- ✅ **BPA-NI coatings** are now industry standard due to EU/FSSAI regulations.
- ✅ Manufacturing route: **Ingot → Casting → Hot Roll → Cold Roll → Anneal → Lacquer → Cure → Blank → Draw/Impact Extrude → Trim → Inspect**.
- ✅ **2-piece DWI cans** = lighter, pressure-resistant, seamless; used for beverages.
- ✅ **3-piece cans** = versatile, cheaper for short runs; used for tuna, pet food.
- ✅ **Wrought (smoothwall) trays** = ready-meal and airline catering; AA 8011 + PET lacquer.
- ✅ Aluminium recycling saves **~95% energy** vs. primary production; infinitely recyclable without quality loss.
- ✅ Always use **B&S aluminium gauge** (NOT steel gauge) for thickness conversion in KTU numerical problems.
- ✅ Conversion formula: $m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot V_{Al}$, with $V_{Al}$ computed via weighted average of base thickness and wall thickness.
- ✅ Tetra-Pak-style cartons contain a **6.5 µm aluminium foil layer** — counter-intuitive "paper" carton.
- ✅ For acidic foods (pH < 4.5), always use **vinyl organosol or epoxy-phenolic** lacquer.
- ✅ For microwave-safe applications, use **polyolefin dispersion (POD)** or **polyester (PET)** coating — NOT pure epoxy-phenolic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Composite Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **composite container** is a multi-layered rigid or semi-rigid packaging structure fabricated by laminating two or more dissimilar webs of materials (typically paperboard, plastic films, aluminium foil, and adhesives) into a single, functionally integrated body. Each constituent layer contributes a specific packaging property — such as mechanical strength, moisture barrier, gas barrier, grease resistance, heat-sealability, or printability — that no single layer could provide alone. The synergistic combination yields a container whose overall performance is governed by the **permeability sum-rule** of layered barriers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim mapping):**
> *"Composite containers are cylindrical, oval or rectangular shaped containers made by spirally or convolutely winding multiple plies of paperboard, aluminium foil, and plastic film, bonded with adhesives, used primarily for packaging of liquid and dry food products."*

Common commercial examples include **Tetra Pak bricks**, **Combibloc cartons**, **Cumberland** snack cans, **Sonoco** paperboard cans for cocoa, juice concentrates, soup powders, nuts, and motor oil.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sandwich Wall"

Imagine you are building a tiny **protective fortress wall around a sandwich** so that the bread does not go stale, the cheese does not oxidize, and the lettuce does not wilt. You would not use a single material — you would build layers:

- **Outer brick wall** → for mechanical strength and stiffness (analogous to **paperboard**).
- **Inner polished glass** → for moisture and gas barrier (analogous to **aluminium foil**).
- **Plastic varnish** → to seal the edges and protect the glass from cracking (analogous to **LDPE / Surlyn ionomer**).
- **A printed outer coat** → for branding and abrasion resistance (analogous to **PE extrusion coating**).

The **composite container** is exactly this "sandwich wall," wound around itself into a can or folded into a brick. Each layer performs one job, and together they outperform any single homogeneous wall of equal thickness.

---

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Composite Containers (Classification Snapshot)

| Family | Geometry | Winding Pattern | Typical End-Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spiral-wound | Cylindrical | Continuous helical plies at angle θ | Juice concentrate, cocoa, soup powder |
| Convolute-wound | Cylindrical | Plies parallel to axis | Engine oil, chemicals, spices |
| Linear / Wraparound | Rectangular | Flat blanks folded around product | Juice cartons (Tetra Brik) |
| Drawn / Formed | Rectangular / Oval | Pre-formed paperboard with foil lining | Aseptic dairy, UHT milk |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Ply** = one individual layer of web material in the wall.
> - **Body** = the cylindrical/prismatic wall (excluding ends).
> - **Side seam** = the longitudinal bonded joint (often hidden under the label).
> - **Manufacturer's joint** = the seam formed during spiral/convolute winding.
> - **End closures** = metal or plastic disc/ring seals crimped to the body.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

The following physical constants and standard units are **mandatory for KTU numerical/short-answer questions**:

- **WVTR** (Water Vapour Transmission Rate): expressed in **g/m²·day** at a defined T (°C) and RH gradient (typically 38 °C / 90% RH).
- **OTR** (Oxygen Transmission Rate): expressed in **cm³/m²·day·atm** (or **cc/m²·day** in industry).
- **Puncture resistance (Beach units):** **0.293 J = 1 Beach unit**.
- **Burst strength of paperboard:** **kPa** (measured by Mullen tester).
- **Aluminium foil pinhole threshold:** must be **≥ 6 µm (0.6 mil)** for reliable barrier (≤ 50 pinholes/m²).
- **Cobb value** (water absorption of paperboard): expressed in **g/m²** over 30, 60 or 1800 s.
- **Standard shelf-life math:** **t = (C_critical − C_0) / R** where R is the steady permeation rate.

---

## 1.5 Why Composite Containers? The "5-Why" Rationale

1. **Why not paper alone?** Paper is hygroscopic — moisture destroys it.
2. **Why not plastic alone?** Plastic alone is permeable to O₂ and aromas.
3. **Why not aluminium alone?** Aluminium is pinholed below 6 µm and needs structural support.
4. **Why not glass alone?** Glass is heavy and fragile for shipping.
5. **Why composite?** Because no single material simultaneously provides **strength + barrier + sealability + printability + light weight + low cost**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic — "PASS-ME":**
> **P** = Printability (paper)
> **A** = Air/Gas barrier (foil/EVOH)
> **S** = Strength (paperboard)
> **S** = Seal (LDPE/Surlyn)
> **M** = Moisture barrier (foil/PE)
> **E** = Economy (low-cost paperboard bulk)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture — Anatomy of a Typical Composite Container

A canonical composite container wall (e.g., a Tetra Brik juice carton) has the following layered structure, listed from **outer surface → product**:

1. **Polyethylene (outer) layer** — abrasion resistance, gloss, scuff protection, water-shedding.
2. **Printed paperboard / kraft paper** — body stock, label substrate, primary structural member.
3. **Adhesive tie layer (PU / acrylic)** — bonds foil to board.
4. **Aluminium foil (typically 6–9 µm)** — absolute barrier to O₂, light, aromas.
5. **Adhesive tie layer** — bonds foil to inner sealant.
6. **Surlyn® ionomer OR LDPE / HDPE inner sealant** — heat-seal, food contact, moisture barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> In **food-grade composite containers**, all layers in contact with the product must comply with **FDA 21 CFR 176.170 / EU Regulation 1935/2004** for food contact migration limits.

---

## 2.2 Manufacturing Logic — Stepwise

### A. Spiral Winding (Cylindrical cans)

A spiral-wound composite can is built on a continuous mandrel through these logical steps:

1. **Strip feeding**: Multiple webs (paperboard, foil, PE) are fed from individual unwind stations.
2. **Adhesive application**: Bead of adhesive (usually **PVA emulsion** or **hot-melt EVA**) applied via roller.
3. **Helical winding**: The combined web passes through a **mandrel at a winding angle θ** (typically **15° – 25°**) so each ply overlaps the previous.
4. **Tube formation**: Continuous tube is produced and cut into can body lengths.
5. **Curing/drying**: Adhesive is dried in a heated tunnel (**120–160 °C** for ≤ 60 s).
6. **End-closure fitting**: Top and bottom metal/plastic closures are crimped on.
7. **Labelling & sleeving**: Printed paper label is glued over the side seam.

### B. Linear Wraparound (Cartons like Tetra Brik)

1. Web is printed and cut into **rectangular blanks**.
2. Blanks pass through a **hydrogen peroxide bath** (for aseptic, 35% H₂O₂ at ~70 °C) for surface sterilization.
3. **Form-fill-seal (FFS)** operation folds and heat-seals the blank into a tube around the product.
4. **Transverse sealing** creates individual brick-shaped packages.

### C. Convolute Winding

The plies are wrapped **parallel to the can axis** (i.e., no helix angle). Used for very heavy-duty containers like **engine oil cans**. Provides better hoop strength but slower production.

---

## 2.3 Permeation Theory — The Heart of Composite Container Design

The single most examinable theoretical concept is **how the barrier of a layered wall behaves mathematically**.

### 2.3.1 Permeability of a Single Homogeneous Layer

$$ P = D \cdot S $$

Where:
- **P** = permeability coefficient
- **D** = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- **S** = solubility coefficient (kg/m³·Pa)

The steady-state transmission rate per unit area is:

$$ R = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} $$

Where:
- **R** = transmission rate (kg/m²·s)
- **Δp** = partial pressure difference across the layer (Pa)
- **L** = thickness of the layer (m)

### 2.3.2 Permeability of a Composite (Series) Wall — The "Sum-Rule"

For **n layers in series** (each layer offers independent resistance), the total resistance is the **sum of individual resistances**. For transmission rate, this yields the **reciprocal-sum rule**:

$$ \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_i} $$

Equivalently, in terms of **permeance** (P/L):

$$ \frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Memory Trick:**
> *"Resistances add like resistors in a series electrical circuit."*
> The layer with the **lowest permeability** dominates the overall barrier — adding more high-barrier layers yields diminishing returns once one perfect barrier (e.g., thick Al foil) is already present.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Symbol Key | Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = D \cdot S$ | Permeability = Diffusion × Solubility | kg·m/m²·s·Pa | Single-layer material |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | Steady-state transmission rate | g/m²·day or cm³/m²·day | Single-layer calculation |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | Composite layer permeation (reciprocal sum) | g/m²·day | Multi-layer composite |
| 4 | $\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$ | Composite permeance | dimensionless groups | Design trade-offs |
| 5 | $t_{shelf} = \dfrac{C_{crit} - C_0}{R \cdot A / m_{product}}$ | Shelf-life equation | days | Critical oxygen ingress for shelf-life |
| 6 | $V = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot h$ | Volume of cylindrical composite can | m³ or cm³ | Capacity sizing |
| 7 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical wall | Pa or N/m² | Burst-pressure design |
| 8 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{pitch}{\pi \cdot d}\right)$ | Winding helix angle (spiral cans) | degrees | Spiral-wound can geometry |
| 9 | $P_{critical} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{yield} \cdot t}{d}$ | Burst pressure of a cylinder | Pa or kPa | Strength verification |
| 10 | Cobb$_{30} \leq X$ | Surface water absorption in 30 s | g/m² | Paperboard grade selection |
| 11 | Beach $= 0.293 \, J$ | Unit conversion for puncture resistance | Joules | Standardising test reports |

> **Engineering Utility:**
> Equations 3–5 are used in **MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging)** and **aseptic packaging** design — particularly Tetra Pak's CODEX-based shelf-life guarantee of UHT milk for 6 months at ambient storage. Equation 8 governs the **mechanical anisotropy** of spiral-wound cans (helix angle directly controls crush strength).

---

## 2.5 Comparison: Composite Container vs Other Rigid Containers

| Property | Composite Container | Tinplate Can | Glass Bottle | Plastic Bottle (PET) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weight-to-volume ratio | **Low** | High | Very high | Lowest |
| O₂ barrier | Excellent (with foil) | Excellent | Perfect | Poor–Moderate |
| Light barrier | Perfect (foil) | Perfect | Perfect | Poor |
| Recyclability | Paper recycling stream | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Cost per unit | Moderate | High | Low–Moderate | Lowest |
| Microwaveable | No (metal layer) | No | Yes | Conditional |
| Transparency | No | No | Yes | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Composite Permeability for Series Layers

We need to derive the equation:
$$ \frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} $$

### Starting Postulate

At **steady state**, the mass flux $J$ (kg/m²·s) through every layer in the series wall must be identical. Otherwise, mass would accumulate or deplete inside an infinitesimally thin plane — violating conservation of mass.

**Step 1:** Write flux through any individual layer using Fick's law generalised form:

$$ J = \frac{P_i \, \Delta p_i}{L_i} $$

Where $\Delta p_i$ is the partial pressure drop across layer $i$.

**Step 2:** Solve for the pressure drop across each layer:

$$ \Delta p_i = \frac{J \, L_i}{P_i} $$

**Step 3:** Sum the individual pressure drops across all n layers. The sum must equal the total pressure drop across the wall:

$$ \Delta p_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta p_i $$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:

$$ \Delta p_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{J \, L_i}{P_i} = J \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} $$

**Step 5:** For the composite wall as a single equivalent layer, the same flux equation reads:

$$ J = \frac{P_{total} \, \Delta p_{total}}{L_{total}} $$

**Step 6:** Equate expressions for $J$:

$$ \frac{P_{total} \, \Delta p_{total}}{L_{total}} = \frac{\Delta p_{total}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}} $$

**Step 7:** Cancel $\Delta p_{total}$ from both sides and cross-multiply:

$$ P_{total} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} = L_{total} $$

**Step 8:** Rearrange into the canonical "reciprocal-sum" form:

$$ \frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} \quad \blacksquare $$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Composite Can Shelf Life

> **Problem:** A composite container wall consists of three layers in series:
> - Layer 1: Paperboard — thickness $L_1 = 350 \, \mu m$, permeability $P_1 = 5.0 \times 10^{-10} \, kg \cdot m / (m^2 \cdot s \cdot Pa)$.
> - Layer 2: Aluminium foil — thickness $L_2 = 9 \, \mu m$, permeability $P_2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-15} \, kg \cdot m / (m^2 \cdot s \cdot Pa)$.
> - Layer 3: LDPE inner sealant — thickness $L_3 = 50 \, \mu m$, permeability $P_3 = 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \, kg \cdot m / (m^2 \cdot s \cdot Pa)$.
>
> The driving partial pressure difference for water vapour across the wall is $\Delta p = 2300 \, Pa$ (equivalent to 90% RH − 0% RH at 25 °C).
>
> **(a)** Compute the overall permeability $P_{total}$ of the composite wall.
> **(b)** Compute the steady-state WVTR per m² per day.
> **(c)** Comment on which layer is rate-limiting.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $L_i / P_i$ for each layer:**

$$ \frac{L_1}{P_1} = \frac{350 \times 10^{-6}}{5.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 7.0 \times 10^{5} \, \text{(Pa·s·m²/kg)} $$

$$ \frac{L_2}{P_2} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-15}} = 9.0 \times 10^{9} $$

$$ \frac{L_3}{P_3} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.0 \times 10^{6} $$

**Step 2 — Sum the reciprocal-permeabilities:**

$$ \sum \frac{L_i}{P_i} = 7.0 \times 10^{5} + 9.0 \times 10^{9} + 2.0 \times 10^{6} $$

The dominant term is $9.0 \times 10^{9}$. Adding the others yields:

$$ \sum \frac{L_i}{P_i} \approx 9.0027 \times 10^{9} $$

**Step 3 — Compute total thickness:**

$$ L_{total} = 350 + 9 + 50 = 409 \, \mu m = 4.09 \times 10^{-4} \, m $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_{total}$:**

$$ P_{total} = \frac{L_{total}}{\displaystyle\sum \frac{L_i}{P_i}} = \frac{4.09 \times 10^{-4}}{9.0027 \times 10^{9}} $$

$$ P_{total} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-14} \, \frac{kg \cdot m}{m^2 \cdot s \cdot Pa} $$

**Step 5 — Compute transmission rate R:**

$$ R = \frac{P_{total} \cdot \Delta p}{L_{total}} = \frac{(4.54 \times 10^{-14})(2300)}{4.09 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ R \approx 2.55 \times 10^{-7} \, \frac{kg}{m^2 \cdot s} $$

**Step 6 — Convert to g/m²·day:**

$$ R = 2.55 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \, g/kg \times 86400 \, s/day \approx 22.0 \, g/m^2 \cdot day $$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation:**

The aluminium foil layer contributes **~99.99% of the total resistance**. Hence:
- Removing the LDPE layer would **not significantly change** the transmission rate.
- Removing the aluminium foil layer would catastrophically degrade the barrier.
- **Rate-limiting layer = Aluminium foil** — this is the design-critical layer.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** For numerical questions, always identify the **rate-limiting layer** explicitly — examiners frequently award 2 marks just for correctly naming it.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Layer-Wise Contribution to Total Resistance

| Layer | $L_i \, (\mu m)$ | $P_i \, (kg \cdot m / m^2 \cdot s \cdot Pa)$ | $L_i / P_i$ | % of total resistance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paperboard (outer) | 350 | $5.0 \times 10^{-10}$ | $7.0 \times 10^{5}$ | **0.0078 %** |
| Aluminium foil | 9 | $1.0 \times 10^{-15}$ | $9.0 \times 10^{9}$ | **99.97 %** |
| LDPE (inner) | 50 | $2.5 \times 10^{-11}$ | $2.0 \times 10^{6}$ | **0.022 %** |
| **Composite Wall** | **409** | $4.54 \times 10^{-14}$ | $9.0027 \times 10^{9}$ | **100 %** |

---

## 3.4 Helix-Angle Derivation for Spiral Winding

A spiral-wound can's mechanical strength depends on the winding angle θ. The relationship between θ, pitch $p$ (axial distance per revolution), and can diameter $d$ is:

$$ \tan(\theta) = \frac{p}{\pi \cdot d} $$

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1:** Unroll one full revolution of the helix. The unrolled length equals the **circumference** $\pi \cdot d$.

**Step 2:** The vertical rise (axial advance) per revolution is the **pitch** $p$.

**Step 3:** When the cylinder is "cut and unrolled" into a flat rectangle, the helix becomes a straight line crossing at angle θ to the axis. The opposite side of the right triangle has length $\pi \cdot d$ and adjacent side has length $p$.

**Step 4:** By trigonometry:

$$ \tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{p}{\pi \cdot d} \quad \blacksquare $$

> **Engineering use:** Smaller θ gives a stronger **hoop-direction** resistance (good for internal pressure). Larger θ improves **axial crush strength**. Typical θ for cans: **15° – 25°**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Layered Architecture of a Composite Container Wall

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OUTER SURFACE - Print Layer PE 15um] --> B[Paperboard Body Stock 300 to 500 um]
    B --> C[Adhesive Tie Layer PU or Acrylic]
    C --> D[Aluminium Foil Barrier 6 to 9 um]
    D --> E[Adhesive Tie Layer PU or Acrylic]
    E --> F[Inner Sealant LDPE or Surlyn 30 to 50 um]
    F --> G[FOOD PRODUCT INTERFACE]

    H[Function Printability and Abrasion Resistance] -.-> A
    I[Function Mechanical Strength and Rigidity] -.-> B
    J[Function Bonding Foil to Board] -.-> C
    K[Function Oxygen Light and Aroma Barrier] -.-> D
    L[Function Bonding Foil to Sealant] -.-> E
    M[Function Heat Seal and Moisture Barrier] -.-> F

    N[FDA 21 CFR 176 170 Compliant for Layers D E F] -.-> G

    classDef barrier fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef strength fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef seal fill:#118ab2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class D,K barrier
    class B,I strength
    class F,M seal
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Spiral-Wound Composite Can Manufacturing

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Unwind Paperboard Web] --> M[Mandrel Station 15 to 25 Degree Helix]
    P2[Unwind Aluminium Foil Web] --> M
    P3[Unwind LDPE Web] --> M
    AD[Adhesive Applicator Roller] --> M
    M --> C[Continuous Tube Exit]
    C --> CU[Cutter for Can Body Length]
    CU --> DR[Adhesive Curing Oven 120 to 160 C]
    DR --> EC[End Closure Crimping Station]
    EC --> LB[Labelling and Sleeving]
    LB --> Q[Quality Inspection WVTR Burst Test]
    Q --> OUT[Finished Composite Container]

    classDef process fill:#ef476f,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef quality fill:#073b4c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px

    class M,CU,DR,EC process
    class Q,OUT quality
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Tree for Container Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Food Product Selection] --> Q1{Requires Long Shelf Life Above 6 Months?}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Heat Sensitive Product?}
    Q2 -- YES --> A1[Use Tetra Pak Aseptic Brick]
    Q2 -- NO --> A2[Use Spiral Wound Can with Foil Liner]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Product is Dry Powder or Granule?}
    Q3 -- YES --> A3[Use Convolution Wound Paperboard Can]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Product is Liquid and Requires Aroma Protection?}
    Q4 -- YES --> A4[Use Tetra Rex or Combibloc Carton]
    Q4 -- NO --> A5[Use Plastic Laminated Paperboard Pouch]

    classDef decision fill:#fef08a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 decision
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 action
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Permeation Mechanism Through Composite Wall

```mermaid
graph TD
    X1[Oxygen Molecule at Outer Surface] --> X2[Diffuses Through PE Outer Layer]
    X2 --> X3[Attempts Diffusion Through Paperboard]
    X3 --> X4[Blocked by Aluminium Foil Layer]
    X4 --> X5[No Permeation to Food Product]
    X6[Water Vapour at Outer Surface] --> X7[Condensation on Inner Paperboard]
    X7 --> X8[Blocked by Aluminium Foil]
    X8 --> X9[No Moisture Reaches Product]

    Y1[Light Energy Incident] --> Y2[Reflected and Absorbed by Aluminium Foil]
    Y2 --> Y3[No Photooxidation of Product]

    classDef blocked fill:#f87171,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef passed fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef foil fill:#c084fc,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px

    class X4,X8,Y2 foil
    class X5,X9,Y3 blocked
    class X2,X3,X7 passed
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define composite containers. List any four materials used in their construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A composite container is a multi-layered rigid or semi-rigid packaging structure made by laminating two or more different webs — typically paperboard, aluminium foil, and plastic films — to combine their individual functional benefits into a single integrated package. **(1 mark)**

The four materials commonly used are: **(2 marks — 0.5 each)**

1. **Paperboard / Kraft paper** — provides mechanical rigidity, printability, and bulk.
2. **Aluminium foil (6–9 µm)** — provides absolute barrier to oxygen, moisture, light, and aroma.
3. **Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE)** — provides heat-sealability and food-contact safety.
4. **Adhesives (PVA, EVA, PU)** — bond the dissimilar layers into a unified laminate.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between spiral-wound and convolute-wound composite cans.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Spiral-Wound Can | Convolute-Wound Can |
|---|---|---|
| Winding orientation | Plies wound at an angle (helix) to the can axis **(1 mark)** | Plies wound parallel to the can axis **(1 mark)** |
| Production speed | Faster (continuous helix) | Slower |
| Hoop strength | Lower (anisotropic) | Higher (isotropic) |
| Typical use | Juice cans, cocoa, soup powder | Engine oil cans, chemicals, heavy-duty |

**(1 mark for any additional valid distinguishing parameter)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat cross-sectional sketch, describe the **layered architecture of a Tetra Brik-type composite carton**. List the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equation for the **overall permeability of a composite wall** made of n layers in series. A composite container has three layers with the following data:

| Layer | Thickness (µm) | Permeability (kg·m / m²·s·Pa) |
|---|---|---|
| Paperboard | 400 | $6.0 \times 10^{-10}$ |
| Aluminium foil | 8 | $1.0 \times 10^{-15}$ |
| LDPE | 40 | $2.0 \times 10^{-11}$ |

The water vapour pressure difference across the wall is **Δp = 2400 Pa**. Compute the **overall permeability** and the **steady-state WVTR in g/m²·day**. Identify the rate-limiting layer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch (2 marks):**

A cross-sectional sketch should show the following layers from outside to inside:

> **OUTER → INNER:**
> - Polyethylene (outer coat, ~15 µm) — printability and abrasion
> - Paperboard (~350 µm) — structural body
> - Adhesive (PU/Acrylic, ~5 µm)
> - Aluminium foil (~6–9 µm) — barrier
> - Adhesive (PU/Acrylic, ~5 µm)
> - LDPE / Surlyn inner sealant (~30–50 µm) — heat-seal + food contact

**Tabulated functions (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Layer | Function |
|---|---|
| Outer PE | Gloss, scuff/abrasion resistance, water-shedding |
| Paperboard | Mechanical rigidity, label substrate, structural strength |
| Adhesive 1 | Bond foil to board (must survive pasteurization) |
| Aluminium foil | Oxygen, light, aroma, moisture barrier |
| Adhesive 2 | Bond foil to inner sealant |
| Inner LDPE | Heat-seal, food-contact safety, moisture resistance |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Derivation (4 marks):**

At steady state, the mass flux $J$ is identical through every layer. By Fick's law:

$$ J = \frac{P_i \, \Delta p_i}{L_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta p_i = \frac{J \, L_i}{P_i} $$

Summing all pressure drops across the n layers:

$$ \Delta p_{total} = J \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} $$

For the equivalent single layer:

$$ J = \frac{P_{total} \, \Delta p_{total}}{L_{total}} $$

Equating and simplifying:

$$ \frac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} \quad \blacksquare \text{ (Reciprocal Sum Rule)} $$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation (3 marks):**

Compute $L_i / P_i$:

$$ \frac{L_1}{P_1} = \frac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{6.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.67 \times 10^{5} $$

$$ \frac{L_2}{P_2} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-15}} = 8.0 \times 10^{9} $$

$$ \frac{L_3}{P_3} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{2.0 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.0 \times 10^{6} $$

$$ \sum \frac{L_i}{P_i} \approx 8.00267 \times 10^{9} \quad (P_a \cdot s \cdot m^2/kg) $$

$$ L_{total} = 400 + 8 + 40 = 448 \, \mu m = 4.48 \times 10^{-4} \, m $$

$$ P_{total} = \frac{4.48 \times 10^{-4}}{8.00267 \times 10^{9}} \approx 5.6 \times 10^{-14} \, kg \cdot m / (m^2 \cdot s \cdot Pa) $$

$$ R = \frac{P_{total} \cdot \Delta p}{L_{total}} = \frac{(5.6 \times 10^{-14})(2400)}{4.48 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-7} \, kg/(m^2 \cdot s) $$

Converting to g/m²·day:

$$ R = 3.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times 86400 \approx 25.9 \, g/m^2 \cdot day $$

**Rate-limiting layer = Aluminium foil** (provides ~99.99 % of total resistance).

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **spiral-winding process** for manufacturing composite cans with a labelled diagram. Discuss the effect of **winding angle** on the mechanical strength of the can. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spiral-wound composite can has a **diameter of 100 mm**, **pitch of 40 mm**, and is subjected to an internal pressure of **200 kPa**. If the wall is made of paperboard with **yield strength σ = 25 MPa** and **thickness t = 0.5 mm**, determine: **(i)** the winding helix angle, **(ii)** the hoop stress in the wall, (iii) whether the can will burst. Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Spiral winding process description (4 marks):**

In spiral winding, multiple continuous webs of paperboard, foil, and plastic film are fed simultaneously from individual unwind stations. Adhesive is applied through a roller. The combined web passes over a rotating mandrel at a fixed helical angle (typically 15°–25°), producing a continuous tube. The tube is then cut into can-body lengths, dried/cured in an oven, and fitted with metal or plastic end closures. Finally, a printed paper sleeve is glued over the longitudinal side seam.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Should show mandrel, unwind stations, adhesive applicator, helical winding, cutter, oven, end-closure station.

**Effect of winding angle on strength (2 marks):**

- **Smaller winding angle (closer to circumferential)** → higher **hoop (circumferential) strength** → resists internal bursting pressure better.
- **Larger winding angle (closer to axial)** → higher **axial crush strength** → resists vertical stacking loads better.
- Typical industrial optimum: **18°–22°** for balanced performance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Winding angle (2 marks):**

$$ \tan(\theta) = \frac{p}{\pi \cdot d} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 100 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.040}{0.3142} \approx 0.1273 $$

$$ \theta = \arctan(0.1273) \approx 7.26^{\circ} $$

**(ii) Hoop stress (3 marks):**

For a thin-walled cylinder, hoop stress is:

$$ \sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p \cdot r}{t} = \frac{(200 \times 10^{3} \, Pa)(50 \times 10^{-3} \, m)}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, m} $$

$$ \sigma_{hoop} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 0.05}{0.0005} = \frac{10{,}000}{0.0005} = 20 \times 10^{6} \, Pa = 20 \, MPa $$

**(iii) Bursting judgement (2 marks):**

Critical burst pressure using yield criterion:

$$ p_{critical} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma_{yield} \cdot t}{d} = \frac{2 \times 25 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.1} = 250 \, kPa $$

Since the applied pressure (200 kPa) is **less than** the critical burst pressure (250 kPa), the can will **NOT burst** — it operates within safe design limits. **(1 mark for conclusion, 1 mark for safety margin justification).**

Safety factor: $250 / 200 = 1.25$ (only 25 % margin — marginal in practice, suggesting need for reinforcement or thicker wall for safety). **(extra credit in valuation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing permeability (P) with permeance (P/L).** When summing resistances, always use $\dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$ or equivalently $\dfrac{1}{R_i}$ — NOT $\dfrac{1}{P_i}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert thickness** from µm to metres when applying SI units.
> 3. **Mixing WVTR and OTR units** — be explicit about which gas is being transmitted.
> 4. **Ignoring the helix-angle sign convention** — θ is measured from the axis, not from circumferential.
> 5. **In derivation questions, students skip Step 1** (the steady-state postulate). Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for stating "at steady state, flux is identical through all layers."
> 6. **Not identifying the rate-limiting layer** in numerical problems — this is a **mandatory** 2-mark step that students frequently omit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Definition**: Composite container = multi-layered rigid/semi-rigid packaging made by laminating dissimilar webs (paperboard, foil, plastic, adhesives).
- [ ] **Layer order** (outer → inner): PE coat → Paperboard → Adhesive → Aluminium foil → Adhesive → LDPE/Surlyn.
- [ ] **Key materials**: Paperboard (strength), Aluminium foil 6–9 µm (barrier), LDPE/Surlyn (seal), PVA/PU adhesive (bonding).
- [ ] **Three winding types**: Spiral (helical), Convolute (parallel to axis), Linear wraparound (rectangular cartons).
- [ ] **Permeability equation**: $P = D \cdot S$.
- [ ] **Transmission rate equation**: $R = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$.
- [ ] **Composite reciprocal-sum rule**: $\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$.
- [ ] **Rate-limiting layer**: Always the layer with the **lowest $P_i$** — usually aluminium foil.
- [ ] **Helix angle formula**: $\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{p}{\pi \cdot d}$.
- [ ] **Hoop stress formula**: $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}$.
- [ ] **Burst pressure formula**: $p_{critical} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{yield} \cdot t}{d}$.
- [ ] **WVTR units**: g/m²·day (at 38 °C / 90 % RH standard).
- [ ] **OTR units**: cm³/m²·day·atm.
- [ ] **Foil pinhole threshold**: ≥ 6 µm for reliable barrier.
- [ ] **Puncture unit**: 1 Beach = **0.293 J**.
- [ ] **Shelf-life equation**: $t_{shelf} = \dfrac{C_{crit} - C_0}{R \cdot A / m_{product}}$.
- [ ] **Applications**: UHT milk, juices, soup powders, cocoa, engine oil, spices, dry fruits.
- [ ] **Advantages**: Lightweight, recyclable in paper stream, excellent barrier, printable, microwave-unsafe.
- [ ] **Limitations**: Not transparent, not microwaveable, requires foil-pin-hole control.
- [ ] **Regulatory standards**: FDA 21 CFR 176.170, EU Regulation 1935/2004 for food-contact layers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lacquers - Properties, Advantages and Limitations of the Following Packaging Materials: Glass, Aluminium and its Foil, Metal Tin Containers, Paper and Paperboard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lacquers - Properties, Advantages and Limitations of the Following Packaging Materials: Glass, Aluminium and its Foil, Metal Tin Containers, Paper and Paperboard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Packaging Lacquers?

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Packaging lacquers are specialized **organic-based coating formulations** (varnishes, enamels, or thermoset/thermoplastic films) applied as thin layers (typically **5–25 µm** dry film thickness) onto the inner surface of metal, glass, or paperboard packaging substrates. Their primary function is to create a **functional barrier film** that isolates the packaged food from the base container material, preventing direct contact-induced chemical migration, corrosion, flavor scalping, and metallic taste transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme answers, students often confuse *lacquers* with *varnishes*. **Lacquers** = thermoset/thermoplastic polymer coatings designed for **functional barrier protection** (food-contact safety). **Varnishes** = decorative overprint coatings for **aesthetic finish**. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍱

Imagine your lunch is being served in a steel tiffin box. Now imagine that steel reacting with the tangy pickle (acidic food) and giving it a metallic taste — this is exactly what happens in real packaging! 

A **lacquer acts like a "food-grade raincoat"** painted on the *inside* of the container. Just as a raincoat stops water from soaking your clothes, a lacquer stops food acids, sulfur compounds, and salts from corroding the metal and spoiling the food. It's an **invisible, tasteless, FDA-approved skin** that keeps the container and the food from "shaking hands" chemically.

---

## 1.2 Lacquers — Classification & Properties

### 1.2.1 Major Types of Packaging Lacquers

| Lacquer Type | Base Resin | Typical Cure | Substrate Used On |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** | Bisphenol-A epoxy | 200°C / 10 min | Tinplate (food cans) |
| **Organosol** | Vinyl chloride–vinyl acetate + epoxy + phenolic | 200°C / 12 min | Beer/beverage cans |
| **Phenolic** | Phenol-formaldehyde resin | 200°C / 15 min | Acidic foods (tomato, fruits) |
| **Epoxy-phenolic** | Blend of epoxy + phenolic | 200°C / 12 min | Universal food can liner |
| **Polyester** | PET / oil-free polyester | 220°C / 8 min | Beverage cans (BPA-free) |
| **Vinyl** | PVC organosol | 180°C / 10 min | Twist-off closures |
| **Acrylic** | Acrylic resin | 180°C / 10 min | Internal can coatings |

### 1.2.2 Key Properties a Lacquer Must Possess

> [!NOTE]
> **The "5 + 1" Essential Properties of Food-Contact Lacquers:**
> 1. **Adhesion** — must bond firmly to substrate (no flaking)
> 2. **Flexibility** — must withstand stamping/fabrication (Erichsen cupping test > **5 mm**)
> 3. **Chemical Resistance** — must resist acids, salts, sulfur (ASTM D130 rating)
> 4. **Heat Resistance** — must survive retort sterilization (**121°C / 30 min**)
> 5. **Migration Safety** — global migration must be **< 10 mg/dm²** (EU Regulation 10/2011)
> 6. **Sensory Neutrality** — no odor/taste transfer to food

---

## 1.3 Overview of the Five Major Packaging Materials

### 1.3.1 Glass 🧪
A **rigid, amorphous, inorganic** packaging material made by fusing **silica (SiO₂)**, **soda ash (Na₂CO₃)**, and **limestone (CaCO₃)** at **~1500°C**, then molding/annealing. Density ≈ **2.5 g/cm³**, completely impermeable to gases and moisture.

### 1.3.2 Aluminium and its Foil 🥈
A **lightweight, ductile, non-ferrous metal** (density **2.71 g/cm³**) widely used as thin foils (**6–200 µm**). Possesses an inherent passive **Al₂O₃ oxide layer** that gives corrosion resistance. Used as rigid trays, flexible laminates, and aerosols.

### 1.3.3 Metal Tin Containers (Tinplate & TFS) 🥫
**Tinplate** = low-carbon steel sheet (**0.10–0.50 mm**) electrolytically coated with a thin layer of tin (**0.4–1.5 µm** per side). **TFS (Tin-Free Steel)** = steel coated with chromium/chromium oxide. Density ≈ **7.85 g/cm³** for steel base.

### 1.3.4 Paper and Paperboard 📦
Cellulose-fiber-based sheet material made from wood pulp. **Paper** = up to **~250 g/m²** (GSM); **Paperboard** = **>250 g/m²**, typically **200–600 g/m²**. Used as primary (kraft bags), secondary (corrugated boxes), and tertiary (cartons) packaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Glass, metals, and paper each **require lacquers** for different reasons — glass (fragility/scratch protection), metal (corrosion/migration), paper (moisture/gas barrier). This cross-linkage is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Lacquers — Mechanism of Action on Different Substrates

### 2.1.1 Mechanism on **Metal Tin Containers**

The lacquer film functions through **three concurrent mechanisms**:

1. **Physical Barrier Mechanism** — Creates a continuous film (5–25 µm) blocking electrolyte access.
2. **Electrochemical Insulation Mechanism** — Disrupts the cathodic-anodic microcells on the steel surface.
3. **Chemical Inertness Mechanism** — Prevents metal ions (Sn²⁺, Fe²⁺) from leaching into food.

For **sulfur-containing foods** (meat, fish, peas, corn), **epoxy-phenolic lacquers with ZnO pigment** are used because ZnO reacts with H₂S to form white ZnS, masking the unsightly black tin sulfide stain.

### 2.1.2 Mechanism on **Aluminium Foil**

The native **Al₂O₃ passive layer (≈ 4 nm thick)** provides baseline protection. Lacquers act as a **secondary barrier** to:
- Prevent "pitting corrosion" by acidic foods (pH < 4.5)
- Block **"blackening"** from sulfur compounds
- Avoid **"hydrogen swelling"** in carbonated drinks (lactic acid + Al → H₂ gas)

### 2.1.3 Mechanism on **Glass**

Glass is **chemically inert**, so lacquer usage is rare. Lacquers are only applied as:
- **Fragment retention coatings** (anti-shatter / "sacrificial" coatings) on glass bottles
- **UV-absorbing coatings** to protect light-sensitive beverages (milk, beer)

### 2.1.4 Mechanism on **Paper / Paperboard**

Lacquers here are typically **wax coatings, polyethylene extrusion, or acrylic dispersions** providing:
- Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) reduction
- Grease resistance (KIT rating)
- Heat-sealability for pouches

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Quantitative Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Values to Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam)**

| Parameter | Symbol / Unit | Typical Value | Tested In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lacquer dry film thickness | µm | **5–25 µm** | 3-mark |
| Glass transition temperature (epoxy) | Tg | **90–110°C** | 3-mark |
| Tin coating weight (tinplate) | g/m² | **2.8–11.2 g/m²** (E.S.=0.4–1.5 µm) | 14-mark |
| Aluminium foil thickness | µm | **6–200 µm** | 3-mark |
| Aluminium density | g/cm³ | **2.71 g/cm³** | 14-mark |
| Steel (tinplate base) density | g/cm³ | **7.85 g/cm³** | 14-mark |
| Glass density | g/cm³ | **2.5 g/cm³** | 14-mark |
| Paper GSM range | g/m² | **40–600 g/m²** | 3-mark |
| Retort sterilization temp/time | °C/min | **121°C / 15–30 min** | 3-mark |
| Global migration limit (EU 10/2011) | mg/dm² | **< 10 mg/dm²** | 14-mark |
| OTR of EVOH (reference) | cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹ | **0.1–3** | 14-mark |
| Erichsen cupping test | mm | **> 5 mm** | 3-mark |
| MWT (melt temperature) of LDPE coating | °C | **110–115°C** | 3-mark |

### 2.2.1 Governing Permeability Equations

The **permeability of a packaging material** to gases/water vapor is fundamental:

$$P = D \cdot S$$

Where:
- $P$ = Permeability coefficient (cm³·cm / cm²·s·cmHg)
- $D$ = Diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $S$ = Solubility coefficient (cm³(STP) / cm³·cmHg)

$$WVTR = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

Where:
- $WVTR$ = Water Vapor Transmission Rate (g/m²·day)
- $\Delta p$ = Partial pressure differential of water vapor across the film
- $L$ = Film/lacquer thickness (m)

**Real-world utility:** Used by packaging engineers to predict **shelf life** of moisture-sensitive foods (crackers, milk powder, dried fruits).

---

## 2.3 Why Lacquers are Mandatory for Metal Food Cans (Engineering Rationale)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:**
> *"Why is tinplate lacquered even though tin is itself corrosion-resistant?"*

The **rationale** is based on the **Electrochemical Series (Galvanic Series)**:

- Tin is **cathodic** relative to steel (Fe) in most food electrolytes
- When **tin layer is scratched** (during can fabrication), exposed steel acts as the **anode** and corrodes rapidly → "iron sulfidation"
- Hence a **lacquer film** is applied as a fail-safe insurance layer

---

## 2.4 Cross-Material Comparison (Engineering Decision Matrix)

| Property | Glass | Aluminium Foil | Tinplate (Steel) | Paperboard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 2.5 | 2.71 | 7.85 | 0.7–1.0 |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 30–100 (brittle) | 70–200 | 300–600 | 30–80 |
| **WVTR** | 0 (impermeable) | 0 (impermeable) | 0 (impermeable) | 50–1000 |
| **OTR (cm³/m²·day)** | 0 | 0–1 | 0–1 | 100–10,000 |
| **Recyclability** | 100% infinite | 100% infinite | 100% infinite | 5–7 cycles |
| **Microwave Safe** | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Arcing risk | ❌ No | ⚠️ Limited |
| **Cost Index (relative)** | Medium | High | Low | Lowest |
| **Transparency** | Excellent | Opaque | Opaque | Opaque |
| **Lacquer Needed?** | Rarely | Yes (acidic foods) | **Mandatory** | Yes (water/grease) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Application:** The Coca-Cola Company uses **aluminium cans with internal epoxy-vinyl lacquer** because the soft-drink pH ≈ 2.5 would otherwise corrode aluminium within weeks. The lacquer extends shelf life to **12+ months** at ambient storage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Material Selection Logic

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Shelf-Life of a Lacquered Tin Can

A KTU 14-mark question often asks to **estimate the shelf life** of a canned food based on the lacquer's oxygen permeability.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given:**
- Can internal volume $V_i$ = 250 cm³
- Headspace oxygen $V_{O_2}$ = 2.5 cm³ (≈ 1% of volume)
- Lacquer film thickness $L$ = **10 µm = 10 × 10⁻⁶ m**
- Internal can surface area $A$ = 250 cm² = 0.025 m²
- Oxygen permeability of epoxy lacquer $P_{O_2}$ = **1.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ cm³·cm / cm²·s·cmHg**
- Outside partial pressure $p_{out}$ ≈ 0 cmHg
- Inside partial pressure $p_{in}$ ≈ 160 cmHg (atmospheric O₂)

**Step 1 — Convert oxygen permeability to standard units:**

$$P_{O_2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-10} \;\frac{cm^3 \cdot cm}{cm^2 \cdot s \cdot cmHg}$$

Converting to SI: multiply cm³ by $4.45 \times 10^{-4}$ to get mol·s, etc. (not required for final ratio).

**Step 2 — Calculate OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate):**

$$OTR = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

$$OTR = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 160}{10 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$OTR = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-3}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \;\frac{cm^3 \cdot cm}{cm^2 \cdot s \cdot cmHg} \text{ (numerical pathway)}$$

**Step 3 — Express in standard packaging units (cm³/m²·day·atm):**

$$OTR_{standard} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{10^4 \, cm^2}{m^2} \times \frac{86400 \, s}{day} \times \frac{76 \, cmHg}{atm}$$

Let us perform the numerical evaluation rigorously:

$$OTR_{standard} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^4 \times 86400 \times 76$$

$$= 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 6.5664 \times 10^{9}$$

$$= 105.06 \;cm^3 / m^2 \cdot day \cdot atm$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total oxygen ingress per day into the can:**

$$Q_{O_2} = OTR_{standard} \times A \times \frac{\Delta p}{760}$$

$$Q_{O_2} = 105.06 \times 0.025 \times \frac{160}{760}$$

$$= 105.06 \times 0.025 \times 0.2105$$

$$= 0.553 \;cm^3 \; O_2 \; / \; day$$

**Step 5 — Calculate shelf life (when all headspace O₂ is consumed):**

$$t_{shelf} = \frac{V_{O_2}}{Q_{O_2}} = \frac{2.5}{0.553}$$

$$t_{shelf} \approx 4.52 \;days$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A real shelf life of **2–5 years** is achieved because (a) the food consumes O₂ rapidly via oxidation then stabilizes, (b) most cans are vacuum-sealed reducing headspace to < 0.5%, and (c) lacquer thickness is often **15–25 µm** in commercial cans, not 10 µm.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Aluminium Foil Pouch — Equivalent Laminate Barrier

For an **Al-foil / PE laminate**, the **permeance** of the multilayer structure follows the **resistance-in-series model**:

$$\frac{1}{P_{total}} = \frac{x_1}{P_1} + \frac{x_2}{P_2} + \cdots + \frac{x_n}{P_n}$$

Where $x_i$ is the thickness and $P_i$ the permeability of each layer $i$.

**Worked Example:**

A **PET (12 µm) / Adhesive / Al-foil (9 µm) / PE (75 µm)** laminate:

| Layer | Thickness (cm) | O₂ Permeability (cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg) |
|---|---|---|
| PET | 1.2 × 10⁻³ | 3.0 × 10⁻¹² |
| Al-foil | 9 × 10⁻⁴ | ≈ 0 (pinhole-free) |
| PE | 7.5 × 10⁻³ | 1.0 × 10⁻⁹ |

Since aluminium foil (pinhole-free) has effectively **zero permeability**:

$$\frac{1}{P_{total}} \approx \frac{1}{P_{PET}} + \frac{1}{P_{PE}}$$

$$P_{total} \approx \frac{P_{PET} \cdot P_{PE}}{P_{PET} + P_{PE}} \approx P_{PET} \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-12}$$

**Conclusion:** The aluminium foil **dominates the barrier**, and the PET/PE layers only contribute marginally. This is why **9 µm aluminium foil** is considered an "absolute" O₂ barrier — it approaches the performance of glass.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Logic — Choosing Between Glass, Aluminium, Tinplate, or Paperboard

**Step 1 — Identify product characteristics:**
- Acidic (pH < 4.5)? → Glass or Lacquered Tinplate
- Carbonated? → Aluminium can or Glass bottle (pressure-resistant)
- Frozen? → Paperboard / Kraft
- Retort (121°C)? → Lacquered Tinplate or Glass jar
- Microwave-ready? → Paperboard / Plastic trays

**Step 2 — Identify barrier requirements:**

$$ \text{Required OTR (cm}^3\text{/m}^2\text{·day·atm)} = \frac{V_{O_2 \, \text{tolerance}}}{A \times t_{shelf}} $$

**Step 3 — Match to material OTR** (refer to Section 2.4 table).

**Step 4 — Apply the "KTU Decision Triangle":**

```
              COST
              /  \
             /    \
            /      \
           /   ?    \
          /__________\
       SHELF LIFE    BARRIER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Principle:** No single packaging material dominates on all three axes. Glass = high barrier + low cost-per-volume; Aluminium = good barrier + premium cost; Tinplate = cost-effective + good barrier; Paperboard = cheap + poor barrier → requires coating.

---

## 3.4 Properties — Advantages & Limitations: Detailed Analytical Matrix

### 3.4.1 **Glass Packaging**

| Property | Advantage ✅ | Limitation ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| Chemical Inertness | Impervious to all foods, no migration | Heavier than plastics |
| Transparency | Premium product visibility | Brittle, shatters on impact |
| Hermetic Seal | Excellent with metal lug caps | Slow production speeds |
| Recyclability | 100% infinite-loop | High energy to manufacture |
| Heat Resistance | Survives retort, autoclave | Thermal shock if cooled rapidly |
| Barrier | Absolute (gas, moisture, odor) | — |
| Flavor Neutrality | No taste transfer | — |

### 3.4.2 **Aluminium and its Foil**

| Property | Advantage ✅ | Limitation ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| Lightweight | 1/3 the weight of steel | Denting/creasing on impact |
| Ductility | Easily laminated (6–9 µm) | Pinhole defects < 9 µm |
| Recyclability | 95% energy savings vs virgin | Reactive to strong acids/alkalis |
| Conductivity | Excellent heat transfer (oven trays) | Cannot be microwaved |
| Barrier | Near-absolute (when pinhole-free) | Lacquer mandatory for acidic foods |
| Printability | Coated surface accepts inks | Surface easily scratched |

### 3.4.3 **Metal Tin Containers (Tinplate / TFS)**

| Property | Advantage ✅ | Limitation ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Strength | Withstands stacking, transport | Heaviest of food metals |
| Hermeticity | Vacuum + retort compatible | Requires lacquer for acidic foods |
| Recyclability | Magnetic separation easy | Tin supply chain (geopolitics) |
| Formability | Draw + iron + weld possible | Welding bay corrosion risk |
| Cost | Lowest metal can cost | — |
| Printability | Excellent after lithography | Tin scratches during forming |

### 3.4.4 **Paper and Paperboard**

| Property | Advantage ✅ | Limitation ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| Renewable | Made from wood pulp (sustainable) | Moisture-sensitive |
| Cost | Lowest-cost food packaging | Poor barrier without coating |
| Printability | Excellent for branding | — |
| Recyclability | 5–7 fiber cycles | Strength drops after each cycle |
| Formability | Folding cartons, corrugated | Cannot be heat-sealed without coating |
| Microwave | Some grades approved | Cannot hold liquid food |
| Stiffness | Good for rigid cartons | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pattern Alert:** When the question asks "compare" the materials, **always tabulate** — never write continuous prose. Tabular answers fetch **+2 bonus marks** in KTU valuation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define packaging lacquers. List any four essential properties of an ideal food-contact lacquer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Packaging lacquers are thin-film organic coatings (typically **5–25 µm** dry thickness) applied on the inner surface of metal or paper packaging substrates to provide a **functional barrier** between the food and the container, preventing corrosion, migration, and flavor transfer.

**Four Essential Properties (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Adhesion** to substrate without flaking during fabrication
2. **Chemical resistance** to acids, salts, and sulfur compounds
3. **Flexibility** to withstand stamping/cupping (Erichsen > **5 mm**)
4. **Heat resistance** to survive retort sterilization (**121°C / 30 min**)
5. **Sensory neutrality** — no taste or odor transfer

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Why is lacquer mandatory on tinplate cans even though tin is corrosion-resistant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tin is **cathodic** to steel in the galvanic series. When the tin layer is scratched during can fabrication (stamping, beading, seaming), the exposed **steel acts as the anode** and corrodes rapidly in the presence of food electrolytes, producing **black iron sulfide staining** and **off-flavors**.

A **lacquer film (5–25 µm)** acts as a fail-safe insurance layer that:
- Physically isolates food from the metal
- Prevents electrochemical corrosion even when tin is damaged
- Blocks sulfur migration in protein-rich foods (meat, fish)

Hence lacquer is mandatory even though tin itself offers sacrificial protection.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never write "aluminium is corrosion-resistant"** — always explain the role of the **passive Al₂O₃ layer** (4 nm).
> 2. **Tabulate** "Advantages vs Limitations" — tabular answers get **+2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Quote numbers** (thickness in µm, density in g/cm³, migration limit in mg/dm²) — students lose marks for qualitative-only answers.
> 4. **Always mention EU 10/2011 or FDA 21 CFR** for food-contact compliance.
> 5. **For tinplate, distinguish** between *electrolytic tinplate (ETP)* and *hot-dipped tinplate (HTP)*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Lacquered Tinplate Focus*

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain in detail the **properties, advantages, and limitations of metal tin containers** as food packaging material. Discuss the role of different lacquers used inside tin cans.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Composition & Structure of Tinplate (2 marks):**

Tinplate = low-carbon steel base (0.10–0.50 mm) + electrolytic tin coating (2.8–11.2 g/m²). The tin layer is **reflowed** (melted & quenched) to form a bright, corrosion-resistant FeSn₂ alloy layer (≈ 0.4 µm thick).

**2. Properties of Tinplate (2 marks):**

| Property | Value/Description |
|---|---|
| Density | **7.85 g/cm³** (steel) |
| Tensile strength | **300–600 MPa** |
| Thickness | **0.10–0.50 mm** |
| Tin coating | **0.4–1.5 µm** per side |
| Magnetic | Yes (enables easy recycling) |
| Formability | Excellent (draw + iron + weld) |

**3. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Highest mechanical strength among food cans
- Excellent hermeticity (vacuum + retort compatible)
- Magnetic — easy recycling via eddy-current separation
- Lithography-friendly surface for branding

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Heaviest among food metals
- Requires lacquer for acidic/sulfur foods
- Tin price volatility

**5. Role of Lacquers (0.5 marks):**
Epoxy, epoxy-phenolic, organosol, polyester, vinyl, acrylic — selected based on food chemistry (pH, sulfur content, retort temperature).

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Describe the **properties, advantages, and limitations of aluminium foil** as a food packaging material. Why is lacquer essential for aluminium packaging of acidic foods?

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. Aluminium Foil — Properties (2 marks):**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Purity | **99.0–99.5%** (food grade) |
| Density | **2.71 g/cm³** |
| Tensile strength | **70–200 MPa** |
| Thickness range | **6–200 µm** |
| Passive oxide layer | **Al₂O₃ ~ 4 nm** |
| Melting point | **660°C** |

**2. Advantages (2 marks):**
- Lightweight (1/3 of steel weight)
- Near-absolute gas/moisture barrier (pinhole-free > 9 µm)
- Excellent formability & dead-fold (retains crease)
- Outstanding heat conductivity (ovenable trays)
- Recyclable with 95% energy savings

**3. Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Cannot be microwaved (arcing risk)
- Acidic foods (pH < 4.5) cause pitting corrosion
- Sulfur-containing foods cause blackening
- Scratches easily; requires protective lacquer
- Hydrogen swelling with carbonated beverages (lactic acid + Al → H₂)

**4. Why Lacquer is Essential for Acidic Foods (1.5 marks):**

Acidic foods (tomato, citrus, pickles — pH < 4.5) attack the **Al₂O₃ passive layer** and expose bare aluminium, triggering **pitting corrosion** and **aluminium ion migration** (toxicity concerns above **5 mg/kg** body weight per week). A **phenolic, epoxy-phenolic, or vinyl lacquer (5–25 µm)** isolates food from metal surface, preventing pitting and extending shelf life from weeks to **12+ months**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Glass & Paperboard Focus*

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Discuss the **properties, advantages, and limitations of glass** as a food packaging material. Mention the types of glass used for food packaging.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Composition of Glass (1 mark):**

| Component | Function | % by weight |
|---|---|---|
| **SiO₂** (silica) | Glass former | **70–74%** |
| **Na₂O** (soda ash) | Flux / lowers melt point | **12–16%** |
| **CaO** (limestone) | Stabilizer | **8–13%** |
| **Al₂O₃** | Chemical durability | **1–3%** |
| **B₂O₃ / PbO** | Speciality | < 1% |

**2. Properties (2 marks):**
- Density: **2.5 g/cm³**
- Amorphous, isotropic, rigid
- Chemically inert — zero migration
- Absolute gas/moisture/odor barrier
- Transparency > 92% in visible range
- Softening point: **~700°C**, annealing at **550–600°C**

**3. Types of Glass for Food Packaging (1.5 marks):**

- **Type I — Borosilicate** (Pyrex): highest chemical resistance, used for baby food, injectable drugs
- **Type II — Soda-Lime (Surface Treated)**: SO₂ treatment reduces alkali leaching, used for wines, infusions
- **Type III — Soda-Lime (Regular)**: most common, used for jams, sauces, beverages
- **Type IV — Soda-Lime (General Purpose)**: for non-aqueous foods (oils, dry goods)

**4. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Premium feel & 100% transparency
- Infinite recyclability (no quality loss)
- Hermetic with metal lug/PT caps
- Microwave & oven safe

**5. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Brittle (shatter on impact)
- Heavy (~ 2.5× plastic)
- High energy to manufacture (~ 1500°C)
- Slow production line speeds

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **properties, advantages, and limitations of paper and paperboard** as food packaging materials. Discuss the role of coatings/lacquers in enhancing paper-based packaging.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**1. Paper vs Paperboard (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Paper | Paperboard |
|---|---|---|
| GSM (g/m²) | **40–250** | **> 250** (typically 200–600) |
| Thickness | 50–300 µm | 350–1000 µm |
| Rigidity | Flexible | Semi-rigid / rigid |
| Typical use | Wraps, bags, sachets | Folding cartons, trays |

**2. Properties of Paper/Paperboard (1.5 marks):**
- Made from **cellulose fibers** (wood pulp), often with additives
- Bursting strength, tear resistance, stiffness proportional to GSM
- Hygroscopic — absorbs moisture from environment
- Smooth, printable surface (clay-coated for lithography)
- Biodegradable & renewable

**3. Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- **Lowest cost** among all primary packaging materials
- Excellent printability for branding
- Sustainable, renewable, biodegradable
- Recyclable 5–7 fiber cycles before strength loss
- Microwave compatible (some grades)

**4. Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Poor moisture barrier (WVTR 50–1000 g/m²·day)
- Poor gas barrier (OTR 100–10,000 cm³/m²·day)
- Cannot hold liquid foods without coating
- Poor grease resistance unless laminated
- Low wet strength — disintegrates in water

**5. Role of Coatings/Lacquers (1.5 marks):**

Paper-based food packaging relies on coatings to overcome its barrier limitations:

| Coating Type | Function | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **PE extrusion (15–30 µm)** | Moisture barrier + heat-seal | Milk cartons, juice pouches |
| **Wax coating** | Moisture + grease barrier | Bread wraps, cheese papers |
| **Acrylic dispersion** | Oil/grease barrier (KIT rating) | Fast-food burger wrappers |
| **Aluminium foil lamination** | Absolute barrier (gas + light + moisture) | Aseptic juice cartons (Tetra Pak) |
| **PVdC or EVOH** | Oxygen barrier | Coffee, dried soup pouches |
| **Clay / mineral pigments** | Printability & opacity | Folding cartons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The **Tetra Pak** carton is an excellent real-world case study — it combines **paperboard (bulk) + PE (sealing) + Al-foil (barrier) + outer PE (moisture protection)** in a 6-layer laminate that enables ambient shelf life of milk for **6+ months** without refrigeration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

### 📌 Lacquers — Quick-Recall Checklist
- **Definition:** 5–25 µm organic film on food-contact surface of metal/glass/paper
- **Mandatory on:** tinplate cans, aluminium foils with acidic foods, paper for liquids
- **Optional on:** glass (only for fragment retention or UV protection)
- **Key tests:** Erichsen cupping > 5 mm; MEK rub; cross-cut adhesion; global migration < **10 mg/dm²**
- **Standard cure:** 200°C / 10–15 min
- **Common types:** Epoxy, Epoxy-Phenolic, Organosol, Polyester, Vinyl, Acrylic, Phenolic

### 📌 Glass — Quick-Recall Checklist
- Composition: SiO₂ (70–74%) + Na₂O + CaO + Al₂O₃
- Density: **2.5 g/cm³**
- **Impermeable to all gases, moisture, and odor**
- 4 types: I (Borosilicate), II (Treated), III (Soda-lime), IV (General purpose)
- Recyclable **infinitely**

### 📌 Aluminium Foil — Quick-Recall Checklist
- Density: **2.71 g/cm³**
- Native oxide Al₂O₃ ≈ **4 nm**
- Foil thickness range: **6–200 µm**
- Pinhole-free barrier requires **≥ 9 µm** thickness
- Avoid microwaving
- Acidic food → must lacquer

### 📌 Metal Tin Containers — Quick-Recall Checklist
- Tinplate = steel (**7.85 g/cm³**) + electrolytic tin (**0.4–1.5 µm**)
- Tin coating weight: **2.8–11.2 g/m²**
- Always lacquered internally for food contact
- Magnetic → recyclable via eddy current
- Three-piece (welded) and two-piece (DWI) construction

### 📌 Paper & Paperboard — Quick-Recall Checklist
- GSM range: paper **40–250**; paperboard **> 250** (200–600)
- Made of cellulose fibers from wood pulp
- Recyclable 5–7 times
- Coatings (PE/wax/PVdC/EVOH/Al) needed for barrier
- Cannot hold liquid food uncoated
- Real-world case: Tetra Pak 6-layer aseptic carton

### 📌 Numerical Values to Memorize 🎯
| Value | Application |
|---|---|
| **10 mg/dm²** | EU global migration limit |
| **5 µm** | Min lacquer thickness for dry film |
| **25 µm** | Max commercial lacquer thickness |
| **9 µm** | Min pinhole-free aluminium foil |
| **0.4 µm** | Min tin coating thickness |
| **2.71 g/cm³** | Aluminium density |
| **7.85 g/cm³** | Steel density |
| **2.5 g/cm³** | Glass density |
| **121°C / 30 min** | Standard retort condition |
| **5 mm** | Erichsen cupping min value |

> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When asked about "packaging materials", always structure your answer as:
> **① Definition + Composition → ② Properties (table) → ③ Advantages → ④ Limitations → ⑤ Real-world application example**. This pattern fetches **full 14 marks** consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition: Active and Intelligent Packaging (The Foundation of Modern Food Preservation)

**Active Packaging (AP)** is defined as a packaging system that deliberately incorporates components designed to **release or absorb substances into/from the packaged food or the surrounding headspace**, thereby extending the shelf-life, maintaining quality, and enhancing sensory attributes — while the *food itself is not considered part of the packaging system*. The European Community (EC) regulation 1935/2004 and 450/2009 legally recognizes this distinction.

**Intelligent Packaging (IP)** is a packaging system that **monitors the condition of packaged food and communicates information about its quality, freshness, and safety** throughout the supply chain, but does not necessarily alter the food's composition. It uses external or internal indicators, sensors, and data carriers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Distinction**
> - **Active** = *does something* (releases O₂ scavengers, absorbs ethylene, emits antimicrobials)
> - **Intelligent** = *tells something* (time-temperature indicators, freshness sensors, RFID tags)
> - **Combined Active-Intelligent (CAI)** = does AND tells (the emerging trend in KTU Module 3)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Smart Refrigerator**
> 
> Imagine a **traditional refrigerator** (passive packaging): it just *holds* the food. The food spoils on its own timeline.
> 
> Now imagine a **smart refrigerator** that:
> 1. **Actively absorbs** the ethylene gas emitted by ripening fruits (Active function) 🍎
> 2. **Displays a color strip** on the door showing whether the milk inside is still fresh (Intelligent function) 🥛
> 3. **Texts your phone** when the internal temperature rises above safe levels (CAI function) 📱
> 
> This is exactly what Active & Intelligent Food Packaging does at the micro-scale of a food package!

### 1.3 Why Module 3 Matters — KTU 2024 Industry Context

| Driver | Quantitative Metric | Industrial Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Global food waste | **~1.3 billion tonnes/year** (UN FAO) | ~$1 trillion economic loss |
| Shelf-life extension demand | **25–400 %** achievable via AP | Reduces cold-chain dependence |
| Consumer shift | **73 %** of millennials prefer "smart labels" | Drives IP market growth |
| KTU Module weightage | **15–20 %** of total marks | High-yield topic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Context for KTU Board Exam**
> The concept of active packaging was commercialized in **Japan in the 1970s** with the "Ageless®" oxygen scavenger (Mitsubishi Gas Chemical). The EU formally regulated it in **2009 (Regulation 450/2009)**. This Japan → EU → Global timeline is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Pillar Framework of Advanced Packaging

Advanced food packaging under KTU Module 3 rests on **two complementary pillars**:

#### **Pillar 1 — Active Packaging (Reactive Systems)**

Active systems are categorized by their **mechanism of action**:

- **A. Absorbers/S scavengers (removing undesirable substances)**
  - Oxygen scavengers (iron-based, ascorbate-based, enzymatic)
  - Ethylene scavengers (KMnO₄ on silica)
  - Moisture absorbers (silica gel, calcium chloride)
  - CO₂ scavengers (calcium hydroxide)
  - Odour/taste removers (activated carbon, zeolites)

- **B. Releasers/Emitters (adding beneficial substances)**
  - CO₂ emitters (for fresh meat MAP extension)
  - Antimicrobial releasers (ethanol, chlorine dioxide, essential oils)
  - Antioxidant releasers (BHA, BHT, α-tocopherol)
  - Flavour emitters

- **C. Other active systems**
  - Temperature regulators (phase change materials)
  - Microwave susceptors (browning/crisping agents)

#### **Pillar 2 — Intelligent Packaging (Informational Systems)**

- **A. Indicators** (visual chemical/biological signals)
  - Time-Temperature Indicators (TTIs)
  - Freshness indicators (pH, H₂S, biogenic amines)
  - Integrity indicators (tamper-evident, leak indicators)

- **B. Sensors** (quantitative measurement devices)
  - Gas sensors (O₂, CO₂ headspace)
  - Biosensors (glucose, lactate detection)
  - Chemical sensors

- **C. Data carriers** (information storage/transmission)
  - RFID (Radio Frequency Identification)
  - QR codes & blockchain traceability
  - Barcodes (1D, 2D)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know for KTU ESE** numerical or derivation questions.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxygen Transmission Rate | $\text{OTR} = \frac{V \cdot dP}{A \cdot t}$ | $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$ | Packaging film barrier property |
| 2 | Water Vapour Transmission Rate | $\text{WVTR} = \frac{m \cdot L}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta P}$ | $\text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$ | Moisture barrier evaluation |
| 3 | Shelf-life extension ratio | $S_{ext} = \frac{t_{\text{active}}}{t_{\text{control}}}$ | Dimensionless | Effectiveness of active system |
| 4 | Oxygen scavenger capacity | $C_{O_2} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{m_{\text{scavenger}}}$ | $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{g}^{-1}$ | Iron-based sachet design |
| 5 | Henry's Law for headspace | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | $\text{mol} \cdot \text{L}^{-1} \cdot \text{atm}^{-1}$ | CO₂/ethylene dissolution in food |
| 6 | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $\text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | Gas permeation through film |
| 7 | Activation Energy (Arrhenius) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $k$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$ | TTI reaction kinetics |
| 8 | Q₁₀ Rule for spoilage | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Dimensionless | TTI calibration |

### 2.3 Operational Working — Step-by-Step Logic

**For an Oxygen Scavenger Sachet (the most common AP system):**

1. **Trigger phase**: Iron powder + moisture + oxygen initiate the redox reaction
2. **Reaction phase**: Iron is oxidized in the presence of salt electrolyte (NaCl) as catalyst
3. **Capacity phase**: Each gram of iron theoretically consumes **~300 cm³ of O₂**
4. **Equilibrium phase**: Headspace O₂ drops below **0.01 %**, inhibiting aerobic spoilage
5. **Termination phase**: Reaction self-terminates when all iron is oxidized

**For a Time-Temperature Indicator (TTI — the most common IP system):**

1. **Diffusion phase**: A colored ester diffuses through a polymer medium
2. **Rate phase**: Diffusion rate is **temperature-dependent** (Arrhenius)
3. **Indication phase**: Color change correlates with cumulative thermal abuse
4. **Decision phase**: Consumer reads color = "still safe" or "discard"
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Oxygen Scavenger Capacity (Iron-Based System)

The chemical reaction governing iron-based oxygen scavengers is:

$$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

$$\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^{-}$$

**Overall redox reaction:**

$$4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3$$

**Step 1 — Moles of O₂ consumed per mole of Fe**

From stoichiometry: 4 mol Fe reacts with 3 mol O₂

$$\text{Moles ratio} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{n_{Fe}} = \frac{3}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Mass-based capacity calculation**

- Molar mass of Fe = **55.85 g/mol**
- At STP, 1 mol O₂ occupies **22,400 cm³ (22.4 L)**

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{55.85 \text{ g}} \times 22{,}400 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{3 \times 22{,}400}{4 \times 55.85}$$

$$\text{Capacity} = \frac{67{,}200}{223.4}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Capacity} \approx 300.8 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ O}_2 \text{ per gram of Fe}}$$

**Engineering Insight**: In practice, only **60–80 %** of theoretical capacity is realized due to:
- Incomplete Fe oxidation (passivation layer of Fe(OH)₃)
- Mass transfer limitations across sachet membrane
- Non-uniform moisture distribution

Therefore, the **practical scavenging capacity ≈ 180–240 cm³ O₂/g Fe**, which is the value KTU exam answers should quote.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of AP vs IP

| Parameter | Active Packaging | Intelligent Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary function** | *Acts* on the food environment | *Reports* on the food status |
| **Energy requirement** | Generally passive (chemical) | May require power (RFID, sensors) |
| **Regulatory status (EU)** | Regulation 450/2009 | No specific regulation yet |
| **Cost addition** | **5–15 %** over conventional | **10–30 %** over conventional |
| **Shelf-life impact** | **+ 25 to 400 %** | **0 %** (informational only) |
| **Consumer interaction** | Indirect (no action needed) | Direct (read the indicator) |
| **KTU module relevance** | Module 3 (this module) | Module 3 + Module 4 (sensors) |
| **Example commercial** | Ageless® (O₂), Peaksfresh® (ethylene) | FreshTag® (TTI), CoolVu® |
| **Failure mode** | Exhaustion of active component | Sensor degradation, false reading |

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A 250 mL ketchup bottle has a headspace of 15 mL filled with air (21 % O₂). Calculate the minimum mass of iron-based scavenger required to reduce headspace O₂ to < 0.1 %.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial O₂ volume in headspace**

$$V_{O_2, \text{initial}} = 0.21 \times 15 \text{ mL} = 3.15 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2 — Target O₂ volume**

$$V_{O_2, \text{target}} = 0.001 \times 15 \text{ mL} = 0.015 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 3 — O₂ to be scavenged**

$$V_{O_2, \text{scav}} = 3.15 - 0.015 = 3.135 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to cm³** (1 mL = 1 cm³)

$$V_{O_2, \text{scav}} = 3.135 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Apply practical capacity (200 cm³ O₂/g Fe)**

$$m_{\text{Fe}} = \frac{3.135 \text{ cm}^3}{200 \text{ cm}^3 / \text{g}}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{Fe}} \approx 0.0157 \text{ g} = 15.7 \text{ mg}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Always convert mL ↔ cm³ and use the **practical** capacity (~200 cm³/g), not the theoretical (~300 cm³/g). Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
