## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT635]**

**Differentiate between Active Packaging and Intelligent Packaging with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active Packaging | Intelligent Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Releases or absorbs substances to extend shelf-life | Monitors and communicates quality information |
| **Mode** | *Reactive* — alters internal package environment | *Informational* — does not alter the product |
| **Example** | Oxygen scavenger sachet (Ageless®) removing O₂ from headspace | Time-Temperature Indicator (FreshTag®) showing cumulative thermal history |
| **Outcome** | Physically slows spoilage | Informs consumer/retailer about actual status |
| **Regulation** | EU 450/2009 regulated | Generally unregulated (but follows EU 1935/2004 framework) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write that intelligent packaging *extends* shelf-life. It does NOT — it only *reports* on it. Marks are lost for this conceptual error.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEFTT635]**

**Explain the principle of an iron-based oxygen scavenger sachet. Write the overall reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

Iron-based oxygen scavengers work on the principle of **corrosion chemistry**, where finely divided iron powder (often with salt catalyst and moisture) reacts with oxygen trapped in the package headspace.

**Overall Reaction:**

$$4\text{Fe}_{(s)} + 3\text{O}_{2(g)} + 6\text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_{3(s)}$$

**Mechanism in three steps:**

1. **Anode (Oxidation):** $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$
2. **Cathode (Reduction):** $\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^{-}$
3. **Precipitation:** $\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_3$

The **theoretical capacity is 300 cm³ O₂ per gram of iron**, but practical capacity is **180–240 cm³/g** due to passivation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write the reaction as $4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ (rust). This is **incomplete** — water is essential as a reactant and is rarely mentioned. Examiners specifically look for the inclusion of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT635, Module 3] — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various types of Active Packaging systems. Describe in detail the working principle of **ethylene scavengers** and **antimicrobial releasing systems** with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a **Time-Temperature Indicator (TTI)**. Discuss its Arrhenius-based calibration and industrial applications.

**Model Answer (a) — Classification of Active Packaging:**

Active packaging is classified into three main categories:

1. **Absorbers (Removers)** — remove undesirable substances from package headspace:
   - Oxygen scavengers (e.g., Ageless® — Mitsubishi)
   - Ethylene scavengers (e.g., Peakfresh® — for climacteric fruits)
   - Moisture absorbers (e.g., silica gel, calcium oxide)
   - CO₂ scavengers (calcium hydroxide)
   - Odour absorbers (activated carbon)

2. **Releasers (Emitters)** — release beneficial substances:
   - Antimicrobial releasers (ethanol, chlorine dioxide, essential oils)
   - Antioxidant releasers (BHT, α-tocopherol)
   - CO₂ emitters (sodium bicarbonate + citric acid)
   - Flavour emitters

3. **Other Active Systems** — temperature control, susceptors, etc.

**Ethylene Scavenger Working Principle:**

Ethylene (C₂H₄) is a plant hormone that accelerates ripening in climacteric fruits like banana, apple, tomato. The most common commercial scavenger uses **potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** supported on an inert carrier (silica gel or alumina).

**Reaction:**

$$3\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 12\text{KMnO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 12\text{MnO}_2 + 12\text{KOH} + 6\text{CO}_2$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Ethylene is oxidized by permanganate (Mn⁷⁺ → Mn⁴⁺, color change from purple to brown)
- The reaction is irreversible and proceeds at ambient temperatures
- The colour change itself acts as an *integrated indicator* (a CAI feature)
- Typical scavengers contain **5–10 % KMnO₄** on silica
- Capacity: ~7–10 mL C₂H₄ per gram of scavenger

**Antimicrobial Releasing Systems Working Principle:**

These release antimicrobial agents in a controlled manner to inhibit surface microbial growth:

- **Ethanol emitters** (e.g., Ethicap® — Nippon Kayaku, Japan): Releases ethanol vapour which denatures microbial proteins. Used in bakery, dried fish, cheese.
- **Chlorine dioxide (ClO₂) emitters** (e.g., MicroActive®, Tristel): Broad-spectrum antimicrobial gas.
- **Essential oil releasers** (thymol, carvacrol, cinnamaldehyde): Embedded in cyclodextrins or chitosan films for GRAS-grade controlled release.

**Fick's law governs release rate:**

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

Where J is the mass flux, D is diffusivity, and ∂C/∂x is the concentration gradient through the polymer matrix.

**Model Answer (b) — Time-Temperature Indicator (TTI):**

**Definition:** A TTI is an inexpensive, irreversible device attached to or printed on a package that integrates the cumulative time-temperature history of the food and visually indicates whether the product has experienced thermal abuse beyond safe limits.

**Schematic (refer to Mermaid 4.3 above):**

$$\boxed{\text{Food Storage Temperature} \rightarrow \text{TTI Internal Reaction} \rightarrow \text{Color Change} \rightarrow \text{Decision by Consumer}}$$

**Three main TTI types:**

1. **Diffusion-based TTI** (e.g., FreshTag® by Temptime, formerly Lifelines):
   - A colored fatty acid ester diffuses through a polymer medium
   - Rate of diffusion follows Arrhenius kinetics
   - Color bar length = cumulative thermal history

2. **Enzymatic TTI** (e.g., CheckPoint® by Vitsab):
   - Lipase enzyme hydrolyses a lipid substrate
   - pH change causes colour shift (yellow → blue)
   - Highly precise, ±0.5 °C accuracy

3. **Microbial TTI** (e.g., TRACEO®):
   - Uses live bacterial spores whose metabolism produces colour
   - Mirrors actual food spoilage kinetics (best correlation)

**Arrhenius Calibration:**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $k$ = reaction rate constant
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Industrial Applications:**
- Frozen food (ice cream, frozen meat) → diffusion TTI
- Chilled dairy, fresh-cut produce → enzymatic TTI
- Vaccine cold-chain monitoring → electronic TTI (RFID-integrated)
- Seafood export shipments → microbial TTI

---

**Q3(b). [Alternative Question for Internal Choice – 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP). Explain its difference from Active Packaging. Discuss the gas composition used for fresh meat, fresh produce, and bakery products.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write notes on: (i) Self-heating and self-cooling packages, (ii) Microwave susceptor technology. Mention their working principles and at least one commercial example each.

**Model Answer (a) — MAP Definition & Gas Compositions:**

**Definition:** Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) is the replacement of the air inside a package with a **specific gas mixture** (typically N₂, O₂, CO₂) to slow the chemical, enzymatic, and microbial deterioration of food. The composition is **fixed at the time of sealing** — it does not change dynamically.

**Difference from Active Packaging:**

| Parameter | MAP | Active Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Set at sealing; passive | Continuously changes during storage |
| **Mode** | One-time modification | Dynamic equilibrium or sustained release |
| **Mechanism** | Physical displacement of air | Chemical/biological interaction |
| **Regulatory class** | Conventional (not classified as "active") | EU Reg 450/2009 (legally distinct) |

**Gas Compositions for KTU Board Reference:**

| Food Type | O₂ (%) | CO₂ (%) | N₂ (%) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fresh red meat** | 70–80 | 20–30 | 0 | High O₂ maintains bright red oxymyoglobin |
| **Fresh poultry/fish** | 0 | 30–60 | 40–70 | Inhibits aerobic spoilage |
| **Fresh-cut vegetables** | 2–5 | 5–10 | 85–93 | Low O₂ prevents browning |
| **Bakery (mould-prone)** | 0 | 50–100 | 0–50 | CO₂ inhibits mould growth |
| **Cheese** | 0 | 30–100 | Balance | Inhibit mould and anaerobic pathogens |
| **Coffee/Roasted nuts** | 0 | 0 | 100 | N₂ prevents oxidation |

**Model Answer (b) — Self-heating, Self-cooling, Microwave Susceptors:**

**(i) Self-Heating & Self-Cooling Packages:**

- **Principle:** An exothermic (or endothermic) chemical reaction is initiated by the consumer pressing a button, which mixes two separated compartments.
- **Self-Heating Reaction:** Quicklime (CaO) + Water → Ca(OH)₂ + Heat (approx. **64 kcal/100 g of CaO**).
- **Self-Cooling Reaction:** Ammonium nitrate + Water dissolution absorbs heat (endothermic, ΔH = +26 kJ/mol).
- **Commercial Example:** Nestlé *Hot Pouch* self-heating coffee (Japan, 2001); self-cooling beverage cans by Tempra Technology (USA).

**(ii) Microwave Susceptor Technology:**

- **Principle:** A thin metallized polyester film (aluminized, <100 Å thickness) is laminated to paperboard. Under microwave irradiation, the film does NOT reflect (unlike foil) but **absorbs** microwaves, heating up to **200–250 °C** within seconds.
- **Effect:** Browning, crisping, and Maillard reaction occur on the food surface (e.g., pizza crust, french fries).
- **Commercial Example:** Kraft's *MicroCrisp®* susceptor for microwave popcorn and pizza.
- **Safety note:** Must NOT be metallic — only thin-film (semi-transparent to microwaves, partially absorptive).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 3:**
> 1. **Never confuse MAP with Active Packaging.** MAP is a *one-time* headspace modification; AP is a *dynamic* interactive system. This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction if confused.
> 2. **Self-heating packages generate heat — they are NOT microwave susceptors.** The latter requires a *separate* electromagnetic field (microwave oven).
> 3. **Mention EU regulation numbers** (450/2009 for AP, 1935/2004 for food contact materials) wherever you write about active packaging — examiners reward regulatory awareness with 1 bonus mark per question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Definition distinction** — AP *acts*, IP *tells*, CAI *does both* (write this verbatim in 3-mark Q)
- ✅ **Iron-oxygen reaction** — $4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3$ (must include $\text{H}_2\text{O}$)
- ✅ **Theoretical scavenging capacity** = 300 cm³ O₂/g Fe ; **Practical** = 180–240 cm³/g
- ✅ **KMnO₄ reaction** for ethylene scavenging — colour change **purple → brown** (Mn⁷⁺ → Mn⁴⁺)
- ✅ **MAP gas tables** — memorize red meat (high O₂), poultry/fish (high CO₂), bakery (high CO₂)
- ✅ **Three TTI types** — diffusion (FreshTag), enzymatic (CheckPoint), microbial (TRACEO)
- ✅ **Arrhenius equation** — $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — must appear in TTI derivation
- ✅ **EU Regulations** — 1935/2004 (general food contact) and 450/2009 (active & intelligent)
- ✅ **Commercial brands** — Ageless® (O₂), Peakfresh® (ethylene), FreshTag® (TTI)
- ✅ **Historical timeline** — 1970s Japan origin → 2009 EU regulation → 2024 AI-CAI
- ✅ **Self-heating = CaO + H₂O** (exothermic) ; **Self-cooling = NH₄NO₃ + H₂O** (endothermic)
- ✅ **Microwave susceptor** = thin metallized film (aluminized, <100 Å), NOT foil
- ✅ **Fick's First Law** — $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ — for any mass-transfer derivation
- ✅ **Q₁₀ rule** — used to relate spoilage rates at different storage temperatures

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "OAKS" for AP Absorbers:**
> **O**xygen · **A**bsorbers · **K**MnO₄ (ethylene) · **S**ilica gel (moisture)
> 
> **Mnemonic — "CARF" for AP Releasers:**
> **C**O₂ · **A**ntimicrobial · **R**eleaser of antioxidants · **F**lavour

**End of Module 3 — Introduction Notes**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Packaging (AP)** is defined as a packaging system in which the package, the product, and the internal environment interact deliberately to extend the shelf-life, improve sensory properties, maintain nutritional quality, or ensure microbial safety of the food product — *beyond the passive barrier properties of the conventional package*.

According to the **European Regulation (EC) No 1935/2004** and **EU Regulation 450/2009**, active packaging materials are defined as:

> *"Materials and articles that are intended to extend the shelf-life of, or to maintain or improve, the condition of, packed food by deliberately incorporating components that would release or absorb substances into or from the packed food or the environment surrounding the food."*

These materials are classified as **"active materials and articles"** under food contact materials legislation, distinguishing them clearly from conventional "passive" packaging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In *passive packaging*, the package only acts as a barrier. In *active packaging*, the package **actively** interacts with the product to perform a function beyond containment (e.g., scavenging O₂, emitting CO₂, releasing antimicrobials).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine packing a freshly baked bread loaf in a simple plastic bag. Over a few days, the bread becomes stale and may grow mold because:
- **Oxygen** in the bag accelerates fat oxidation and supports mold growth.
- **Moisture** trapped inside creates a humid microclimate ideal for microbial proliferation.
- The bag does **nothing** to fight these changes — it just sits there.

Now imagine slipping a small **sachet containing iron powder** inside the same bag. The iron *actively* reacts with the oxygen:

$$4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3$$

The oxygen is chemically consumed. The bread stays fresher for weeks. The sachet is doing work — *that is active packaging*.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of active packaging as giving the package a **"set of lungs"** or **"tiny organs"**. Just as our bodies actively regulate oxygen, CO₂, and moisture, an active package regulates the headspace atmosphere to keep food alive and safe longer.

---

## 1.3 Core Functions Summarized

The four pillar functions of active packaging are:

1. **Scavenging** — removing unwanted substances (O₂, CO₂, ethylene, moisture, off-odors).
2. **Emitting / Releasing** — delivering beneficial substances (CO₂, ethanol, antimicrobials, antioxidants, flavors).
3. **Absorbing** — controlling moisture or taints.
4. **Regulating Temperature** — phase-change materials, self-heating/cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shelf-life extension achieved through active packaging is **complementary** to, not a replacement for, **Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)**, **HACCP**, and **cold-chain logistics**.

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Active System | Primary Function | Typical Active Agent |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen Scavenger | Removes O₂ from headspace | Iron-based, ascorbate, enzymatic |
| CO₂ Scavenger / Emitter | Controls CO₂ level | Calcium hydroxide / sodium bicarbonate |
| Ethanol Emitter | Releases ethanol vapour | Encapsulated ethanol on silica |
| Moisture Absorber | Lowers a_w inside pack | Silica gel, molecular sieves, salt |
| Antimicrobial (AM) | Inhibits microbes | Ag-zeolites, essential oils, nisin |
| Antioxidant | Slows lipid oxidation | BHT, tocopherols, green tea extract |
| Ethylene Scavenger | Removes ripening gas | KMnO₄, activated carbon, TiO₂ |
| Flavour/Odour Absorber | Removes off-odours | Activated carbon, cyclodextrins |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation

The active package can be conceptualized as a **mass-transfer reactor** sandwiched between the food product and the external environment. Its operation can be broken down into four sequential engineering steps:

1. **Recognition / Trigger** — A stimulus exists in the headspace (e.g., excess O₂, microbial growth, moisture).
2. **Activation** — The active agent (sachet, film, coating, label) is triggered by that stimulus.
3. **Action** — A chemical/physical reaction occurs (oxidation of iron, release of ethanol, absorption of water).
4. **Termination** — The reaction reaches equilibrium or the agent is exhausted, signalling the end of shelf-life.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Major Active Systems

### A. Oxygen Scavengers

**Oxygen scavengers** are the most commercially successful active packaging technology. The dominant mechanism is **iron-based oxidation**:

$$4\text{Fe}_{(s)} + 3\text{O}_{2(g)} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_{3(s)}$$

**Engineering Logic:**
- Iron powder is the active ingredient.
- A salt (e.g., **NaCl**) acts as an electrolyte/catalyst.
- Moisture present in the food equilibrates into the sachet and initiates the corrosion reaction.
- The reaction **rate is diffusion-limited** by O₂ permeation through the sachet wall.

**Alternative oxygen scavenger chemistries:**

| Chemistry | Reaction Type | Speed |
|---|---|---|
| Iron-based (Fe°) | Oxidation | Days to weeks |
| Ascorbic acid (Vit C) | Auto-oxidation | Hours |
| Enzymatic (glucose oxidase) | Catalytic | Hours |
| Unsaturated hydrocarbons (oxidizable polymer) | Oxidation | Weeks |
| Palladium-based | Catalytic (H₂ + O₂ → H₂O) | Very fast |

**Key Design Equation — Oxygen Scavenging Capacity:**

$$\text{Scavenging Capacity (mL O}_2\text{)} = \frac{m_{\text{Fe}} \times 1000 \times 3}{4 \times M_{\text{Fe}} \times \rho_{\text{O}_2}}$$

Where:
- $m_{\text{Fe}}$ = mass of iron (g)
- $M_{\text{Fe}} = 55.85 \text{ g/mol}$
- $\rho_{\text{O}_2} = 1.429 \text{ g/L}$ at **STP (273.15 K, 1 atm)**

---

### B. Carbon Dioxide Scavengers and Emitters

**CO₂ Scavenging (excess CO₂ removal — e.g., roasted coffee):**

$$\text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**CO₂ Emitting (modified atmosphere maintenance — e.g., red meat, cheese):**

$$\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why emit CO₂ for meat?** Elevated CO₂ (20–80%) suppresses *Pseudomonas* and *Brochothrix thermosphacta* growth, extending the chilled shelf-life of fresh red meat from ~3 days to ~10 days.

---

### C. Ethanol Emitters

Ethanol vapour is a broad-spectrum antimicrobial. The sachet typically contains **ethanol adsorbed on silica powder** (60–90% w/w ethanol).

**Vapour-pressure driven release:**

$$P_{\text{EtOH}} = P^0_{\text{EtOH}} \cdot x_{\text{EtOH}} \cdot \gamma_{\text{EtOH}}$$

Where $\gamma$ is the activity coefficient in the carrier. In practice, packaging engineers control release by:
- Varying the **permeability of the sachet film**.
- Adjusting the **ethanol/silica ratio**.

**Typical application:** Japanese *sake* cakes, semi-dry fish, baked goods — where a target headspace ethanol concentration of **1–3% v/v** inhibits surface moulds.

---

### D. Moisture Absorbers (Desiccants)

**Moisture activity ($a_w$)** is the master variable for microbial safety:

$$a_w = \frac{P_{\text{headspace}}}{P^*_{\text{water}}(T)}$$

| $a_w$ Range | Microbial Inhibition |
|---|---|
| $> 0.91$ | Most bacteria, yeasts, moulds grow |
| $0.86 - 0.91$ | Most bacteria inhibited |
| $0.70 - 0.86$ | Only xerophilic moulds grow |
| $< 0.60$ | Microbial growth ceases |

**Common absorbents and their capacities:**

| Absorbent | Capacity (g H₂O / g) | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Silica gel | 0.3 – 0.4 | Physical adsorption in pores |
| Molecular sieve 4A | 0.20 – 0.25 | Size-exclusion adsorption |
| NaCl (drip pad) | Dissolves in exudate | Osmotic capture |
| CaCl₂ | ~0.75 | Hygroscopic salt |
| Polyacrylate (superabsorbent) | up to 300 | Polymer swelling |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *moisture content* (g H₂O / 100 g food) with *water activity $a_w$*. They are **not** the same. Microbial safety is governed by $a_w$, not total moisture.

---

### E. Antimicrobial Packaging

The agent is delivered into the headspace or onto the food surface via:
- **Migration from a coating or film matrix** (Fickian diffusion).
- **Volatilization** (e.g., essential oils: thymol, carvacrol, cinnamaldehyde).
- **Immobilization on packaging surface** (contact-killing, e.g., Ag-zeolites, chitosan).

**Migration kinetics (Fick's Second Law, 1D, semi-infinite slab):**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Analytical solution (constant surface concentration, $C_s$):**

$$C(x, t) = C_s \left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sherwood number** quantifies mass transfer from packaging to food. KTU 2024 expects you to know the dimensionless mass-transfer parameters: Sherwood (Sh), Schmidt (Sc), Reynolds (Re).

$$\text{Sh} = \frac{k \cdot L}{D}, \quad \text{Sc} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D}, \quad \text{Re} = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

---

### F. Ethylene Scavengers

Ethylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$, ripening hormone) is removed primarily by **oxidation on potassium permanganate**:

$$3\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 2\text{KMnO}_4 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + 2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{KOH}$$

Applied for climacteric fruits: banana, tomato, mango, kiwifruit.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Active Packaging.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Iron-oxygen scavenger stoichiometry | $4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3$ | Fe moles, O₂ moles | mol |
| 2 | Oxygen scavenging capacity per gram Fe | $V_{\text{O}_2} = \dfrac{3 \times 22.4}{4 \times 55.85} \approx 0.30 \text{ L/g}$ | STP molar volume = 22.4 L | L O₂ / g Fe |
| 3 | Water activity | $a_w = P / P^*(T)$ | $P$: partial pressure; $P^*$: sat. vapour pressure | dimensionless |
| 4 | Fick's 2nd law (1D) | $\partial C / \partial t = D \, \partial^2 C / \partial x^2$ | $D$: diffusivity | m² / s |
| 5 | Migration from constant surface | $C(x,t) = C_s\!\left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\right]$ | erf: error function | varies |
| 6 | Sherwood number | $\text{Sh} = kL / D$ | $k$: mass transfer coeff. | dimensionless |
| 7 | CO₂ emission from bicarbonate | $\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$ | Acid acts as trigger | mol CO₂ |
| 8 | Permeation through sachet wall | $J = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{l}$ | $P$: permeability; $A$: area; $l$: thickness | mol / s |
| 9 | Permeability (general) | $P = D \cdot S$ | $S$: solubility coeff. | m² / s · (mol/m³·Pa) |
| 10 | Ethanol headspace concentration | $C_{EtOH}(\%v/v) = \dfrac{n_{EtOH} \cdot V_m^{-1}}{V_{headspace}} \times 100$ | $V_m$: molar volume | % v/v |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion tip:** 1 mol O₂ at STP = 22.4 L = 32 g. 1 mol CO₂ at STP = 22.4 L. Memorize these for KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry:** Sliced bread, cured meats, cheese, fresh produce, ready-meals.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Oxygen-sensitive drugs, diagnostic strips.
- **Cosmetics & Electronics:** Corrosion-sensitive components, moisture-sensitive ICs.
- **Logistics & Defence:** MREs (Meals Ready-to-Eat), space food (NASA uses O₂ scavengers in thermostabilized pouches).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Design Insight:** When answering design questions, always state whether the active agent is *incorporated into the polymer matrix*, *co-extruded as a separate layer*, *applied as a coating*, or *placed as a loose sachet inside the package*. Each method has different regulatory and migration implications.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Oxygen Scavenging Capacity of an Iron Sachet

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A commercial oxygen scavenger sachet contains **5.0 g of iron powder**. Calculate (a) the theoretical maximum volume of oxygen (in mL at STP) the sachet can consume, and (b) the time required to reduce the headspace O₂ from 21% to < 0.1% in a **500 mL** package initially at atmospheric pressure.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation.**

$$4\text{Fe}_{(s)} + 3\text{O}_{2(g)} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_{3(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate moles of iron.**

$$n_{\text{Fe}} = \frac{m_{\text{Fe}}}{M_{\text{Fe}}} = \frac{5.0 \text{ g}}{55.845 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.08954 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Apply stoichiometry (4 mol Fe : 3 mol O₂).**

$$n_{\text{O}_2} = n_{\text{Fe}} \times \frac{3}{4} = 0.08954 \times 0.75 = 0.06716 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Convert moles of O₂ to volume at STP.**

$$V_{\text{O}_2} = n_{\text{O}_2} \times V_m = 0.06716 \times 22{,}400 \text{ mL/mol} = 1504.4 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical maximum oxygen removed.**

$$\boxed{V_{\text{O}_2,\,\max} \approx 1504 \text{ mL} \approx 1.5 \text{ L}}$$

**Step 6 — Oxygen present in the 500 mL headspace at 21% (v/v).**

$$V_{\text{O}_2,\,\text{headspace}} = 0.21 \times 500 \text{ mL} = 105 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 7 — Compare and conclude on excess capacity.**

Since the sachet can consume **1504 mL** but only **105 mL** is present, the sachet has a massive (~14×) over-capacity. This is **deliberate** by design because:
- The sachet must also scavenge O₂ permeating in over the product's shelf life.
- Side reactions (e.g., direct rusting) consume some iron.
- Manufacturers target a **safety factor** of ~3–10×.

**Step 8 — Rate estimate.** Empirical scavenging rate of commercial Fe-based sachets: **~50 mL O₂ per sachet per day** (at 25 °C, 75% RH).

$$t \approx \frac{105 \text{ mL}}{50 \text{ mL/day}} \approx 2.1 \text{ days}$$

However, in practice diffusion-limited kinetics lead to asymptotic behaviour:

$$C_{\text{O}_2}(t) = C_0 \exp(-k t)$$

with $k \approx 0.5$ /day for a 5 g sachet at 25 °C:

$$t_{99.9\%} = \frac{-\ln(0.001)}{k} = \frac{6.91}{0.5} = 13.8 \text{ days}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass of Ascorbic Acid Needed as Oxygen Scavenger

**Reaction (auto-oxidation of ascorbic acid):**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_8\text{O}_6 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6\text{O}_6 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 1 — Moles of O₂ to be removed** (assume 200 mL of O₂ in headspace):

$$n_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{0.200 \text{ L}}{22.4 \text{ L/mol}} = 8.93 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry 1 mol ascorbate : 0.5 mol O₂:**

$$n_{\text{asc}} = 2 \times n_{\text{O}_2} = 1.786 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass required** ($M_{\text{asc}} = 176.12 \text{ g/mol}$):

$$m_{\text{asc}} = 1.786 \times 10^{-2} \times 176.12 = 3.146 \text{ g}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{asc}} \approx 3.15 \text{ g of ascorbic acid}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Water Activity Equilibrium for a Moisture Absorber

**Scenario:** A dried fruit has initial $a_w = 0.75$. Silica gel (5 g, capacity 0.35 g H₂O/g) is added to a 250 mL headspace at 25 °C. Saturated vapour pressure of water at 25 °C: $P^*(25°C) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$.

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of water in headspace initially:**

$$P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}, i} = a_w \times P^* = 0.75 \times 3.169 = 2.377 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of water vapour in the headspace (ideal gas):**

Using $n = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$ with $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $T = 298.15$ K:

$$n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \frac{2377 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}}{8.314 \times 298.15} = 2.40 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}, \text{head}} = 2.40 \times 10^{-4} \times 18.015 = 4.32 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g} = 4.32 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 3 — Silica gel capacity:**

$$m_{\text{cap}} = 5 \text{ g} \times 0.35 = 1.75 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Compare:** Headspace contains only **4.32 mg** of water; silica gel can absorb **1.75 g**. Silica is vastly over-designed for the *headspace* — but it must also buffer water *migrating from the food* over shelf life.

$$\boxed{\text{Silica gel capacity} \gg \text{headspace water} \rightarrow \text{design is safe}}$$

**Step 5 — Final $a_w$ after equilibration** (assuming food releases negligible extra water):

$$a_w \rightarrow \frac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O, final}}}{P^*} \rightarrow 0 \text{ (effectively)}$$

In practice, food releases water until $a_w$ equilibrium is reached; the design ensures the food's critical $a_w$ is not exceeded.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Migration and Shelf-Life Estimation

**Problem:** A PLA film (thickness 50 µm) contains 2% w/w thymol. Film density = 1.24 g/cm³. Thymol diffusivity in PLA at 25 °C = $3 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the surface concentration after 30 days (assuming infinite reservoir of thymol in film).

**Step 1 — Compute characteristic diffusion length:**

$$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{3 \times 10^{-13} \times 30 \times 24 \times 3600} = \sqrt{7.78 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.82 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply semi-infinite solution at film surface (x = 0):**

$$C(0, t) = C_s \left[1 - \text{erf}(0)\right] = C_s \times 1 = C_s$$

Surface remains saturated with thymol for this timescale. This is the **constant-surface boundary condition** assumed for active packaging.

**Step 3 — Concentration at depth 25 µm (mid-film):**

$$z = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 8.82 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0142$$

$$\text{erf}(0.0142) \approx 0.016$$

$$C(25\,\mu\text{m}, 30\,\text{d}) = C_s(1 - 0.016) = 0.984 \, C_s$$

**Step 4 — Conclude:** After 30 days, even the mid-film concentration is still 98% of $C_s$. The film acts as a long-duration thymol reservoir — desirable for AM packaging.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix of Active Systems

| Property | O₂ Scavenger | CO₂ Emitter | Ethanol Emitter | Moisture Absorber | Antimicrobial Film |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Chemical oxidation | Acid-base reaction | Volatilization | Hygroscopic capture | Diffusion / contact |
| Speed | Hours – Days | Hours | Days | Hours | Days – Weeks |
| Capacity (typical) | 50–1000 mL O₂/sachet | 100–500 mL CO₂ | 1–3% v/v headspace | 0.3–0.7 g H₂O/g | 1–100 mg agent/g polymer |
| Trigger | O₂ + H₂O | H⁺ activator | Temperature, RH | RH gradient | Microbial contact |
| Commercial use | Very high | High | Medium | Very high | Rising |
| Regulatory complexity | High (EU 450/2009) | Medium | Medium | Low | Very high |
