> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 ESE questions frequently test the student's ability to compute shelf-life, OTR, and to identify the correct polymer for a given frozen food application. Memorise the units and the order of magnitude of the values.

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Value for Frozen Foods | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Temperature** | Standard commercial frozen storage | **−18** | °C |
| **Quick-freezing temperature** | Blast / cryogenic freezing | −35 to −196 | °C |
| **OTR (target for fatty foods)** | $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1} \cdot \text{atm}^{-1}$ | **< 10** | cm³/m²·day·atm |
| **WVTR (target)** | $\text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$ | **< 5** | g/m²·day |
| **Freezer burn onset time** | $t_{fb} \propto \dfrac{m_{water}}{WVTR \cdot A}$ | Depends on package | days |
| **Heat seal strength** | Force per unit width | **> 15** | N / 15 mm |
| **Burst strength (vacuum pack)** | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{yield}}{D}$ | 50–100 | kPa |
| **Glass Transition (LDPE)** | Empirical | **−110** | °C |
| **Glass Transition (PET)** | Empirical | **+70** | °C |
| **Cold Crack Resistance** | ASTM D1790 | Must pass at −30 | °C |

> **Notation Key:** $\sigma_{yield}$ = tensile yield stress of the laminate, $t$ = laminate thickness, $D$ = package diameter (for cylindrical/rigid packs).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles learned in frozen food packaging have direct cross-industry applications:

- **Pharmaceutical Cold Chain:** Vaccine vials, biotherapeutics stored at −70 °C use the same multilayer barrier logic.
- **Aerospace:** Spacecraft thermal insulation follows similar principles of vapor-barrier protection.
- **Civil Engineering:** Cold-climate building insulation uses vapour barriers to prevent condensation — same physics, different scale.
- **Cryogenic Storage:** LNG transport, biological sample preservation — all rely on extreme-low-temperature packaging principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Modelling Freezer Burn Onset Time

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numericals):** Compute the time required for the onset of visible freezer burn on a frozen fish fillet, given a known WVTR of the packaging film.

### Step 1 — Identify Governing Physical Process

Freezer burn occurs when surface ice **sublimates** and the resulting water vapor escapes through the packaging film. The rate of mass loss is governed by the film's **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)**:

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2O} = WVTR \cdot A
$$

where $A$ is the effective film area through which vapor escapes.

### Step 2 — Define the Critical Moisture Loss Threshold

A frozen food is considered to have visible freezer burn when it has lost approximately **3–5%** of its initial water mass:

$$
\Delta m_{critical} = f \cdot m_0
$$

where $f \in [0.03, 0.05]$ for most foods, and $m_0$ is the initial mass of water in the food.

### Step 3 — Set Up the Time Integral

Assuming constant WVTR (steady-state permeation):

$$
\int_0^{t_{fb}} \dot{m}_{H_2O} \, dt = \Delta m_{critical}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve the Integral

$$
WVTR \cdot A \cdot t_{fb} = f \cdot m_0
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{fb} = \frac{f \cdot m_0}{WVTR \cdot A}}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

A **200 g** fish fillet (moisture content 75%) is packed in a film with **WVTR = 3 g/m²·day** and effective moisture-exit area **0.04 m²**. Compute the time to visible freezer burn (use $f = 0.04$).

**Solution:**

$$
m_0 = 0.75 \times 200 = 150 \text{ g}
$$

$$
\Delta m_{critical} = 0.04 \times 150 = 6.0 \text{ g}
$$

$$
t_{fb} = \frac{6.0}{3 \times 0.04} = \frac{6.0}{0.12} = 50 \text{ days}
$$

**Result:** Freezer burn becomes visible after approximately **50 days** of storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** To extend $t_{fb}$ to, say, 180 days, the package designer must either (a) reduce effective $A$ (use a tighter-fitting pack), or (b) reduce the film's WVTR by switching to a metallised or foil-based laminate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Oxygen Ingress and Lipid Oxidation Limit

For high-fat frozen foods, the dominant degradation is **oxidative rancidity**. We compute the time until the dissolved oxygen inside the package rises to a critical threshold.

### Step 1 — Headspace Oxygen Mass

The mass of oxygen initially in the headspace:

$$
m_{O_2}^{head} = \rho_{O_2} \cdot V_{head} \cdot x_{O_2}
$$

where $\rho_{O_2} = 1.429 \text{ g/L}$ at STP, $V_{head}$ is headspace volume, and $x_{O_2} = 0.21$ (atmospheric oxygen fraction).

### Step 2 — Oxygen Influx Through Film

$$
\dot{m}_{O_2}^{in} = OTR \cdot A
$$

### Step 3 — Time to Critical Oxygen

$$
t_{oxid} = \frac{m_{O_2}^{critical} - m_{O_2}^{head}}{\dot{m}_{O_2}^{in}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitution

$$
\boxed{t_{oxid} = \frac{\rho_{O_2} \cdot V_{head} \cdot (x_{O_2}^{crit} - 0.21)}{OTR \cdot A}}
$$

For most frozen fatty foods, the critical oxygen partial pressure for rancidity onset is around **0.5–1%** by volume inside the package. To achieve this from an initial 21% atmosphere, the package must either:
1. Be **vacuum-sealed** (eliminating headspace oxygen), or
2. Be **flushed with N₂** (replacing headspace O₂ with inert gas), or
3. Use an **oxygen scavenger sachet** inside the pack.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Materials for Common Frozen Foods

| Frozen Food | Primary Concern | Recommended Packaging Structure | Why This Material? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peas, Corn, Mixed Vegetables** | Moisture loss, freezer burn | LDPE / EVA bag, or PET/LDPE pouch | Low cost, good moisture barrier, flexibility at −20 °C. |
| **Fish Fillets (fatty)** | Lipid oxidation, odour | PA/EVOH/LDPE vacuum pouch | EVOH provides O₂ barrier; PA provides puncture resistance. |
| **Meat (red meat, steaks)** | Myoglobin oxidation (browning) | PA/PE vacuum skin pack, or Al-foil laminate | Vacuum + foil gives near-zero O₂. |
| **Ice Cream** | Recrystallisation, surface dehydration | Paperboard / metallised PET / LDPE carton + LDPE lid | Rigid paperboard protects from handling; metallised layer reflects heat. |
| **Frozen Pizza / Ready Meals** | Multi-mode (moisture, O₂, flavour) | PET / Al-foil / CPP retort pouch (or CPET tray + lidding film) | Tray is oven/microwave safe; lidding film seals hermetically. |
| **Berries (whole)** | Cellular damage from ice crystals | PET clamshell + LDPE overwrap | Rigid container prevents crushing; breathable film prevents anaerobic respiration issues. |
| **Seafood (prawns, shellfish)** | Ammonia odour, blackspot | Vacuum skin pack (PA/PE) | Vacuum removes O₂; skin-tight film prevents movement. |
| **Frozen Baked Goods** | Staling, moisture migration | OPP / metallised PET / LDPE bag | Metallised layer blocks light and moisture. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Recurring Concept:** Note how **PA (Nylon)** appears repeatedly in high-performance packs. This is because PA has a very low Tg (≈ 50 °C as a dry polymer, drops further with moisture absorption), excellent puncture resistance, and good oxygen barrier properties — making it the workhorse of frozen food packaging.

## 3.4 Critical Property Comparison Table

| Property | LDPE | LLDPE | HDPE | PP | PET | PA (Nylon) | EVOH | Al Foil |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 10 | 20 | 30 | 35 | 75 | 75 | 80 | 80 |
| **WVTR (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹)** | 15 | 15 | 8 | 10 | 20 | 250 | 50 | **< 0.1** |
| **OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹)** | 8000 | 8000 | 3000 | 3000 | 100 | 50 | **0.5–5** | **< 0.1** |
| **Tg (°C)** | **−110** | −110 | −110 | −20 | **+70** | +50 | +60 | N/A |
| **Sealability** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good | None | None | None | None |
| **Low-Temp Flexibility** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | Good | Poor | Brittle | Good | Brittle | Excellent |

> **Reading Tip:** Notice the inverse relationship between barrier and low-temperature flexibility. **LDPE** is the most flexible at cold temperatures but is the *worst* oxygen barrier. **EVOH** is the *best* oxygen barrier but is *brittle* at low humidity and low temperature. **This is why multi-layer structures exist** — to combine the strengths of multiple polymers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Frozen Food Packaging Line

The diagram below illustrates the **end-to-end frozen food packaging workflow** — from raw material reception to finished palletised goods ready for cold-chain dispatch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Material Reception] --> B[Sorting and Grading]
    B --> C[Pre-treatment: Washing, Blanching, Cutting]
    C --> D[Quick Freezing Tunnel: -35 C to -40 C]
    D --> E[Tempering Station: Glaze or Anti-freeze Dip]
    E --> F[Weighing and Portioning]
    F --> G{Vacuum / MAP / Standard?}
    G -->|Vacuum| H1[Vacuum Packing Machine: PA/PE or Al Laminate]
    G -->|MAP| H2[Modified Atmosphere Packaging: N2 Flush, O2 Scavenger]
    G -->|Standard| H3[Form-Fill-Seal: LDPE or PET/LDPE Pouch]
    H1 --> I[Metal Detection and X-ray Inspection]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Coding and Labelling: Batch, Date, Barcode]
    J --> K[Secondary Packaging: Corrugated Carton]
    K --> L[Tertiary Packaging: Shrink Wrap, Pallet]
    L --> M[Cold Storage Warehouse: -18 C to -20 C]
    M --> N[Distribution Reefer Truck: -18 C Maintained]
    N --> O[Retail Display Cabinet: -16 C to -18 C]
    O --> P[Consumer Freezer: -18 C or Lower]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - Each block is a **functional station** in the production-distribution chain.
> - The decision block **"Vacuum / MAP / Standard"** represents the *packaging technology choice* — this is the most critical decision for KTU exam questions.
> - The colour-coded path (in a colour display) would show: blue → freezing, green → packaging, orange → secondary packaging, red → cold chain.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Multilayer Laminate Architecture

The following diagram shows the **cross-section view** of a typical high-barrier frozen food laminate, with each layer's function annotated.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OL[Outer Layer: PET 12 micron]
        OL1[Printable, Gloss, Puncture Resistance]
    end
    subgraph AD1[Adhesive: PU Solvent-based or Solventless]
        AD1F[Bonds PET to Barrier]
    end
    subgraph BL[Barrier Layer: EVOH or Al Foil 7-9 micron]
        BL1[Oxygen and Moisture Barrier, Light Block]
    end
    subgraph AD2[Adhesive: PU]
        AD2F[Bonds Barrier to Sealant]
    end
    subgraph SL[Sealant Layer: LDPE or LLDPE 50-80 micron]
        SL1[Heat Seal, Food Contact, Low Temp Flexibility]
    end
    OL --> AD1 --> BL --> AD2 --> SL
    EXT[External Environment] -.Cold air, moisture, O2.-> OL
    SL -.Hermetic protection.-> FOOD[Frozen Food Inside]
```

## 4.3 Cause-and-Effect Diagram — Freezer Burn Failure Modes

This Ishikawa-style (fishbone) diagram maps the **root causes of freezer burn** in frozen foods — useful for KTU quality management questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FB[Freezer Burn: Visible Surface Dehydration]
    FB --> M1[Materials]
    FB --> M2[Methods]
    FB --> M3[Machine]
    FB --> M4[Manpower]
    FB --> M5[Measurement]
    FB --> M6[Environment]
    M1 --> M1a[Insufficient WVTR]
    M1 --> M1b[Poor Seal Strength]
    M1 --> M1c[Wrong Layer Order]
    M2 --> M2a[Inadequate Vacuum]
    M2 --> M2b[Improper Glazing]
    M2 --> M2c[Long Freeze Delay]
    M3 --> M3a[Leaky Sealer Jaws]
    M3 --> M3b[Stale Thermocouples]
    M3 --> M3c[Pin Holes in Film]
    M4 --> M4a[Inexperienced Operator]
    M4 --> M4b[Skipped Inspection]
    M5 --> M5a[No OTR WVTR Testing]
    M5 --> M5b[No Burst Test]
    M6 --> M6a[Temperature Cycling]
    M6 --> M6b[Humidity in Cold Room]
    M6 --> M6c[Long Cold Chain Transit]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Cold Chain Integrity Monitoring

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROD[Production: -18 C] --> QC[Quality Check 1]
    QC --> WH[Warehouse: -18 C]
    WH --> QC2[Quality Check 2]
    QC2 --> DIST[Reefer Truck: -18 C with Data Logger]
    DIST --> QC3[Quality Check 3 at Receiving]
    QC3 --> RET[Retail: -16 to -18 C]
    RET --> CONS[Consumer: -18 C or Lower]
    QC -.Temperature Excursion.-> ALERT[Alert System: Reject or Downgrade]
    DIST -.GPS Temp Logger.-> CLD[Cloud Data Dashboard]
    CLD -.Real-time.-> QC
```

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Modern frozen food packaging is no longer just a *container* — it is part of an *integrated cold-chain ecosystem* that includes **time-temperature indicators (TTIs)**, **RFID temperature loggers**, and **smart labels** that change colour if the cold chain is broken.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define "Freezer Burn" and explain how packaging can prevent it.**

**Model Answer:**

Freezer burn is the **surface dehydration and oxidative degradation** of frozen food caused by the **sublimation of ice** from the food surface and the subsequent **escape of water vapor** through the packaging film. It manifests as dry, discoloured, leathery patches on the food surface, accompanied by off-flavours from oxidation.

**Prevention by packaging:**
1. Use films with **very low WVTR** (e.g., metallised PET, EVOH laminates) so water vapor cannot escape.
2. Ensure a **hermetic heat seal** to eliminate leak paths.
3. Use **vacuum packaging** to remove headspace and reduce the partial pressure driving sublimation.
4. **Tight-fitting packs** minimise headspace, reducing the volume of dry air in contact with the food.
5. Use **glazing** (ice coating) on bulk-frozen products to act as a sacrificial moisture barrier.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is LDPE commonly used as the inner sealant layer in frozen food packaging?**

**Model Answer:**

LDPE is the **inner sealant layer** of choice in frozen food packaging for the following reasons:

1. **Very low glass transition temperature** ($T_g \approx -110 \ ^\circ\text{C}$), so it remains **flexible and ductile** at typical frozen storage temperatures (−18 °C to −40 °C), unlike PET or PP which become brittle.
2. **Excellent heat sealability** at relatively low temperatures (110–140 °C), forming a strong, hermetic seal.
3. **Food contact compliance** — LDPE is approved by **FSSAI** and **USFDA** for direct food contact.
4. **Good moisture barrier** (WVTR ≈ 15 g/m²·day, acceptable for most frozen foods).
5. **Cost-effectiveness** — LDPE is one of the cheapest food-grade polymers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different types of frozen food packaging formats with examples. Discuss the critical material properties required for frozen food packaging. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A frozen fish fillet of mass 250 g (moisture content 78%) is vacuum-packed in a film with effective permeation area 0.05 m². If the film's WVTR is 2.5 g/m²·day and the critical moisture loss for freezer burn onset is 4% of the initial water mass, calculate the time (in days) at which freezer burn will become visible. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Frozen food packaging can be classified into four major formats:

1. **Flexible Pouches (Bags):**
   - **Examples:** Flat pouches, stand-up pouches (SUP), pillow pouches.
   - **Structure:** Typically PET/AL/PE or PET/EVOH/PE.
   - **Used for:** Vegetables, fish fillets, berries, ready meals.

2. **Rigid Containers (Trays and Clamshells):**
   - **Examples:** CPET trays (crystallised PET, oven-safe), PP trays, PET clamshells.
   - **Used for:** Ready meals, ice cream, berries, baked goods.
   - **Lidding films:** Heat-sealed PE-based films.

3. **Vacuum Skin Packaging (VSP):**
   - **Structure:** A heated upper film shrinks tightly around the product on a rigid base tray.
   - **Used for:** Premium meat, seafood, cheese.

4. **Cartons and Boxes:**
   - **Examples:** Paperboard cartons with internal LDPE coating (e.g., ice cream tubs).
   - **Used for:** Ice cream, frozen desserts, pizzas.

**Critical material properties:**

| Property | Requirement |
|---|---|
| **Low Temperature Flexibility** | $T_g$ of sealant layer must be well below storage temperature. |
| **Low WVTR** | To prevent freezer burn (typically < 5 g/m²·day for high-moisture foods). |
| **Low OTR** | To prevent oxidation (typically < 10 cm³·m²·day·atm). |
| **Heat Sealability** | Hermetic seal at low temperature (110–140 °C). |
| **Puncture Resistance** | To survive handling of sharp frozen products (fish bones, shellfish). |
| **Grease Resistance** | For fatty foods (butter, sausages). |
| **Transparency (optional)** | For consumer visibility — e.g., vegetables, berries. |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Mass of fish fillet, $m_0 = 250$ g
- Moisture content = 78% → Initial water mass $= 0.78 \times 250 = 195$ g
- Effective permeation area, $A = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$
- WVTR $= 2.5 \text{ g/m}^2\cdot\text{day}$
- Critical moisture loss fraction, $f = 0.04$

**Step 1:** Calculate the critical moisture loss mass.

$$
\Delta m_{critical} = f \times m_{water} = 0.04 \times 195 = 7.8 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the daily moisture loss rate.

$$
\dot{m}_{loss} = WVTR \times A = 2.5 \times 0.05 = 0.125 \text{ g/day}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the time to freezer burn.

$$
t_{fb} = \frac{\Delta m_{critical}}{\dot{m}_{loss}} = \frac{7.8}{0.125} = 62.4 \text{ days}
$$

**Result:** Freezer burn becomes visible after approximately **62.4 days** (≈ **62 days**).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 - Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the role of Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) and Vacuum Packaging in extending the shelf life of frozen foods. What gas mixtures are used in MAP? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the following polymer films used in frozen food packaging with respect to their barrier properties, Tg, and typical applications: (i) LDPE, (ii) PET, (iii) EVOH, (iv) PA (Nylon). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Vacuum Packaging:**
- Removes air (and hence oxygen) from the package before sealing.
- Reduces oxidative degradation of fats and pigments.
- Prevents the growth of aerobic microorganisms.
- Used for **meat, fish, and cheese**.
- Limitation: Cannot be used for soft products that may be crushed by vacuum (e.g., berries, cakes) — these need **modified atmosphere** or **atmospheric** packaging.

**Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):**
- Replaces the air inside the package with a **controlled gas mixture**, then seals the package.
- For frozen foods, the typical gas mix is **100% Nitrogen (N₂)** or **N₂ + CO₂** combinations, since **O₂ is actively excluded**.

**Common gas mixtures in frozen food MAP:**

| Gas | Function |
|---|---|
| **N₂ (Nitrogen)** | Inert filler gas; prevents package collapse; displaces O₂. |
| **CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)** | Antimicrobial — inhibits bacterial and mould growth. |
| **O₂ (Oxygen)** | Rarely used in frozen MAP; reserved for products needing bloom (e.g., red meat). |

**Typical mixes:**
- **Red meat:** 70% O₂ + 20% CO₂ + 10% N₂ (to maintain bright red myoglobin colour).
- **Fish/seafood:** 30% CO₂ + 70% N₂ (to suppress spoilage bacteria).
- **Vegetables:** 100% N₂ (to prevent oxidation without anaerobic respiration issues).
- **Bakery:** 100% CO₂ (to delay mould growth).

**Shelf-life extension:** MAP combined with freezing can extend shelf life from a few weeks (chilled) to **6–24 months** (frozen).

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Polymer | WVTR (g/m²·day) | OTR (cm³/m²·day·atm) | Tg (°C) | Key Features | Typical Application in Frozen Foods |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LDPE** | ≈ 15 | ≈ 8000 | **−110** | Very flexible at low temperature, excellent seal | Inner sealant layer, low-cost pouches |
| **PET** | ≈ 20 | ≈ 100 | **+70** | High tensile strength, printable, transparent | Outer print/structural layer, rigid trays |
| **EVOH** | ≈ 50 | **0.5–5** | +60 | Outstanding O₂ barrier, but moisture-sensitive | Middle barrier layer in co-extruded films |
| **PA (Nylon)** | ≈ 250 | ≈ 50 | +50 (dry) | Excellent puncture and abrasion resistance | Vacuum bags, mechanical protection |

**Key comparative insights:**

- **LDPE** is the *flexibility champion* at sub-zero temperatures, but has the *worst oxygen barrier* — therefore never used as the primary barrier.
- **PET** is the *strength and printability champion*, but is *brittle at low temperature* — used as the outer layer, not in contact with cold air.
- **EVOH** has the *best oxygen barrier* of all polymers listed, but is *hydrophilic* — its barrier properties degrade in humid environments unless protected by a moisture barrier layer (like LDPE).
- **PA (Nylon)** offers the *best mechanical protection* against punctures and abrasion — critical for fish bones and shellfish edges.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write that PA has a Tg of −50 °C because of moisture plasticisation. **Do not confuse dry Tg with moisture-conditioned Tg.** Always state the **dry Tg** in KTU answers and mention that *absorbed moisture acts as a plasticiser and lowers the effective Tg*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Frozen Food Packaging Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WVTR and OTR:** WVTR = Water Vapor Transmission Rate; OTR = Oxygen Transmission Rate. They are **not interchangeable**. Markers deduct 1 mark for this error.
> 2. **Storing Tg in °F or K:** Always use **°C** unless the question explicitly specifies otherwise. KTU strictly follows SI conventions.
> 3. **Skipping the "why" in material selection:** It is not enough to say "LDPE is used as the sealant." You must explain *why* — e.g., "LDPE has a Tg of −110 °C, well below the −18 °C storage temperature, so it remains flexible and seals reliably."
> 4. **Forgetting unit conversion in numericals:** OTR units can be cm³·m⁻²·day·atm **or** cc·100 in²·day — failing to convert correctly is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring the role of adhesives:** Multi-layer laminates need **tie layers / adhesives** to bond incompatible polymers. Many students forget to include this in their diagrams.
> 6. **Misnaming "Crystallisation" as "Freezer Burn":** Freezer burn is **sublimation + dehydration + oxidation**, not crystallisation. Recrystallisation is a *related but separate* phenomenon.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Frozen Food Packaging (Module 4)**

- **Standard frozen storage temperature:** **−18 °C** (Codex Alimentarius CXS 190).
- **Quick freezing temperature:** **−35 °C to −40 °C** (blast freezer) or **−196 °C** (liquid nitrogen cryogenic).
- **Freezer burn** is caused by **sublimation + moisture loss + oxidation**. Prevention: low WVTR films + hermetic seal + vacuum/glazing.
- **Key packaging formats:** flexible pouches, rigid trays (CPET, PP), vacuum skin packs (VSP), cartons, and modified atmosphere packs.
- **Standard laminate structure:** PET (print/strength) / Adhesive / Barrier (EVOH or Al-foil) / Adhesive / LDPE (sealant).
- **LDPE** is the universal sealant due to its **T_g = −110 °C**.
- **EVOH** is the best O₂ barrier but is **moisture-sensitive** — must be sandwiched between moisture barriers.
- **PA (Nylon)** is the puncture-resistance champion — used in vacuum packs for meat and seafood.
- **PET** is the structural / printable outer layer.
- **Common gas for frozen food MAP:** **N₂** (inert) and **CO₂** (antimicrobial). O₂ is mostly excluded.
- **Time to freezer burn formula:** $t_{fb} = \dfrac{f \cdot m_0}{WVTR \cdot A}$.
- **Time to oxidation formula:** $t_{oxid} = \dfrac{\rho_{O_2} \cdot V_{head} \cdot \Delta x_{O_2}}{OTR \cdot A}$.
- **Cold chain integrity** is monitored by **time-temperature indicators (TTIs)**, **RFID loggers**, and **smart labels**.
- **Regulatory standards:** FSSAI (India), USFDA, EU Regulation 1935/2004, Codex CXS 190, ISTA 2A/3A for distribution testing.
- **Cold crack test:** ASTM D1790 — every frozen food film must pass the **−30 °C cold crack** test.
- **Sustainability note (KTU 2024 emphasis):** Bio-based and recyclable frozen food packaging (e.g., PLA, mono-material PE structures) is an emerging trend. KTU questions increasingly include a 2–3 mark sub-part on *sustainability* in packaging design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fish and Fish Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fish and Fish Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fish and Fish Products Packaging** refers to the specialized science and engineering discipline concerned with the design, selection, and application of protective barrier systems (primary, secondary, and tertiary) for highly perishable aquatic food matrices — ranging from whole fresh fish, fillets, and minced products to value-added derivatives such as surimi, smoked, dried, fermented, and canned seafood — to retard **microbial spoilage**, **lipid oxidation**, **enzymatic autolysis**, and **moisture loss** while extending shelf life, preserving nutritional quality, ensuring food safety, and meeting regulatory and consumer demands.

Fish are categorized biologically as **finfish** (e.g., salmon, cod, mackerel) and **shellfish** (e.g., shrimp, crab, lobster, mollusks). From a packaging engineering standpoint, fish and fish products are classified as **highly perishable, high-water-activity, high-protein, and unsaturated-lipid-rich commodities** — making them one of the most challenging matrices to preserve.

## 1.2 Composition of Fish (Why Packaging is Critical)

| Component | Lean Fish (e.g., Cod) | Fatty Fish (e.g., Mackerel) |
|---|---|---|
| Water | **80–85 %** | **65–75 %** |
| Protein | **15–20 %** | **16–20 %** |
| Lipid (Fat) | **0.5–3 %** | **8–25 %** |
| Minerals/Ash | **1–2 %** | **1–2 %** |
| Vitamins (A, D) | Low | High (fat-soluble) |
| pH (post-mortem) | **6.0–6.5** | **5.8–6.2** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Fatty fish have higher unsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs – Omega-3: EPA and DHA), making them extremely susceptible to **oxidative rancidity**, while lean fish are more prone to **microbial spoilage** due to higher water activity and neutral pH.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Living Refrigerator":** Imagine fresh fish as a **biological countdown timer** that is *still running* the moment it leaves water. The fish's own enzymes (cathepsins) begin digesting its muscle from the inside (autolysis), while bacteria on its skin (especially *Pseudomonas*, *Shewanella*, *Photobacterium*) start a *demolition crew* on its surface. Spoilage compounds such as **trimethylamine (TMA)**, **hydrogen sulfide (H₂S)**, and **biogenic amines (histamine)** are the "smoke signals" of this demolition. **Packaging is the fire-extinguisher, the bodyguard, and the climate-control system** — all rolled into one — that slows down or halts this countdown so the fish reaches the consumer in edible, safe, and nutritious condition.

## 1.4 Classification of Fish Products Relevant to Packaging

1. **Fresh / Chilled Whole Fish** — on ice or refrigerated.
2. **Fresh Fillets and Steaks** — skin-on / skin-off, deboned.
3. **Frozen Fish & Fish Products** — IQF (Individually Quick Frozen), block-frozen.
4. **Minced / Comminuted Fish** — burgers, sausages, fish balls.
5. **Surimi** — washed, refined myofibrillar protein paste (intermediate product).
6. **Smoked Fish** — hot-smoked, cold-smoked, liquid-smoked.
7. **Dried / Salted / Fermented Fish** — low water activity products.
8. **Canned Fish** — retort-sterilized in hermetically sealed containers.
9. **Marinated / Pickled Fish** — acid-preserved.
10. **Ready-to-Eat (RTE) Seafood** — cooked, vacuum-packed, MAP.
11. **Breaded / Coated Fish Products** — fish fingers, nuggets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Spoilage in Fish (The "Enemies" Packaging Must Combat)

### 2.1.1 Microbial Spoilage
- **Specific Spoilage Organisms (SSOs):** *Shewanella putrefaciens* (produces H₂S, TMA — anaerobic), *Pseudomonas* spp. (aerobic, psychrotrophic, slime producers), *Photobacterium phosphoreum* (mod-CO₂, vacuum-packed fish), *Brochothrix thermosphacta*.
- **Initial Microbial Load (N₀):** Typically **10²–10⁵ CFU/g** on freshly caught fish skin.
- **Generation Time:** At chill temperatures (**0–4 °C**), doubling time is **20–30 hours**; at abuse temperatures (**10 °C**), it drops to **~6 hours**.

### 2.1.2 Enzymatic Autolysis
- **Cathepsins** (lysosomal proteases) — break down myofibrillar proteins → texture softening.
- **Calpains** — Ca²⁺-activated proteases.
- **Lipases** — hydrolyze triglycerides → free fatty acids (FFAs) → rancidity precursors.
- Begins immediately post-mortem; even at **0 °C**, autolysis is ongoing.

### 2.1.3 Chemical Oxidation
- **Lipid Oxidation (Primary):** Initiation → Propagation → Termination.
- **Oxidative Indicators:** Peroxide Value (PV), Thiobarbituric Acid Reactive Substances (TBARS), anisidine value.
- **Critical PUFA:** EPA (C20:5) and DHA (C22:6) — highly unsaturated, *5–6 double bonds* — extremely oxidation-prone.

### 2.1.4 Physical Deterioration
- **Moisture Loss / Dehydration:** → weight loss ("drip loss"), surface "freezer burn" in frozen fish.
- **Oxymyoglobin → Metmyoglobin** → brown discoloration of red-muscle fish.
- **Mechanical damage** during handling — bruising accelerates spoilage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Principle:** Packaging must simultaneously address **all four spoilage modes** — microbial, enzymatic, oxidative, and physical — through a combination of barrier, modified atmosphere, and active functions.

## 2.2 Packaging Requirements for Fish and Fish Products

1. **High Oxygen Barrier** — to prevent oxidative rancidity (OTR < 10 cm³/m²·day for fatty fish).
2. **High Moisture Barrier** — to prevent dehydration/drip loss (WVTR < 5 g/m²·day).
3. **Odor Containment** — fish odors (TMA, amines) must not contaminate other foods.
4. **Low Temperature Compatibility** — performance at **−18 °C** to **−25 °C** (freezer-grade).
5. **Mechanical Strength** — puncture resistance for sharp fins, bones, and shell fragments.
6. **Seal Integrity** — hermetic seals for vacuum and MAP applications.
7. **Transparency / Display** — for retail fresh fish.
8. **Food Contact Safety** — compliance with **FSSAI (India)**, **EU 1935/2004**, **US FDA 21 CFR**.
9. **Sustainability** — recyclable / mono-material structures (KTU 2024 emphasis).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **First-Order Microbial Growth (Log-linear)** | $N = N_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$ | CFU/g, h⁻¹ | Spoilage kinetics |
| 2 | **Specific Growth Rate (µ)** | $\mu = \frac{\ln N - \ln N_0}{t}$ | h⁻¹ | Microbial kinetics |
| 3 | **Decimal Reduction Time (D-value)** | $D = \frac{t}{\log N_0 - \log N}$ | min | Thermal sterilization |
| 4 | **Z-value (Thermal Death Time)** | $z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log D_1 - \log D_2}$ | °C | Heat resistance |
| 5 | **F-value (Sterilization Lethality)** | $F = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z} \cdot \Delta t$ | min | Canning process |
| 6 | **Arrhenius Equation (Spoilage Rate)** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$ | h⁻¹, kJ/mol | Temperature dependence |
| 7 | **Q₁₀ Rule (Approximate)** | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | dimensionless | Shelf-life temperature correction |
| 8 | **Clausius–Clapeyron (WVP)** | $\ln P = -\frac{\Delta H_vap}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C$ | Pa, K | Permeation modeling |
| 9 | **Fick's First Law (Permeation)** | $J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | kg/m²·s | Mass transfer through film |
| 10 | **Henry's Law (Gas Dissolution in MAP)** | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | mol/m³, Pa | CO₂ / O₂ in food |
| 11 | **Water Activity & Microbial Growth** | $a_w \geq 0.86$ → spoilage bacteria | dimensionless | Stability threshold |
| 12 | **TBARS Calculation** | $\text{TBARS} = \frac{A_{532}}{m \cdot \varepsilon}$ | mg MDA/kg | Oxidative rancidity |
| 13 | **Shelf-life from Arrhenius** | $\text{SL} = \text{SL}_{ref} \cdot Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}$ | days | Temperature-corrected shelf life |
| 14 | **Lag Phase Duration (Mod. Gompertz)** | $\lambda$ (lambda) = lag time | h | Predictive microbiology |
| 15 | **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** | $\text{OTR} = \frac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}$ | cm³/m²·day·atm | Film barrier rating |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for spoilage formula chain:** ***"N → µ → D → z → F → k → Q₁₀ → Shelf-life"*** — the logical progression of equations from microbial count to predicted shelf life.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in the Real World

- A **1 °C drop** in the cold chain (e.g., from 4 °C to 3 °C) can extend fish shelf life by **~30–40 %** (Q₁₀ ≈ 3 for spoilage).
- A **reduction in O₂ from 21 % to 0.5 %** (using MAP) can extend fatty-fish shelf life from **3 days to 10–14 days** at 0–2 °C.
- **Pouch-based retort canning** (vs. metal can) reduces cook-time by **30–50 %** and improves product texture — a direct application of F-value optimization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shelf-Life Estimation from Q₁₀ Approximation

**Problem Statement:** A chilled fillet of *Oncorhynchus nerka* (Sockeye Salmon) has a measured shelf life of **6 days at 4 °C**. Estimate the shelf life if the cold chain is broken and the product is held at **10 °C**. Assume **Q₁₀ = 3** for psychrotrophic spoilage bacteria.

### Step 1: Identify the Parameters

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 10 - 4 = 6 \text{ °C}$$

$$\text{SL}_{ref} = 6 \text{ days}, \quad Q_{10} = 3$$

### Step 2: State the Q₁₀ Shelf-Life Equation

$$\text{SL} = \text{SL}_{ref} \cdot Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$\text{SL} = 6 \cdot 3^{6/10}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Exponent

$$3^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \cdot \ln 3} = e^{0.6 \cdot 1.0986} = e^{0.6592}$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Exponential

$$e^{0.6592} \approx 1.933$$

### Step 6: Final Calculation

$$\text{SL} = 6 \times 1.933 \approx 11.6 \text{ days}$$

### Step 7: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Wait — this is *wrong direction*! As temperature increases, shelf life *decreases*. The Q₁₀ relation is correctly:

$$\text{SL} = \text{SL}_{ref} \cdot Q_{10}^{-\Delta T / 10}$$

$$\text{SL} = 6 \cdot 3^{-0.6} = \frac{6}{1.933} \approx 3.10 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** A **6 °C** temperature abuse reduces the shelf life from **6 days to ~3.1 days** — a **~48 % loss**. This justifies why fish is classified as a "must-refrigerate" commodity and why **time-temperature indicators (TTIs)** are widely applied as intelligent packaging elements.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Microbial Growth Log-Reduction During Chilled Storage

**Problem Statement:** Freshly caught *Gadus morhua* (Atlantic Cod) fillets have an initial psychrotrophic count of **N₀ = 10³ CFU/g**. The specific growth rate at **2 °C** is **µ = 0.025 h⁻¹**. Determine the bacterial count after **96 hours** of storage.

### Step 1: Apply First-Order Growth Model

$$N = N_0 \cdot e^{\mu \cdot t}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$N = 10^3 \cdot e^{0.025 \times 96}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Exponent

$$0.025 \times 96 = 2.4$$

$$e^{2.4} = 11.02$$

### Step 4: Final Result

$$N = 10^3 \times 11.02 = 1.10 \times 10^4 \text{ CFU/g}$$

### Step 5: Compare to Spoilage Threshold

> [!NOTE]
> The **spoilage rejection threshold** for chilled cod is approximately **10⁷ CFU/g**. At this growth rate, time to threshold is calculated as:

$$t_{spoilage} = \frac{\ln(10^7 / 10^3)}{0.025} = \frac{\ln(10^4)}{0.025} = \frac{9.21}{0.025} \approx 368 \text{ h} \approx 15.3 \text{ days}$$

This value closely matches reported **pre-rigor / in-ice shelf life** of whole cod (12–16 days), validating the model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Oxygen Permeation Through a Packaging Film

**Problem Statement:** A MAP package for smoked salmon uses an **EVOH (Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol)** barrier layer of thickness **L = 50 µm**. The OTR at 23 °C / 0 % RH is reported as **3 cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹**. The headspace has **pO₂ = 0.05 atm**; outside air is **0.21 atm**. Find the **steady-state O₂ flux** into the package.

### Step 1: Apply Fick's First Law (Steady State)

$$J = \frac{P_{O_2}}{L} \cdot \Delta p$$

where $P_{O_2}$ is the permeability × thickness product (effectively OTR × thickness in normalized form).

### Step 2: Re-express Using OTR

Since OTR is defined at standard conditions, the flux through a film of thickness *L* is:

$$J_{O_2} = \text{OTR} \times \Delta p$$

### Step 3: Compute the Partial Pressure Difference

$$\Delta p = 0.21 - 0.05 = 0.16 \text{ atm}$$

### Step 4: Substitute

$$J_{O_2} = 3 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ m}^{-2} \text{ day}^{-1} \text{ atm}^{-1} \times 0.16 \text{ atm}$$

### Step 5: Result

$$J_{O_2} = 0.48 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Such a low O₂ ingress rate is the design target for **fatty fish MAP** — it maintains the headspace pO₂ **below 1 %** for the entire 14–21 day chilled shelf life, preventing oxidation of EPA/DHA.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Packaging System for Fish Products

| Product | Primary Spoilage Risk | Recommended Packaging | Key Parameters | Expected Shelf Life (0–2 °C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Whole fresh fish** | Microbial + autolysis | Wax-coated corrugated box + ice | Drainage, ventilation | 8–12 days on ice |
| **Skinless fillet** | Microbial + oxidation | MAP (60 % N₂ / 40 % CO₂), EVOH/PET tray | OTR < 5, CO₂ solubility | 10–14 days |
| **Fatty fish (salmon)** | **Lipid oxidation** | Vacuum skin-pack (VSP) or high-barrier MAP | OTR < 1, opaque | 14–21 days |
| **Frozen fillet** | Freezer burn + oxidation | LDPE/PA/EVOH pouch, vacuum | WVTR < 3, OTR < 5 | 6–9 months (−18 °C) |
| **Smoked salmon (cold)** | *Listeria* + oxidation | Vacuum + high-CO₂ MAP | OTR < 1, pH < 5.0 | 21–28 days |
| **Surimi (frozen)** | Freeze-denaturation | PE/PA/EVOH cryovac | Oxygen scavenger optional | 12 months (−20 °C) |
| **Canned tuna** | *Clostridium botulinum* | Retort metal can / retort pouch | F₀ > 3 min, hermetic | 3–5 years (ambient) |
| **Breaded fish fingers** | Lipid oxidation, moisture | OPP/PE flow-wrap or MAP tray | OTR < 50, WVTR < 10 | 6–9 months (frozen) |
| **Dried/salted cod** | *Halophilic* mold, oxidation | VPack or N₂-flushed MET-PET/PE | a_w < 0.75, low O₂ | 6–12 months (ambient) |
| **RTE fish curry** | *L. monocytogenes* | Retort pouch, MAP | F₀ > 6, pasteurization | 12–18 months (ambient) |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Retort Process Lethality (F-value) for Canned Fish

**Problem Statement:** A tuna retort process holds the can at **121 °C** for **45 minutes** with a z-value of **10 °C** and reference temperature **T_ref = 121 °C**. Compute the **F₀ value**.

### Step 1: F-value Equation

$$F_0 = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/z} \, dt$$

### Step 2: Constant Temperature Assumption

At constant $T = 121$ °C, the integrand becomes $10^{0} = 1$.

### Step 3: Result

$$F_0 = 1 \times 45 = 45 \text{ minutes}$$

### Step 4: Verification Against Botulinum Cook

> [!NOTE]
> **Commercial sterility** for low-acid canned fish (pH > 4.6) requires **F₀ ≥ 3 min** for *Clostridium botulinum* Type A & B. Our result of **45 min** represents a **15× safety margin**, which is standard industrial practice for tuna and sardine canning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spoilage Cascade in Fish — The "Four Enemies"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvested Fish] --> B[Post-mortem Autolysis]
    A --> C[Microbial Proliferation]
    A --> D[Lipid Oxidation]
    A --> E[Physical Dehydration]
    B --> F1[Cathepsins, Calpains, Lipases]
    C --> F2[Shewanella, Pseudomonas, Photobacterium]
    D --> F3[PUFA breakdown to aldehydes, ketones]
    E --> F4[Drip loss, freezer burn]
    F1 --> G[Quality Loss]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Sensory Rejection: off-odor, slime, browning]
    H --> I[Shelf-life End]
```

## 4.2 Packaging Technology Decision Flow for Fish Products

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Identify Fish Product Type] --> P1{High water activity? a_w greater than 0.85}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Lean or Fatty?}
    P1 -- No --> P3{Dried/Salted/Fermented}
    P2 -- Lean --> P4[High CO2 MAP or Vacuum Skin Pack]
    P2 -- Fatty --> P5[Vacuum + O2 scavenger OR opaque high-barrier MAP]
    P3 --> P6[VPack with low OTR, optional O2 scavenger]
    P4 --> P7[EVOH PET PE tray, 60 percent N2 40 percent CO2]
    P5 --> P8[PET EVOH PE pouch, 70 percent N2 30 percent CO2]
    P6 --> P9[MET PET PE or Alu laminate, N2 flush]
    P7 --> P10[Shelf life 10 to 14 days at 0 to 2 C]
    P8 --> P11[Shelf life 14 to 21 days at 0 to 2 C]
    P9 --> P12[Shelf life 6 to 12 months ambient]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Active-Intelligent MAP System for Fish

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProductLayer[Product Matrix Layer]
        F1[Fresh Fish Fillet]
        F2[Drip Absorber Pad]
        F3[Antimicrobial Pad optional]
    end
    subgraph GasLayer[Atmosphere Control Layer]
        G1[Gas Flush: N2 dominant]
        G2[CO2 High for SSO inhibition]
        G3[O2 below 0.5 percent]
    end
    subgraph FilmLayer[Packaging Film Stack]
        M1[Outer PET Print layer]
        M2[Adhesive]
        M3[EVOH Oxygen Barrier]
        M4[Adhesive]
        M5[LDPE Sealant Food Contact]
    end
    subgraph IntellLayer[Intelligent Features]
        I1[Time Temperature Indicator TTI]
        I2[Freshness Sensor Colorimetric]
        I3[QR Code Traceability]
    end
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> M1
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5
    I1 -. monitors .-> F1
    I2 -. monitors .-> G1
    I3 -. provides .-> M1
```

## 4.4 Surimi Production and Packaging Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Whole Fish mince heading] --> S2[Mechanical Deboning]
    S2 --> S3[Washing cycles 3 to 4 with cold water]
    S3 --> S4[Refining Sieving 0.5 to 1 mm]
    S4 --> S5[Dewatering / Centrifugation]
    S5 --> S6[Addition of Cryoprotectants Sucrose, Sorbitol, STPP]
    S6 --> S7[Vacuum Mixing]
    S7 --> S8[Block Freezing or IQF]
    S8 --> S9[PE/PA/EVOH Cryovac Packaging]
    S9 --> S10[Storage minus 20 to minus 25 C]
    S10 --> S11[Shelf life 12 to 18 months]
```

## 4.5 Cross-Section of a Typical Frozen Fish Pouch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Outer PET 12 micron Print] --> L2[LDPE Adhesive 3 micron]
    L2 --> L3[Nylon PA 15 micron Puncture resistance]
    L3 --> L4[Adhesive]
    L4 --> L5[EVOH 5 micron O2 barrier]
    L5 --> L6[Adhesive]
    L6 --> L7[LDPE Sealant 60 micron Food contact]
```
