- Materials are kept in a **quarantine zone** for 24–48 h to equilibrate to warehouse **T and RH**.
- This prevents **condensation ("sweating")** when cold-stored materials are suddenly exposed to warm, humid ambient air.

#### Step 3 — Zone Segregation
- **Hygroscopic** materials (paper, PVOH-coated films, Nylon) must be stored **away from moisture sources**.
- **Oxidation-sensitive** materials (PE, PP, PVdC-coated films) must be kept **away from sunlight and heat sources**.
- **Cross-contamination** of food-contact vs. non-food-contact stocks must be prevented by **colour-coded racking**.

#### Step 4 — FIFO / FEFO Rotation
- **FIFO (First In First Out):** Material used in the order received — standard for non-perishable substrates.
- **FEFO (First Expiry First Out):** Material with the earliest **shelf-life / retest date** is used first — mandatory for inks, adhesives, and some laminates.

#### Step 5 — Issuance and Line-Side Storage
- Only **24–48 hours' worth** of packaging stock is released to the line at a time.
- **Line-side stores** must mirror the climatic conditions of the main warehouse.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **highest-yield formulas** for KTU 2024 Module 4 numericals and short-answer derivations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_w = \dfrac{P}{P_0} = \dfrac{\%ERH}{100}$ | Water activity (ratio of partial to saturation vapour pressure) | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $M_{eq} = f(a_w)$ via Henderson: $M = \left[\dfrac{-\ln(1-a_w)}{A \cdot T}\right]^{1/B}$ | Equilibrium moisture content (Henderson equation) | % dry basis |
| 3 | $\ln(a_w) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{sorp}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)$ | Clausius–Clapeyron sorption isotherm shift with T | a_w dimensionless, T in K |
| 4 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ (Arrhenius) | Degradation rate constant vs. temperature | k in 1/time |
| 5 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Temperature acceleration factor (typical 2–3) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $t_{s} = \dfrac{t_{ref}}{Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}}$ | Accelerated shelf-life from reference storage | Days / months |
| 7 | $RH_{critical} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{P_{sat}(T_{store})}{P_{sat}(T_{room})}$ | Dew-point condensation check (often > 60% RH at 20 °C dewpoint ≈ 12 °C) | % |
| 8 | $\sigma_{burst} = \sigma_0 \cdot (1 - k \cdot \Delta RH)$ | Empirical loss of paper burst strength with RH | kPa, k ≈ 0.05–0.07 |
| 9 | $C_{COB} = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{A}$ (Cobb test) | Water absorption of paperboard in g/m² | g·m⁻² |
| 10 | $S_{pack} = \dfrac{V_{pack}}{V_{pallet}} \times 100\%$ | Pallet space utilization | % |

> **Legend:** $a_w$ = water activity; $E_a$ = activation energy (J·mol⁻¹); $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹; $T$ = absolute temperature (K); $M$ = moisture content (% db); $C_{COB}$ = Cobb value.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Packaging

- **Pharma / Nutraceuticals** — Predicted shelf life of blister foils is set by **Q₁₀ accelerated ageing at 40 °C / 75% RH** for 6 months (per ICH Q1A), then back-extrapolated to 25 °C.
- **Dairy / Beverage** — Aseptic carton reels must be stored at **20–25 °C / 50–55% RH** to preserve the **polyethylene / aluminium / paper lamination integrity**.
- **Snack Foods** — Biaxially Oriented Polypropylene (BOPP) / Cast Polypropylene (CPP) laminates must be conditioned for **24 h** at line conditions to prevent **curl** on sealing.
- **Frozen Foods** — Pouches stored at –18 °C must be pre-acclimatized to **15–20 °C** for 4–6 h before opening to avoid **condensation** on the inner surface.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equilibrium Moisture Content via the Henderson Equation

The Henderson equation is a **semi-empirical sorption isotherm model** widely used in packaging science to predict the equilibrium moisture content ($M_{eq}$) of paper, paperboard, and some biopolymer films at a given relative humidity and temperature. The derivation begins with the **BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) monolayer concept** integrated over a temperature-dependent heat of sorption.

Starting from the integrated form of the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation** for differential heat of sorption:

$$\frac{\partial(\ln a_w)}{\partial(1/T)} = -\frac{\Delta H_{sorp}}{R}$$

Integrating between the reference state and a generic state:

$$\ln(a_w) = \frac{\Delta H_{sorp}}{R} \cdot \left( C_1 - \frac{1}{T} \right)$$

Henderson postulated that the heat of sorption is **inversely proportional to the square of moisture content**, $\Delta H_{sorp} \propto 1/M^2$, and substituted this dependence into the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron form. After integration with the boundary condition that $M \to 0$ as $a_w \to 0$ and $M \to \infty$ as $a_w \to 1$, the closed-form **Henderson equation** emerges:

$$M = M_0 \cdot \left[ \frac{-\ln(1 - a_w)}{A \cdot T} \right]^{1/B}$$

where:
- $M$ = equilibrium moisture content (% dry basis),
- $a_w$ = water activity (decimal),
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K),
- $M_0$, $A$, $B$ = empirical material-specific constants (for paperboard, typically $M_0 \approx 0.01$, $A \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-4}$, $B \approx 1.5$).

> **Interpretation for KTU 2024:** A higher $a_w$ (humid warehouse) drives $M$ **upward** — meaning the paperboard picks up moisture, its **fibre bonds weaken**, **Cobb value rises**, and **burst strength drops**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius-Based Accelerated Shelf Life of Plastic Films

The storage stability of a packaging polymer is governed by a single **rate-limiting degradation reaction** — typically **chain scission, oxidation, or additive migration**. The Arrhenius hypothesis states that the rate constant $k$ of this reaction depends on absolute temperature $T$ as:

$$k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

> Where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor (frequency of molecular collisions) and $E_a$ is the activation energy (energy barrier in J·mol⁻¹).

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

For two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, taking the ratio:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[ -\frac{E_a}{R}\left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right) \right]$$

Solving for the **acceleration factor** $\alpha$ between $T_2$ (accelerated) and $T_1$ (real storage):

$$\alpha = \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[ \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 \cdot T_2} \right]$$

> **Why this matters:** A polyethylene film stored at **40 °C** degrades roughly **3–4 times faster** than the same film stored at **25 °C** for $E_a \approx 70$ kJ·mol⁻¹. The **shelf life at real conditions** is therefore recovered as:

$$t_{real} = \alpha \cdot t_{accelerated}$$

#### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A PE film is stored at 25 °C with an expected real-time shelf life of 24 months. Predict the shelf life if stored at 35 °C. Assume $E_a = 70$ kJ·mol⁻¹ and $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$\frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 \cdot T_2} = \frac{308 - 298}{298 \times 308} = \frac{10}{91{,}784} = 1.0896 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent:**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{70{,}000}{8.314} = 8422.4 \text{ K}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2} = 8422.4 \times 1.0896 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9178$$

**Step 4 — Compute the acceleration factor:**

$$\alpha = e^{0.9178} = 2.504$$

**Step 5 — Compute the real-time shelf life at 35 °C:**

$$t_{real} = \frac{t_{ref}}{\alpha} = \frac{24 \text{ months}}{2.504} \approx 9.59 \text{ months}$$

> **Answer:** The shelf life drops from **24 months to ~9.6 months** — a **60% reduction** for a mere 10 °C rise. This is why **climate-controlled stores** are non-negotiable for barrier films.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Q₁₀ Rule of Thumb (Industry-Standard Approximation)

The Arrhenius derivation is exact but requires knowledge of $E_a$, which is rarely available on the shop floor. Industry therefore uses the empirical **Q₁₀ model** (van't Hoff rule):

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

For typical food-packaging polymers, $Q_{10} \approx 2$ to $3$. With $Q_{10} = 2.5$ and $\Delta T = 10$ °C:

$$\alpha = 2.5^{10/10} = 2.5$$

**Generalized shelf-life correction:**

$$t_{s}(T) = t_{s}(T_{ref}) \cdot \frac{1}{Q_{10}^{(T - T_{ref})/10}}$$

> **Implication:** Each 10 °C rise halves (approximately) the safe storage life of an oxygen-/moisture-sensitive laminate.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material-Specific Storage Risk Map

| Material | Critical Climate | Failure Mode if Mismanaged | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Corrugated fibreboard** | RH < 70%, T < 30 °C | Loss of compression strength (Edge Crush Test ↓) | Climate-controlled godown |
| **SBS / FBB paperboard** | 50 ± 5% RH | Curl, warp, print misregister | Conditioning 24 h before printing |
| **PE / PP film reels** | 15–25 °C, < 60% RH | Blocking, telescoping of reels | Vertical spindle storage, FIFO |
| **PET film** | 20–25 °C, dry | Hydrolysis at > 70% RH, dimensional drift | Sealed shrink-wrap pallets |
| **Aluminium foil** | Dry, no acid vapours | Pinhole corrosion, ink oxidation | Hermetic PE-wrap, segregated acid-free zone |
| **Glass bottles** | T < 35 °C, dry pallets | Thermal shock fracture | Slip-sheets, no sudden T gradient |
| **Tinplate / TFS coils** | Dry, no chlorides | Rust ("white rust"), tin pest (< 13 °C) | VCI (Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor) paper |
| **PVdC-coated films** | 15–20 °C, dark | UV-yellowing, chlorine loss | Amber light / blackout stores |
| **Inks & adhesives** | 15–25 °C, sealed | Skin formation, viscosity drift | Sealed drums, retest-date rotation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Incoming Packaging Material Storage Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Truck Arrival at Warehouse] --> B{Visual Inspection of Vehicle and Pallet Condition}
    B -- Damage Detected --> C[Reject and Return to Supplier with NCR]
    B -- Intact --> D[Move to Quarantine Zone for 24 to 48 h]
    D --> E[Sample for QC Lab Testing]
    E --> F{Tests Pass as per COA Specs}
    F -- No --> G[Quarantine Reject and Raise CAPA]
    F -- Yes --> H[Transfer to Climate Controlled Main Store]
    H --> I[Apply FIFO and FEFO Labelling with Date and Batch]
    I --> J{Production Requisition Raised}
    J -- Yes --> K[Issue 24 to 48 h Stock to Line Side Store]
    J -- No --> L[Continue Storage with Monthly Stock Audit]
    K --> M[Line Side Stock used within Shift]
    M --> N[End of Shift Reconciliation and Return of Surplus to Main Store]
    L --> O[Re check at 6 month Retest Date]
    O --> P{Material Still within Spec}
    P -- Yes --> I
    P -- No --> G
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Material-Specific Climate Zones in a Packaging Warehouse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z1[Zone 1 Dry Ambient 20 to 25 C and 50 to 55 percent RH]
        Z1A[Paper and Paperboard Pallets]
        Z1B[Corrugated Boxes]
        Z1C[Labels and Cartons]
    end
    subgraph Z2[Zone 2 Climate Controlled 18 to 22 C and 45 to 50 percent RH]
        Z2A[Plastic Film Reels BOPP and PET and PE]
        Z2B[Aluminium Foil Reels and Laminates]
        Z2C[Inks and Adhesives in Sealed Drums]
    end
    subgraph Z3[Zone 3 Glass Store Cool and Dry 25 C max]
        Z3A[Glass Bottles and Jars on Slip Sheets]
        Z3B[Preforms for PET and HDPE]
    end
    subgraph Z4[Zone 4 Metal Store Dry and Acid Free]
        Z4A[Tinplate Coils and Sheets]
        Z4B[Aluminium Cans and Ends]
        Z4C[VCI Wrapped Bundles]
    end
    subgraph Z5[Zone 5 Hazardous Store Fire Rated and Ventilated]
        Z5A[Solvent Based Inks]
        Z5B[Adhesive Hardeners]
        Z5C[Cleaning Solvents]
    end
    Z1 --> QC[Central QC Lab]
    Z2 --> QC
    Z3 --> QC
    Z4 --> QC
    Z5 --> QC
    QC --> ISS[Issue to Production Line]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Storage Action Based on Material Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Identify Material Type] --> S1{Is Material Hygroscopic}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Store at RH below 60 percent and Condition for 24 h]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Is Material Light Sensitive}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Store in Amber Light or Blackout Zone]
    S3 -- No --> S5{Is Material Heat Sensitive below 30 C}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Air Conditioned Store at 20 to 25 C]
    S5 -- No --> S7{Is Material Corrosion Sensitive}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Dry Store with VCI Paper and No Acid Vapours]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Standard Ambient Dry Store 50 to 60 percent RH]
    S2 --> S10[Document and Apply FIFO Label]
    S4 --> S10
    S6 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "First-Expiry-First-Out" (FEFO) and state when it is preferred over FIFO in a packaging material warehouse.*

**Model Answer:**
FEFO is an inventory rotation policy in which the material with the **earliest expiry or retest date** is issued first, irrespective of the order in which it was received. It is preferred over FIFO for materials that possess a **time-dependent quality attribute** such as inks, adhesives, solvent-based coatings, PVdC-coated films, and sterilization-grade laminates, where the technical specification (e.g., viscosity, bond strength, chlorine retention) degrades over time. FIFO, in contrast, is used for **inert** substrates (glass, tinplate, corrugated) where the physical/chemical properties are stable for years.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the ideal storage conditions (temperature and RH) for plastic films and aluminium foil laminates in a food-packaging warehouse.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Plastic films (PE, PP, PET, BOPP, multi-layer laminates):** 20–25 °C, 50–55% RH, dark or low-UV light, away from heat sources and direct sunlight. Reels must be stored **vertically on spindles**, not stacked flat, to prevent **telescoping** and core-crush.
- **Aluminium foil and laminates containing aluminium:** 20–25 °C, **< 50% RH**, no acid or alkaline vapours, hermetically PE-wrapped pallets. Foil is highly susceptible to **pitting corrosion** in chloride-rich humid air.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **five key functions of a packaging material warehouse**, with reference to the **climatic, hygienic, and inventory-control** requirements under FSSAI / GMP guidelines.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Using the **Arrhenius equation**, compute the reduction in shelf life of a multilayer PET/PE film stored at 35 °C compared to 25 °C, given $E_a = 80$ kJ·mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, and a real-time shelf life at 25 °C of 30 months.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q3(a):**

The five key functions of a packaging material warehouse are:

1. **Preservation of functional properties** — Maintaining temperature, RH, and light conditions so that the barrier, mechanical, and sealing properties of films, foils, and laminates are not degraded before use. **Target:** 20–25 °C and 50–55% RH for most plastic-based materials.
2. **Prevention of contamination** — Segregating food-contact stocks from non-food-contact stocks, chemicals, inks, and cleaning agents; using **colour-coded racking** and a defined **positive-pressure HVAC** system to exclude dust and pests.
3. **Inventory traceability** — Maintaining a **batch-wise logbook** with COA, receipt date, retest date, and issue records, enabling a one-step forward and one-step backward trace as mandated by **FSSAI Schedule 4**.
4. **Pest and rodent control** — Routine **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** with bait stations, flying-insect monitors, and quarterly fumigation cycles; sealed floor drains; rodent-proof doors.
5. **Workplace safety and statutory compliance** — Fire-rated storage for solvent-based inks, MSDS availability, earthing for metal coils, FEFO rotation for hazardous chemicals, and a calibrated **temperature/RH data logger** with 24/7 monitoring.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q3(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$\frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 \cdot T_2} = \frac{10}{298 \times 308} = \frac{10}{91{,}784} = 1.0896 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent:**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314} = 9623.3 \text{ K}$$

$$\text{Exponent} = 9623.3 \times 1.0896 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0486$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration factor:**

$$\alpha = e^{1.0486} = 2.854$$

**Step 5 — Real shelf life at 35 °C:**

$$t_{real} = \frac{t_{ref}}{\alpha} = \frac{30}{2.854} \approx 10.51 \text{ months}$$

> **Answer:** Shelf life falls from **30 months to ~10.5 months** — a **65% loss** in serviceable life for a 10 °C rise.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Differentiate between **FIFO** and **FEFO** with suitable examples of packaging materials for each. Add a note on why **line-side stock** must be limited to 24–48 hours of production.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **specific storage requirements** for **(i)** corrugated fibreboard, **(ii)** glass bottles, and **(iii)** tinplate coils, citing the relevant **Indian Standard (IS)** codes and the failure mode addressed by each control.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q4(a):**

**FIFO (First In First Out):** Material issued in the order of receipt. Used for **stable, non-perishable** substrates — e.g., **corrugated boxes, glass bottles, tinplate cans, HDPE crates** — whose mechanical and chemical properties are time-invariant under recommended storage.

**FEFO (First Expiry First Out):** Material issued in the order of earliest expiry or retest date. Used for **time-sensitive** stocks — e.g., **PVdC-coated films (retest 6 months), solvent-based inks (shelf life 12 months), polyurethane adhesives (pot life 6 months), sterilization reels (retest 3 months)**.

**Why line-side stock is limited to 24–48 h:**
1. **Climatic drift** — Line-side areas rarely have the same HVAC precision as the main store; longer dwell times cause moisture pickup and T gradients.
2. **Mechanical damage** — Stock kept beside the line risks **splashes of product, lubricants, or cleaning agents**, and is exposed to **operator traffic**.
3. **Inventory accuracy** — A short pull window simplifies **reconciliation at end of shift** and reduces the risk of **misuse or pilferage** of premium laminates.
4. **FIFO discipline** — A 24-h pull window forces strict rotation and prevents **obsolete reels** from being forgotten on the line.

---

#### **Model Answer to Q4(b):**

**(i) Corrugated Fibreboard:**
- **IS 2771** (corrugated fibreboard) and **IS 7063** (boxes).
- **Conditions:** RH < 70%, T < 30 °C, dry pallet, away from direct sunlight.
- **Failure addressed:** Loss of **Edge Crush Test (ECT)** and **Bursting Strength (BS)** due to fibre-bond hydrolysis at high RH. Prolonged high humidity causes **flute delamination** and box collapse in the stack.

**(ii) Glass Bottles:**
- **IS 1392** (glass containers) and **IS 11011** (storage of bottles).
- **Conditions:** Stored on **slip-sheets or plastic pails**, max stacking height as per supplier's stacking test, T < 35 °C, no thermal shock (e.g., avoid moving cold pallets into hot sun).
- **Failure addressed:** **Thermal-stress fracture** (especially in flint and amber glass with high coefficient of thermal expansion) and **scratches** at the contact line that weaken the sidewall.

**(iii) Tinplate Coils and Sheets:**
- **IS 9993** and **IS 1993** (tinplate for food cans).
- **Conditions:** Dry storage with **VCI (Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor) paper**, RH < 60%, no acid or chloride vapours, no contact with wet wood pallets.
- **Failure addressed:** **"Rust" or "white rust"** (tin-oxide) formation that ruins the **enamel lacquer adhesion** and causes **can corrosion** in the finished product, leading to **hydrogen swells** in acidic foods.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "FIFO" with "FEFO":** Examiners award zero if you interchange these in the answer script. Remember — FIFO = order of receipt; FEFO = order of expiry/retest.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Arrhenius numericals:** $E_a$ must be in **J·mol⁻¹** (not kJ·mol⁻¹) and $T$ in **Kelvin** (not °C). This single slip can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Writing "cool and dry" without numbers:** The KTU 2024 marking scheme penalizes qualitative-only answers for storage questions. Always quote **T (°C)** and **RH (%)** explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up Cobb value, Burst strength, and ECT:** Cobb = water absorption (g/m²); Burst = bursting strength (kPa); ECT = edge crush (kN/m). Examiners test this distinction every year.
> 5. **Ignoring the FIFO/FEFO aspect in long answers on warehouses:** Even if the question focuses on climatic controls, examiners expect **inventory rotation** to be mentioned as the **fifth** function of a warehouse.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **FSSAI** mandates traceable storage of all food-contact packaging materials under **Schedule 4** of the Licensing Regulations, 2011.
- **Plastic films & laminates** → 20–25 °C, 50–55% RH, dark, vertical spindle storage, FIFO.
- **Paper & paperboard** → 50 ± 5% RH to prevent curl and loss of burst strength; condition 24 h before use.
- **Aluminium foil** → < 50% RH, no acid/alkaline vapours, hermetically wrapped.
- **Glass** → Cool (T < 35 °C), slip-sheet separated, no thermal shock.
- **Tinplate / TFS** → VCI paper, dry, no chlorides; watch for **tin pest** below 13 °C.
- **Inks & adhesives** → 15–25 °C, sealed drums, **FEFO**, retest-date label.
- **FIFO** for inert substrates; **FEFO** for time-sensitive inks, adhesives, and laminates.
- **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — every 10 °C rise roughly **doubles** polymer degradation.
- **Q₁₀ rule:** Typical value 2–3 for plastic films.
- **Cobb value** (IS 1060 Part III) measures water absorption of paperboard in **g/m²**.
- **Line-side stock** should be limited to **24–48 hours** of production to maintain quality.
- **Henderson equation** is the empirical sorption isotherm used to predict $M_{eq}$ at given T and RH.
- **Clausius–Clapeyron** describes how $a_w$ shifts with T at constant moisture content.
- **HACCP / GMP** principles must govern warehouse hygiene — pest control, segregation, calibration of T/RH loggers.
- **MSDS** must be available for every hazardous chemical stored (solvents, adhesives, inks).
- **VCI paper** inhibits corrosion of metal coils during storage and transit.
- **Pallet shrink-wrap** should be **UV-stabilized** for outdoor-stored materials to prevent film degradation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Properties and Barrier Properties of Packaging Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT635 - ADVANCES IN FOOD PACKAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Physical Properties and Barrier Properties of Packaging Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Physical Properties" of Packaging Materials?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT635)** syllabus, the *physical properties* of a packaging material refer to those measurable, non-chemical attributes that describe **how the material behaves under mechanical, thermal, optical, and dimensional stresses** during processing, distribution, storage, and end-use.

These include:
- **Mechanical** — tensile strength, Young's modulus, elongation at break, tear resistance, impact strength, puncture resistance, bursting strength.
- **Optical** — transmittance, haze, gloss, opacity, clarity.
- **Thermal** — melting point ($T_m$), glass transition temperature ($T_g$), heat deflection temperature (HDT), heat seal strength, coefficient of thermal expansion.
- **Dimensional / Surface** — density, coefficient of friction (COF), surface energy, contact angle, thickness, basis weight (GSM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** *Physical properties dictate whether a packaging film can be processed (extrusion, lamination, forming), survive logistics (drop, stack, vibration), and protect the food product from physical hazards (light, oxygen, water vapour, mechanical damage).*

## 1.2 What Are "Barrier Properties"?

**Barrier properties** describe the material's resistance to the permeation (mass-transfer) of gases, vapours, liquids, aromas, and light through its bulk structure.

The four classical barrier attributes evaluated in food packaging are:

| Symbol | Property | Standard Metric | Common Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **WVTR** | Water Vapour Transmission Rate | **moisture permeation rate** | g / m² · day |
| **OTR** | Oxygen Transmission Rate | gas permeation rate | cm³ / m² · day · atm |
| **COTR / CO₂TR** | Carbon Dioxide Transmission Rate | gas permeation rate | cm³ / m² · day · atm |
| **AVTR** | Aroma / Volatile Transmission Rate | flavour permeation rate | g / m² · day |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Wall and Sieve" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a packaging film as a *brick wall* protecting a *delicate room (the food)*.
> - **Physical properties** = the wall's *strength, colour, smoothness, and heat tolerance*. A weak wall collapses; a rough wall scratches the food.
> - **Barrier properties** = the wall's *tightness*. Tiny pores let rain (oxygen, water vapour) seep in. A *porous wall* = poor barrier; a *dense crystalline wall* (e.g., EVOH, aluminium foil) = excellent barrier.
>
> **In one sentence:** *Physical properties tell you whether the package survives the journey; barrier properties tell you whether the food inside survives the journey.*

## 1.4 Why This Topic Is a KTU High-Weight Module

Most KTU end-semester questions on Module 4 (last 2 modules of the syllabus) revolve around **two computational clusters**:
1. **Permeation calculations** (steady-state flux, time-lag, activation energy).
2. **Material selection logic** (matching polymer barrier profile to a food product).

Mastering this module directly addresses **Course Outcomes CO3** and **CO4** of PEFTT635 — *"Evaluate advanced packaging materials and recommend suitable barrier systems for specific food products."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Physical Property Evaluation

### (a) Mechanical Behaviour — The Stress–Strain Response
1. **Tensile Strength (TS):** Maximum stress a film sustains before breaking.
   $$\sigma_{TS} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} \quad \text{[MPa]}$$
2. **Young's Modulus (E):** Slope of the linear elastic region; stiffness indicator.
3. **Elongation at Break ($\varepsilon_b$):** Percentage increase in length before fracture.
4. **Tear Resistance:** Force required to propagate a pre-cut tear (Elmendorf test).
5. **Puncture / Impact Resistance:** Energy absorbed before perforation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Packaging Logic:** Films with **low modulus (flexible)** — like LDPE — suit pouch applications. Films with **high modulus (rigid)** — like PET or PS — suit trays and bottles.

### (b) Optical Behaviour — Light–Matter Interaction
- **Transmittance ($T$):** Fraction of incident light passing through; governs *see-through* clarity.
- **Haze ($H_z$):** Percentage of transmitted light deviated more than **2.5°** by forward scattering. High haze = *frosted/foggy*.
- **Gloss ($G$):** Specular reflectance at a defined angle (commonly **60°**). High gloss = *shiny surface*.
- **Opacity:** Inverse of transmittance for opaque (e.g., aluminium-foil-laminate) structures.

### (c) Thermal Behaviour — Phase Transitions & Sealing
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** Temperature where amorphous polymer transitions from glassy to rubbery state. Below $T_g$ the polymer is rigid; above, it is flexible.
- **Melting Temperature ($T_m$):** Phase change in semi-crystalline polymers.
- **Heat Seal Strength:** Force per unit width required to peel a heat-sealed joint. Critical for **form-fill-seal (FFS)** operations.
- **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT):** Temperature at which a polymer deflects **0.25 mm** under a load of **0.45 MPa** or **1.82 MPa**.

### (d) Dimensional / Frictional Behaviour
- **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume — affects yield and cost.
- **Coefficient of Friction (COF):** Ratio of frictional to normal force between film and a reference surface. Critical for *machinability* on HFFS lines.
- **Surface Energy / Contact Angle:** Governs printability and lamination adhesion.

### (e) Barrier Behaviour — The Permeation Triplet

Permeation through a polymer film is governed by **three coupled physical phenomena**, conceptually serialised as:

1. **Sorption** of the penetrant on the *upstream* face (Henry's Law + Langmuir).
2. **Diffusion** of the penetrant through the bulk (Fick's Laws).
3. **Desorption** from the *downstream* face into the receiving medium.

The **Permeability Coefficient ($P$)** is the product:

$$P = D \times S$$

where $D$ = diffusion coefficient $[m^2 \cdot s^{-1}]$ and $S$ = solubility coefficient.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permeation equations are the most-tested numerical cluster in KTU Module 4. Memorise all entries below.**

| # | Equation | Expression | Standard Unit | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Steady-state flux (Fick's 1st Law) | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | mol / m² · s | Driving force is concentration gradient |
| 2 | Permeability coefficient | $P = D \cdot S$ | mol · m / m² · s · Pa | Bulk transport capability of film |
| 3 | Steady-state permeation rate | $\dfrac{Q}{t} = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | mol / s | Quantity $Q$ permeating in time $t$ |
| 4 | Water Vapour Transmission Rate | $WVTR = \dfrac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | g / m² · day | Practical barrier metric for moisture |
| 5 | Oxygen Transmission Rate | $OTR = \dfrac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | cm³ / m² · day · atm | Shelf-life governing metric |
| 6 | Time-lag (diffusion constant) | $\theta = \dfrac{L^2}{6D}$ | s | Experimental method to determine $D$ |
| 7 | Arrhenius temperature dependence | $P = P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_p}{RT}\right)$ | — | Permeability rises exponentially with $T$ |
| 8 | Linearised Arrhenius form | $\ln P = \ln P_0 - \dfrac{E_p}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | — | Slope gives activation energy $E_p$ |
| 9 | Henry's Law (sorption) | $C = S \cdot p$ | mol / m³ | Linear sorption at low concentration |
| 10 | Henry's constant relation | $S = \dfrac{C}{p}$ | mol / m³ · Pa | Solubility coefficient definition |
| 11 | Permeance (practical metric) | $\text{Permeance} = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta p}$ | mol / m² · s · Pa | Inverse of resistance |
| 12 | Barrier Improvement Factor | $BIF = \dfrac{P_{\text{reference}}}{P_{\text{improved}}}$ | dimensionless | Comparative barrier enhancement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** Throughout KTU answer sheets, examiners expect unit conversions **every time** — especially between **WVTR in g/m²·day** and **OTR in cm³/m²·day·atm**. Losing unit-marks is the **single biggest reason** students drop from full-marks to 12/14.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Snack-food industry** (potato chips, biscuits): require *very low* WVTR → use **BOPP / metallised PET / aluminium foil laminates**.
- **Carbonated beverages** (CSD): require *moderate* OTR, *very low* CO₂TR → use **PET bottle** + **EVOH tie-layer** in multi-layer structures.
- **MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging) of fresh meat**: requires *high* OTR (≈ 10,000 cm³/m²·day) to maintain 70–80% O₂ — **PE/EVA** films are chosen.
- **Retort pouches (ready-to-eat meals)**: require *very high* thermal stability + *very low* OTR — use **PET / aluminium foil / CPP** tri-laminates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Permeation Equation (Fick's First Law + Henry's Law)

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 4 examinations.

**Step 1 — Statement of Fick's First Law.**

Molar flux $J$ of a penetrant diffusing through a film is proportional to its concentration gradient:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient $[m^2 \cdot s^{-1}]$ and $C$ is the concentration $[mol \cdot m^{-3}]$.

**Step 2 — Assumption of Steady State.**

At steady state, the flux $J$ is constant in time and independent of position $x$. Integrating across the film thickness from $x = 0$ to $x = L$:

$$\int_{0}^{L} J \cdot dx = -\int_{C_1}^{C_2} D \cdot dC$$

Since $D$ is assumed constant (isotropic, homogeneous polymer), and $J$ is constant:

$$J \cdot L = -D \cdot (C_2 - C_1) = D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Henry's Law at Both Film Surfaces.**

At the upstream face (high partial pressure $p_1$):

$$C_1 = S \cdot p_1$$

At the downstream face (low partial pressure $p_2$):

$$C_2 = S \cdot p_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Integrated Equation.**

$$J \cdot L = D \cdot S \cdot (p_1 - p_2)$$

$$J \cdot L = P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)$$

where $P = D \cdot S$ is the **permeability coefficient**.

**Step 5 — Express the Final Permeation Rate Equation.**

Rearranging for the flux:

$$J = \frac{P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

The quantity $Q$ permeating through area $A$ in time $t$ is therefore:

$$\frac{Q}{t} = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** This equation says *permeation is inversely proportional to thickness*. Doubling the film thickness halves the permeation rate — but doubles the material cost. Packaging engineers therefore choose the **minimum thickness** that delivers the **target shelf-life**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Permeability

**Step 1 — Phenomenological Assumption.**

Empirically, permeability follows an Arrhenius-type law:

$$P = P_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_p}{R \cdot T}\right)$$

where $P_0$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_p$ is the **activation energy of permeation** $[\text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}]$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$, and $T$ is absolute temperature $[\text{K}]$.

**Step 2 — Linearise by Taking the Natural Logarithm.**

$$\ln P = \ln P_0 - \frac{E_p}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret as a Straight Line.**

A plot of $\ln P$ versus $1/T$ is a straight line:
- **Slope** $= -E_p / R$ → from which $E_p$ is calculated.
- **Intercept** $= \ln P_0$ → the pre-exponential factor.

**Step 4 — Practical Consequence for Food Packaging.**

Higher storage temperatures **dramatically increase** the barrier problem. For a typical polymer with $E_p \approx 40 \text{ kJ/mol}$, raising the temperature from **25 °C** to **45 °C** increases permeability by approximately:

$$\frac{P_{45}}{P_{25}} = \exp\!\left[-\frac{40000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{318} - \frac{1}{298}\right)\right] \approx 3.5$$

This is why **chilled distribution chains** are used for oxygen-sensitive foods — they reduce $T$, which exponentially reduces $P$, which exponentially extends shelf life.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-Lag Method for Diffusion Coefficient

When a film is suddenly exposed to a permeant, the downstream flux rises from zero and asymptotically approaches the steady-state value. The **time-lag** $\theta$ is the intercept of the asymptote on the time axis:

$$\theta = \frac{L^2}{6 \cdot D}$$

Solving for $D$:

$$D = \frac{L^2}{6 \cdot \theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Experimental Relevance:** The *time-lag apparatus* (Daynes-Barrer method) is the **standard laboratory technique** for measuring $D$ in polymer films. A 7-mark KTU question on this is a *very high-probability* paper setter target.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Barrier Performance of Common Polymers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is a KTU examiner favourite.** Knowing the *order-of-magnitude* barrier ranking allows you to answer 14-mark design questions convincingly.

| Polymer | OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹) | WVTR (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | Typical Food Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **EVOH (ethylene vinyl alcohol)** | 0.5 – 5 | 15 – 60 | High-barrier tie layer in retort pouches |
| **PVDC (polyvinylidene chloride)** | 5 – 25 | 1 – 5 | Cheese, meat wrap (Saran™) |
| **PET (polyethylene terephthalate)** | 50 – 100 | 15 – 25 | Beverage bottles, snack laminates |
| **PVC (rigid)** | 100 – 300 | 25 – 40 | Blister packs, fresh produce |
| **HDPE** | 1500 – 3000 | 5 – 12 | Milk bottles, grocery bags |
| **LDPE** | 7000 – 12000 | 15 – 25 | Bread bags, frozen-food pouches |
| **PP (cast / oriented)** | 2500 – 4000 | 8 – 15 | Hot-fill bottles, snack wrappers |
| **Aluminium foil (25 µm)** | ~ 0 | ~ 0 | Aseptic cartons, retort pouches |
| **Metallised BOPP** | 5 – 30 | 0.3 – 1 | Snack food bags, biscuit wrappers |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A **LDPE film** of thickness **$L = 50 \, \mu m$** is used to package a moisture-sensitive biscuit. The water-vapour permeability is $P_{H_2O} = 6 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{mol} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1}$. The partial-pressure difference across the film is $\Delta p = 1500 \, \text{Pa}$. Calculate the **steady-state WVTR in g/m²·day**. (Molar mass of $H_2O = 18 \text{ g/mol}$.)

**Solution:**

$$WVTR = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} = \frac{(6 \times 10^{-13})(1500)}{50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$WVTR = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

Converting to g/m²·day:

$$WVTR = (1.8 \times 10^{-5})(18)(86400) \approx 28.0 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Forgetting to convert **mol/s → g/day** is the most common mark-loser in KTU scripts. Always state the conversion factor explicitly.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Packaging Material Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Packaging Material Properties] --> B1[Mechanical Properties]
    A0 --> B2[Optical Properties]
    A0 --> B3[Thermal Properties]
    A0 --> B4[Dimensional and Surface Properties]
    A0 --> B5[Barrier Properties]

    B1 --> C1a[Tensile Strength]
    B1 --> C1b[Young Modulus]
    B1 --> C1c[Elongation at Break]
    B1 --> C1d[Tear Resistance]
    B1 --> C1e[Puncture Resistance]

    B2 --> C2a[Transmittance]
    B2 --> C2b[Haze]
    B2 --> C2c[Gloss]
    B2 --> C2d[Opacity]

    B3 --> C3a[Glass Transition Tg]
    B3 --> C3b[Melting Point Tm]
    B3 --> C3c[Heat Seal Strength]
    B3 --> C3d[Heat Deflection Temperature]

    B4 --> C4a[Density]
    B4 --> C4b[Coefficient of Friction]
    B4 --> C4c[Surface Energy]
    B4 --> C4d[Thickness and GSM]

    B5 --> C5a[WVTR Moisture Barrier]
    B5 --> C5b[OTR Oxygen Barrier]
    B5 --> C5c[COTR Carbon Dioxide Barrier]
    B5 --> C5d[Aroma Barrier]
    B5 --> C5e[Light Barrier]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Solution-Diffusion Permeation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Up[Upstream Side High Partial Pressure p1] -->|Sorption Henry Law| Surf1[Film Upstream Surface]
    Surf1 -->|Diffusion Fick Law| Bulk[Polymer Bulk Thickness L]
    Bulk -->|Diffusion Fick Law| Surf2[Film Downstream Surface]
    Surf2 -->|Desorption Henry Law| Down[Downstream Side Low Partial Pressure p2]

    Surf1 --> Perm1[Step 1 Concentration C1 equals S times p1]
    Bulk --> Perm2[Step 2 Steady state flux J equals P times p1 minus p2 divided by L]
    Surf2 --> Perm3[Step 3 Concentration C2 equals S times p2]
    Perm1 --> Perm2
    Perm2 --> Perm3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Multi-Layer Barrier Film Functional Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Outer Print Substrate BOPP or PET] --> L2[Layer 2 Adhesive or Tie Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Primary Barrier EVOH or PVDC or Al Foil]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Adhesive or Tie Layer]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Inner Sealant PE or CPP]

    L1 --> F1[Function Printability and Mechanical Strength]
    L3 --> F3[Function OTR and WVTR Resistance]
    L5 --> F5[Function Heat Seal and Food Contact]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Each layer in a real laminate **adds a separate permeation resistance in series**. For *n* layers, the total resistance is the sum:

$$ \frac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{P_i} $$

> This equation is often asked as a **Part-A (3-mark)** application question in KTU.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the *water vapour transmission rate (WVTR)* of a packaging film. State its standard unit and explain how it influences the shelf-life of a moisture-sensitive product such as a **biscuit**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
WVTR is the steady-state mass of water vapour that permeates through a unit area of packaging film per unit time, under defined conditions of temperature and relative humidity gradient across the film. Standard unit: **g / m² · day**.
For a moisture-sensitive food such as a biscuit, the texture (crispness) is lost once the product's moisture content rises above a *critical water activity* ($a_w \approx 0.40$ for crisp biscuits). A film with **low WVTR** (e.g., metallised BOPP, $WVTR \approx 0.3 - 1 \, g \cdot m^{-2} \cdot day^{-1}$) slows this moisture ingress and extends the crisp shelf-life from a few weeks to **6–12 months**. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** What is the *time-lag* in the diffusion of gases through polymer films? Write the equation relating time-lag to the diffusion coefficient and film thickness.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The time-lag $\theta$ is the transient interval required for the downstream flux to reach steady state after the upstream permeant pressure is suddenly applied. It is determined graphically as the intercept of the steady-state asymptote on the time axis in a flux-vs-time plot.
The relationship is:

$$\theta = \frac{L^2}{6 \cdot D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \frac{L^2}{6 \cdot \theta}$$

A longer time-lag indicates a *smaller* diffusion coefficient — i.e., a *better* barrier film. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question A]**
>
> **Q-A (a)** Derive the steady-state permeation equation $\dfrac{Q}{t} = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ for a penetrant diffusing through a polymer film. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q-A (b)** A **BOPP film** of thickness **$L = 30 \, \mu m$** has oxygen permeability $P_{O_2} = 2.0 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{mol} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1}$. Calculate the **OTR** (in $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1} \cdot \text{atm}^{-1}$) when the partial-pressure difference is **$\Delta p = 0.21 \, \text{atm}$**. State two food applications where this OTR is acceptable. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Q-A (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law Statement:** *(1 mark)*
$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state integration across thickness L:** *(2 marks)*
$$J \cdot L = D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)$$

**Step 3 — Henry's Law application at the two film surfaces:** *(2 marks)*
$$C_1 = S \cdot p_1 \quad ; \quad C_2 = S \cdot p_2$$

**Step 4 — Definition of permeability coefficient $P = D \cdot S$ and final expression:** *(1 mark)*
$$J = \frac{P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Converting flux to cumulative quantity Q in time t through area A:** *(1 mark)*
$$\frac{Q}{t} = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

### Model Solution — Q-A (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Calculate flux $J$:** *(2 marks)*

$$J = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-12})(0.21 \times 101325)}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$J = \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-12})(21278.25)}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$J \approx 1.42 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mol/m²·s to cm³/m²·day:** *(2 marks)*

Using ideal-gas molar volume **24.0 L/mol = 24,000 cm³/mol** at ambient conditions:

$$J = (1.42 \times 10^{-6})(24000)(86400)$$

$$J \approx 2944 \, \text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Normalise by $\Delta p = 0.21$ atm:** *(1 mark)*

$$OTR = \frac{2944}{0.21} \approx 14{,}020 \, \text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1} \cdot \text{atm}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Acceptable food applications:** *(2 marks)*
- **Snack-food packaging** (potato chips, namkeen) — oxygen exposure acceptable, as the food is shelf-stable.
- **Outer layer of multi-layer laminate** combined with EVOH for medium-barrier requirement (e.g., pasta, bread).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question B]**
>
> **Q-B (a)** Explain the **Arrhenius-type temperature dependence** of permeability in polymer films. Derive the linearised form and discuss its practical significance in cold-chain logistics. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q-B (b)** Compare the **oxygen barrier performance** of LDPE, PET, EVOH, and aluminium foil. Justify the use of EVOH in a **3-layer PET/EVOH/PE retort pouch** for ready-to-eat meals. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Q-B (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State Arrhenius equation:** *(1 mark)*
$$P = P_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_p}{R \cdot T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm:** *(2 marks)*
$$\ln P = \ln P_0 - \frac{E_p}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 — Identify slope and intercept:** *(1 mark)*
A plot of $\ln P$ versus $1/T$ yields slope $-E_p/R$ and intercept $\ln P_0$.

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration:** *(1 mark)*
For $E_p = 40 \text{ kJ/mol}$, raising T from 25 °C (298 K) to 45 °C (318 K) increases P by factor ≈ 3.5.

**Step 5 — Cold-chain significance:** *(2 marks)*
Cold-chain (4 °C) reduces $T$, exponentially reducing $P$, exponentially extending shelf life. For every 10 °C reduction, oxygen-driven oxidation of fats, vitamin C, and colour pigments in fresh foods slows by ~50–70%.

### Model Solution — Q-B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Comparative ranking of OTR (highest to lowest):** *(2 marks)*

LDPE (≈ 10,000) > PET (≈ 80) >> EVOH (≈ 2) >> Al foil (≈ 0). LDPE is a poor oxygen barrier; aluminium foil is an *absolute* barrier.

**Step 2 — Why EVOH is not used alone:** *(2 marks)*
EVOH is sensitive to moisture — its OTR degrades 100× under humid retort conditions. Therefore EVOH is sandwiched between two moisture-resistant layers.

**Step 3 — Role of each layer in the 3-layer pouch:** *(2 marks)*
- **PET (outer)** — mechanical strength, printability, high-temperature resistance.
- **EVOH (middle)** — primary oxygen barrier.
- **PE (inner)** — heat sealability, food contact safety, moisture resistance.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** *(1 mark)*
The PET/EVOH/PE laminate exploits the **complementary barrier properties** of each polymer, achieving sub-1 cm³/m²·day OTR even after retort sterilisation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 4:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** (mol/s → g/day; Pa → atm) account for **≈ 40% of mark loss** in numerical problems. Always write the unit-conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing $D$, $S$, and $P$.** Examiners frequently *intentionally* give $D$ or $S$ and ask for $P$, or vice-versa. Show the relation $P = D \cdot S$ **every time**.
> 3. **Omitting the *steady-state* assumption.** Fick's first law applies at steady state. If you don't state this, you lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up WVTR and OTR units.** WVTR is reported *without* the pressure term (g/m²·day); OTR *requires* the /atm normaliser.
> 5. **No diagram in design questions.** A 14-mark "select a packaging for X" question **must** include a labelled cross-section of the proposed laminate. Examiners allot **2–3 marks** specifically for the diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet Dump:**

- **Permeation** = Sorption + Diffusion + Desorption; modelled by **Solution-Diffusion** mechanism.
- **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ → at steady state, $J \cdot L = P \cdot \Delta p$.
- **Permeability coefficient:** $P = D \cdot S$ — combines diffusive and solubility contributions.
- **Time-lag equation:** $\theta = L^2 / (6D)$ — used to experimentally determine $D$.
- **Arrhenius form:** $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ gives activation energy of permeation $E_p$.
- **OTR ranking (best → worst barrier):** Al foil > EVOH > PVDC > PET > PVC > PP > HDPE > LDPE.
- **WVTR ranking (best → worst barrier):** Al foil > metallised BOPP > HDPE > PP > LDPE ≈ PET > EVOH (EVOH is *poor* for moisture!).
- **Tensile strength, Young's modulus, elongation** define the **mechanical response** of the package to logistics stresses.
- **Optical properties** (transmittance, haze, gloss) define **consumer appeal** and **light barrier**.
- **Thermal properties** ($T_g$, $T_m$, HDT, heat-seal strength) define **processability** and **retort suitability**.
- **Multi-layer laminate resistance (in series):** $\dfrac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$.
- **Barrier Improvement Factor (BIF):** $BIF = P_{reference} / P_{improved}$.
- **Cold-chain** reduces $T$ → reduces $P$ exponentially → extends shelf-life linearly.
- **Standard testing standards:** ASTM E96 (WVTR), ASTM D3985 (OTR), ASTM F1927 (OTR at high humidity).
- **Always** show unit conversions and state the steady-state assumption in derivations.
- **Design questions** demand: (i) cross-section diagram, (ii) function of each layer, (iii) justification with reference to product $a_w$, $pH$, oxidation sensitivity.
